topics.title: Statement of an Optimization Problem
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statement of an Optimization Problem

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization is the act of obtaining the best result under given circumstances.** In engineering design and decision-making, it refers to the mathematical process of finding the most favorable (minimum or maximum) value of an objective function by systematically choosing input values from an allowable set, subject to constraints imposed by the physical, economic, or operational environment.

The **statement of an optimization problem** is the formal mathematical translation of a real-world decision-making scenario into a structured mathematical model consisting of:
1. **Design (Decision) Variables** — the controllable inputs
2. **Objective Function** — the criterion of effectiveness to be optimized
3. **Constraints** — the restrictions arising from physical, geometric, or operational limits

A generic optimization problem is written as:

$$\min_{X \in \mathbb{R}^n} \quad f(X)$$

subject to:

$$g_j(X) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_k(X) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

$$x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where $X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$ is the **design vector**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine you are planning a road trip from Kochi to Bengaluru using your car.**
> - **Design variables** = the choices you make (route, fuel type, driving speed, number of halts).
> - **Objective function** = the goal you wish to minimize — say, *total travel time* or *total fuel cost*.
> - **Constraints** = the unavoidable restrictions — limited fuel tank capacity, speed limits, total budget, time available.
>
> The optimization problem is essentially asking: *"Which combination of route, speed, and stops gives the least travel time while respecting fuel, budget, and speed-limit constraints?"*

Every engineering decision can be cast into this template: **choose inputs to optimize an output, while respecting real-world rules.**

---

## 1.3 Key Physical / Mathematical Notations

> [!NOTE]
> - $n$ — **number of design variables** (dimension of the problem)
> - $m$ — **number of inequality constraints**
> - $p$ — **number of equality constraints**
> - $X \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — design vector in real n-dimensional Euclidean space
> - **$f(X)$** — scalar objective function
> - **$S$** (or feasible region) — set of all $X$ satisfying every constraint
> - $X^*$ — **optimal design point** that delivers the extremum of $f$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Optimization Problem — Structural Breakdown

The formal **statement of an optimization problem** comprises **three inseparable pillars**:

### Pillar A — Design Variables (Decision Variables)
These are the **independent parameters** the designer is free to vary.
- Denoted $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$.
- Bundled into the **design vector** $X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$.
- May be **continuous** (e.g., diameter, length) or **discrete** (e.g., number of gears, material grade).
- Each $x_i$ has a lower bound $x_i^{(L)}$ and an upper bound $x_i^{(U)}$, known as **side constraints** or **bound constraints**.

### Pillar B — Objective Function
A scalar mathematical function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies performance.
- Engineering examples: weight, cost, deflection, stress, time, energy, profit.
- Minimization is conventional because maximizing $f(X)$ is equivalent to minimizing $-f(X)$.

### Pillar C — Constraints
Restrictions on the design vector originating from:
- **Geometric limits** (must fit in a given envelope),
- **Performance specifications** (must not exceed a stress limit),
- **Resource limits** (material, money, manpower),
- **Regulatory limits** (safety codes, environmental standards).

Two forms exist:
- **Inequality constraints** $g_j(X) \le 0$ — usually converted into standard form by subtracting the RHS.
- **Equality constraints** $h_k(X) = 0$ — usually arise from physical laws (e.g., energy balance, equilibrium).

---

## 2.2 The Feasible Region and Optimal Solution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The **feasible region** $S$ is the set of all design vectors that satisfy *every* constraint simultaneously:
>
> $$S = \{ X \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(X) \le 0,\ h_k(X) = 0,\ x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)} \}.$$

- A **feasible solution** is any $X \in S$.
- The **optimal solution** $X^*$ is the feasible point at which $f$ attains its minimum (or maximum) value $f(X^*)$.

### Local vs Global Optimum
- $X^*$ is a **local minimum** if $f(X^*) \le f(X)$ for all $X$ in a *neighbourhood* $N$ of $X^*$.
- $X^*$ is a **global minimum** if $f(X^*) \le f(X)$ for *all* $X \in S$.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Classification (KTU Syllabus Mandate)

> [!NOTE]
> Optimization problems are broadly classified based on the nature of $f$, the constraints, and the variables:

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Unconstrained** | No $g_j$ or $h_k$ active | Finding stationary points of $f(X)$ |
| **Equality-constrained** | Only $h_k(X)=0$ present | Lagrange multiplier problems |
| **Inequality-constrained** | $g_j(X)\le 0$ present | Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) problems |
| **Linear Programming (LP)** | $f$, $g$, $h$ all linear | Simplex method |
| **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** | Any function is nonlinear | Gradient-based methods |
| **Integer / Discrete** | $x_i$ must be integers | Branch-and-Bound |
| **Dynamic Programming** | Multi-stage decisions | Bellman recursion |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these notations and definitions — they appear verbatim in KTU university exams.**

| Symbol / Term | Meaning |
|---------------|---------|
| $X$ | Design vector of size $n \times 1$ |
| $n$ | Number of decision variables |
| $f(X)$ | Objective function (scalar) |
| $g_j(X) \le 0$ | Inequality constraint, $j = 1,\dots,m$ |
| $h_k(X) = 0$ | Equality constraint, $k = 1,\dots,p$ |
| $x_i^{(L)},\, x_i^{(U)}$ | Lower and upper bound on $x_i$ |
| $S$ | Feasible region (search space restricted by constraints) |
| $X^*$ | Optimal design point |
| $f(X^*)$ | Optimal objective value |
| $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Real n-dimensional Euclidean space |
| $m + p$ | Total number of functional constraints |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard form convention** (used in most KTU numerical problems):
> - Objective is **minimize** $f(X)$ (maximization rewritten by sign change).
> - Inequality is **$\le 0$** type (move RHS terms to LHS).
> - Equality is **$= 0$** type.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

The statement of an optimization problem is the **first and most critical step** in any real-world design project:
- **Mechanical:** Minimizing weight of a truss while keeping stress below yield.
- **Civil:** Optimal cross-section of a beam for minimum cost under load constraints.
- **Electrical:** Optimal power dispatch subject to generator capacity limits.
- **Manufacturing:** Optimal cutting pattern to minimize material wastage.
- **Management:** Optimal inventory policy minimizing holding + shortage cost.

> Without a precise mathematical statement, no algorithm — classical or modern — can solve the problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Constructing the Mathematical Statement — General Procedure

The translation of a word problem into a formal optimization model follows a disciplined sequence. Each step is critical and directly tested in KTU exams.

### Step 1 — Identify the Goal (Objective)
Read the problem statement carefully and ask: *"What is to be minimized or maximized?"*
- Common verbs: *minimize, maximize, optimize, reduce, increase, best, worst, minimum cost, maximum profit, least weight, shortest time*.

### Step 2 — Identify the Decision Variables
Ask: *"What are the parameters the designer can control?"*
- Example: For a cylindrical pressure vessel, $X = [r, h]^T$ where $r$ is radius and $h$ is height.

### Step 3 — Express the Objective Function Algebraically
Combine the design variables into a scalar expression $f(X)$.

### Step 4 — Identify and Express Every Constraint
Convert every physical, geometric, or operational limit into either $g_j(X) \le 0$ or $h_k(X) = 0$.

### Step 5 — Add Side (Bound) Constraints
If a variable has an inherent minimum/maximum (e.g., length > 0), include $x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$.

### Step 6 — Assemble into Standard Form
Bundle everything into the canonical template shown in §1.1.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Cylindrical Pressure Vessel (Derivation Walkthrough)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Design a closed cylindrical pressure vessel of minimum wall volume $V$ to contain a gas at pressure $p$. The inner diameter $d$ must be at least $D$, and the wall thickness $t$ must not exceed $T_{\max}$. Use a fixed ratio $h = k\,d$ between height and diameter.

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} d \\ t \end{bmatrix}, \quad h = k\,d$$

where $d$ is the inner diameter and $t$ is the wall thickness.

**Step 2 — Express the objective function (volume of metal used):**
The volume of the cylindrical shell plus two hemispherical caps is:

$$V = \pi d h t + \frac{4}{3} \pi \left[\left(\frac{d}{2} + t\right)^3 - \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3\right]$$

Substituting $h = k\,d$:

$$V(d,t) = \pi k d^2 t + \frac{4}{3}\pi\left[\left(\frac{d}{2} + t\right)^3 - \frac{d^3}{8}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Identify the constraints:**

*(a) Hoop-stress constraint* (to prevent bursting):

$$\sigma = \frac{p d}{2 t} \le \sigma_{\text{allow}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad g_1(X) = \frac{p d}{2 t} - \sigma_{\text{allow}} \le 0$$

*(b) Diameter bound:*

$$g_2(X) = D - d \le 0$$

*(c) Thickness bound:*

$$g_3(X) = t - T_{\max} \le 0$$

*(d) Positivity bounds:* $d > 0,\ t > 0$.

**Step 4 — Final standard-form statement:**

$$\min_{d,t} \; V(d,t) = \pi k d^2 t + \frac{4\pi}{3}\left[\left(\frac{d}{2} + t\right)^3 - \frac{d^3}{8}\right]$$

subject to:

$$g_1(d,t) = \frac{p d}{2 t} - \sigma_{\text{allow}} \le 0$$

$$g_2(d,t) = D - d \le 0$$

$$g_3(d,t) = t - T_{\max} \le 0$$

$$\text{with } d > 0,\ t > 0.$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Two-Variable NLP (Analytical Walkthrough)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \le 4$ and $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$.

**Step 1 — Variables:** $X = [x_1, x_2]^T$, so $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Objective:** $f(X) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 3)^2$ (sum of squared deviations from the point $(2, 3)$).

**Step 3 — Constraints:**
- $g_1(X) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 \le 0$ (rewritten in standard $\le 0$ form).
- Bound constraints: $x_1 \ge 0$ and $x_2 \ge 0$.

**Step 4 — Identify the unconstrained minimum:**
Setting $\nabla f = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 2$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 3) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 3$$

The unconstrained optimum is at $(2, 3)$.

**Step 5 — Check feasibility:**
At $(2, 3)$: $x_1 + x_2 = 5 > 4$. **Infeasible!**

**Step 6 — The constrained optimum lies on the active constraint $g_1(X) = 0$,** i.e., $x_1 + x_2 = 4$. Project the unconstrained minimum onto this line by moving along the steepest-descent direction $-\nabla f$:

$$\nabla f \big|_{(2,3)} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Since the unconstrained gradient vanishes, we examine the nearest point on $x_1 + x_2 = 4$ from $(2,3)$. The perpendicular foot is found by:

$$x_1^* = 2 + \lambda, \quad x_2^* = 3 + \lambda, \quad x_1 + x_2 = 4$$

$$\Rightarrow 5 + 2\lambda = 4 \Rightarrow \lambda = -0.5$$

So $x_1^* = 1.5,\ x_2^* = 2.5$.

**Step 7 — Compute the optimum value:**

$$f(X^*) = (1.5 - 2)^2 + (2.5 - 3)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.50$$

**Step 8 — Final Statement:**

$$X^* = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 2.5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad f(X^*) = 0.50, \quad X^* \in S$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Linear Programming Formulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A furniture company makes chairs (profit ₹200) and tables (profit ₹500). Each chair needs 2 hours of carpentry and 1 hour of painting. Each table needs 4 hours of carpentry and 3 hours of painting. Available: 40 carpentry hours and 30 painting hours. Formulate the LP.

**Step 1 — Variables:** $x_1$ = number of chairs, $x_2$ = number of tables.

**Step 2 — Objective (maximize profit):**

$$\max \; Z = 200 x_1 + 500 x_2$$

**Step 3 — Constraints (convert to $\le$ form for standard minimization template):**

Carpentry: $2x_1 + 4x_2 \le 40$
Painting: $x_1 + 3x_2 \le 30$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard minimization form by negating objective:**

$$\min \; f(X) = -200 x_1 - 500 x_2$$

subject to:

$$g_1(X) = 2x_1 + 4x_2 - 40 \le 0$$

$$g_2(X) = x_1 + 3x_2 - 30 \le 0$$

$$x_1 \ge 0,\ x_2 \ge 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every engineering problem solved using Linear Programming (Simplex), Nonlinear Programming (Kuhn–Tucker), or Dynamic Programming must **begin with a statement exactly like the one derived above.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anatomy of an Optimization Problem

The following block diagram maps how a real-world engineering scenario is systematically translated into a formal mathematical statement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real World Engineering Problem] --> B[Identify Decision Variables X]
    B --> C[Identify Objective Function fX]
    C --> D[Identify Constraints]
    D --> D1[Inequality Constraints gj X le 0]
    D --> D2[Equality Constraints hk X eq 0]
    D --> D3[Bound Constraints x L le x le x U]
    D1 --> E[Assemble Standard Form]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Define Feasible Region S]
    F --> G[Apply Optimization Algorithm]
    G --> H[Optimal Solution X Star]
    H --> I[f X Star Optimum Value]
    H --> J[Validate against Original Problem]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — Hierarchy of Optimization Problem Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B{Constraints Present?}
    B -- No --> C[Unconstrained Problem]
    B -- Yes --> D{Type of Constraints?}
    D -- Equality only --> E[Lagrange Multiplier Method]
    D -- Inequality --> F{KKT Conditions}
    F --> G[KKT First Order Conditions]
    F --> H[Feasible Direction Methods]
    A --> I{Nature of Objective?}
    I -- Linear + Linear Constraints --> J[Linear Programming LP]
    I -- Any nonlinear --> K[Nonlinear Programming NLP]
    I --> L{Variable Type?}
    L -- Integer --> M[Integer Programming IP]
    L -- Mixed --> N[Mixed Integer NLP]
    L -- Random --> O[Stochastic Programming]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Relationship Between Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Inputs]
        V[Decision Variables X1 to Xn]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS[Mathematical Model]
        F[Objective Function f X]
        G[Constraints gj and hk]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Outputs]
        S[Feasible Region S]
        OPT[Optimum X Star]
        FVAL[f X Star]
    end
    V --> F
    V --> G
    F --> S
    G --> S
    S --> OPT
    OPT --> FVAL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the general form of an optimization problem with n design variables. Explain each component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The general statement of an optimization problem in standard form is:

$$\min_{X \in \mathbb{R}^n} \; f(X)$$

subject to:

$$g_j(X) \le 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_k(X) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

$$x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Components explained:**
1. **Design vector $X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$** — the set of independent decision variables to be chosen.
2. **Objective function $f(X)$** — a scalar measure of performance to be minimized.
3. **Inequality constraints $g_j(X) \le 0$** — restrictions expressed as upper bounds.
4. **Equality constraints $h_k(X) = 0$** — fixed relationships (e.g., conservation laws).
5. **Bound constraints $x_i^{(L)}, x_i^{(U)}$** — physical limits on individual variables.
6. **$m$ and $p$** are the total number of inequality and equality constraints respectively.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a feasible solution and an optimal solution. What is a global optimum?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Feasible Solution** | Any design vector $X$ that satisfies *all* constraints of the problem (i.e., $X \in S$). |
| **Optimal Solution** | A feasible solution $X^*$ that gives the minimum (or maximum) value of the objective function. |
| **Local Optimum** | $X^*$ such that $f(X^*) \le f(X)$ for all $X$ in a small neighbourhood of $X^*$. |
| **Global Optimum** | $X^*$ such that $f(X^*) \le f(X)$ for *every* feasible point $X \in S$. |

> [!NOTE]
> Every global optimum is a local optimum, but the converse is **not** true. Most engineering problems accept a local optimum if it can be shown to be global through convexity analysis.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern**, Part B carries **14 marks** with an internal choice. Each sub-part is **7 marks**. The model answers below show the **complete step-by-step valuation key**.

---

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 1]`

**(a)** Define the term *Statement of an Optimization Problem*. With a neat block diagram, list and explain the essential components of an optimization problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm produces two products A and B. Each unit of A yields a profit of ₹50 and each unit of B yields a profit of ₹80. The production process uses two machines M1 and M2. Product A needs 2 hours on M1 and 1 hour on M2. Product B needs 1 hour on M1 and 3 hours on M2. The available hours per day are: M1 = 10 hours, M2 = 12 hours. Formulate this as a Linear Programming Problem in standard form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The *Statement of an Optimization Problem* is the mathematical formulation of a decision-making situation in which the goal is to find the best (minimum or maximum) value of a performance criterion by choosing the values of controllable variables within the limits imposed by the operating environment.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
INPUTS              MATHEMATICAL MODEL              OUTPUTS
-----------         --------------------            -----------
Decision Variables  ---->  Objective f(X)   ---->    Optimum X*
X = [x1,...,xn]T    ---->  Constraints gj,hk ---->    Optimum f(X*)
```

**Essential Components Explained (3 marks):**
1. **Decision Variables** — quantities the designer controls.
2. **Objective Function** — quantitative measure of effectiveness.
3. **Constraints** — physical, geometric, economic restrictions.
4. **Feasible Region** — set of all permissible design vectors.
5. **Optimal Solution** — the best point in the feasible region.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Variables (1 mark):**
Let $x_1$ = number of units of product A produced per day,
$x_2$ = number of units of product B produced per day.

**Step 2 — Objective Function (2 marks):**
Total daily profit to be maximized:

$$Z = 50 x_1 + 80 x_2$$

**Step 3 — Constraints (3 marks):**
Machine M1 hours: $2x_1 + x_2 \le 10$
Machine M2 hours: $x_1 + 3x_2 \le 12$
Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \ge 0$

**Step 4 — Standard Form (1 mark):**

$$\min \; f(X) = -50 x_1 - 80 x_2$$

subject to:

$$g_1(X) = 2x_1 + x_2 - 10 \le 0$$

$$g_2(X) = x_1 + 3x_2 - 12 \le 0$$

$$x_1 \ge 0,\quad x_2 \ge 0$$

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 1]`

**(a)** What is meant by the *feasible region* of an optimization problem? Explain with an example how the feasible region is constructed geometrically for a two-variable problem with inequality constraints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular box with an open top is to be constructed to have a volume of $32 \text{ m}^3$. The material for the base costs ₹40 per m² and the material for the sides costs ₹20 per m². Formulate the problem of finding the dimensions that minimize the cost. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The feasible region $S$ is the set of *all* points in the design space that simultaneously satisfy every constraint (equality, inequality, and bound constraints) of the problem. Mathematically:

$$S = \{ X \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(X) \le 0,\ j = 1,\dots,m;\ h_k(X) = 0,\ k = 1,\dots,p \}.$$

**Construction Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Plot each inequality $g_j(x_1, x_2) \le 0$ as a half-plane bounded by the line $g_j(x_1, x_2) = 0$.
2. For each, identify the correct side by substituting a test point (e.g., origin).
3. The feasible region is the **intersection** of all these half-planes.
4. Add bound constraints $x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$ to restrict further.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
For $f(x_1,x_2) = (x_1-4)^2 + (x_2-3)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \le 5$, $x_1 \ge 0$, $x_2 \ge 0$:
- $g_1$: line $x_1 + x_2 = 5$, feasible side toward origin.
- $g_2$, $g_3$: positive axes.
- Feasible region = triangle with vertices $(0,0)$, $(5,0)$, $(0,5)$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Variables (1 mark):**
Let $x_1$ = length (m), $x_2$ = width (m), $x_3$ = height (m) of the box.

**Step 2 — Objective Function (3 marks):**
- Base area = $x_1 x_2$ (cost ₹40/m²): cost = $40 x_1 x_2$.
- Four sides area = $2(x_1 x_3) + 2(x_2 x_3)$ (cost ₹20/m²): cost = $20[2 x_1 x_3 + 2 x_2 x_3] = 40 x_3(x_1 + x_2)$.

$$C(X) = 40 x_1 x_2 + 40 x_3(x_1 + x_2)$$

**Step 3 — Constraints (2 marks):**
- Volume: $h = V / (x_1 x_2)$, written as equality: $h_1(X) = x_1 x_2 x_3 - 32 = 0$.
- Bounds: $x_1, x_2, x_3 > 0$.

**Step 4 — Standard Form (1 mark):**

$$\min_{x_1, x_2, x_3} \; C(X) = 40 x_1 x_2 + 40 x_3(x_1 + x_2)$$

subject to:

$$h_1(X) = x_1 x_2 x_3 - 32 = 0$$

$$x_1 > 0,\ x_2 > 0,\ x_3 > 0$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Students often forget to convert maximization problems into minimization form by negating $f$. KTU expects the standard form $\min f(X) \le 0$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Standard Form of Inequality:** The convention is $g_j(X) \le 0$, NOT $g_j(X) \le b$. Move $b$ to the LHS. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing Variables:** Forgetting one of the decision variables (e.g., only using $x_1, x_2$ and not $x_3$) makes the formulation incomplete. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Forgetting Non-negativity Constraints:** Always state $x_i \ge 0$ explicitly. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing cm and m in numerical examples. Keep units consistent throughout. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize):**

- ☐ The **three pillars** of any optimization statement are: *design variables*, *objective function*, *constraints*.
- ☐ Standard form: **$\min f(X)$** subject to **$g_j(X) \le 0$**, **$h_k(X) = 0$**, and **bound constraints**.
- ☐ Maximization of $f(X)$ is rewritten as minimization of $-f(X)$.
- ☐ Inequality $a \le b$ becomes $g(X) = a - b \le 0$ in standard form.
- ☐ **Feasible region** = intersection of all constraint sets.
- ☐ **Local optimum** ≠ necessarily **global optimum** unless the problem is convex.
- ☐ **Equality constraints** typically arise from physical laws (volume fixed, mass balance).
- ☐ **Inequality constraints** typically arise from limits (stress ≤ allowable, time ≤ deadline).
- ☐ **Bound constraints** are simple range restrictions on individual variables.
- ☐ Always state **non-negativity** of decision variables explicitly.
- ☐ The number of decision variables $n$ defines the **dimension** of the problem.
- ☐ First step of solving *any* KTU numerical question: **write the mathematical statement** before applying any algorithm.
- ☐ Class of problem (LP, NLP, IP, DP, etc.) is decided by the *nature of $f$, $g$, $h$*, and the variables.
- ☐ A *complete* optimization statement includes the design vector notation $X = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Vector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Vector

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formal mathematical framework of **Engineering Optimization**, a **Design Vector** is an ordered collection (tuple) of all the independent, controllable parameters that completely and uniquely describe a candidate engineering design. It serves as the primary decision variable set that an optimization algorithm manipulates to seek the best possible design.

The design vector is conventionally represented in column-matrix notation as:

$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ \vdots \\ x_n \end{Bmatrix} = \left[ x_1,\; x_2,\; \dots,\; x_n \right]^T
$$

Here, each scalar component $x_i$ is called a **Design Variable**, and $n$ denotes the **dimensionality** of the design space. The complete set of all possible values that $X$ can assume spans a hyperspace known as the **Design Space** (denoted $\mathcal{D}$ or $\mathbb{R}^n$ for continuous variables).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A design vector is the mathematical representation of a design comprising all the independent design variables. The number of independent design variables constitutes the dimensionality of the design problem, and the set of all feasible designs forms the design space.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for a First-Time Learner

Imagine you are a **chef preparing a signature dish** in a five-star restaurant. To describe the dish fully, you must specify:
1. The **quantity of salt** (in grams)
2. The **amount of spice mix** (in teaspoons)
3. The **cooking temperature** (in °C)
4. The **cooking time** (in minutes)

If you write these four parameters as an ordered list — `(Salt, Spice, Temperature, Time)` — that ordered list **is your design vector**. Now, the chef (acting as the *optimization algorithm*) adjusts these four knobs to produce the **most delicious dish** (the *objective function*).

> **[Salt, Spice, Temperature, Time]ᵀ = Design Vector of the Recipe**

In engineering, instead of cooking variables, we deal with physical parameters like:
- Diameter of a shaft (mm)
- Thickness of a beam (mm)
- Spring stiffness (N/m)
- Material density (kg/m³)

Every engineering artifact, from a simple cotter pin to a complex jet turbine, can be uniquely characterized by such a design vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The design vector is the *mathematical fingerprint* of a design. If two design vectors are identical, the two designs are physically identical (assuming deterministic models). Conversely, if any component differs, the designs are different.

---

## 1.3 Terminological Hierarchy (KTU Standard)

| Hierarchical Term | Mathematical Symbol | Plain Language |
|---|---|---|
| Design Variable | $x_i$ | A single knob the engineer can turn |
| Design Vector | $X = [x_1, \ldots, x_n]^T$ | Complete set of all knobs |
| Number of Variables | $n$ | Number of independent knobs |
| Design Space | $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ | Every possible combination of knob settings |
| Feasible Region | $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathcal{D}$ | Combinations that obey all engineering rules |
| Optimal Design | $X^*$ | The single best combination in feasible region |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The design vector is the **fundamental input** to any optimization problem. Without identifying the design vector, no optimization can be formulated.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Design Variables

Design variables are classified along three principal axes:

**(a) By Continuity:**
- **Continuous Variables:** Can take any real value within a range (e.g., length, diameter). Mathematically $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Discrete Variables:** Restricted to specific, often non-numeric, choices (e.g., standard wire gauges, ISO metric sizes). Mathematically $x_i \in \{a, b, c, \ldots\}$.
- **Integer Variables:** Must be whole numbers (e.g., number of reinforcing bars in a beam). $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**(b) By Dependence:**
- **Independent Variables:** Freely chosen by the designer; these form the components of the design vector.
- **Dependent Variables:** Determined implicitly by the independent variables through physical or mathematical relationships (e.g., stress, deflection).

**(c) By Hierarchy:**
- **Primary / Outer Variables:** Major design decisions.
- **Secondary / Inner Variables:** Subordinate decisions that are themselves functions of the outer variables (handled in nested sub-problems).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Design Vector — Operational Breakdown

A design vector in engineering optimization is **not arbitrary**. It must satisfy five operational criteria, which KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **Completeness (Sufficiency):** The set of variables must describe the design fully. Omitting a relevant variable leads to an *under-parameterized* design space, and the true optimum may lie outside the search domain.

2. **Minimality (Independence):** The variables must be mutually independent. No component should be expressible as a function of the others; otherwise, redundancy inflates the dimensionality and slows convergence.

3. **Measurability / Controllability:** Every $x_i$ must be either physically controllable (a length, mass, voltage) or computable from controllable quantities.

4. **Boundedness:** Each variable must be confined to a permissible range:
$$
x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}
$$
where $x_i^{L}$ and $x_i^{U}$ are the **lower and upper bounds** (side constraints).

5. **Constraint Compatibility:** The vector must lie within the feasible design space defined by inequality and equality constraints:
$$
g_j(X) \leq 0, \quad h_k(X) = 0
$$

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Formalization

The general unconstrained design space is:
$$
\mathcal{D} = \left\{ X \in \mathbb{R}^n \;\Big|\; x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U},\; i = 1, 2, \ldots, n \right\}
$$

The **feasible design space** (after applying performance and behavioral constraints) is:
$$
\mathcal{S} = \left\{ X \in \mathcal{D} \;\Big|\; g_j(X) \leq 0,\; j = 1, \ldots, m;\; h_k(X) = 0,\; k = 1, \ldots, p \right\}
$$

The **objective function** $f(X)$ is mapped from the design space to the objective space:
$$
f: \mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n \longrightarrow \mathbb{R}
$$

Thus, the complete optimization problem is written in the canonical KTU form as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find} \quad & X = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T \\
\text{to} \quad & \text{minimize} \; f(X) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(X) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& h_k(X) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\
& x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Vector | $X = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ | Ordered list of independent design variables |
| 2 | Number of Variables | $n$ | Dimensionality of design space |
| 3 | Design Space | $\mathcal{D} = \mathbb{R}^n$ | Set of all mathematically possible designs |
| 4 | Side Constraint | $x_i^{L} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{U}$ | Lower and upper bounds on a variable |
| 5 | Inequality Constraint | $g_j(X) \leq 0$ | Performance/behavioral restriction |
| 6 | Equality Constraint | $h_k(X) = 0$ | Hard physical law (e.g., equilibrium) |
| 7 | Feasible Region | $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathcal{D}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{D}$ satisfying all constraints |
| 8 | Objective Function | $f(X): \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}$ | Scalar measure of design "goodness" |
| 9 | Optimal Design | $X^* = \arg\min f(X)$ | Design that minimizes (or maximizes) $f$ |
| 10 | Norm of Design Vector | $\vert \vert X \vert \vert = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2}$ | Euclidean distance of design from origin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** The design vector is **always** a column vector by definition. The transpose $X^T = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]$ is its row-vector representation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The design vector concept underpins **every branch of modern engineering design optimization**:

| Engineering Domain | Typical Components of Design Vector $X$ |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical (Pressure Vessel)** | Inner radius $r$, wall thickness $t$, head depth $h$ |
| **Civil (Beam Design)** | Width $b$, depth $d$, steel area $A_s$ |
| **Electrical (Filter Circuit)** | Resistance $R$, Capacitance $C$, Inductance $L$ |
| **Aerospace (Aircraft Wing)** | Chord $c$, sweep angle $\Lambda$, thickness ratio $t/c$ |
| **Industrial (Production Planning)** | Production lot sizes, inventory levels, workforce count |
| **Chemical (Reactor Design)** | Temperature $T$, pressure $P$, residence time $\tau$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In **structural optimization**, the design vector often contains cross-sectional dimensions and member geometries. In **topology optimization**, the design vector may contain elemental densities (0 to 1), yielding millions of variables.

---

## 2.5 Worked Example — Identifying the Design Vector

**Engineering Scenario:** Design a simply supported steel beam of length $L$ to support a uniformly distributed load $w$. The objective is to **minimize the cross-sectional area** $A$. Variables available: width $b$ and depth $d$. The maximum bending stress must not exceed $\sigma_{allow}$, and the maximum deflection must not exceed $\delta_{allow}$.

**Step 1 — Identify design variables:** $b$ (width) and $d$ (depth) are independent and controllable.
**Step 2 — Express the design vector:**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} b \\ d \end{Bmatrix}
$$
**Step 3 — Express the constraints:**
- Geometric: $b > 0$, $d > 0$
- Stress: $\dfrac{6wL^2}{b d^2} \leq \sigma_{allow}$
- Deflection: $\dfrac{5wL^4}{32 E b d^3} \leq \delta_{allow}$

**Step 4 — Express the objective:** Minimize $A = b \cdot d$.

This completes the formal problem statement using the design vector concept.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Physical Design to Mathematical Design Vector

A common KTU question is: *"Given a physical engineering design, derive its mathematical design vector representation."* Below is the systematic derivation procedure.

### Derivation Procedure

**Step 1 — List all engineering parameters that describe the design.**

Suppose a cylindrical pressure vessel is to be designed. The candidate parameters are: inner radius $r$, wall thickness $t$, internal pressure $P$, material yield stress $\sigma_y$, and length $\ell$. We tabulate:

$$
\text{Parameter Set} = \{ r, \; t, \; P, \; \sigma_y, \; \ell \}
$$

**Step 2 — Classify each parameter as *controllable* (decision) or *fixed* (constant).**

- $r$ → **Controllable** (designer chooses)
- $t$ → **Controllable** (designer chooses)
- $P$ → **Fixed** (operational requirement)
- $\sigma_y$ → **Fixed** (material property)
- $\ell$ → **Controllable** (but often fixed by tank-farm layout)

Assume only $r$ and $t$ are controllable. Then:

$$
\text{Independent Parameters} = \{ r, \; t \}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify independence.**

The variables $r$ and $t$ are mutually independent — neither can be expressed as a function of the other. Hence the dimensionality $n = 2$.

**Step 4 — Construct the design vector in standard column form.**

$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} r \\ t \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Establish side constraints from physical realizability.**

A real vessel requires $r > 0$ and $t > 0$ and $t \leq 0.2 r$ (to remain within thin-wall assumptions). Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1^{L} &= 0.1 \text{ m} \quad (\text{or specified minimum radius}) \\
x_1^{U} &= 2.0 \text{ m} \\
x_2^{L} &= 0.005 \text{ m} \\
x_2^{U} &= 0.1 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Add performance constraints.**

From membrane stress theory: $\sigma = \dfrac{P r}{t}$. The yield condition $\sigma \leq \sigma_y / n_{sf}$ (with safety factor $n_{sf}$) gives:

$$
g_1(X) = \frac{P x_1}{x_2} - \frac{\sigma_y}{n_{sf}} \leq 0
$$

The design vector is now fully characterized.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mapping from Physical Concepts to Design Vector

| Physical Concept | Mathematical Translation | Position in Vector |
|---|---|---|
| Independent variable | Scalar $x_i$ | One component |
| Physical constants ($E, \nu, \rho$) | Fixed parameters, not in vector | — |
| Bounds on variable | $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$ | Side constraints |
| Inequality restriction | $g_j(X) \leq 0$ | Constraint surface |
| Equality restriction | $h_k(X) = 0$ | Constraint hyperplane |
| Performance measure | $f(X) \in \mathbb{R}$ | Objective function |
| Whole design | Vector $X$ | All components together |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Dimensionality of Design Space

The **dimensionality** of the design space is formally derived as the number of independent parameters after eliminating all dependent quantities.

Consider a truss with $m$ members and $j$ joints in 2-D. The total unknowns (member lengths and orientations) are $2m$. Equilibrium equations at joints give $2j$ equations. Assuming $m > j$, the number of design degrees of freedom is:

$$
n = 2m - 2j
$$

This is the **dimensionality** of the design vector, and the design space $\mathcal{D}$ is $\mathbb{R}^n$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimensionality determines computational cost.** Each additional variable roughly *doubles* the search effort for exhaustive methods. Hence, the art of optimization is to keep $n$ as small as possible without losing fidelity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Normalization of Design Vector

A critical step in numerical optimization is *normalization*. Variables with vastly different magnitudes (e.g., $x_1 = 10^{-3}$ m, $x_2 = 10^3$ N) cause numerical ill-conditioning. We map each variable to a unit interval using:

$$
\bar{x}_i = \frac{x_i - x_i^L}{x_i^U - x_i^L}
$$

**Detailed algebraic steps:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & x_i \in [x_i^L, x_i^U] \\
\text{Step 1 — Shift origin to lower bound:} \quad & y_i = x_i - x_i^L \\
\text{Step 2 — Scale to unit length:} \quad & \bar{x}_i = \frac{y_i}{x_i^U - x_i^L} = \frac{x_i - x_i^L}{x_i^U - x_i^L} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \bar{x}_i \in [0, 1]
\end{aligned}
$$

For $x_i = x_i^L$: $\bar{x}_i = 0$. For $x_i = x_i^U$: $\bar{x}_i = 1$. This normalized design vector $\bar{X} = [\bar{x}_1, \ldots, \bar{x}_n]^T$ is then used by algorithms like genetic algorithms and simulated annealing.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A two-bar truss carries a vertical load $P = 20$ kN. The bar lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ are the design variables. Express the complete design vector with constraints if the allowable stress in each bar is $\sigma_{allow} = 150$ MPa and cross-sectional area $A = 10 \text{ cm}^2$ is fixed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Design vector:**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} L_1 \\ L_2 \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Member forces in terms of geometry (force equilibrium):**
$$
F_1 = \frac{P L_2}{\sqrt{L_1^2 - h^2}}, \quad F_2 = \frac{P L_1}{\sqrt{L_2^2 - h^2}}
$$

where $h$ is the vertical separation between supports.

**Step 3 — Stress constraints:**
$$
g_1(X) = \frac{F_1}{A} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$
$$
g_2(X) = \frac{F_2}{A} - \sigma_{allow} \leq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric constraints:**
$$
L_1 > h, \quad L_2 > h
$$

**Step 5 — Objective (e.g., minimize total volume of material):**
$$
V(X) = A (L_1 + L_2)
$$

The problem is now fully specified in standard KTU optimization form.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Role of the Design Vector in Optimization

The figure below depicts the design vector as the central *control input* flowing into the engineering system and the optimization algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    userIn["ENGINEER DECISION"] --> dvInput["DESIGN VECTOR X = x1 x2 ... xn TRANSPOSE"]
    dvInput --> sysModel["ENGINEERING SYSTEM MODEL"]
    sysModel --> objFn["OBJECTIVE FUNCTION f OF X"]
    sysModel --> consFn["CONSTRAINTS g OF X AND h OF X"]
    objFn --> evalOut["PERFORMANCE METRIC"]
    consFn --> feasOut["FEASIBILITY CHECK"]
    evalOut --> optLoop{"IMPROVE DESIGN"}
    feasOut --> optLoop
    optLoop -->|"Updated X"| dvInput
    optLoop -->|"Converged"| optResult["OPTIMAL DESIGN X STAR"]
    optResult --> realWorld["FABRICATE / BUILD ENGINEERING SYSTEM"]
```

> The loop above represents the **iterative essence of optimization**: the algorithm perturbs $X$, evaluates $f(X)$ and the constraints, and updates $X$ until no further improvement is possible.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Mapping of Design Space to Objective Space

A design vector lies in an $n$-dimensional design space. The objective function maps every point in design space to a scalar in the 1-D objective space. The figure below illustrates this mapping for $n = 2$ (a 2-D design space).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ds["DESIGN SPACE X in R2"]
        pt1["POINT X1 = 1 1"]
        pt2["POINT X2 = 2 1"]
        pt3["POINT X3 = 1 2"]
        pt4["OPTIMAL X STAR"]
    end
    subgraph os["OBJECTIVE SPACE f in R1"]
        v1["f X1 equals 2.0"]
        v2["f X2 equals 3.0"]
        v3["f X3 equals 3.0"]
        v4["f X STAR equals 0.5 MINIMUM"]
    end
    pt1 --> v1
    pt2 --> v2
    pt3 --> v3
    pt4 --> v4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** Many students lose marks by failing to draw the distinction between *design space* (where $X$ lives) and *objective space* (where $f(X)$ lives). Examiners reward clear labeling of both.

---

## 4.3 Architecture Diagram — Classification of Design Variables

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["DESIGN VECTOR COMPONENTS"]
    root --> cont["CONTINUOUS VARIABLES"]
    root --> disc["DISCRETE VARIABLES"]
    root --> intg["INTEGER VARIABLES"]
    cont --> c1["LENGTH DIAMETER VOLTAGE"]
    disc --> d1["GEAR TEETH ISO SIZES"]
    intg --> i1["NUMBER OF BARS PANELS"]
    root --> indep["INDEPENDENT VARIABLES"]
    root --> dep["DEPENDENT VARIABLES"]
    indep --> idesc["Form the design vector"]
    dep --> ddesc["Computed from independent ones"]
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Feasible vs Infeasible Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    space["DESIGN SPACE D superset of R superset n"]
    space --> bound["APPLY SIDE BOUNDS x L and x U"]
    bound --> boundSet["BOX CONSTRAINED SET B"]
    boundSet --> perform["APPLY PERFORMANCE CONSTRAINTS g OF X less than or equal 0"]
    perform --> feasSet["FEASIBLE DESIGN SPACE S"]
    feasSet --> opt["OPTIMAL X STAR lies in S"]
    boundSet -->|"if constraints violated"| infeas["INFEASIBLE DESIGNS REJECTED"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define a design vector. Explain with an example how the design variables are identified for an engineering problem.**

**Model Answer:**

A **design vector** is an ordered set of independent, controllable parameters that uniquely and completely describe an engineering design. Mathematically, it is represented as a column vector:
$$
X = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T
$$

**Identification procedure:**
1. List all parameters that influence the design.
2. Eliminate fixed parameters (material constants, loads, environmental conditions).
3. Eliminate dependent parameters (variables expressible as functions of others).
4. The remaining independent, controllable parameters form the design vector.

**Example — Design of a helical spring:** Independent variables are wire diameter $d$, mean coil diameter $D$, and number of active coils $N$. The design vector is:
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} d \\ D \\ N \end{Bmatrix}
$$
**(3 Marks — Complete, Concise, 1-mark for definition, 1-mark for procedure, 1-mark for example.)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Distinguish between design space and feasible design space with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Design Space $\mathcal{D}$ | Feasible Design Space $\mathcal{S}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Set of **all** mathematically admissible values of $X$ | Subset of $\mathcal{D}$ satisfying **all** constraints |
| Origin | Inherent to the design problem | Result of applying constraints |
| Symbol | $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ | $\mathcal{S} = \{ X \in \mathcal{D} \mid g_j(X) \leq 0, \; h_k(X) = 0 \}$ |
| Example (2-bar truss) | $X = (L_1, L_2)$ with $L_1 > 0, L_2 > 0$ | Same plus $\sigma_i \leq \sigma_{allow}$ |

**(3 Marks)** — 1 mark for each distinguishing row with an example.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

A cylindrical pressure vessel of inner diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$ is to be designed to hold an internal pressure $P = 2$ MPa. The material has yield strength $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa, and a factor of safety $n = 2.5$ is prescribed.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Identify the design vector for this problem. State all side constraints and inequality constraints mathematically.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the objective is to minimize the mass $M$ of the vessel, formulate the complete optimization problem in standard KTU notation. State the explicit objective function in terms of the design variables, assuming length $\ell$ and density $\rho$ are constants.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify independent, controllable parameters:**
The vessel's geometry is fully characterized by inner diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$. The pressure $P$ is a fixed operating condition; $\sigma_y$, $\rho$, and $\ell$ are constants.

**Step 2 — Design Vector:**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} D \\ t \end{Bmatrix}
$$

The dimensionality is $n = 2$.

**Step 3 — Side Constraints (lower and upper bounds):**
Realistic engineering bounds:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0.1 \text{ m} \leq D \leq 2.0 \text{ m} \\
& 0.005 \text{ m} \leq t \leq 0.05 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Performance Constraint (hoop stress in thin-walled cylinder):**
$$
\sigma = \frac{P D}{2 t} \leq \frac{\sigma_y}{n}
$$

Substituting numerical values:
$$
g_1(X) = \frac{(2 \times 10^6) x_1}{2 x_2} - \frac{250 \times 10^6}{2.5} \leq 0
$$

$$
g_1(X) = \frac{10^6 \cdot x_1}{x_2} - 10^8 \leq 0
$$

**Complete mathematical statement:**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} D \\ t \end{Bmatrix}, \quad g_1(X) = \frac{10^6 D}{t} - 10^8 \leq 0, \quad 0.1 \leq D \leq 2.0, \quad 0.005 \leq t \leq 0.05
$$

**(7 Marks — 2 for vector, 2 for bounds, 3 for stress constraint derivation.)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

The mass of a cylindrical pressure vessel (ignoring end caps) is:
$$
M = \rho \cdot V = \rho \cdot \left( \pi D \ell t \right)
$$

Since $\rho$ and $\ell$ are constants, the optimization problem in standard KTU form is:

**Find**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} D \\ t \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**to minimize**
$$
f(X) = \pi \rho \ell D t
$$

**subject to**
$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1(X) &= \frac{10^6 D}{t} - 10^8 \leq 0 \\
g_2(X) &= -D + 0.1 \leq 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } D \geq 0.1) \\
g_3(X) &= D - 2.0 \leq 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } D \leq 2.0) \\
g_4(X) &= -t + 0.005 \leq 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } t \geq 0.005) \\
g_5(X) &= t - 0.05 \leq 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } t \leq 0.05)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently, in compact notation:
$$
\min_{X} \; f(X) = \pi \rho \ell x_1 x_2 \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_j(X) \leq 0, \; j = 1, \ldots, 5
$$

The objective function is **bilinear** in $x_1$ and $x_2$ — a non-trivial 2-variable optimization with one active performance constraint at the optimum.

**(7 Marks — 2 for objective derivation, 3 for full KTU formulation, 2 for compactness of notation.)**

---

### Question B (Alternative 14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

A steel column of length $L = 4$ m must support an axial compressive load $P = 500$ kN without buckling. The cross-section is hollow circular with outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Identify the design vector. Express Euler's buckling constraint and slenderness constraints in terms of the design vector.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the cost is proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$, formulate the cost-minimization problem in standard KTU form and identify the feasible design space.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Independent, controllable geometric parameters:**
The hollow circular column is fully described by $D_o$ (outer diameter) and $D_i$ (inner diameter). Material properties ($E$) and load ($P$) are fixed.

**Step 2 — Design Vector:**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} D_o \\ D_i \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Euler's Buckling Constraint:**
The critical buckling load is:
$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L^2}, \quad \text{where} \quad I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left(D_o^4 - D_i^4\right)
$$

The safety condition is $P \leq P_{cr}/n$ with safety factor $n = 2$:
$$
g_1(X) = P - \frac{\pi^2 E}{n L^2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{64}\left(x_1^4 - x_2^4\right) \leq 0
$$

Substituting $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $L = 4$ m, $P = 500 \times 10^3$ N, $n = 2$:
$$
g_1(X) = 5 \times 10^5 - \frac{\pi^3 (200 \times 10^9)}{2 \cdot 16 \cdot 64}\left(x_1^4 - x_2^4\right) \leq 0
$$

**Step 4 — Slenderness Constraint (to remain in long-column regime):**
$$
g_2(X) = \frac{L}{r_{\min}} - 200 \leq 0, \quad r_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}
$$

For a hollow circular section, $r_{\min} = \sqrt{(D_o^2 + D_i^2)/16}$.

**Step 5 — Geometric Constraints:**
$$
g_3(X) = x_2 - x_1 \leq 0 \quad (D_i \leq D_o)
$$
$$
0.05 \text{ m} \leq x_1 \leq 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad 0 \leq x_2 \leq 0.95 x_1
$$

**(7 Marks — 2 for vector, 3 for Euler's constraint, 2 for slenderness.)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Cross-sectional area of hollow circular column:**
$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}(D_o^2 - D_i^2)
$$

If cost is $C = k \cdot A$ (where $k$ is a unit cost constant), then the optimization problem in KTU form is:

**Find**
$$
X = \begin{Bmatrix} D_o \\ D_i \end{Bmatrix}
$$

**to minimize**
$$
f(X) = \frac{\pi k}{4}\left(x_1^2 - x_2^2\right)
$$

**subject to**
$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1(X) &= P - \frac{\pi^3 E}{128 n L^2}\left(x_1^4 - x_2^4\right) \leq 0 \\
g_2(X) &= \frac{4L}{\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}} - 200 \leq 0 \\
g_3(X) &= x_2 - x_1 \leq 0 \\
g_4(X) &= 0.05 - x_1 \leq 0 \\
g_5(X) &= x_1 - 1.0 \leq 0 \\
g_6(X) &= -x_2 \leq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Feasible design space:**
$$
\mathcal{S} = \left\{ X \in \mathbb{R}^2 \mid g_j(X) \leq 0,\; j = 1, \ldots, 6 \right\}
$$

The problem is a **nonlinear constrained optimization** with a 2-dimensional design space and 6 inequality constraints.

**(7 Marks — 2 for objective function, 3 for constraint translation, 2 for feasible set.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bounds to $\leq 0$ form** — Side constraints $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$ **must** be rewritten as $x_i - x_i^U \leq 0$ and $x_i^L - x_i \leq 0$. Many students lose **1–2 marks** by writing the inequality in the wrong direction.
> 2. **Including fixed parameters in the design vector** — Material constants ($E, \rho, \sigma_y$) and operating loads ($P, w, T$) are **not** design variables. Including them inflates $n$ and is **incorrect**.
> 3. **Ignoring dependent variables** — If two candidate variables are linked by a physical relation (e.g., $A_s = b \cdot d$), only **one** is independent. Counting both inflates dimensionality.
> 4. **Confusing design space and objective space** — Design space $\mathcal{D} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is multi-dimensional. Objective space is **always** $\mathbb{R}^1$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Design vector** $X = [x_1, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the ordered tuple of all independent, controllable design variables.
- [ ] **Dimensionality** $n$ = number of independent design variables; defines the design space.
- [ ] **Design space** $\mathcal{D}$ = every mathematically possible combination of variable values.
- [ ] **Feasible space** $\mathcal{S} \subset \mathcal{D}$ = subset of $\mathcal{D}$ that obeys all constraints.
- [ ] **Three constraint types:** (i) side bounds $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$, (ii) inequality $g_j(X) \leq 0$, (iii) equality $h_k(X) = 0$.
- [ ] **Objective function** $f(X)$ maps the design vector to a single scalar performance metric.
- [ ] **Design variables are classified** as continuous, discrete, or integer; and as independent or dependent.
- [ ] **Normalization formula:** $\bar{x}_i = (x_i - x_i^L) / (x_i^U - x_i^L)$ maps $x_i \in [x_i^L, x_i^U]$ to $\bar{x}_i \in [0, 1]$.
- [ ] **Identification procedure:** List all parameters → eliminate fixed → eliminate dependent → remaining = design vector.
- [ ] **KTU standard optimization form** is *minimize* (or *maximize*) $f(X)$ subject to $g_j(X) \leq 0$, $h_k(X) = 0$, and side bounds.
- [ ] **Dimensionality controls computational cost** — keep $n$ minimal but sufficient.
- [ ] **The design vector is the *only* input to the optimizer** — once defined, the optimization machinery takes over.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, a question asking to *"formulate the optimization problem"* gives **3 marks** for correctly identifying the design vector alone. Never skip this step, even if pressed for time. The vector definition is the foundation of every subsequent derivation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the formal framework of **Engineering Optimization**, a **Design Constraint** is a mathematical condition, expressed as an equation or inequality, that the design vector **x** must satisfy to represent a physically realizable, safe, and functional engineering solution. The constraint set mathematically restricts the otherwise unrestricted **n-dimensional design space** ($\mathbb{R}^n$) into a smaller, permissible sub-region known as the **Feasible Region** ($\mathcal{F}$).

Mathematically, the constraint set is expressed as:

$$
\mathcal{F} = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \;\vert\; g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; j = 1, 2, \dots, m; \quad h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; k = 1, 2, \dots, p \}
$$

where:
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ represents the **inequality constraints**
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ represents the **equality constraints**
- $m$ is the total number of inequality constraints
- $p$ is the total number of equality constraints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In standard KTU optimization notation, **inequality constraints are conventionally written in the $\le 0$ form**, not $\ge 0$. The sign convention is critical for K1/K2 short-answer questions. The **Lagrangian formulation** later depends on this sign.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are packing a suitcase for a 7-day trip to Kerala. The suitcase has a physical limit — say, a volume of **60 liters** and a weight limit of **23 kg** (typical airline check-in baggage policy).

| Real-World Analogy | Optimization Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Items to pack (clothes, shoes, books) | **Design variables** $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ |
| Suitcase volume = 60 L | **Inequality constraint** $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ |
| Suitcase weight = 23 kg | **Inequality constraint** $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ |
| Zipping the suitcase closed | **Equality constraint** $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ |
| Valid packing arrangements | **Feasible Region** $\mathcal{F}$ |
| Combinations where the lid won't close | **Infeasible Region** |

> [!NOTE]
> Just as you cannot over-pack the suitcase without breaking the zipper, an optimized engineering design that violates a constraint is **physically unrealizable** — it cannot be manufactured, operated, or sold. Constraints define the **boundary between "possible" and "impossible" designs.**

### 1.3 Physical Significance in Engineering

Design constraints are not arbitrary — they originate from:
1. **Physical Laws** (e.g., stress $\sigma \le \sigma_{yield}$, deflection $\delta \le \delta_{max}$)
2. **Geometric Limits** (e.g., length $\le L_{max}$, diameter $\ge d_{min}$)
3. **Economic Budgets** (e.g., cost $\le \$5000$)
4. **Regulatory / Code Requirements** (e.g., IS 800, AISC, ASME standards)
5. **Performance Specifications** (e.g., natural frequency $\ge 50$ Hz)

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Constraints are the **"invisible walls"** of the design space. The optimizer's job is to find the **best point on (or within) these walls** that minimizes or maximizes the objective function.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Design Constraints

Design constraints in optimization are classified along multiple dimensions. The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus emphasizes the following taxonomy:

#### A. By Mathematical Form

**1. Equality Constraints** $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$
- Must be **exactly satisfied** at the optimum.
- Reduce the **degrees of freedom** of the problem by 1 each.
- Example: Volume of a pressure vessel = $\pi r^2 L$ (must equal a specified value).

**2. Inequality Constraints** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$
- Form the boundary of the **feasible region**.
- May be **active** (binding at optimum) or **inactive** (slack at optimum).
- Example: Maximum allowable stress, cost ceilings, displacement caps.

#### B. By Functional Dependence

**3. Linear Constraints** — Both the constraint function and variables are linear (LP problems).

**4. Nonlinear Constraints** — At least one constraint is nonlinear (NLP problems).

#### C. By Physical Origin

**5. Behavioural (Performance) Constraints** — Limit the response of the system (e.g., $\sigma \le \sigma_{allow}$, $\delta \le \delta_{max}$).

**6. Geometric (Side) Constraints** — Bound the design variables directly (e.g., $x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$).

**7. Coupling Constraints** — Relate multiple design variables simultaneously.

### 2.2 Active vs. Inactive vs. Violated Constraints

This distinction is **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 — frequently asked as 3-mark questions.

| Constraint Type | Definition | Mathematical Condition at $\mathbf{x}^*$ | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active (Binding)** | Holds as equality at optimum | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Optimum lies **on** the constraint boundary |
| **Inactive (Slack)** | Strictly satisfied at optimum | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ | Optimum lies in the **interior** of the feasible region |
| **Violated (Infeasible)** | Disobeyed | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ | Point lies **outside** the feasible region |
| **Redundant** | Inactive and has no influence | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$, no binding effect | Can be removed without changing optimum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Rule:** At the optimal point, if a constraint is **inactive**, its associated **Lagrange multiplier** $\lambda_j^* = 0$. If it is **active**, then $\lambda_j^* \ge 0$ (for $g_j \le 0$ convention). This is the famous **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) complementarity condition**: $\lambda_j^* \cdot g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$.

### 2.3 Degrees of Freedom and Design Space Reduction

The **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as:

$$
\text{DOF} = n - p - (\text{number of active inequality constraints at } \mathbf{x}^*)
$$

where $n$ = number of design variables and $p$ = number of equality constraints.

**Interpretation:** Each equality constraint removes 1 DOF. Each active inequality at the optimum also reduces DOF by 1.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notation Notes | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design vector | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$ | Column vector in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | Dimensional: $n$ |
| 2 | Feasible region | $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \;\vert\; g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ | Set notation | Set-theoretic |
| 3 | Inequality constraint | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; j = 1, \dots, m$ | KTU convention $\le 0$ | Scalar |
| 4 | Equality constraint | $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; k = 1, \dots, p$ | Must hold exactly | Scalar |
| 5 | Side constraint | $x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$ | Bounds on variables | Scalar |
| 6 | Active constraint test | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | At optimum | Scalar |
| 7 | Inactive constraint test | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ | Strict inequality | Scalar |
| 8 | Degrees of freedom | $\text{DOF} = n - p - q$ | $q$ = active inequality count | Integer |
| 9 | Number of inequality constraints | $m$ | Usually $m \ge 1$ in NLP | Integer |
| 10 | Number of equality constraints | $p$ | Often $p = 0$ | Integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** The absolute value and "such that" symbols have been written using `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. KTU examiners verify LaTeX rendering in typed answer sheets.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mechanical Design:** Shaft diameter $d$ must satisfy torsional shear stress $\tau \le \tau_{allow}$.
- **Structural Engineering:** Beam deflection $\delta_{max} \le L/360$ (IS 800 / AISC code).
- **Thermal Systems:** Heat exchanger outlet temperature $T_{out} \ge T_{required}$.
- **Aerospace:** Lift coefficient $C_L \ge C_{L,min}$ for stall margin.
- **Manufacturing:** Machining cost $C \le C_{budget}$.

In every field, the optimization problem is incomplete without constraints — an unconstrained optimum is rarely physically meaningful.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Standard Form Transformation of an Inequality Constraint

A common source of confusion is the direction of the inequality. Suppose a KTU problem states: *"Maximum stress in a beam must not exceed $250$ MPa."* In raw form:

$$
\sigma(\mathbf{x}) \le 250 \text{ MPa}
$$

To convert this into the KTU standard $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ form, we **bring everything to the Left-Hand Side (LHS) and subtract the RHS**:

**Step 1:** Identify the LHS function and RHS bound.

$$
\sigma(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{and} \quad 250
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the RHS from the LHS to define $g(\mathbf{x})$.

$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{x}) - 250
$$

**Step 3:** Verify the sign convention — when the constraint is **exactly satisfied** (at limit), $g = 0$. When **satisfied** (safe design), $g \le 0$. When **violated**, $g > 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma = 250 \;&\Rightarrow\; g = 250 - 250 = 0 \quad \text{(Active)} \\
\sigma = 200 \;&\Rightarrow\; g = 200 - 250 = -50 < 0 \quad \text{(Inactive / Satisfied)} \\
\sigma = 300 \;&\Rightarrow\; g = 300 - 250 = +50 > 0 \quad \text{(Violated / Infeasible)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the transformation is correct.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Constraint Classification at an Optimum

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 2-variable ($x_1, x_2$) design problem with the following three inequality constraints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_1(\mathbf{x}) &= x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 25 \le 0 \\
g_2(\mathbf{x}) &= x_1 + x_2 - 8 \le 0 \\
g_3(\mathbf{x}) &= -x_1 \le 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } x_1 \ge 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Suppose a candidate optimum is $\mathbf{x}^* = (3, 4)^T$.

**Step 1: Evaluate each constraint at $\mathbf{x}^*$.**

For $g_1$:
$$
g_1(3, 4) = (3)^2 + (4)^2 - 25 = 9 + 16 - 25 = 0
$$

For $g_2$:
$$
g_2(3, 4) = 3 + 4 - 8 = -1
$$

For $g_3$:
$$
g_3(3, 4) = -3
$$

**Step 2: Classify each constraint.**

| Constraint | Value at $\mathbf{x}^*$ | Status | $\lambda_j^*$ (KKT) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $g_1$ | $0$ | **Active (Binding)** | $\lambda_1^* \ge 0$ |
| $g_2$ | $-1 < 0$ | **Inactive (Slack)** | $\lambda_2^* = 0$ |
| $g_3$ | $-3 < 0$ | **Inactive (Slack)** | $\lambda_3^* = 0$ |

**Step 3: Compute the Degrees of Freedom.**

With $n = 2$ variables and $p = 0$ equality constraints, only $g_1$ is active at $\mathbf{x}^*$:

$$
\text{DOF} = n - p - q = 2 - 0 - 1 = 1
$$

This means the optimum, although sitting on a 2-D boundary curve, has only **1 effective degree of freedom locally** — it can move along the tangent to $g_1 = 0$.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Standardizing a Constraint from a Real KTU-Style Problem

**Original problem statement (hypothetical KTU 2024 style):**
> *"Design a cylindrical pressure vessel of internal radius $r$ and wall thickness $t$ such that the hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pr}{t} \le 150$ MPa, and the mass $M = 2\pi r t \rho \le 200$ kg."*

**Step 1:** Identify design variables.

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{pmatrix} r \\ t \end{pmatrix}, \quad n = 2
$$

**Step 2:** Standardize each constraint to $g_j \le 0$ form.

**Hoop stress constraint:**

$$
g_1(r, t) = \frac{pr}{t} - 150 \le 0
$$

**Mass constraint:**

$$
g_2(r, t) = 2\pi r t \rho - 200 \le 0
$$

**Step 3:** Identify any equality constraints (here, none).

$$
p = 0
$$

**Step 4:** Express the feasible region in set notation.

$$
\mathcal{F} = \left\{ (r, t) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;\middle|\; \frac{pr}{t} - 150 \le 0, \;\; 2\pi r t \rho - 200 \le 0, \;\; r > 0, \; t > 0 \right\}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute initial DOF (before identifying active set):

$$
\text{DOF} = n - p = 2 - 0 = 2
$$

If, at optimum, **both** $g_1$ and $g_2$ are active, then $q = 2$ and:

$$
\text{DOF at optimum} = 2 - 0 - 2 = 0
$$

This is a **fully constrained design** — the optimum is uniquely pinned down by the two binding constraints, with no remaining freedom to vary.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **zero-DOF optimum** means the design is **fully determined** by the constraints. This is typical in **minimum-weight design** problems where every constraint is forced to its limit to economize material — the famous **fully stressed design** philosophy.

### 3.4 Derivation: Active Set Method Concept (Qualitative)

The **Active Set Method** is the workhorse algorithm for solving constrained NLP problems in practice (e.g., in MATLAB's `fmincon`). The logic:

**Step 1:** Start with an initial guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ and an initial **active set** $\mathcal{A}^{(0)}$ of constraints guessed to be binding.

**Step 2:** Solve the **Equality-Constrained Subproblem** (ECP):

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \;\; \forall j \in \mathcal{A}
$$

**Step 3:** Check if any **inactive** constraint is violated by the solution.

**Step 4:** If yes, add the most-violated constraint to $\mathcal{A}$ and repeat.

**Step 5:** If no, the solution is the KKT point.

The Lagrangian for the subproblem is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{A}} \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

The KKT stationarity condition:

$$
\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} \mathcal{L} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{A}} \lambda_j \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

This system of $n$ equations (gradient = 0) plus $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ active constraints gives $n + \vert \mathcal{A} \vert$ unknowns and the same number of equations — **a square, solvable system**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing the sign of the Lagrange multiplier. In the $g_j \le 0$ convention, **all** multipliers $\lambda_j \ge 0$ at optimum. In the alternative $g_j \ge 0$ convention, $\lambda_j \le 0$. Mixing conventions causes sign errors in KKT conditions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Design Constraints

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the complete taxonomy of design constraints as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Design Constraints"]:::root --> B["By Mathematical Form"]
    A --> C["By Functional Dependence"]
    A --> D["By Physical Origin"]
    A --> E["By Status at Optimum"]

    B --> B1["Equality h(x) equals 0"]
    B --> B2["Inequality g(x) leq 0"]

    C --> C1["Linear Constraints LP"]
    C --> C2["Nonlinear Constraints NLP"]

    D --> D1["Behavioural or Performance"]
    D --> D2["Geometric or Side"]
    D --> D3["Coupling Constraints"]

    E --> E1["Active g x star equals 0"]
    E --> E2["Inactive g x star less than 0"]
    E --> E3["Violated g x star greater than 0"]
    E --> E4["Redundant no binding effect"]

    B1 --> F["Feasible Region F"]
    B2 --> F
    E1 --> G["Lagrange Multipliers"]
    E2 --> G
    G --> H["KKT Conditions"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef constraint fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000
    classDef status fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    classDef kkt fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    class A root
    class B1,B2,C1,C2,D1,D2,D3 constraint
    class E1,E2,E3,E4 status
    class F,G,H kkt
```

### 4.2 Geometric Block Diagram: Feasible Region Construction

The following diagram shows how individual constraints combine to form the **Feasible Region** $\mathcal{F}$, and how the optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ interacts with active and inactive boundaries.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Constraint 1 g1 x leq 0"]
        S1A["Hyper surface in Rn"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Constraint 2 g2 x leq 0"]
        S2A["Hyper surface in Rn"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Constraint k gk x leq 0"]
        S3A["Hyper surface in Rn"]
    end
    subgraph Reg["Resulting Feasible Region F"]
        RegA["Intersection of all half spaces"]
        RegB["Optimum x star on active boundary"]
        RegC["Slack region interior"]
    end

    S1 --> RegA
    S2 --> RegA
    S3 --> RegA
    RegA --> RegB
    RegA --> RegC

    RegB -.lambda gt 0.-> Lam["Active Lagrange Multipliers"]
    RegC -.lambda equals 0.-> Lam

    classDef surf fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000
    classDef reg fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    classDef lambda fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000000
    class S1A,S2A,S3A surf
    class RegA,RegB,RegC reg
    class Lam lambda
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture: Constraint Processing in an Optimization Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    In["Input Design Variables x"]:::in --> Eval["Evaluate Constraints g_j x and h_k x"]:::proc
    Eval --> Check{"Is Constraint Satisfied?"}:::dec
    Check -- Yes --> Slack["Mark as Inactive lambda equals 0"]:::slack
    Check -- No Equal --> Active["Mark as Active lambda ge 0"]:::active
    Check -- No Greater --> Violate["Mark as Violated Infeasible"]:::violate

    Slack --> Update["Update Active Set"]
    Active --> Update
    Violate --> Reject["Reject Candidate or Apply Penalty"]
    Reject --> Update

    Update --> KKT{"KKT Conditions Satisfied?"}:::dec
    KKT -- Yes --> Converge["Converged Optimum x star"]:::conv
    KKT -- No --> Iterate["Iterate Search Direction"]
    Iterate --> In

    classDef in fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000000
    classDef proc fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca,color:#000000
    classDef dec fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#000000
    classDef slack fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000000
    classDef active fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000
    classDef violate fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000
    classDef conv fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#14532d,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid does not natively render 2-D contour plots or physical drawings. The above block diagrams serve as **functional architecture maps** of constraint handling — equivalent in pedagogical value for KTU Board Examinations, where schematic flowcharts are accepted and frequently rewarded with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

> Define the terms **feasible region**, **active constraint**, and **infeasible point** in the context of engineering optimization. Use a 2-variable example to illustrate each.

**Model Answer:**

A **feasible region** $\mathcal{F}$ is the set of all design vectors $\mathbf{x}$ that satisfy every constraint of the problem simultaneously. Formally, $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$.

An **active constraint** at an optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ is one that holds as an equality, i.e., $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$. It **bounds** the optimum — the optimizer cannot improve the objective without violating this constraint.

An **infeasible point** is any $\mathbf{x}$ that violates at least one constraint, i.e., some $g_j(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ or $h_k(\mathbf{x}) \ne 0$.

**Illustration with $x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$:**

$$
g_1 = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4 \le 0, \quad g_2 = x_1 + x_2 - 2 \le 0
$$

- Point $(1, 1)$: $g_1 = -2 < 0$, $g_2 = 0$ — **Feasible**, with $g_2$ **active**.
- Point $(0.5, 0.5)$: $g_1 = -3.5$, $g_2 = -1$ — **Feasible**, both **inactive**.
- Point $(2, 2)$: $g_1 = 4 > 0$ — **Infeasible** (outside circle).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

> Distinguish between **equality** and **inequality constraints**. State two examples of each from a mechanical design context, and explain how each affects the **degrees of freedom** of the problem.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Equality Constraint $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Inequality Constraint $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ |
|---|---|---|
| Holds as | Exact equality always | $\le$ inequality (slack possible) |
| Effect on DOF | Always removes 1 DOF | Removes 1 DOF only when active |
| Example (Mech.) | Pin joint: $u_x = 0$ | Bending stress: $\sigma \le \sigma_{allow}$ |
| Example (Mech.) | Gear ratio: $N_1/N_2 = 2.5$ | Cost: $C \le \$\;1000$ |

**DOF Impact:**
- Each equality constraint **always** reduces DOF by 1: $\text{DOF} = n - p$.
- Each inequality constraint reduces DOF by 1 **only if active** at $\mathbf{x}^*$: $\text{DOF}^* = n - p - q_{active}$.

**Example:** For a 3-variable ($n=3$) shaft design with 1 equality ($p=1$) and 2 active inequality constraints at optimum ($q=2$):
$$
\text{DOF}^* = 3 - 1 - 2 = 0 \quad \text{(Fully constrained design)}
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Regulation (2024 Scheme):** Each Module in the End Semester Examination contains one 14-mark question with internal choice (Choice A or Choice B). Sub-parts (a) and (b) carry 7 marks each.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Classify design constraints in engineering optimization into **four major categories**. Provide one engineering example for each category and write its mathematical standard form $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ or $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four major classifications are:

**1. Equality Constraints** $h(\mathbf{x}) = 0$
- *Definition:* Must hold exactly.
- *Example:* A pressure vessel designed for exactly 1 m³ internal volume:
$$
h(\mathbf{x}) = \pi r^2 L - 1 = 0
$$

**2. Inequality Constraints** $g(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$
- *Definition:* Bound a quantity from above/below.
- *Example:* Maximum von Mises stress $\sigma_v$ in a shaft must be $\le \sigma_{yield}$:
$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma_v - \sigma_{yield} \le 0
$$

**3. Behavioural / Performance Constraints**
- *Definition:* Restrict the system response (stress, deflection, frequency).
- *Example:* Maximum deflection of a cantilever beam of length $L$ under tip load $P$:
$$
g(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} - \delta_{max} \le 0
$$

**4. Geometric / Side Constraints**
- *Definition:* Bound the design variables directly.
- *Example:* Wall thickness $t$ of a pressure vessel between $t_{\min}$ and $t_{\max}$:
$$
g_1 = t - t_{max} \le 0, \quad g_2 = t_{min} - t \le 0
$$

**(b)** Consider a design problem with $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^T$ subject to:
$$
g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 16 \le 0, \quad g_2(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 6 \le 0, \quad g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 \le 0
$$

A candidate optimum is $\mathbf{x}^* = (2, \sqrt{12})^T$. Determine the **status** (active / inactive / violated) of each constraint. Compute the **degrees of freedom** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ and comment on the design.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Evaluate $g_1$.**

$$
g_1(2, \sqrt{12}) = (2)^2 + (\sqrt{12})^2 - 16 = 4 + 12 - 16 = 0
$$

$\Rightarrow g_1 = 0$: **Active (Binding)**.

**Step 2: Evaluate $g_2$.**

$$
g_2(2, \sqrt{12}) = 2 + \sqrt{12} - 6 = 3.464 - 6 = -2.536
$$

$\Rightarrow g_2 < 0$: **Inactive (Slack)**.

**Step 3: Evaluate $g_3$.**

$$
g_3(2, \sqrt{12}) = -2
$$

$\Rightarrow g_3 < 0$: **Inactive (Slack)**.

**Step 4: Summarize in a table.**

| Constraint | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| $g_1$ | $0$ | Active |
| $g_2$ | $-2.536$ | Inactive |
| $g_3$ | $-2$ | Inactive |

**Step 5: Compute DOF.**

With $n = 2$, $p = 0$ (no equalities), $q = 1$ (only $g_1$ active):
$$
\text{DOF}^* = n - p - q = 2 - 0 - 1 = 1
$$

**Step 6: Engineering Comment.**

The candidate has **1 degree of freedom**, meaning the optimum lies on the circular boundary $g_1 = 0$. The design can still be varied locally along the tangent of the circle without violating constraints. To reach a unique optimum (DOF = 0), one more inequality would need to be active.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **active set method** used in solving constrained nonlinear optimization problems. Why is the classification of constraints into active and inactive sets critical for convergence?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Active Set Method — Conceptual Explanation:**

The **Active Set Method** (also called the **Method of Feasible Directions** or **Generalized Reduced Gradient Method**, depending on variant) solves constrained NLP problems by iteratively solving a sequence of **Equality-Constrained Subproblems (ECPs)**.

**Algorithm Logic:**

**Step 1:** Initialize with a feasible guess $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}$ and an active set $\mathcal{A}^{(0)}$ (typically empty).

**Step 2:** At iteration $k$, treat the constraints in $\mathcal{A}^{(k)}$ as equalities, solve:
$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{s.t.} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; j \in \mathcal{A}^{(k)}
$$

using the **Lagrangian**:
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j \in \mathcal{A}^{(k)}} \lambda_j g_j(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 3:** Compute search direction $\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ from $\nabla_\mathbf{x} \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{0}$.

**Step 4:** Check all **inactive** constraints along the search direction. If any is violated, add the most-violated one to $\mathcal{A}$. If any **active** constraint becomes strictly satisfied, drop it.

**Step 5:** Repeat until KKT conditions are satisfied to tolerance.

**Why Active/Inactive Classification is Critical:**
