1. **Computational Efficiency:** Only active constraints need be enforced as equalities; inactive constraints can be ignored in the subproblem, reducing dimensionality.
2. **Convergence:** Wrong active-set identification causes the algorithm to oscillate between constraint boundaries or stall.
3. **KKT Consistency:** Only active constraints have $\lambda_j > 0$; inactive ones must have $\lambda_j = 0$ — the active set must match the set of positive multipliers at the optimum.
4. **Feasibility Maintenance:** Infeasibility of intermediate iterates is avoided by careful active-set management.

---

**(b)** A 3-bar truss is to be optimized. Design variables are the cross-sectional areas $A_1, A_2, A_3$. The problem is formulated as:
$$
\min \; f(\mathbf{A}) = 2\sqrt{2} A_1 + A_2 + A_3
$$
subject to:
$$
g_1 = \frac{P}{A_2} - \sigma_{allow} \le 0, \quad g_2 = \frac{P}{A_3} - \sigma_{allow} \le 0, \quad g_3 = \frac{\sqrt{2} P}{A_1} - \sigma_{allow} \le 0
$$
At the optimal solution $A_1^* = \sqrt{2} A_2^* = \sqrt{2} A_3^*$ and all three constraints are active. Verify the optimality and compute the DOF at the optimum.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Substitute the optimal relations into each constraint.**

Let $A_2^* = A_3^* = a$ (some positive value). Then $A_1^* = \sqrt{2}\, a$.

**Step 2: Substitute into $g_1$.**

$$
g_1 = \frac{P}{A_2^*} - \sigma_{allow} = \frac{P}{a} - \sigma_{allow} = 0 \quad \text{(Given active)}
$$

Solving: $a = P / \sigma_{allow}$. So $A_2^* = A_3^* = P/\sigma_{allow}$.

**Step 3: Verify $g_2$.**

$$
g_2 = \frac{P}{A_3^*} - \sigma_{allow} = \frac{P}{a} - \sigma_{allow} = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Verify $g_3$.**

$$
g_3 = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, P}{A_1^*} - \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sqrt{2}\, P}{\sqrt{2}\, a} - \sigma_{allow} = \frac{P}{a} - \sigma_{allow} = 0 \;\checkmark
$$

**All three constraints are simultaneously active**, consistent with the given assumption. The design is the **classical optimal 3-bar truss**.

**Step 5: Compute DOF.**

With $n = 3$ variables, $p = 0$ equalities, $q = 3$ active inequalities:

$$
\text{DOF}^* = n - p - q = 3 - 0 - 3 = 0
$$

The design is **fully constrained** (DOF = 0) — every design variable is uniquely determined by the binding constraints, leaving no freedom to vary further. This is the hallmark of a **minimum-weight fully stressed design**.

**Step 6: Optimal objective value.**

$$
f^* = 2\sqrt{2}\, (\sqrt{2}\,a) + a + a = 4a + 2a = 6a = \frac{6P}{\sigma_{allow}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Forgetting that the KTU standard form is $g_j \le 0$, not $g_j \ge 0$. Half the marks in classification questions are lost here.
> 2. **DOF Misconception:** Computing DOF using only $n$ and ignoring active inequality constraints. Always use $\text{DOF} = n - p - q_{active}$.
> 3. **Active ≠ Always Binding:** A constraint being **active at one point** does not make it globally active. Active set varies during the search.
> 4. **Feasibility Check:** Many students assume the candidate point is feasible without verifying $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \le 0$ explicitly. This loses the "verification" step marks.
> 5. **Lagrange Multiplier Sign:** In $g_j \le 0$ form, $\lambda_j \ge 0$. In $g_j \ge 0$ form, $\lambda_j \le 0$. Mixing conventions causes KKT sign errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Design Constraints (Module 1, KTU 2024)**

- **Design Constraint:** A condition (equation or inequality) that a design vector must satisfy.
- **Feasible Region ($\mathcal{F}$):** The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying **all** constraints.
- **Standard Form (KTU):** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ (inequality) and $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ (equality).
- **Equality Constraint:** Always exactly satisfied; reduces DOF by 1 always.
- **Inequality Constraint:** May be active ($g = 0$) or inactive ($g < 0$) at optimum.
- **Active Constraint:** Holds as equality at $\mathbf{x}^*$; its multiplier $\lambda_j^* \ge 0$.
- **Inactive Constraint:** Strictly satisfied at $\mathbf{x}^*$; its multiplier $\lambda_j^* = 0$.
- **Violated Constraint:** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) > 0$ — point is infeasible.
- **Redundant Constraint:** Inactive and does not affect the optimum; can be removed.
- **Behavioural Constraint:** Limits response quantities (stress, deflection, frequency).
- **Geometric / Side Constraint:** Bounds design variables directly ($x_i^{(L)} \le x_i \le x_i^{(U)}$).
- **Coupling Constraint:** Involves multiple variables simultaneously.
- **Degrees of Freedom:** $\text{DOF} = n - p - q_{active}$.
- **Fully Constrained Design:** DOF = 0 at optimum; all variables pinned by active constraints.
- **KKT Complementarity:** $\lambda_j^* \cdot g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ for all inequality constraints.
- **Active Set Method:** Iteratively solves ECPs over the active set, updating $\mathcal{A}$ as constraints activate/deactivate.
- **Sign Convention is Critical:** All transformations to $g \le 0$ form must subtract RHS from LHS.
- **Real-World Origin:** Constraints arise from physics, geometry, economics, codes, and performance specs — not from the optimizer.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Tip:** For 14-mark questions, always **draw the feasible region** (even schematically), **label each constraint boundary**, and **explicitly mark active vs. inactive**. This visual aid alone typically earns 2-3 extra marks due to board examiner preference for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constraint Surface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Constraint Surface ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the rigorous mathematical framework of **Engineering Optimization**, a **Constraint Surface** is defined as the geometric locus (the set of all points) in the **n-dimensional Euclidean design space** $\mathbb{R}^n$ that satisfies one or more **equality constraints** of the form: $$g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$ where $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$ is the vector of decision (design) variables, and $g_j$ is a real-valued, continuously differentiable function. When the constraints are of the **inequality type**: $$g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$ the constraint **boundary** (or constraint surface) is given by the locus where $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$. The feasible region is then the closed half-space on the side of this boundary that satisfies the inequality. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The constraint surface is the *geometrical boundary* of the feasible region. It is a **hypersurface of dimension $(n-1)$** embedded in an $n$-dimensional design space. For $n=2$, it manifests as a **curve**; for $n=3$, it manifests as a **2-D surface**; for general $n$, it is a hyper-surface. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing in a large open field (the design space). The field is bounded by an invisible fence (the constraint). The fence itself is the **constraint surface** — the line you must not cross. The interior of the field is the **feasible region**, where all constraints are satisfied. If the fence is curved, then at any point on the fence, you can draw a small arrow sticking straight outward — that arrow is the **gradient vector** $\nabla g(\mathbf{x})$, which is mathematically perpendicular to the fence at that point. A more relatable analogy: > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Mountain Climber:** Suppose a mountaineer wants to find the highest peak (objective function) while staying on the safe trail (constraint). The *trail* itself is the **constraint surface** — the only places the climber is allowed to stand. The altitude the climber reaches is the value of the objective function. To ascend optimally, the climber must move along the trail in the direction where the slope of the altitude is steepest while staying glued to the safe path. The mathematics of optimization tells us *exactly* which direction that is. --- ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology | Term | Symbol / Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Design Space** | $\mathbb{R}^n$, the $n$-dimensional space of decision variables | | **Constraint Surface** | Locus defined by $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | | **Feasible Region** | $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \, \forall j\}$ | | **Active Constraint** | A constraint that holds as equality at a given point: $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | | **Inactive Constraint** | A constraint strictly satisfied: $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ | | **Normal Vector** | $\nabla g_j(\mathbf{x})$, pointing outward from the feasible region | | **Dimensionality of Surface** | $(n-1)$ for a single equality constraint in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mathematical Anatomy of a Constraint Surface A constraint function $g: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ implicitly defines a hypersurface. The geometry of this surface is governed by the **level set theorem** of multivariable calculus. **Step-by-step geometric reasoning:** 1. **Implicit Definition:** The constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) = c$ partitions $\mathbb{R}^n$ into level sets. The constraint surface corresponds to a specific level $c = 0$. 2. **Dimensional Reduction:** Each independent equality constraint reduces the effective dimensionality of the feasible set by **one**. Thus, $m$ independent equality constraints reduce the space from $\mathbb{R}^n$ to $\mathbb{R}^{n-m}$. 3. **Local Linearity:** At any point $\mathbf{x}_0$ where $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0) \neq \mathbf{0}$, the constraint surface can be approximated locally by its **tangent hyperplane**: $$T(\mathbf{x}_0) = \left\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0) = 0 \right\}$$ 4. **Normal Vector:** The gradient $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is the unique vector orthogonal to the tangent hyperplane, and hence orthogonal to the constraint surface itself. 5. **Feasibility Direction:** A direction $\mathbf{d}$ is *feasible* at $\mathbf{x}_0$ on the surface if it lies in the tangent hyperplane, i.e., $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)^T \mathbf{d} = 0$. --- ### 2.2 Classification of Constraints in KTU 2024 Framework | Type | Mathematical Form | Geometric Interpretation | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Equality Constraint** | $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Locus is the surface itself; feasible set *lies on* the surface | | **Inequality (≤ type)** | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Feasible region is the **interior + surface** on one side | | **Inequality (≥ type)** | $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \geq 0$ | Feasible region is the **interior + surface** on the opposite side | | **Bound Constraint** | $x_i^L \leq x_i \leq x_i^U$ | Defines a hyper-box in $\mathbb{R}^n$ | | **Active at $\mathbf{x}^*$** | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Constraint is *binding* at the optimum | | **Inactive at $\mathbf{x}^*$** | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ | Constraint has *slack*; not binding | --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Reference for KTU Board Exams** | # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Implicit Surface Definition | $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | Defines constraint surface | | 2 | Gradient (Normal Vector) | $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}) = \left[\dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_1}, \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_2}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_n}\right]^T$ | Normal to surface at $\mathbf{x}$ | | 3 | Tangent Hyperplane | $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)^T (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0) = 0$ | Local linearization | | 4 | Tangency Condition | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \lambda \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)$ | Optimality (Lagrange, preview) | | 5 | Feasibility Direction | $\nabla g_j(\mathbf{x})^T \mathbf{d} \leq 0$ | For inequality $g_j \leq 0$ | | 6 | Regular Point | $\nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}_0) \neq \mathbf{0} \; \forall j \in \mathcal{A}$ | Constraint qualification | | 7 | Active Set | $\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}) = \{j : g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ | Index of binding constraints | | 8 | Dimension of Surface | $n - \vert\mathcal{A}\vert$ (for active equalities) | DOF after binding | > **Standard Metric Reminder:** All KTU numerical answers must include units where applicable; however, the constraint surface itself is a **dimensionless geometric construct** in variable space. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Constraint surfaces are not abstract toys — they are at the heart of every engineering design: - **Structural Design:** Stress limits ($\sigma \leq \sigma_{allow}$) define a constraint surface; the optimal cross-section lies *on* this surface. - **Process Optimization:** Material balance equations act as equality constraints, restricting the design to a *process surface*. - **Aerospace Trajectory:** Boundary conditions of rocket trajectories are equality constraints — the trajectory **must** lie on the constraint surface. - **Economic Dispatch:** Power generation must satisfy demand equality, restricting feasible operating points to a hyperplane. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: The Normal Vector to a Constraint Surface We seek to prove rigorously that the gradient $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is perpendicular to the constraint surface $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ at the regular point $\mathbf{x}_0$. **Assumption:** $g: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is $C^1$ (continuously differentiable) and $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0) \neq \mathbf{0}$. **Step 1 — Parametrize the surface locally:** Let $\mathbf{x}(t)$ be a smooth curve lying entirely on the surface, with $\mathbf{x}(0) = \mathbf{x}_0$. Since the curve is on the surface: $$g(\mathbf{x}(t)) = 0 \quad \text{for all } t \text{ in a neighborhood of } 0$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$:** Using the chain rule: $$\frac{d}{dt} g(\mathbf{x}(t)) = \nabla g(\mathbf{x}(t))^T \cdot \dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = 0$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate at $t = 0$:** $$\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)^T \cdot \dot{\mathbf{x}}(0) = 0$$ This shows that $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is orthogonal to **every** tangent vector $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(0)$ of every curve passing through $\mathbf{x}_0$ on the surface. **Step 4 — Conclude:** Since $\nabla g(\mathbf{x}_0)$ is orthogonal to all tangent vectors, it must be the **normal vector** to the constraint surface at $\mathbf{x}_0$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Constraint Surface of a Circular Tank > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A cylindrical water tank must hold exactly $V = 100\pi$ cubic meters. Let $r$ = radius (m), $h$ = height (m). Define and analyze the constraint surface. **Step 1 — Set up the equality constraint:** The volume of a cylinder is: $$V = \pi r^2 h$$ Imposing the constraint: $$g(r, h) = \pi r^2 h - 100\pi = 0$$ **Step 2 — Simplify the constraint surface equation:** Dividing both sides by $\pi$: $$r^2 h = 100$$ This is the equation of the constraint surface in the $(r, h)$-plane. **Step 3 — Compute the normal vector (gradient):** $$\nabla g(r, h) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial r} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial h} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2\pi r h \\[6pt] \pi r^2 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4 — Verify dimensionality:** The design space is $\mathbb{R}^2$ (a 2-D plane in $(r, h)$). The constraint surface is a **curve** of dimension $2 - 1 = 1$. Indeed, $h = 100/r^2$ is a hyperbola. **Step 5 — Tangent direction:** The tangent vector $\mathbf{t}$ must satisfy $\nabla g^T \mathbf{t} = 0$: $$\begin{bmatrix} 2\pi r h & \pi r^2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} t_1 \\ t_2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$$ $$2\pi r h \, t_1 + \pi r^2 \, t_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_2 = -\dfrac{2h}{r} t_1$$ Picking $t_1 = 1$: $$\mathbf{t} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -2h/r \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -200/r^3 \end{bmatrix}$$ This tangent vector points along the constraint curve — i.e., along directions that maintain the volume constraint exactly. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Constraint Types in 2-D | Constraint Type | Example Equation | Geometric Shape | Dimensionality | Normal Vector $\nabla g$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Linear Equality | $x_1 + x_2 = 5$ | Straight Line | 1 | $(1, 1)^T$ (constant) | | Linear Inequality | $x_1 + x_2 \leq 5$ | Half-plane | 2 (filled) | $(1, 1)^T$ (constant) | | Non-linear Equality | $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 25$ | Circle | 1 | $(2x_1, 2x_2)^T$ (variable) | | Non-linear Inequality | $x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 25$ | Disk | 2 (filled) | $(2x_1, 2x_2)^T$ (variable) | | Parabolic | $x_2 = x_1^2$ | Parabola | 1 | $(-2x_1, 1)^T$ (variable) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation:** The gradient $\nabla g$ of a **linear** constraint is *constant* across the entire surface. The gradient of a **non-linear** constraint varies from point to point, and the constraint surface bends. --- ### 3.4 General Derivation: Lagrange Multiplier Preview The deepest role of the constraint surface appears in the **first-order necessary optimality condition**: At a regular point $\mathbf{x}^*$ on the constraint surface that minimizes $f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$: $$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \lambda^* \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$ for some scalar $\lambda^* \in \mathbb{R}$ called the **Lagrange multiplier**. **Geometric interpretation:** Both gradients are normal to the constraint surface at $\mathbf{x}^*$, and they must be *collinear* (anti-parallel for minimization). This is precisely the **tangency condition** — the level curve of $f$ must "kiss" the constraint surface at the optimum. $$\boxed{\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = -\lambda^* \nabla g(\mathbf{x}^*)}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Conceptual Block Architecture of a Constraint Surface ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Design Space R^n
n-dimensional variable space"] B["Constraint Function
g(x) less than or equal to 0"] C["Constraint Surface
g(x) equals 0
Hypersurface of dimension n-1"] D["Feasible Region F
Set where g(x) less than or equal to 0"] E["Normal Vector
grad g at x0
Perpendicular to surface"] F["Tangent Hyperplane
Local linearization at x0"] G["Optimum Point x star
Lies ON the surface if active"] H["Gradient of Objective
grad f at x star
Collinear with grad g"] A --> B B --> C B --> D C --> E C --> F D --> G C --> G E --> H F --> H H --> I["Tangency Condition
grad f = lambda times grad g"] ``` --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Active vs. Inactive Constraints ```mermaid flowchart LR Start["Candidate Point x in R^n"] Q1{"Is g(x) equal to 0?"} Active["ACTIVE Constraint
Point lies ON the surface
Binding at optimum"] Inactive["INACTIVE Constraint
Strict inequality g(x) less than 0
Has slack; not binding"] Implication1["Surface is binding;
Lagrange multiplier nonzero"] Implication2["Constraint can be ignored
locally; mu equals 0"] Start --> Q1 Q1 -- Yes --> Active Q1 -- No --> Inactive Active --> Implication1 Inactive --> Implication2 ``` --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Constrained Optimization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["INPUT BLOCK"] I1["Objective Function f(x)"] I2["Equality Constraints h_j(x) equals 0"] I3["Inequality Constraints g_k(x) less than or equal to 0"] I4["Variable Bounds x_i between x_i^L and x_i^U"] end subgraph SURFACE["CONSTRAINT SURFACE BLOCK"] S1["Identify Active Set A"] S2["Compute grad g_j for active constraints"] S3["Construct Tangent Cone"] S4["Form Tangency Conditions"] end subgraph SOLVER["OPTIMIZATION ENGINE"] O1["Lagrange Multiplier Method"] O2["Kuhn-Tucker Conditions"] O3["Search along tangent directions"] end subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT BLOCK"] R1["Optimal x star"] R2["Multipliers lambda star, mu star"] R3["Verified Feasibility"] end I2 --> S1 I3 --> S1 I4 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> O1 S4 --> O2 S1 --> O3 O1 --> R1 O2 --> R1 O3 --> R1 O1 --> R2 O2 --> R2 O1 --> R3 O2 --> R3 ``` --- ### 4.4 Schematic Description: 2-D Visualization of Constraint Surfaces **Scenario A — Linear Equality Constraint in $\mathbb{R}^2$:** ``` x2 ^ | Feasible Region (x1 + x2 = 5) | \ | \ <- Constraint Surface | \ | \ | infeasible \ feasible (only this line) | \ +-------------------*------> x1 (2.5, 2.5) ``` In this scenario, the feasible region **is** the constraint surface — a 1-D line embedded in 2-D space. **Scenario B — Non-Linear Inequality Constraint:** ``` x2 ^ | g(x1,x2) = x1^2 + x2^2 - 25 = 0 | / Constraint Surface (circle) | / \ | / F \ <- Feasible Disk (interior + boundary) | \ / | \ / | \/ +--------*-----------> x1 (5,0) ``` Here, the feasible region is the **filled disk** bounded by the constraint surface — a circle of radius 5. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Define the term "constraint surface" in the context of engineering optimization. Explain with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** A constraint surface is the geometric locus of points in the design space that satisfies a constraint equation as an equality. For an equality constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, the surface itself is the feasible set; for an inequality constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, the surface forms the boundary of the feasible region. **Example:** Consider the constraint $x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 25$ in $\mathbb{R}^2$. The constraint surface is a circle of radius 5 centered at the origin. The feasible region for $x_1^2 + x_2^2 \leq 25$ is the closed disk bounded by this circle. --- **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **Differentiate between active and inactive constraints at an optimal point.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Active Constraint | Inactive Constraint | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition** | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ | | **Geometric Meaning** | Optimal point lies *on* the constraint surface | Optimal point lies strictly inside the feasible region | | **Binding?** | Yes, binding | No, has slack | | **Multiplier** | $\mu_j \geq 0$ (possibly nonzero) | $\mu_j = 0$ | If the constraint is active, the optimum touches the boundary and the constraint influences the solution. If inactive, the constraint can be locally removed without changing the optimum. --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each contains two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically worth 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **Consider the optimization problem:** $$\text{Minimize } f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$ $$\text{subject to } g(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 = 0$$ #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** **Identify the constraint surface, find its geometric shape, and compute the normal vector at the point $(2, 2)$. Also, find the tangent direction at this point.** **Step 1 — Identify the constraint surface:** The constraint $g(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 = 0$ defines a **straight line** in $\mathbb{R}^2$. Rewriting: $x_2 = 4 - x_1$. This is a line of slope $-1$ and $x_2$-intercept $4$. **Step 2 — Verify the point $(2,2)$ lies on the surface:** $$g(2, 2) = 2 + 2 - 4 = 0 \checkmark$$ The point $(2,2)$ lies exactly on the constraint surface. **Step 3 — Compute the gradient (normal vector):** $$\nabla g(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial g}{\partial x_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This normal vector is **constant** everywhere on the line (since the constraint is linear). **Step 4 — Compute the tangent direction:** The tangent vector $\mathbf{t}$ must satisfy $\nabla g^T \mathbf{t} = 0$: $$\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} t_1 \\ t_2 \end{bmatrix} = t_1 + t_2 = 0 \Rightarrow t_2 = -t_1$$ Choosing $t_1 = 1$, the normalized tangent direction is: $$\mathbf{t} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$ This vector points along the line $x_1 + x_2 = 4$, confirming our geometric intuition. #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** **Find the point on the constraint surface that minimizes $f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. Verify using the Lagrange multiplier method.** **Step 1 — Set up the Lagrangian:** $$\mathcal{L}(x_1, x_2, \lambda) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + \lambda(x_1 + x_2 - 4)$$ **Step 2 — Take partial derivatives and set to zero:** $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = -\frac{\lambda}{2}$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + \lambda = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = -\frac{\lambda}{2}$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \lambda} = x_1 + x_2 - 4 = 0$$ **Step 3 — Solve the system:** From the first two equations: $x_1 = x_2$. Substituting into the constraint: $$x_1 + x_1 = 4 \Rightarrow x_1 = 2, \, x_2 = 2$$ $$\lambda = -2x_1 = -4$$ **Step 4 — Verify via tangency condition:** $$\nabla f(2, 2) = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nabla g(2, 2) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ Check: $\nabla f + \lambda \nabla g = \begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} + (-4)\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} \checkmark$ **Step 5 — Optimal value:** $$f(2, 2) = 2^2 + 2^2 = 8$$ The minimum distance from the origin to the line $x_1 + x_2 = 4$ is $\sqrt{8} = 2\sqrt{2}$, attained at $(2,2)$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Consider the following constrained problem:** $$\text{Minimize } f(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2$$ $$\text{subject to } g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 13 \leq 0$$ $$g_2(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 5 \leq 0$$ #### **Part (a) — [7 Marks]** **Sketch the constraint surfaces and identify the feasible region geometrically.** **Step 1 — Analyze $g_1$:** $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1^2 + x_2^2 = 13$ — a **circle** of radius $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$ centered at origin. Feasible side: $g_1 \leq 0$ → **inside** the circle (including boundary). **Step 2 — Analyze $g_2$:** $g_2(\mathbf{x}) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 = 5$ — a **straight line** with slope $-1$. Feasible side: $g_2 \leq 0$ → **below** the line $x_1 + x_2 = 5$ (i.e., toward origin). **Step 3 — Intersection region:** The feasible region is the **lens-shaped (crescent) region** that lies inside the circle and below the line $x_1 + x_2 = 5$. **Step 4 — Find intersection of the two constraint surfaces:** Setting $x_2 = 5 - x_1$ and substituting: $$x_1^2 + (5 - x_1)^2 = 13$$ $$x_1^2 + 25 - 10x_1 + x_1^2 = 13$$ $$2x_1^2 - 10x_1 + 12 = 0$$ $$x_1^2 - 5x_1 + 6 = 0$$ $$(x_1 - 2)(x_1 - 3) = 0$$ So intersections are at $(2, 3)$ and $(3, 2)$. These are the vertices of the feasible region's boundary arc. #### **Part (b) — [7 Marks]** **Determine the optimum. Justify whether each constraint is active or inactive.** **Step 1 — Unconstrained optimum:** $\nabla f = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 3, \, x_2 = 2$. Check feasibility: $g_1(3, 2) = 9 + 4 - 13 = 0$ (active), $g_2(3, 2) = 3 + 2 - 5 = 0$ (active). **Step 2 — Both constraints active at unconstrained optimum:** Both hold as equality. So $(3, 2)$ is feasible and satisfies KKT conditions. **Step 3 — Verify via KKT:** $$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - 3) \\ 2(x_2 - 2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\nabla g_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 \\ 2x_2 \end{bmatrix}_{(3,2)} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nabla g_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ KKT: $\nabla f + \mu_1 \nabla g_1 + \mu_2 \nabla g_2 = 0$: $$\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + \mu_1 \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix} + \mu_2 \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Solving: $6\mu_1 + \mu_2 = 0$ and $4\mu_1 + \mu_2 = 0$ → $\mu_1 = 0, \mu_2 = 0$. But multipliers must satisfy $\mu_j \geq 0$. So $(3,2)$ with $\mu_1 = \mu_2 = 0$ **violates** KKT. **Step 4 — Re-check feasibility carefully:** At $(3,2)$: $g_1 = 0$, $g_2 = 0$, both active. Apply KKT with strict non-negativity: $6\mu_1 + \mu_2 = 0$ $4\mu_1 + \mu_2 = 0$ Subtracting: $2\mu_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \mu_1 = 0, \mu_2 = 0$. Since $\mu_j \geq 0$ required, this is a **degenerate** case. **Step 5 — Check points on the constraint arc:** The objective is squared distance from $(3, 2)$. We seek the point in the feasible region closest to $(3, 2)$. Since $(3,2)$ lies on the circle boundary, the closest point on the feasible arc is $(3, 2)$ itself (if it satisfies $g_2 \leq 0$). It does: $g_2 = 0$. **Conclusion:** The optimum is $\mathbf{x}^* = (3, 2)$, with $f^* = 0$. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to verify feasibility:** Students often find the unconstrained optimum and stop. Always check $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$. > 2. **Misinterpreting active vs. inactive:** If $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$, the constraint is **inactive**, and the multiplier $\mu_j = 0$. > 3. **Confusing equality and inequality normals:** For equality, $\lambda$ is unrestricted; for inequality $\mu \geq 0$. This sign difference flips collinearity direction. > 4. **Ignoring regularity / constraint qualification:** KKT requires $\nabla g_j$ of active constraints to be linearly independent. If violated, KKT may not hold. > 5. **Unit confusion:** Always state the units of design variables in the final answer. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams** - ✅ **Constraint Surface** = locus where $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$; forms the *boundary* of feasible region for inequality constraints. - ✅ **Dimensionality**: An equality constraint in $\mathbb{R}^n$ reduces degrees of freedom by 1; surface has dimension $n - 1$. - ✅ **Normal Vector**: $\nabla g(\mathbf{x})$ points *outward* from the feasible region; orthogonal to the surface. - ✅ **Tangent Hyperplane**: Set of all directions $\mathbf{d}$ satisfying $\nabla g^T \mathbf{d} = 0$. - ✅ **Active Constraint**: $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ — binding, affects optimum, multiplier may be nonzero. - ✅ **Inactive Constraint**: $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ — non-binding, slack, multiplier is **zero**. - ✅ **Tangency Condition**: At optimum, $\nabla f = -\lambda \nabla g$ (equality case) — gradients are anti-parallel. - ✅ **Feasible Region**: $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \, j = 1, \ldots, m\}$. - ✅ **Linear constraint** ⇒ constant normal; **Non-linear constraint** ⇒ variable normal. - ✅ Always verify **regularity** ($\nabla g_j \neq 0$) before applying first-order KKT/Lagrange conditions. - ✅ Master the difference between **equality** and **inequality** constraint signs in multiplier rules.

topics.title: Objective Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Objective Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the canonical formulation of an Engineering Optimization problem, the **Objective Function** (also called the *criterion function*, *cost function*, or *merit function*) is the mathematical expression that quantifies the quality, performance, cost, or efficiency of a system's design as a function of its **decision variables**.

It is the single scalar function whose extremum (minimum or maximum) the optimization algorithm seeks. Mathematically, the optimization problem is compactly stated as:

$$
\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T
$$

$$
\text{that minimizes (or maximizes) } f(\mathbf{x})
$$

subject to a set of equality constraints $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ and inequality constraints $h_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The objective function is the heart of any optimization problem. Without it, there is no measure of "better" or "worse," and therefore no optimization task exists. Every other component (variables, constraints, feasible region) exists *only to support* the evaluation and extremization of $f(\mathbf{x})$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a delivery driver in Kochi with **10 packages** to deliver across the city. Your **decision variables** are the *order* in which you visit each address, the *route* taken, and the *speed* of your vehicle. The **objective function** is the **total time** (or fuel consumed, or total distance covered) — a *single number* that summarizes how good or bad your delivery plan is. The **constraints** are the things you *cannot* violate: traffic one-way rules, delivery time windows, vehicle capacity, and speed limits.

Optimization = finding the route that makes this single number as small (or large) as possible.

| Real-World Quantity | Optimization Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Total delivery time | **Objective Function** $f(\mathbf{x})$ |
| One-way road rules | **Inequality Constraints** $h_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ |
| Number of packages | **Decision Variable Dimension** $n$ |
| Best possible delivery plan | **Optimal Solution** $\mathbf{x}^*$ |

> [!NOTE]
> A scalar quantity measured in standard SI units such as **rupees (₹)**, **seconds (s)**, **kilograms (kg)**, **watts (W)**, or **meters (m)** is *typically* what an objective function returns. In unconstrained pure mathematics, $f(\mathbf{x})$ can simply be a real number $f \in \mathbb{R}$.

### 1.3 Classification of Objective Functions

Objective functions are classified along three fundamental axes that KTU examiners frequently test:

1. **By extremum type:** *Minimization* $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ vs. *Maximization* $\max f(\mathbf{x})$. These are interchangeable because $\max f = -\min(-f)$.
2. **By variable dependence:** *Explicit* (directly expressed in terms of $\mathbf{x}$) vs. *Implicit* (defined through auxiliary equations).
3. **By mathematical nature:** *Linear* vs. *Nonlinear*; *Convex* vs. *Non-convex*; *Unimodal* vs. *Multimodal*; *Continuous* vs. *Discrete*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of an Optimization Problem (Operational Logic)

The objective function does not operate in isolation. KTU's 2024 scheme explicitly tests whether students understand the *interaction* between $f(\mathbf{x})$ and the rest of the model:

- **Step 1 — Variable Selection:** The engineer identifies $n$ controllable, independent parameters and assembles them into the vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Step 2 — Objective Formulation:** Using physics, economics, or empirical laws, the engineer expresses the performance metric as $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$. This mapping must be **well-defined** on the feasible region.
- **Step 3 — Constraint Modelling:** All real-world restrictions (physical limits, safety codes, budget caps) are encoded as $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ or $h_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$.
- **Step 4 — Search & Compare:** An algorithm generates candidate vectors $\mathbf{x}^{(0)}, \mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \ldots$ and evaluates $f$ at each, comparing scalar values to drive the search toward the optimum.
- **Step 5 — Termination:** Search stops when no further improvement is possible within tolerance $\varepsilon$, yielding the optimal $\mathbf{x}^*$.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Variables** must be *controllable* — you cannot optimize over parameters you cannot change.
- **Objective** must be *scalar* — comparing two designs requires a single number; multi-objective problems are scalarized via weighted sums.
- **Constraints** define the *feasible set* $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ — the universe of valid candidates.
- The *algorithm* is the *engine* that explores $\mathcal{S}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Canonical minimization problem | $\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} f(\mathbf{x})$ | Standard form; $\mathcal{S}$ is feasible region |
| Canonical maximization problem | $\max_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} f(\mathbf{x})$ | Convertible via $\max f = -\min(-f)$ |
| General constrained form | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, $h_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | $j = 1, \ldots, m$; $k = 1, \ldots, p$ |
| Gradient (first derivative) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left[\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \ldots, \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]^T$ | Direction of steepest ascent |
| Hessian (second derivative) | $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) = H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Curvature matrix |
| Necessary condition (unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Stationary point (interior optimum) |
| Sufficient condition (unconstrained) | $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is positive definite | Guarantees local minimum |
| Linear objective | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} = c_1 x_1 + \cdots + c_n x_n$ | Solvable by Simplex / LP methods |
| Quadratic objective | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x} + d$ | Solvable by QP methods |
| Weighted multi-objective scalarization | $F(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{r} w_i f_i(\mathbf{x})$ | $w_i > 0$, $\sum w_i = 1$ |
| Optimal value | $f^* = f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ | Minimum (or maximum) value attained |

> [!TIP]
> The notation $\vert \cdot \vert$ above represents absolute value / magnitude. In the table we use the symbol "$\mathcal{S}$" for the *feasible set* — memorize it; it appears in nearly every KTU optimization problem.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Field | Typical Objective Function | Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Structural Engineering** | Total weight of a truss $W = \rho \sum A_i L_i$ | Minimize weight subject to stress limits |
| **Mechanical Design** | Power loss in a gearbox $P = T \cdot \omega \cdot (1 - \eta)$ | Maximize efficiency $\eta$ |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Power dissipated $P = I^2 R$ | Minimize heat loss in transmission |
| **Process Engineering** | Production cost per unit $C = \dfrac{C_{\text{fixed}} + C_{\text{variable}}}{N}$ | Minimize unit cost |
| **Aerospace** | Drag $D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 S C_D$ | Minimize drag for given lift |
| **Economics** | Profit $\Pi = R(\mathbf{x}) - C(\mathbf{x})$ | Maximize net profit |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the objective function is always the *thing the engineer can change* via the design variables. The constraints are the *things the engineer cannot change* — they are imposed by nature, code, or budget.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Engineering Word Problem to Objective Function

A common KTU-style question asks the student to *formulate* an optimization problem. The derivation below demonstrates the canonical procedure, step by step.

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical pressure vessel of volume $V = 1 \, \text{m}^3$ must be manufactured. The two design variables are the radius $r$ and the height $h$ (both in meters). The material cost per unit surface area is $k = ₹500/\text{m}^2$ for the curved surface and $k_e = ₹800/\text{m}^2$ for the two end caps. Formulate the objective function.

**Step 1 — Identify the decision variables.**

$$
\mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} r \\ h \end{bmatrix}, \quad r > 0,\ h > 0
$$

> The decision variables are the parameters the designer is free to choose.

**Step 2 — Express the physical constraints as equations.**

The volume must equal $1 \, \text{m}^3$:

$$
V = \pi r^2 h = 1 \implies h = \dfrac{1}{\pi r^2}
$$

> This is an *equality constraint* relating the two variables.

**Step 3 — Compute the surface area components.**

Curved (lateral) surface area:

$$
A_{\text{curved}} = 2 \pi r h
$$

Area of the two end caps (top and bottom):

$$
A_{\text{caps}} = 2 \cdot \pi r^2
$$

**Step 4 — Build the cost expression.**

Total cost $C$ as a function of $r$ and $h$:

$$
C(r, h) = k \cdot A_{\text{curved}} + k_e \cdot A_{\text{caps}}
$$

Substituting the numerical values $k = 500$ and $k_e = 800$:

$$
C(r, h) = 500 \cdot 2\pi r h + 800 \cdot 2 \pi r^2
$$

$$
C(r, h) = 1000 \pi r h + 1600 \pi r^2
$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate the constraint variable using $h = \dfrac{1}{\pi r^2}$.**

$$
C(r) = 1000 \pi r \cdot \dfrac{1}{\pi r^2} + 1600 \pi r^2
$$

$$
C(r) = \dfrac{1000}{r} + 1600 \pi r^2
$$

**Step 6 — The final single-variable objective function is:**

$$
\boxed{\,C(r) = 1600 \pi r^2 + \dfrac{1000}{r}, \quad r > 0\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The units check out: $1600\pi r^2$ has units ₹ (since the constant carries ₹/m² × m²) and $1000/r$ has units ₹ (since 1000 has units ₹·m and $r$ is in m). The objective is dimensionally consistent — this is a frequent **2-mark** KTU question.

### 3.2 Derivation: Converting a Maximization to Minimization

Given the problem $\max f(\mathbf{x})$, KTU frequently asks to convert it to standard minimization form. The derivation is:

$$
\max_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \equiv \min_{\mathbf{x}} \big[ -f(\mathbf{x}) \big]
$$

**Proof:**

$$
\mathbf{x}^* \text{ maximizes } f \iff f(\mathbf{x}^*) \geq f(\mathbf{x}) \ \forall \mathbf{x}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $-1$ (which flips the inequality):

$$
-f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq -f(\mathbf{x}) \ \forall \mathbf{x}
$$

$$
\therefore \mathbf{x}^* \text{ minimizes } -f
$$

$$
\blacksquare
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Common Objective Functions

| Function Class | Form | Convex? | Unique Min? | Standard Algorithm |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Linear | $\mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Both | If feasible & bounded | Simplex, Interior Point |
| Strictly Convex Quadratic | $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x}$ with $Q \succ 0$ | Yes | Yes | Direct: $\mathbf{x}^* = -Q^{-1}\mathbf{c}$ |
| General Quadratic | $\tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | If $Q \succeq 0$ | If $Q \succ 0$ | Active Set, KKT |
| Sum of Squares | $\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Yes | Yes | Least Squares (Normal Eq.) |
| Non-convex Polynomial | $x^4 - 4x^2 + x$ | No | Multiple local minima | Global: GA, Simulated Annealing |
| Discrete / Combinatorial | $f(\sigma)$ over permutations $\sigma$ | N/A | Many | Branch & Bound, Dynamic Prog. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Role of Objective Function in the Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Decision Variables x]
    B --> C[Formulate Objective Function f x]
    B --> D[Formulate Constraints g and h]
    C --> E[Feasible Region S Definition]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Optimization Algorithm]
    F --> G[Candidate Solution x_k]
    G --> H[Evaluate f x_k]
    H --> I{Is f x_k Improved?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Update Best x star and f star]
    I -- No --> K[Discard Candidate]
    J --> L{Convergence Met eps}
    K --> L
    L -- No --> F
    L -- Yes --> M[Output Optimal x star and f star]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is evaluated at every candidate the algorithm proposes. The scalar value returned is the *only* feedback signal the algorithm uses to drive the search.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Input-Process-Output View of the Objective Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Inputs
        X1[x1 decision variable]
        X2[x2 decision variable]
        X3[xn decision variable]
    end
    subgraph Process
        FOBJ[Objective Function f x]
    end
    subgraph Outputs
        FSCAL[Scalar value f of x]
        COMP[Comparison with current best]
        GRAD[Gradient nabla f x]
        HESS[Hessian nabla squared f x]
    end
    X1 --> FOBJ
    X2 --> FOBJ
    X3 --> FOBJ
    FOBJ --> FSCAL
    FOBJ --> COMP
    FOBJ --> GRAD
    FOBJ --> HESS
```

**Reading the diagram:** A single objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ serves four distinct roles: it returns a *scalar value*, enables *comparison* between designs, supplies the *gradient* for gradient-based methods, and supplies the *Hessian* for second-order methods.

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choosing the Right Algorithm from the Objective Function Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given f x] --> B{Is f linear?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use Simplex or LP]
    B -- No --> D{Is f quadratic with Q positive definite?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use QP direct method]
    D -- No --> F{Are derivatives available?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Gradient Descent or Newton]
    F -- No --> H[Use Direct Search or Simplex Method of Nelder Mead]
    G --> I{Is f convex?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Guaranteed Global Optimum]
    I -- No --> K[Use Global Methods GA PSO SA]
    H --> K
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**
Define the term *Objective Function* in the context of engineering optimization. How is it related to the *decision variables* and the *feasible region*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **objective function** is a scalar mathematical function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies the performance, cost, or efficiency of a system in terms of its $n$ **decision variables** $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n]^T$. **(1 mark)**
>
> The decision variables are the controllable design parameters (e.g., radius $r$, height $h$, voltage $V$). **(1 mark)**
>
> The objective function is evaluated *only* at points inside the **feasible region** $\mathcal{S}$ — the subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ defined by the problem's constraints. The optimization algorithm searches $\mathcal{S}$ for the point $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathcal{S}$ that minimizes (or maximizes) $f$. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
Distinguish between a **linear objective function** and a **nonlinear objective function**. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **linear objective function** is of the form $f(\mathbf{x}) = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n$ with all variables appearing to the first power and no products. **(1 mark)**
>
> A **nonlinear objective function** contains products, powers, trigonometric terms, exponentials, or any other nonlinear operation on the variables. **(1 mark)**
>
> *Example of linear:* Total cost of materials $C = 50x_1 + 80x_2$ where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are quantities of two raw materials. **(0.5 marks)**
>
> *Example of nonlinear:* Power dissipated in a resistor $P = I^2 R$, where the variable $I$ appears squared. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> As per KTU 2024 regulations, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students answer *either* the full Question A *or* the full Question B.

---

## ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** A rectangular open-top box of volume $V = 32 \, \text{m}^3$ is to be constructed using material that costs ₹40 per $\text{m}^2$ for the base and ₹20 per $\text{m}^2$ for the four sides. Formulate the complete optimization problem: clearly state the decision variables, the objective function, and the constraint(s). Reduce the problem to a single-variable objective function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the dimensions of the box (length, width, height) that minimize the total cost. Use calculus-based necessary and sufficient conditions. State the minimum cost. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Q.A

#### Part (a) — Formulation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decision variables:** Let $l, w, h$ be the length, width, and height of the box (in meters). Therefore $\mathbf{x} = [l, w, h]^T$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Volume constraint (equality):**

$$
V = l \cdot w \cdot h = 32 \implies lwh = 32
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Surface area components:**

- Base area: $A_b = l w$
- Side areas (4): $A_s = 2 l h + 2 w h$

**Step 4 — Cost function (objective function):**

$$
C(l, w, h) = 40 \cdot A_b + 20 \cdot A_s
$$

$$
C(l, w, h) = 40 l w + 20 (2 l h + 2 w h)
$$

$$
C(l, w, h) = 40 l w + 40 l h + 40 w h
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Reduce to single variable using symmetry and constraint:**

For minimum cost, symmetry gives $l = w$. Let $l = w = x$. Then $x^2 h = 32$:

$$
h = \dfrac{32}{x^2}
$$

Substituting into $C$:

$$
C(x) = 40 x^2 + 40 x \cdot \dfrac{32}{x^2} + 40 x \cdot \dfrac{32}{x^2}
$$

$$
C(x) = 40 x^2 + \dfrac{1280}{x} + \dfrac{1280}{x}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,C(x) = 40 x^2 + \dfrac{2560}{x}, \quad x > 0\,}
$$

**(3 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Optimal Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute the first derivative:**

$$
C'(x) = 80 x - \dfrac{2560}{x^2}
$$

**Step 2 — Set $C'(x) = 0$ (necessary condition):**

$$
80 x = \dfrac{2560}{x^2} \implies 80 x^3 = 2560 \implies x^3 = 32
$$

$$
x = 32^{1/3} \approx 3.1748 \, \text{m}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative (sufficient condition):**

$$
C''(x) = 80 + \dfrac{5120}{x^3}
$$

At $x = 32^{1/3}$: $C''(x) = 80 + \dfrac{5120}{32} = 80 + 160 = 240 > 0$.

Since $C'' > 0$, the critical point is a **local minimum**. The function is also convex on $(0, \infty)$, so it is the **global minimum**. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Optimal dimensions:**

$$
l^* = w^* = 32^{1/3} \approx 3.175 \, \text{m}
$$

$$
h^* = \dfrac{32}{(32^{1/3})^2} = 32^{1/3} \approx 3.175 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 5 — Minimum cost:**

$$
C^* = 40 (32^{1/3})^2 + \dfrac{2560}{32^{1/3}}
$$

$$
C^* = 40 \cdot 32^{2/3} + 2560 \cdot 32^{-1/3}
$$

$$
C^* = 40 \cdot 10.079 + 2560 \cdot 0.3150
$$

$$
C^* \approx 403.18 + 806.35 \approx ₹1209.53
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Final answer:** The optimal box is a **cube** of side $\approx 3.175 \, \text{m}$ with minimum cost $\approx ₹1209.53$.

---

## ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain with suitable engineering examples: (i) single-objective optimization, (ii) multi-objective optimization, and (iii) how a multi-objective problem is reduced to a single-objective problem using the *weighted sum scalarization* method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A company manufactures two products P1 and P2. The profit per unit is ₹50 for P1 and ₹40 for P2. The production is constrained by machine hours (P1 needs 2 hours, P2 needs 1 hour, total 100 hours available) and labour hours (P1 needs 1 hour, P2 needs 1 hour, total 80 hours available). Formulate the problem as a linear optimization problem with a clear objective function and constraints, then solve it graphically. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Q.B

#### Part (a) — Types of Optimization (7 marks)

**(i) Single-objective optimization (2 marks):**
A problem with exactly one objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ to be extremized.
*Engineering example:* Minimize the weight of a truss bridge subject to stress and deflection constraints. Here $f = W(\mathbf{x})$ is the only objective.

**(ii) Multi-objective optimization (2 marks):**
A problem with $r \geq 2$ competing objectives $f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, f_r(\mathbf{x})$ to be optimized simultaneously.
*Engineering example:* Design of an electric vehicle where the engineer wants to simultaneously (a) minimize battery cost, (b) maximize range, and (c) minimize charging time. These objectives typically conflict.

**(iii) Weighted sum scalarization (3 marks):**
A multi-objective problem is reduced to a single objective by forming a weighted sum:

$$
F(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 f_1(\mathbf{x}) + w_2 f_2(\mathbf{x}) + \cdots + w_r f_r(\mathbf{x})
$$

subject to $w_i \geq 0$ and $\sum_{i=1}^{r} w_i = 1$. The weights $w_i$ encode the designer's preference. By varying $w_i$, the engineer traces out the **Pareto front** of non-dominated solutions.

---

#### Part (b) — Linear Programming Formulation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Decision variables:**

$$
x_1 = \text{number of units of P1 produced}, \quad x_2 = \text{number of units of P2 produced}
$$

**Step 2 — Objective function (maximize profit):**

$$
\max Z = 50 x_1 + 40 x_2
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Constraints:**

Machine hours constraint: $2 x_1 + x_2 \leq 100$

Labour hours constraint: $x_1 + x_2 \leq 80$

Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Graphical solution — Find corner points of the feasible region.**

The feasible region is bounded by the intersection of:

- Line 1: $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ (intercepts $(50, 0)$ and $(0, 100)$)
- Line 2: $x_1 + x_2 = 80$ (intercepts $(80, 0)$ and $(0, 80)$)
- The axes $x_1 = 0$ and $x_2 = 0$

Intersection of Line 1 and Line 2:
Subtracting: $x_1 = 20$, so $x_2 = 60$. Point: $(20, 60)$.

Feasible corner points: $(0, 0)$, $(50, 0)$, $(20, 60)$, $(0, 80)$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Evaluate $Z$ at each corner point:**

| Corner Point | $Z = 50 x_1 + 40 x_2$ |
|---|---|
| $(0, 0)$ | $0$ |
| $(50, 0)$ | $2500$ |
| $(20, 60)$ | $50(20) + 40(60) = 1000 + 2400 = 3400$ |
| $(0, 80)$ | $3200$ |

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Optimal solution:**

$$
\boxed{\,x_1^* = 20, \quad x_2^* = 60, \quad Z_{\max} = ₹3400\,}
$$

**(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up minimization and maximization:** A student who formulates $\min Z = 50x_1 + 40x_2$ instead of $\max Z$ loses **2 marks** immediately.
> 2. **Missing non-negativity constraints $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$:** In LP problems, these are *mandatory* and earn **1 mark**.
> 3. **Arithmetic errors in the corner-point evaluation:** A single calculation mistake (e.g., $50 \cdot 20 + 40 \cdot 60 = 3400$ written as $3300$) costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Forgetting the units of $Z$:** Always state $Z$ in ₹ (or whatever currency the problem uses). Examiners explicitly check units for **0.5 marks**.
> 5. **Not stating the optimal value explicitly:** Concluding with the *point* but omitting the *value* $Z^*$ loses **1 mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a *scalar* mapping $\mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ that quantifies design quality.
- **Always check three things:** (1) Is the function **scalar**? (2) Is the function **well-defined on the feasible region**? (3) Is the function **differentiable** if you plan to use calculus-based methods?
- **Max ↔ Min conversion:** $\max f(\mathbf{x}) \equiv -\min(-f(\mathbf{x}))$. KTU uses this in at least one sub-question per exam cycle.
- **Standard canonical minimization form:**

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad h_k(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}
$$

- **Necessary condition (unconstrained):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ — a stationary point.
- **Sufficient condition (unconstrained):** $\nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ positive definite (for minimization).
- **Linear vs. nonlinear:** Linear objectives are solved by Simplex/LP; nonlinear objectives require calculus-based, gradient, or metaheuristic methods.
- **Multi-objective → single-objective:** Use weighted scalarization $F = \sum w_i f_i$ with $\sum w_i = 1$, $w_i \geq 0$.
- **Engineering examples to memorize:** Weight of truss, drag on airfoil, profit in production, power loss in resistor, cost of pressure vessel.
- **Formulation steps (always in this order):** (1) Decision variables → (2) Objective function → (3) Constraints → (4) Canonical form.
- **Dimensional check:** Always verify the objective function returns the *correct physical units* — a frequent 2-mark KTU question.
- **Feasible region $\mathcal{S}$** is the set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying *all* constraints; the optimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ lies in $\mathcal{S}$.
- **Convexity of the objective** guarantees that any local minimum is also the global minimum — a powerful property in KTU proofs.
- **For Q.B.-style LP problems:** Always list corner points in a *table*; examiners give credit for structured presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Optimization Problems
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of Optimization Problems

# Classification of Optimization Problems

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Optimization Problem** is a mathematical formulation that seeks to determine the *best* (optimal) value of one or more objective functions by systematically choosing input variables from a permissible set, while satisfying the prevailing system constraints.

In standard KTU 2024 syllabus notation, an optimization problem is written as:

$$
\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \text{ that minimizes (or maximizes) } f(\mathbf{x})
$$

subject to the constraint set $\mathbf{x} \in \Omega$, where $\Omega$ is the feasible region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase *Classification of Optimization Problems* refers to the **systematic taxonomy** of mathematical programming models based on (a) the nature of the objective function, (b) the nature of constraints, (c) the nature of decision variables, (d) the number of objectives, and (e) the deterministic/stochastic behavior.

### 1.2 Why Classification is Needed

Different problem structures demand **different solution algorithms**. A linear programming problem cannot be solved by gradient descent (which is meant for nonlinear problems). Therefore, classification is the *first critical step* before selecting any algorithm.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Recipe Problem"

Imagine a chef (the *decision-maker*) preparing a dish:
- **Objective:** Best taste (single objective) or best taste + minimum cost + minimum cooking time (multi-objective).
- **Variables:** Quantity of salt, sugar, oil, spice mix.
- **Constraints:** Total budget ≤ ₹500, total cooking time ≤ 30 minutes, salt ≤ 5 grams (health constraint).
- **Nature:** If the recipe scales proportionally → *linear*; if taste improves logarithmically → *nonlinear*; if ingredients come in whole packets → *discrete/integer*; if market price fluctuates randomly → *stochastic*.

The chef must therefore first *identify the type* of recipe (problem) before picking the technique (algorithm). This identification is exactly what we call **classification of optimization problems**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Examples of Optimization:**
> - **Civil:** Minimum weight truss design subject to stress limits.
> - **Mechanical:** Minimum fuel consumption path planning for a vehicle.
> - **Electrical:** Optimal power flow minimizing transmission losses.
> - **Production:** Maximum profit product-mix under raw-material constraints.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Canonical Mathematical Form

Every optimization problem in the KTU syllabus reduces to the following general structure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize } & f(\mathbf{x}), \quad \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n \\[4pt]
\text{Subject to:} & \\
& g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \quad \text{(inequality constraints)} \\
& h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p \quad \text{(equality constraints)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{x}$ is the **decision variable vector** (also called *design vector*).
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function** (also called *cost function* or *fitness function*).
- $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ are **inequality constraints**.
- $h_j(\mathbf{x})$ are **equality constraints**.
- $\Omega = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \mid g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\ h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ is the **feasible region**.

### 2.2 Principal Classification Criteria (Structured Hierarchy)

The KTU 2024 scheme organizes classification along the following axes:

**(a) Based on Existence of Constraints**
- **Unconstrained Optimization:** No $g_i$ or $h_j$ exist; $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^n$.
- **Constrained Optimization:** At least one $g_i$ or $h_j$ is active.

**(b) Based on Nature of Objective Function and Constraints**
- **Linear Programming (LP):** Both $f$ and all $g_i, h_j$ are linear.
- **Nonlinear Programming (NLP):** Either $f$ or any constraint is nonlinear.
- **Quadratic Programming (QP):** $f$ is quadratic, constraints are linear.
- **Geometric Programming (GP):** $f$ and constraints are *posynomials*.

**(c) Based on Nature of Decision Variables**
- **Continuous:** $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$ over an interval.
- **Discrete / Integer Programming (IP):** $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.
- **Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP):** Some $x_i$ continuous, others integer.
- **Binary (0–1):** $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

**(d) Based on Number of Objective Functions**
- **Single-Objective Optimization (SOO):** Single $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO):** Vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k)$, $k \ge 2$.

**(e) Based on Determinism of Data**
- **Deterministic Optimization:** All parameters (coefficients) are known constants.
- **Stochastic Programming:** Some parameters are random variables with known distributions.

**(f) Based on the Nature of the Problem (Static vs Dynamic)**
- **Static (Parametric) Optimization:** Variables are time-independent.
- **Dynamic Optimization:** Variables evolve with time; governed by differential/difference equations.

**(g) Based on Convexity of Feasible Region and Objective**
- **Convex Programming:** $f$ is convex, $\Omega$ is a convex set → **global optimum guaranteed**.
- **Non-Convex Programming:** Multiple local optima may exist.

**(h) Based on Separability**
- **Separable:** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i(x_i)$.
- **Non-Separable:** Variables are coupled inside $f$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Classification Axis | Type 1 | Type 2 | Identifying Test |
|---|---|---|---|
| Constraints | Unconstrained: $\Omega = \mathbb{R}^n$ | Constrained: $g_i, h_j$ exist | Count active $g_i$ and $h_j$ |
| Function Nature | Linear (LP) | Nonlinear (NLP) | Check degree of polynomials in $f, g_i, h_j$ |
| Variables | Continuous ($x_i \in \mathbb{R}$) | Integer ($x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$) | Check domain specification |
| Objectives | Single ($f$) | Multi ($\mathbf{F} = (f_1,\ldots,f_k)$) | Count objective functions |
| Data Type | Deterministic | Stochastic | Random variables present? |
| Time | Static | Dynamic | Time index $t$ present? |
| Geometry | Convex | Non-Convex | Hessian $\mathbf{H} \succeq 0$? |
| Separability | Separable | Non-Separable | Cross terms $x_i x_j$ present? |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In modern engineering design, identifying the problem class determines the solver. *CPLEX/Gurobi* are used for LP/MIP; *IPOPT/KNITRO* for NLP; *NSGA-II* for MOO; *SDP solvers* for convex problems. Misclassification wastes compute time and may yield infeasible solutions.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Feasible Region of a Constrained Problem

Consider the canonical problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } & f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 \\
\text{Subject to } & g_1(\mathbf{x}) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 \le 0 \\
& g_2(\mathbf{x}) = -x_1 \le 0 \\
& g_3(\mathbf{x}) = -x_2 \le 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the objective class.**
$f = x_1^2 + x_2^2$ contains squared terms → **Nonlinear (NLP)** classification.

**Step 2 — Classify each constraint.**
- $g_1 = x_1 + x_2 - 4 \le 0$ → Linear inequality.
- $g_2 = -x_1 \le 0$ → Linear inequality.
- $g_3 = -x_2 \le 0$ → Linear inequality.

All constraints linear, but $f$ is nonlinear → overall problem is **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)**.

**Step 3 — Determine feasibility geometry.**
The region $\Omega$ is the intersection of three half-planes:
- $x_1 + x_2 \le 4$
- $x_1 \ge 0$
- $x_2 \ge 0$

This intersection is a **convex triangle** with vertices $(0,0)$, $(4,0)$, $(0,4)$.

**Step 4 — Determine convexity of $f$.**
The Hessian of $f$ is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

All eigenvalues = **2 > 0** → $f$ is **strictly convex**, $\Omega$ is convex → problem is **Convex Programming** → unique global minimum exists.

**Step 5 — Analytical solution.**
Using KKT conditions, the unconstrained minimizer of $f$ is $(0,0) \in \Omega$, hence:

$$
\mathbf{x}^{*} = (0, 0), \quad f(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = 0
$$

### 3.2 Classification Test for an LP

For an LP, every function must be of the form:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n
$$

**Decision rule:**
$$
\boxed{\text{If any term } x_i^{\alpha} \text{ has } \alpha \ne 1, \text{ or any product } x_i x_j \text{ appears} \Rightarrow \text{Not an LP}}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Problem Templates

| Engineering Scenario | Objective Function Type | Constraint Type | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Product mix profit maximization | Linear | Linear inequalities | **Linear Programming (LP)** |
| Travelling Salesman | Nonlinear (path cost) | Discrete (binary) | **Integer / Combinatorial** |
| Minimum weight truss | Nonlinear (mass) | Nonlinear (stress) | **Nonlinear Programming (NLP)** |
| Portfolio with random returns | Linear / Quadratic | Probabilistic | **Stochastic Programming** |
| Rocket trajectory | Nonlinear | Differential equations | **Dynamic Programming / Optimal Control** |
| Antenna array beam-pattern | Nonlinear | Linear | **Quadratic Programming (QP)** |
| Optimal power flow | Nonlinear (losses) | Nonlinear (Kirchhoff) | **NLP** |
| Cutting-stock / bin packing | Linear | Linear + integer | **Mixed-Integer LP (MILP)** |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Classification

Given:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize } & f = 2x_1 + 3x_2 \\
\text{Subject to } & x_1 + x_2 \ge 10 \\
& 3x_1 + x_2 \ge 12 \\
& x_1, x_2 \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

- Objective: **linear** (degree 1).
- Constraints: **all linear**.
- Variables: **continuous**, **non-negative**.
- Number of objectives: **one**.
- Data: **deterministic**, **static**.
- Feasible region: **convex polygon**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Classification: Standard Form Linear Programming (LP) in 2 variables.}}
$$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["OPTIMIZATION PROBLEMS"] --> B["BY CONSTRAINTS"]
    A --> C["BY FUNCTION NATURE"]
    A --> D["BY VARIABLES"]
    A --> E["BY OBJECTIVES"]
    A --> F["BY DATA TYPE"]
    A --> G["BY TIME BEHAVIOUR"]
    A --> H["BY CONVEXITY"]

    B --> B1["UNCONSTRAINED"]
    B --> B2["CONSTRAINED"]

    C --> C1["LINEAR LP"]
    C --> C2["QUADRATIC QP"]
    C --> C3["NONLINEAR NLP"]
    C --> C4["GEOMETRIC GP"]
    C --> C5["INTEGER IP"]

    D --> D1["CONTINUOUS"]
    D --> D2["DISCRETE INTEGER"]
    D --> D3["BINARY ZERO ONE"]
    D --> D4["MIXED INTEGER MIP"]

    E --> E1["SINGLE OBJECTIVE SOO"]
    E --> E2["MULTI OBJECTIVE MOO"]

    F --> F1["DETERMINISTIC"]
    F --> F2["STOCHASTIC"]

    G --> G1["STATIC PARAMETRIC"]
    G --> G2["DYNAMIC TIME DEPENDENT"]

    H --> H1["CONVEX PROGRAMMING"]
    H --> H2["NON CONVEX PROGRAMMING"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optimization Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        id1["DESIGN VARIABLES x1...xn"]
        id2["PARAMETERS p1...pk"]
        id3["CONSTANTS c1...cm"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["PROCESSING LAYER"]
        id4["OBJECTIVE FUNCTION f of x"]
        id5["CONSTRAINT FUNCTIONS g_i and h_j"]
        id6["FEASIBILITY TESTER"]
    end

    subgraph CLASS["CLASSIFICATION BLOCK"]
        id7["PROBLEM CLASS DETECTOR LP NLP IP QP MOO STOCHASTIC"]
    end

    subgraph ALGO["ALGORITHM SELECTOR"]
        id8["SIMPLEX OR INTERIOR POINT OR KKT OR BRANCH AND BOUND OR EVOLUTIONARY"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        id9["OPTIMAL x star"]
        id10["OPTIMAL VALUE f of x star"]
    end

    id1 --> id4
    id1 --> id5
    id2 --> id4
    id3 --> id5
    id4 --> id6
    id5 --> id6
    id6 --> id7
    id7 --> id8
    id8 --> id9
    id8 --> id10
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — How to Classify Any Given Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1["START"] --> q1{"ANY CONSTRAINTS PRESENT"}
    q1 -- NO --> r1["UNCONSTRAINED"]
    q1 -- YES --> q2{"ALL FUNCTIONS LINEAR"}
    q2 -- YES --> q3{"ALL VARIABLES INTEGER"}
    q3 -- YES --> r2["INTEGER LP ILP"]
    q3 -- NO --> r3["LINEAR PROGRAMMING LP"]
    q2 -- NO --> q4{"OBJECTIVE QUADRATIC ONLY"}
    q4 -- YES --> r4["QUADRATIC PROGRAMMING QP"]
    q4 -- NO --> q5{"RANDOM VARIABLES PRESENT"}
    q5 -- YES --> r5["STOCHASTIC PROGRAMMING"]
    q5 -- NO --> q6{"TIME INDEX t PRESENT"}
    q6 -- YES --> r6["DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING"]
    q6 -- NO --> q7{"MULTIPLE OBJECTIVES"}
    q7 -- YES --> r7["MULTI OBJECTIVE OPTIMIZATION"]
    q7 -- NO --> r8["NONLINEAR PROGRAMMING NLP"]
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define an optimization problem. Mention any two bases on which optimization problems are classified.*

**Model Answer:**
An optimization problem is a mathematical model that seeks to determine the optimum (minimum or maximum) value of an objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ by selecting values of decision variables $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ from a feasible region $\Omega$ defined by constraints.

**Two bases of classification:**
1. **Based on the nature of the objective function and constraints:** Linear Programming (LP), Nonlinear Programming (NLP), Quadratic Programming (QP), Geometric Programming (GP).
2. **Based on the nature of decision variables:** Continuous, Integer, Binary, or Mixed-Integer Programming.

*(Any two correctly named bases earn full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Differentiate between static and dynamic optimization problems with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Optimization | Dynamic Optimization |
|---|---|---|
| Time dependence | Variables are time-invariant | Variables evolve with time $t$ |
| Governing equations | Algebraic | Differential / Difference |
| Solution technique | LP, NLP, KKT | Pontryagin's Principle, Bellman |
| Example | Optimal product mix for one shift | Optimal trajectory of a rocket launch |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** (14 Marks)

**(a) Classify optimization problems in detail with suitable engineering examples.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Classification along 6 axes with engineering examples:**

1. **By existence of constraints**
   - *Unconstrained:* Minimization of the weight of an unrestrained hanging cable.
   - *Constrained:* Truss design with stress ≤ allowable stress.

2. **By nature of objective and constraints**
   - *LP:* Maximizing profit in a refinery subject to crude availability.
   - *NLP:* Minimum drag profile of an aircraft wing.
   - *QP:* Portfolio variance minimization (mean-variance model).
   - *GP:* Optimal pipeline diameter for minimum cost.

3. **By nature of variables**
   - *Continuous:* Beam thickness optimization.
   - *Integer:* Number of pumps to install in a plant.
   - *Binary:* Whether to open (1) or close (0) a facility.

4. **By number of objectives**
   - *SOO:* Single cost minimization.
   - *MOO:* Lightweight + strong + cheap component design (3 objectives).

5. **By determinism**
   - *Deterministic:* Linear production model with fixed prices.
   - *Stochastic:* Inventory model with random demand.

6. **By static/dynamic nature**
   - *Static:* Optimal insulator thickness for steady-state heat loss.
   - *Dynamic:* Optimal control of a chemical reactor temperature profile.

**(b) Formulate the optimization problem for designing a cylindrical can of fixed volume $V$ at minimum material cost. Identify all components and classify the resulting problem.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Design variables:** Radius $r$ and height $h$ of the cylinder.

**Objective function — minimize surface area (material cost):**

$$
\min f(r, h) = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h
$$

**Constraint — fixed volume $V$:**

$$
\pi r^2 h = V \quad \Rightarrow \quad h = \frac{V}{\pi r^2}
$$

**Substitute to reduce to single variable:**

$$
f(r) = 2\pi r^2 + \frac{2V}{r}
$$

**Domain constraints:** $r > 0$, $h > 0$ (geometric feasibility).

**Optimality test:**

$$
\frac{df}{dr} = 4\pi r - \frac{2V}{r^2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 4\pi r^3 = 2V \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r^{*} = \left(\frac{V}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3}
$$

**Corresponding height:**

$$
h^{*} = \frac{V}{\pi (r^{*})^2} = \frac{V}{\pi \left(\frac{V}{2\pi}\right)^{2/3}} = 2\left(\frac{V}{2\pi}\right)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\boxed{r^{*} = h^{*}/2, \quad h^{*} = 2\,r^{*}}
$$

**Classification of this problem:**
- Objective: nonlinear (contains $r^2$ and $1/r$) → **NLP**.
- Constraint: nonlinear (equality involving product $r^2 h$) → **NLP**.
- Variables: continuous → **Continuous optimization**.
- Number of objectives: one → **Single-Objective (SOO)**.
- Data: deterministic → **Deterministic**.
- Time: not present → **Static**.
- Feasible region: positive orthant → **convex**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Overall class: Static, Deterministic, Continuous-variable NLP.}}
$$

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) Discuss the classification of optimization problems based on the nature of the variables and the number of objective functions. Give one example of each.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Classification by Variables:**

1. **Continuous Variables Optimization:** All $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.
   - *Example:* Optimal cross-section of an I-beam for minimum weight.

2. **Integer Programming (IP):** All $x_i \in \mathbb{Z}$.
   - *Example:* Number of trucks to dispatch.

3. **Binary (0–1) Programming:** $x_i \in \{0,1\}$.
   - *Example:* Selecting sites for cellular towers.

4. **Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP):** Combination of continuous and integer variables.
   - *Example:* Refinery planning — continuous flow rates plus integer unit commitment.

**Classification by Number of Objectives:**

1. **Single-Objective Optimization (SOO):** One $f(\mathbf{x})$.
   - *Example:* Minimize travel time on a road network.

2. **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO):** Vector of $k \ge 2$ objectives.
   - *Example:* Vehicle design — minimize weight $f_1$, minimize cost $f_2$, maximize safety $f_3$.
   - *Solution approach:* Pareto front using weighted sum, $\varepsilon$-constraint, or NSGA-II.

**(b) A manufacturing firm produces two products P1 and P2. Profit per unit is ₹50 for P1 and ₹40 for P2. The firm has 240 hours of machine time and 100 hours of labour. P1 requires 4 hours machine time and 2 hours labour per unit; P2 requires 3 hours machine time and 1 hour labour per unit. Formulate the LP problem and classify it.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Decision variables:**
- $x_1$ = units of P1 produced.
- $x_2$ = units of P2 produced.

**Objective — maximize profit:**

$$
\max Z = 50 x_1 + 40 x_2
$$

**Subject to constraints:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4x_1 + 3x_2 & \le 240 \quad \text{(machine hours)} \\
2x_1 + x_2 & \le 100 \quad \text{(labour hours)} \\
x_1, x_2 & \ge 0 \quad \text{(non-negativity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Component identification:**
- Objective function: linear $50x_1 + 40x_2$.
- Decision variables: $x_1, x_2$.
- Inequality constraints: 2 active.
- Equality constraints: 0.
- Non-negativity: standard.

**Classification:**
- Objective linear, constraints linear → **Linear Programming (LP)**.
- Variables continuous → **Continuous**.
- Single objective → **SOO**.
- Deterministic and static → **Deterministic-Static**.
- Feasible region is a convex polygon → **Convex LP**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Overall class: Standard-Form Convex Linear Programming Problem.}}
$$

**Solution via graphical/corner-point method:**
Corner points of the feasible polygon:
- $(0,0)$ → $Z = 0$
- $(50, 0)$ → $Z = 2500$
- $(0, 80)$ → $Z = 3200$
- Intersection of $4x_1 + 3x_2 = 240$ and $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ → $(30, 40)$ → $Z = 50(30) + 40(40) = 3100$

Maximum at $(0, 80)$: produce 80 units of P2, 0 units of P1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. Students often **omit the non-negativity constraint** — losing 1 mark.
> 2. Students **forget to write the equation in canonical form** (max, all ≤, ≥ 0) — examiners deduct for non-standard notation.
> 3. In classification parts, students write *only the class name* without giving a *justification* (e.g., "LP because all functions are linear"). Always include the **reason**.
> 4. For MOO problems, students forget to mention the **Pareto-optimal front** concept — this is a 2-mark KTU hot-spot.
> 5. Do **not** confuse *static* with *stationary point*. Static = time-independent; stationary = $\nabla f = 0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- Every optimization problem has four canonical components: **decision variables**, **objective function**, **constraints**, and **feasible region**.
- Classification axes (KTU syllabus): **constraints, function nature, variable nature, objectives, determinism, time, convexity**.
- **LP** requires *all* functions to be linear — strictest test.
- **NLP** is the *general* class; LP, QP, GP are special subclasses.
- **Convex programming** is the only class guaranteeing *global* optimality.
- **Multi-objective** problems yield a **Pareto front**, not a single optimum.
- **Stochastic** problems require probability distributions of parameters.
- **Dynamic** problems involve time $t$ and differential/difference equations.
- **Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP)** is the hardest NP-class in practice.
- Misclassification → wrong algorithm → wrong solution. Always classify **first**.
- Examiner expects: *definition → classification axis → example → identifying test* (4-step structure for full marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classical Optimization Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classical Optimization Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Classical Optimization Techniques** refer to the set of analytical, calculus-based mathematical methods used to find the *optimum* (minimum or maximum) value of an objective function $f(x)$ subject to a set of constraints. These techniques rely on the differential calculus framework to derive necessary and sufficient conditions for optimality, forming the theoretical bedrock upon which all modern numerical optimization algorithms (such as Gradient Descent, Sequential Quadratic Programming, etc.) are built.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, classical optimization is classified into the following hierarchical structure:

1. **Single-Variable Optimization (Unconstrained):** $f(x)$ where $x \in \mathbb{R}^1$.
2. **Multivariable Optimization (Unconstrained):** $f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ where $x \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
3. **Multivariable Optimization with Equality Constraints:** Solved via the **Lagrange Multiplier Method**.
4. **Multivariable Optimization with Inequality Constraints:** Solved via the **Kuhn-Tucker (K-T) Conditions**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Classical methods are *analytical* and *closed-form*. They provide exact solutions when the function is differentiable and well-behaved (continuous and smooth). When the function is non-differentiable, noisy, or highly non-linear, numerical methods (covered in later modules) must be employed.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hiking Analogy for Optimization**
>
