> Imagine you are hiking in a thick fog on a mountain. You cannot see the peak (maximum) or the valley (minimum) of the terrain. You only know the **slope** of the ground directly beneath your feet.
>
> - **Analytical Approach (Classical):** If someone gave you the *exact 3D mathematical map* (equation) of the mountain, you could mathematically calculate the exact coordinates of the peak by solving $\nabla f(x) = 0$. This is **classical optimization**.
> - **Numerical Approach:** If you only have the terrain, you would repeatedly step in the steepest uphill direction until you reach the peak. This is **numerical optimization** (e.g., Gradient Ascent).
>
> The **gradient** $\nabla f(x)$ is the mathematical equivalent of the "slope under your feet." At the peak, the slope is **zero** (flat ground at the summit).

### 1.3 Classification of Optimization Problems

The general non-linear programming (NLP) problem is formally stated as:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & x = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \in \mathbb{R}^n \\
\text{to minimize } & f(x) \\
\text{subject to } & g_j(x) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \quad \text{(inequality constraints)} \\
& h_k(x) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \quad \text{(equality constraints)}
\end{aligned}$$

The standard assumptions for classical optimization are:
- The objective function $f(x)$ and constraints are **continuous** and **differentiable** (at least twice).
- The problem has a **bounded feasible region** (otherwise the optimum may escape to infinity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Single-Variable Optimization (Unconstrained)

For a function $f(x)$, the analytical procedure to find the optimum is two-fold:

**Step 1 — Necessary Condition (First Derivative Test):**
The optimum $x^*$ must satisfy the first-order necessary condition (FONC):

$$\frac{df(x^*)}{dx} = f'(x^*) = 0$$

These stationary points could be local minima, local maxima, or **saddle/inflection points**. The first derivative test alone cannot distinguish between them.

**Step 2 — Sufficient Condition (Second Derivative Test):**
The second-order sufficient condition (SOSC) is used to classify the stationary point:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } f''(x^*) &> 0 \implies \text{Local MINIMUM (Convex)} \\
\text{If } f''(x^*) &< 0 \implies \text{Local MAXIMUM (Concave)} \\
\text{If } f''(x^*) &= 0 \implies \text{TEST FAILS (Higher-order test required)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 2.2 Multivariable Optimization (Unconstrained)

For $f(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$, the first-order partial derivatives are stacked into the **gradient vector** $\nabla f(x)$.

**Necessary Condition (FONC):**

$$\nabla f(x^*) = 0 \implies \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Sufficient Condition (SOSC):** The **Hessian Matrix** $H(x)$ of second partial derivatives must be tested for *definiteness*.

$$H(x) = \nabla^2 f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hessian $H$ is always symmetric** (Clairaut's Theorem: $\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_j \partial x_i}$) provided the function has continuous second partials.

### 2.3 Definiteness Tests for the Hessian

| Test Applied to $H(x^*)$ | Conclusion | Nature of Stationary Point |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| All leading principal minors are **positive** | $H$ is **Positive Definite (PD)** | $x^*$ is a **strict local MINIMUM** |
| Leading principal minors alternate in sign, starting with **negative** | $H$ is **Negative Definite (ND)** | $x^*$ is a **strict local MAXIMUM** |
| $H$ is neither PD nor ND | **Indefinite** (saddle point) | $x^*$ is a **SADDLE POINT** (not an optimum) |
| $H$ is PD or ND but test is inconclusive | **Positive / Negative Semi-Definite** | **Inconclusive** (higher-order test needed) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Eigenvalue Test (alternate method):** If all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $H$ are $> 0 \Rightarrow$ PD. If all $< 0 \Rightarrow$ ND. If mixed signs $\Rightarrow$ Indefinite.

### 2.4 Lagrange Multiplier Method (Equality Constraints)

To minimize $f(x)$ subject to a single equality constraint $h(x) = 0$, we construct the **Lagrangian function**:

$$L(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \lambda \cdot h(x)$$

where $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$ is the **Lagrange multiplier**. The necessary conditions are:

$$\nabla_x L = 0 \implies \frac{\partial L}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} + \lambda \frac{\partial h}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad \forall i$$

$$\nabla_\lambda L = 0 \implies h(x) = 0$$

The Hessian of the Lagrangian, bordered by the gradient of the constraint, is used for the sufficient condition.

### 2.5 Kuhn-Tucker (K-T) Conditions (Inequality Constraints)

For minimization of $f(x)$ subject to $g_j(x) \leq 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{(Primal Feasibility):} \quad & g_j(x^*) \leq 0 \\
\text{(Dual Feasibility):} \quad & \mu_j \geq 0 \\
\text{(Complementary Slackness):} \quad & \mu_j \cdot g_j(x^*) = 0 \\
\text{(Stationarity):} \quad & \nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(x^*) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Complementary Slackness** is the *defining* feature of K-T conditions. It states that if a constraint is *inactive* ($g_j(x^*) < 0$), then its multiplier must be $\mu_j = 0$. If a constraint is *active* ($g_j(x^*) = 0$), then $\mu_j \geq 0$.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Topic | Key Formula / Condition | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| FONC (Single Variable) | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | Finding critical points in structural mechanics |
| SOSC (Single Variable) | $f''(x^*) > 0$ (min) or $< 0$ (max) | Stability analysis of equilibrium |
| Gradient (FONC Multi) | $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$ | First-order optimality in design optimization |
| Hessian Matrix | $H_{ij} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Second-order curvature information |
| Positive Definite (PD) | All leading principal minors $> 0$ | Guarantees local MINIMUM |
| Negative Definite (ND) | Alternating signs starting with $(-1)^n$ | Guarantees local MAXIMUM |
| Lagrangian | $L = f(x) + \lambda h(x)$ | Resource allocation under strict constraints |
| K-T Stationarity | $\nabla f + \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j = 0$ | Constrained engineering design (stress, cost) |
| Complementary Slackness | $\mu_j g_j(x^*) = 0$ | Identifies which constraints are *active* |
| Hessian Bordered (Suff.) | Determinant of bordered Hessian $> 0$ | Sufficiency for constrained problems |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Second-Order Sufficient Condition for Single Variable

**Statement:** If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $x^*$ is a local minimum.

**Derivation via Taylor Series Expansion:**

We expand $f(x)$ about the stationary point $x^*$ using the second-order Taylor polynomial.

$$f(x) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*)(x - x^*) + \frac{1}{2!} f''(x^*)(x - x^*)^2 + \mathcal{O}((x-x^*)^3)$$

Since $x^*$ is a stationary point, the first-order term vanishes by the FONC:

$$f'(x^*)(x - x^*) = 0 \cdot (x - x^*) = 0$$

The equation reduces to:

$$f(x) - f(x^*) = \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*)(x - x^*)^2 + \mathcal{O}((x-x^*)^3)$$

Now, the sign of the left-hand side is dictated by the leading term on the right. The squared term $(x - x^*)^2$ is strictly positive for any $x \neq x^*$. Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{If } f''(x^*) > 0 \implies \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*)(x - x^*)^2 > 0 &\implies f(x) > f(x^*) \implies \text{Local MIN} \\
\text{If } f''(x^*) < 0 \implies \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*)(x - x^*)^2 < 0 &\implies f(x) < f(x^*) \implies \text{Local MAX}
\end{aligned}$$

The $\mathcal{O}((x-x^*)^3)$ term is dominated by the quadratic term in a sufficiently small neighborhood, which completes the proof.

### 3.2 Derivation: Lagrange Multiplier Necessary Condition

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2)$ subject to $h(x_1, x_2) = 0$.

**Geometric Intuition:** At an optimum on the constraint curve, the gradient of the objective function $\nabla f$ must be perpendicular to the constraint curve. But the gradient of the constraint $\nabla h$ is *also* perpendicular to the constraint curve. Therefore, the two gradient vectors must be **parallel**.

Mathematically, two vectors being parallel means one is a scalar multiple of the other:

$$\nabla f(x^*) = -\lambda \nabla h(x^*)$$

Rearranging:

$$\nabla f(x^*) + \lambda \nabla h(x^*) = 0$$

This is exactly the stationarity condition of the Lagrangian $L(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \lambda h(x)$, since:

$$\nabla_x L = \nabla f + \lambda \nabla h = 0$$

The constraint condition $h(x) = 0$ is recovered by setting:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = h(x) = 0$$

Thus, the $(n+1)$ equations $\nabla_x L = 0$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = 0$ form a system of $n+1$ unknowns ($n$ variables plus $\lambda$), yielding a unique solution.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration: Multivariable Optimization

**Problem:** Find the minimum of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 + 13$.

**Step 1 — Compute First Partial Derivatives (Gradient):**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 - 6$$

**Step 2 — Apply FONC: Set gradient to zero vector:**

$$2x_1 - 4 = 0 \implies x_1^* = 2$$

$$2x_2 - 6 = 0 \implies x_2^* = 3$$

The candidate stationary point is $x^* = (2, 3)$.

**Step 3 — Construct the Hessian Matrix:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\ \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Test Definiteness using Leading Principal Minors:**

$$\Delta_1 = \vert H_{11} \vert = \vert 2 \vert = 2 > 0$$

$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (2)(2) - (0)(0) = 4 > 0$$

Since **all** leading principal minors are strictly positive, the Hessian is **Positive Definite (PD)**.

**Conclusion:** $x^* = (2, 3)$ is a **strict local (and global) minimum**. The minimum value is:

$$f(2, 3) = 2^2 + 3^2 - 4(2) - 6(3) + 13 = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration: Lagrange Multipliers

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x, y) = x^2 + 2y^2$ subject to $h(x, y) = x + y - 4 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$L(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + 2y^2 + \lambda(x + y - 4)$$

**Step 2 — Compute Partial Derivatives and Set to Zero:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 2x + \lambda = 0 \implies x = -\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = 4y + \lambda = 0 \implies y = -\frac{\lambda}{4}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = x + y - 4 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve the System:**

Substitute the expressions for $x$ and $y$ into the constraint:

$$-\frac{\lambda}{2} - \frac{\lambda}{4} - 4 = 0$$

$$-\frac{3\lambda}{4} = 4 \implies \lambda = -\frac{16}{3}$$

Back-substitute to find the variables:

$$x^* = -\frac{1}{2} \left(-\frac{16}{3}\right) = \frac{8}{3}$$

$$y^* = -\frac{1}{4} \left(-\frac{16}{3}\right) = \frac{4}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Minimum Value:**

$$f\left(\frac{8}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right) = \left(\frac{8}{3}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{64}{9} + 2 \cdot \frac{16}{9} = \frac{64}{9} + \frac{32}{9} = \frac{96}{9} = \frac{32}{3}$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum of $f$ on the constraint is $\frac{32}{3}$, occurring at $\left(\frac{8}{3}, \frac{4}{3}\right)$ with Lagrange multiplier $\lambda = -\frac{16}{3}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Optimization Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Optimization Problem] --> classA{Objective Function Type}
    classA -->|Linear| classB[Linear Programming LP]
    classA -->|Non-Linear| classC{Non-Linear Programming NLP}
    classB --> methodA[Simplex Method]
    classC --> methodB{Analytical vs Numerical}
    methodB -->|Analytical| methodC[Classical Optimization]
    methodB -->|Numerical| methodD[Modern Optimization]
    methodC --> branch1{Number of Variables}
    methodC --> branch2{Constraints Present}
    branch1 -->|Single Variable| svA[Set df/dx = 0]
    branch1 -->|Multi Variable| mvA[Set Gradient nabla f = 0]
    svA --> svB[Test f double prime]
    mvA --> mvB[Test Hessian H Definiteness]
    branch2 -->|Equality h x = 0| lagA[Lagrange Multiplier Method]
    branch2 -->|Inequality g x le 0| ktA[Kuhn Tucker K T Conditions]
    lagA --> lagB[Form L = f + lambda h]
    ktA --> ktB[Apply K T Four Conditions]
    methodD --> numA[Gradient Descent]
    methodD --> numB[Newtons Method]
    methodD --> numC[Genetic Algorithm GA]
    methodD --> numD[Simulated Annealing SA]
```

### 4.2 Decision Logic for KKT Active vs Inactive Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode[Constraint gj of x le 0] --> condA{Is gj of x star Equal to Zero?}
    condA -->|Yes - Active| actA[Multiplier mu j is greater or equal to 0]
    condA -->|No - Inactive| inactA[Multiplier mu j must be Equal to 0]
    actA --> outA[Constraint actively bounds the optimum]
    inactA --> outB[Constraint is redundant and ignored]
    outA --> resultNode[Apply Complementary Slackness mu j dot gj = 0]
    outB --> resultNode
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Classical Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Input Objective Function f of x] --> procA[Module A Parser and Differentiator]
    inpB[Input Constraints g of x and h of x] --> procA
    procA --> procB[Module B Gradient and Hessian Builder]
    procB --> procC[Module C First Order Stationarity Solver]
    procC --> statA{Stationary Point Found?}
    statA -->|No| errA[Error Function Non Convex or Infeasible]
    statA -->|Yes| procD[Module D Definiteness Classifier]
    procD --> outMin[Output Local Minimum if Hessian PD]
    procD --> outMax[Output Local Maximum if Hessian ND]
    procD --> outSad[Output Saddle Point if Indefinite]
    procD --> outInc[Output Inconclusive if Semi Definite]
```

### 4.4 Logical Map of Sufficient Condition Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Stationary Point x star where nabla f = 0] --> rootB{Evaluate Hessian H at x star}
    rootB --> rootC{Check Leading Principal Minors}
    rootC --> rootD{All Minors Strictly Positive?}
    rootD -->|Yes| outA[Strict Local MINIMUM]
    rootC --> rootE{Alternating Signs Starting Negative?}
    rootE -->|Yes| outB[Strict Local MAXIMUM]
    rootC --> rootF[Neither PD nor ND]
    rootF --> outC[Saddle Point or Higher Order Test Required]
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define a stationary point. How is it classified using the second derivative test for a single variable function $f(x)$?**

> **Model Answer:**
> A *stationary point* $x^*$ of a differentiable function $f(x)$ is a point where the first derivative vanishes, i.e., $f'(x^*) = 0$. The second derivative test classifies the stationary point as follows:
> - If $f''(x^*) > 0$, the point is a **local minimum**.
> - If $f''(x^*) < 0$, the point is a **local maximum**.
> - If $f''(x^*) = 0$, the test is **inconclusive** and a higher-order derivative test is required.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State the Kuhn-Tucker conditions for the non-linear programming problem: Minimize $f(x)$ subject to $g_j(x) \leq 0$ for $j = 1, 2, \dots, m$.**

> **Model Answer:**
> For minimization of $f(x)$ subject to $g_j(x) \leq 0$, a feasible point $x^*$ is a K-T point if there exist multipliers $\mu_j$ such that:
> 1. **Primal Feasibility:** $g_j(x^*) \leq 0$ for all $j$.
> 2. **Dual Feasibility:** $\mu_j \geq 0$ for all $j$.
> 3. **Complementary Slackness:** $\mu_j \cdot g_j(x^*) = 0$ for all $j$.
> 4. **Stationarity:** $\nabla f(x^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(x^*) = 0$.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Carry-Over Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Find the minimum value of $f(x) = 2x^3 - 3x^2 - 12x + 5$ using classical optimization. Verify whether the stationary point is a local minimum, maximum, or saddle point.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Determine the critical point of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 - 4x_1 x_2 + 6x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 4x_2$ and classify it using the Hessian definiteness test.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Find the first derivative:**

$$f'(x) = 6x^2 - 6x - 12$$

**Step 2 — Apply the FONC, set $f'(x) = 0$:**

$$6x^2 - 6x - 12 = 0 \implies x^2 - x - 2 = 0$$

$$(x-2)(x+1) = 0 \implies x = 2 \text{ or } x = -1$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second derivative:**

$$f''(x) = 12x - 6$$

**Step 4 — Classify each stationary point:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{At } x = 2: \quad f''(2) &= 12(2) - 6 = 18 > 0 \implies \text{Local MINIMUM} \\
\text{At } x = -1: \quad f''(-1) &= 12(-1) - 6 = -18 < 0 \implies \text{Local MAXIMUM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate function values:**

$$f(2) = 2(8) - 3(4) - 12(2) + 5 = 16 - 12 - 24 + 5 = -15$$

$$f(-1) = 2(-1) - 3(1) - 12(-1) + 5 = -2 - 3 + 12 + 5 = 12$$

**Conclusion:** Local minimum is $-15$ at $x = 2$; local maximum is $12$ at $x = -1$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient (first partial derivatives):**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4x_2 - 4$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -4x_1 + 12x_2 - 4$$

**Step 2 — Apply the FONC: set gradient to zero vector:**

$$2x_1 - 4x_2 - 4 = 0 \implies x_1 - 2x_2 = 2$$

$$-4x_1 + 12x_2 - 4 = 0 \implies x_1 - 3x_2 = -1$$

**Step 3 — Solve the system of linear equations:**

Subtracting the first equation from the second:

$$(x_1 - 3x_2) - (x_1 - 2x_2) = -1 - 2 \implies -x_2 = -3 \implies x_2 = 3$$

Substituting $x_2 = 3$ back:

$$x_1 = 2x_2 + 2 = 2(3) + 2 = 8$$

The critical point is $x^* = (8, 3)$.

**Step 4 — Construct the Hessian matrix:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -4 \\ -4 & 12 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Test definiteness via leading principal minors:**

$$\Delta_1 = \vert 2 \vert = 2 > 0$$

$$\Delta_2 = (2)(12) - (-4)(-4) = 24 - 16 = 8 > 0$$

Since both leading principal minors are strictly positive, $H$ is **Positive Definite (PD)**.

**Conclusion:** The critical point $x^* = (8, 3)$ is a **strict local minimum**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Use the **Lagrange multiplier method** to find the optimum of $f(x, y) = x^2 + y^2$ subject to the constraint $2x + y = 6$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] State and explain the **complementary slackness** condition in Kuhn-Tucker theory. Why is this condition not applicable in the Lagrange multiplier method?

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Form the Lagrangian function:**

$$L(x, y, \lambda) = x^2 + y^2 + \lambda(2x + y - 6)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivatives:**

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = 2x + 2\lambda = 0 \implies x = -\lambda$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial y} = 2y + \lambda = 0 \implies y = -\frac{\lambda}{2}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda} = 2x + y - 6 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the constraint:**

$$2(-\lambda) + \left(-\frac{\lambda}{2}\right) = 6$$

$$-2\lambda - \frac{\lambda}{2} = 6 \implies -\frac{5\lambda}{2} = 6 \implies \lambda = -\frac{12}{5}$$

**Step 4 — Back-substitute to find the variables:**

$$x^* = -\lambda = \frac{12}{5} = 2.4$$

$$y^* = -\frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the optimum value:**

$$f(2.4, 1.2) = (2.4)^2 + (1.2)^2 = 5.76 + 1.44 = 7.20$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum value of $f$ is $7.20$ at $(2.4, 1.2)$ with $\lambda = -12/5$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Complementary Slackness** is one of the four Kuhn-Tucker necessary conditions for a non-linear programming problem with inequality constraints. For a minimization problem with constraints $g_j(x) \leq 0$ and multipliers $\mu_j \geq 0$, it is stated as:

$$\mu_j \cdot g_j(x^*) = 0 \quad \text{for all } j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

**Explanation:**
The condition states that for each inequality constraint, at least one of the two following statements must be true:
1. The constraint is *active* at the optimum, i.e., $g_j(x^*) = 0$ (the constraint is "tight" or "binding").
2. The corresponding multiplier is zero, i.e., $\mu_j = 0$ (the constraint is *inactive* and does not influence the optimum).

**Why is it not applicable in Lagrange's method?**
Lagrange multipliers are used exclusively for **equality constraints** of the form $h_k(x) = 0$. Equality constraints are *always active* by definition (they can never be "inactive" since the equality sign must hold at every feasible point). Therefore, the concept of a constraint switching between active and inactive states does not arise, and the complementary slackness condition (which governs this switching) is structurally absent in the pure Lagrange framework.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to classify the stationary point** in single-variable problems: a derivative zero only gives a *necessary* condition, not a *classification*. You will lose **2 marks** if you skip the $f''$ test.
> 2. **Miscounting the principal minors of the Hessian**: For a $3 \times 3$ Hessian, you must evaluate $\Delta_1$, $\Delta_2$, AND $\Delta_3$. Stopping at $\Delta_2$ is incomplete and loses marks.
> 3. **Sign confusion in K-T stationarity**: For *minimization*, the gradient equation is $\nabla f + \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j = 0$. For *maximization*, the sign FLIPS to $\nabla f - \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j = 0$. Wrong sign = full marks loss on the sub-question.
> 4. **Forgetting dual feasibility ($\mu_j \geq 0$)**: Writing the K-T conditions without the non-negativity constraint on $\mu_j$ is treated as a major conceptual gap.
> 5. **Confusing positive definite with positive semi-definite**: Semi-definiteness makes the SOSC *inconclusive*, not conclusive. Do not mark it as a minimum without higher-order verification.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Classical optimization** uses differential calculus; numerical optimization uses iterative algorithms.
- [ ] The **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)** for unconstrained problems is $\nabla f(x^*) = 0$.
- [ ] The **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)** requires the Hessian to be **PD (minimum)** or **ND (maximum)**.
- [ ] The **Hessian matrix** is symmetric, with entries $H_{ij} = \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$.
- [ ] **Definiteness Test Rule:** PD = all leading principal minors $> 0$; ND = alternating signs starting with negative.
- [ ] **Indefinite Hessian** $\implies$ **saddle point**, not an optimum.
- [ ] **Lagrange multiplier method** handles equality constraints by forming $L = f + \lambda h$ and solving $\nabla L = 0$.
- [ ] **Kuhn-Tucker (K-T) conditions** handle inequality constraints; there are **four** conditions: primal feasibility, dual feasibility, complementary slackness, stationarity.
- [ ] **Complementary slackness** is unique to inequality constraints and identifies which constraints are *active*.
- [ ] Lagrange's method is a **special case** of K-T conditions where all inequality constraints are replaced by equalities.
- [ ] At an optimum, the gradient of the objective is a **linear combination** of the gradients of the active constraints.
- [ ] **Geometric meaning:** The level curves of $f$ are **tangent** to the constraint surface at the optimum.
- [ ] The K-T conditions are **necessary** under constraint qualification (e.g., LICQ) and **sufficient** only if the Hessian of the Lagrangian restricted to the tangent space is positive definite.
- [ ] Always check the **feasibility** of the obtained candidate point before classifying it as an optimum.
- [ ] The bordered Hessian (used in constrained sufficiency tests) is a $(n+m) \times (n+m)$ determinant, not the standard Hessian.
- [ ] Engineering applications: structural weight minimization, economic dispatch, beam design, process control tuning.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Variable Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Variable Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Single Variable Optimization** is the branch of unconstrained optimization theory that deals with finding the optimal value (either minimum or maximum) of an objective function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ defined over a single real-valued variable $x$. Mathematically, it seeks to determine $x^* \in \mathbb{R}$ such that:

$$
f(x^*) \le f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{(Global Minimum)}
$$

or

$$
f(x^*) \ge f(x) \quad \forall \, x \in \mathbb{R} \quad \text{(Global Maximum)}
$$

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the function $f(x)$ is assumed to be **continuous and differentiable** of appropriate order (usually twice continuously differentiable, i.e., $f \in C^2$) over the domain of interest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The optimization problem is termed "Unconstrained" because the variable $x$ is free to take any value within $\mathbb{R}$ or a specified interval $[a, b]$. This is in contrast to "Constrained Optimization" (covered in later modules) where equality or inequality constraints limit the feasible region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Foggy Mountain Hike

Imagine you are standing somewhere on a mountain range in thick fog, with no map, but you can feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet. Your **goal** is to find the **lowest valley** (global minimum) or the **highest peak** (global maximum) in the terrain.

- **The mountain terrain** = the objective function $f(x)$
- **Your feet measuring the slope** = evaluating the first derivative $f'(x)$
- **The change in steepness** = the second derivative $f''(x)$ (curvature)
- **Flat ground (slope = 0)** = a stationary point where you might pause — either at a peak, a valley, or a *saddle point* (in 1D this is the inflection point, $f'(x^*) = f''(x^*) = 0$ but not an extremum)

To decide whether the flat ground is a peak, valley, or just a hilltop plateau, you must measure the **curvature** — exactly analogous to applying the **second derivative test** in calculus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** A *necessary* condition identifies *candidates* for optima (the slope must be zero), while a *sufficient* condition *confirms* whether a candidate is genuinely optimal (by examining curvature).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following quantities are fundamental to the analysis of single variable optimization problems:

- **Critical Point ($x_c$):** Any point where $f'(x_c) = 0$
- **Stationary Point:** Synonymous with critical point
- **Inflection Point:** A point where $f''(x) = 0$ and the concavity changes sign
- **Saddle Point (in 1D):** A critical point that is neither a maximum nor a minimum (rare in single variable; more common in multivariable)
- **Tolerance $\varepsilon$:** A small positive threshold (typically $\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-3}$) used as a stopping criterion in numerical algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Hierarchy of Optimality Conditions

The theory of single variable optimization rests upon three hierarchical tiers of conditions. We must understand the **logical flow** between them:

### Tier 1: Necessary Conditions (First-Order)
A point $x^*$ is a **local extremum** of $f(x)$ only if the first derivative vanishes at $x^*$ (provided $f$ is differentiable):

$$
f'(x^*) = 0
$$

### Tier 2: Sufficient Conditions (Second-Order)
If $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) \neq 0$, then $x^*$ is a genuine local extremum:

- **Local Minimum** if $f''(x^*) > 0$ (curve is convex / concave-up at $x^*$)
- **Local Maximum** if $f''(x^*) < 0$ (curve is concave / concave-down at $x^*$)

### Tier 3: Higher-Order Derivative Test (Second-Order Failure)
When $f'(x^*) = 0$ **and** $f''(x^*) = 0$, the second-order test is inconclusive. We proceed to the **first non-zero higher-order derivative**:

- Let $n$ be the smallest positive integer such that $f^{(n)}(x^*) \neq 0$
- If $n$ is **even**: $x^*$ is a local minimum when $f^{(n)}(x^*) > 0$; a local maximum when $f^{(n)}(x^*) < 0$
- If $n$ is **odd**: $x^*$ is **neither** a minimum nor a maximum (it is an inflection point)

## 2.2 Global Optimality — The Boundary & Convexity Argument

For a function defined on a **closed interval $[a, b]$** (Weierstrass Theorem guarantees that a continuous function attains its global extrema on such an interval), the global extremum must occur at either:

1. A **stationary point** inside $(a, b)$ where $f'(x) = 0$, OR
2. An **endpoint** $x = a$ or $x = b$

For **unbounded domains**, we require an additional check on the function's behavior as $x \to \pm \infty$.

If $f(x)$ is **convex** on the entire domain (i.e., $f''(x) \ge 0$ everywhere), then **any local minimum is automatically the global minimum**. This is the most powerful tool in KTU-level problems.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Condition

- **Why must $f'(x^*) = 0$?** Because at a true local extremum, the tangent must be horizontal. If $f'(x^*) > 0$, then moving left would decrease the function value (contradicting minimality). If $f'(x^*) < 0$, then moving right would decrease the function value.

- **Why does $f''(x^*) > 0$ imply a minimum?** Because the Taylor expansion around $x^*$ is $f(x^* + h) \approx f(x^*) + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*) h^2$. When $f''(x^*) > 0$, this quadratic in $h$ is non-negative for all small $h$, meaning $f(x)$ near $x^*$ is bounded below by $f(x^*)$.

- **Why does an odd $n$ imply no extremum?** Because the leading non-zero term in the Taylor expansion is an odd power of $h$, which changes sign as $h$ crosses zero — so $x^*$ has higher function values on both sides.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Condition** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Interpretation / When to Use** |
|---|---|---|
| First-Order Necessary Condition | $f'(x^*) = 0$ | Identifies candidate stationary points |
| Second-Order Sufficient (Min) | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$ | Confirms local minimum |
| Second-Order Sufficient (Max) | $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) < 0$ | Confirms local maximum |
| Higher-Order Test (Min) | First non-zero derivative is even-order, positive | Used when $f''(x^*) = 0$ |
| Higher-Order Test (Max) | First non-zero derivative is even-order, negative | Used when $f''(x^*) = 0$ |
| Higher-Order Test (No extremum) | First non-zero derivative is odd-order | $x^*$ is an inflection point |
| Taylor Expansion (local) | $f(x^*+h) \approx f(x^*) + f'(x^*)h + \frac{1}{2}f''(x^*)h^2$ | Foundation of all derivative tests |
| Convexity Condition | $f''(x) \ge 0 \quad \forall \, x \in \text{domain}$ | Local min = Global min |
| Closed-Interval Global Check | Compare $f(x^*_{\text{cand}})$ with $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ | Weierstrass Theorem on $[a,b]$ |
| Newton's Method Update | $x_{k+1} = x_k - \dfrac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$ | Iterative solver for $f'(x)=0$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Single variable optimization is the foundational building block for virtually every engineering design problem. Real-world applications include:

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Minimizing the weight of a structural beam subject to a fixed length
- **Civil Engineering:** Optimizing the cross-section of a column for maximum load-bearing capacity
- **Electrical Engineering:** Tuning a single circuit parameter (e.g., resistance) for maximum power transfer (Thevenin's theorem)
- **Operations Research:** Profit maximization as a function of a single production quantity in introductory economic models
- **Signal Processing:** Minimizing the mean-squared error with respect to a scalar filter coefficient

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Second-Order Sufficient Condition from Taylor's Theorem

Let $x^*$ be a point such that $f'(x^*) = 0$. We wish to determine whether $x^*$ is a local minimum. We examine the function value at a nearby point $x = x^* + h$ where $h$ is small but non-zero.

By Taylor's Theorem with remainder (for $f \in C^2$):

$$
f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + f'(x^*) \cdot h + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^* + \xi) \cdot h^2
$$

where $\xi$ lies between $0$ and $h$ (i.e., $\xi \in (0, h)$).

**Step 1 — Apply the necessary condition:** Since $f'(x^*) = 0$ by assumption, the first-order term vanishes:

$$
f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + 0 \cdot h + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^* + \xi) \cdot h^2
$$

$$
f(x^* + h) = f(x^*) + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^* + \xi) \cdot h^2
$$

**Step 2 — Analyze the sign of the remainder:** Since $f''$ is continuous and $h$ is small, $f''(x^* + \xi)$ maintains the same sign as $f''(x^*)$ for sufficiently small $h$.

$$
f(x^* + h) - f(x^*) = \frac{1}{2} f''(x^* + \xi) \cdot h^2
$$

**Step 3 — Determine extremum type:** Note that $h^2 > 0$ for any $h \neq 0$. Therefore:

- If $f''(x^*) > 0$, then $f''(x^* + \xi) > 0$ for small $h$, which gives $f(x^* + h) > f(x^*)$. Hence $x^*$ is a **local minimum**.
- If $f''(x^*) < 0$, then $f''(x^* + \xi) < 0$ for small $h$, which gives $f(x^* + h) < f(x^*)$. Hence $x^*$ is a **local maximum**.

This completes the rigorous derivation of the second-order sufficient condition. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example

**Problem:** Find all local extrema and the global minimum (if any) of the function:

$$
f(x) = x^3 - 3x^2 - 9x + 5
$$

### Step 1: Compute the First Derivative

Differentiate term-by-term using the power rule:

$$
f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(x^3\right) - \frac{d}{dx}\left(3x^2\right) - \frac{d}{dx}\left(9x\right) + \frac{d}{dx}\left(5\right)
$$

$$
f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x - 9
$$

### Step 2: Apply the First-Order Necessary Condition

Set $f'(x) = 0$:

$$
3x^2 - 6x - 9 = 0
$$

Divide both sides by **3** to simplify:

$$
x^2 - 2x - 3 = 0
$$

Factor the quadratic (find two numbers whose product is $-3$ and sum is $-2$: these are $-3$ and $+1$):

$$
(x - 3)(x + 1) = 0
$$

Therefore the critical points are:

$$
x_1^* = -1, \quad x_2^* = 3
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Second Derivative

Differentiate $f'(x) = 3x^2 - 6x - 9$:

$$
f''(x) = 6x - 6
$$

### Step 4: Apply the Second-Order Sufficient Condition

**Evaluate at $x_1^* = -1$:**

$$
f''(-1) = 6(-1) - 6 = -6 - 6 = -12 < 0
$$

Since $f''(-1) < 0$, $x_1^* = -1$ is a **local maximum**.

**Evaluate at $x_2^* = 3$:**

$$
f''(3) = 6(3) - 6 = 18 - 6 = 12 > 0
$$

Since $f''(3) > 0$, $x_2^* = 3$ is a **local minimum**.

### Step 5: Compute the Function Values

$$
f(-1) = (-1)^3 - 3(-1)^2 - 9(-1) + 5 = -1 - 3 + 9 + 5 = 10
$$

$$
f(3) = (3)^3 - 3(3)^2 - 9(3) + 5 = 27 - 27 - 27 + 5 = -22
$$

### Step 6: Determine the Global Extrema on $\mathbb{R}$

Since $f(x)$ is a cubic polynomial with positive leading coefficient, its end behavior is:

$$
\lim_{x \to +\infty} f(x) = +\infty, \quad \lim_{x \to -\infty} f(x) = -\infty
$$

- **No global maximum exists** because $f(x) \to +\infty$ as $x \to +\infty$.
- **No global minimum exists** because $f(x) \to -\infty$ as $x \to -\infty$.

The function has only **local** extrema: a local maximum of $\mathbf{10}$ at $x = -1$ and a local minimum of $\mathbf{-22}$ at $x = 3$.

### Final Analytical Matrix Summary

| **Candidate Point** | **$f'(x)$** | **$f''(x)$** | **Classification** | **$f(x)$ Value** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x = -1$ | $0$ | $-12$ | Local Maximum | $10$ |
| $x = 3$ | $0$ | $12$ | Local Minimum | $-22$ |

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example: Higher-Order Test

**Problem:** Classify the critical point of $f(x) = x^4 - 4x^3 + 12x^2 - 16x + 8$ at $x = 2$.

### Step 1: Compute the First Derivative

$$
f'(x) = 4x^3 - 12x^2 + 24x - 16
$$

### Step 2: Evaluate at $x = 2$

$$
f'(2) = 4(8) - 12(4) + 24(2) - 16 = 32 - 48 + 48 - 16 = 16
$$

Since $f'(2) = 16 \neq 0$, $x = 2$ is **not a critical point** of this function. (We will instead test $x = 2$ against another function below.)

Let us instead test $g(x) = (x-1)^4$ at $x = 1$.

### Step 1 (Revised): Critical Point

$$
g'(x) = 4(x-1)^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g'(1) = 0
$$

### Step 2 (Revised): Second Derivative

$$
g''(x) = 12(x-1)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g''(1) = 0
$$

The second-order test fails. We proceed to higher-order derivatives.

### Step 3 (Revised): Third Derivative

$$
g'''(x) = 24(x-1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad g'''(1) = 0
$$

Still zero. We proceed to the fourth derivative.

### Step 4 (Revised): Fourth Derivative

$$
g^{(4)}(x) = 24 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g^{(4)}(1) = 24 \neq 0
$$

### Step 5 (Revised): Apply the Higher-Order Test

The first non-zero derivative is the **fourth-order** derivative ($n = 4$, which is **even**), and its value is **positive** ($+24$). Therefore $x = 1$ is a **local minimum**.

Verification: $g(1) = 0$, and $g(x) = (x-1)^4 \ge 0$ for all $x$, so indeed $x = 1$ is both the local and global minimum. $\checkmark$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Decision Flow for Single Variable Optimization

The following flowchart illustrates the complete decision-making procedure a KTU student should follow when solving a single variable optimization problem. This procedural mapping aligns directly with the analytical steps demonstrated in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Receive Objective Function f of x"] --> B["Compute First Derivative f prime of x"]
    B --> C["Set f prime x equals 0 and Solve"]
    C --> D{"Are Critical Points Found?"}
    D -- "NO" --> E["Check End Behavior as x tends to Infinity"]
    E --> E1["If f tends to + Infinity: No Min exists"]
    E --> E2["If f tends to - Infinity: No Max exists"]
    E1 --> Z["END: Report Findings"]
    E2 --> Z
    D -- "YES" --> F["Compute Second Derivative f double prime of x"]
    F --> G["Evaluate f double prime at each Critical Point"]
    G --> H{"Is f double prime at x star GREATER THAN 0?"}
    H -- "YES" --> I["CLASSIFY: Local MINIMUM at x star"]
    H -- "NO" --> J{"Is f double prime at x star LESS THAN 0?"}
    J -- "YES" --> K["CLASSIFY: Local MAXIMUM at x star"]
    J -- "NO" --> L["Second Order Test FAILS"]
    L --> M["Compute Higher Order Derivatives"]
    M --> N["Find Smallest n with f to the n non zero"]
    N --> O{"Is n EVEN?"}
    O -- "YES" --> P{"Is f to the n at x star POSITIVE?"}
    P -- "YES" --> I
    P -- "NO" --> K
    O -- "NO" --> Q["CLASSIFY: Inflection Point, NOT an Extremum"]
    I --> R["Check for GLOBAL Optimum"]
    K --> R
    Q --> Z
    R --> S{"Is f convex on Entire Domain?"}
    S -- "YES" --> T["Local Min equals Global Min"]
    S -- "NO" --> U["Compare f at All Critical Points AND Endpoints a and b"]
    U --> V["Select Largest or Smallest Value"]
    T --> Z
    V --> Z
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Optimality Conditions Hierarchy

The diagram below maps the logical relationships between the three tiers of optimality conditions described in Section 2.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TIER1["TIER 1: NECESSARY CONDITION"]
        A1["f prime at x star EQUALS 0"]
        A2["Identifies CANDIDATE Points Only"]
    end
    subgraph TIER2["TIER 2: SUFFICIENT CONDITION"]
        B1["f double prime at x star POSITIVE"]
        B2["CONFIRMS Local Minimum"]
        C1["f double prime at x star NEGATIVE"]
        C2["CONFIRMS Local Maximum"]
    end
    subgraph TIER3["TIER 3: HIGHER ORDER TEST"]
        D1["All Derivatives Up to n minus 1 are ZERO"]
        D2["First Non Zero Derivative is EVEN Order"]
        D3["First Non Zero Derivative is ODD Order"]
        D4["CONFIRMS Extremum if EVEN"]
        D5["REJECTS Extremum if ODD"]
    end
    TIER1 --> TIER2
    TIER1 --> TIER3
    TIER2 --> E1["OUTPUT: Verified Optimal Solution"]
    TIER3 --> E1
```

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic: Behavior Around a Critical Point

The schematic below visualizes how the Taylor expansion governs the local geometry around a stationary point. Each branch corresponds to one of the three possible classifications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["CRITICAL POINT: f prime equals 0"] --> P1{"Inspect f double prime"}
    P1 -- "f double prime GREATER THAN 0" --> P2["CONVEX SHAPE LIKE A CUP"]
    P2 --> P3["Neighborhood: f star less than f star plus h"]
    P3 --> P4["VERDICT: LOCAL MINIMUM"]
    P1 -- "f double prime LESS THAN 0" --> P5["CONCAVE SHAPE LIKE A CAP"]
    P5 --> P6["Neighborhood: f star greater than f star plus h"]
    P6 --> P7["VERDICT: LOCAL MAXIMUM"]
    P1 -- "f double prime EQUALS 0" --> P8["TEST FAILS AT ORDER 2"]
    P8 --> P9["Proceed to HIGHER ORDER Derivatives"]
    P9 --> P10{"Order of First Non Zero Derivative"}
    P10 -- "EVEN ORDER" --> P11["VERDICT: Local Extremum"]
    P10 -- "ODD ORDER" --> P12["VERDICT: NOT AN EXTREMUM"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Question 1
**State and explain the first-order necessary condition for a point $x^*$ to be a local extremum of a differentiable function $f(x)$. Why is this condition "necessary but not sufficient"?**

**Model Answer:**

The **first-order necessary condition** states that if $f(x)$ is differentiable at a local extremum point $x^*$, then:

$$
f'(x^*) = 0
$$

This means the tangent line at $x^*$ must be horizontal (parallel to the x-axis). The reasoning is as follows: if $f'(x^*) > 0$, then $f$ is increasing at $x^*$, so points slightly to the left would have smaller function values — contradicting that $x^*$ is a minimum. Similarly, $f'(x^*) < 0$ contradicts $x^*$ being a maximum.

**Why "necessary but not sufficient":** The condition $f'(x^*) = 0$ is satisfied at any **stationary point**, but stationary points include local minima, local maxima, and **inflection points** (where the function merely levels off momentarily without being an extremum). For instance, $f(x) = x^3$ has $f'(0) = 0$, but $x = 0$ is not an extremum. Hence, additional conditions (second-derivative test) must be applied to confirm optimality.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

### Question 2
**What is the higher-order derivative test for extrema? Under what circumstances is it invoked in place of the second-derivative test?**

**Model Answer:**

The **higher-order derivative test** is invoked when both $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) = 0$, causing the second-derivative test to be inconclusive.

**Procedure:** Let $n$ be the smallest positive integer such that $f^{(n)}(x^*) \neq 0$.

- If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x^*) > 0$: $x^*$ is a **local minimum**
- If $n$ is **even** and $f^{(n)}(x^*) < 0$: $x^*$ is a **local maximum**
- If $n$ is **odd**: $x^*$ is **neither** a local minimum nor a local maximum; it is an **inflection point**

This test is necessary because certain functions (e.g., $f(x) = x^4$, $f(x) = -x^6$) have extrema but possess zero second derivatives at those points.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** Find the local extrema of the function $f(x) = 2x^3 - 9x^2 + 12x + 5$ using the first and second derivative tests. Clearly state the optimality conditions used at each step.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Determine whether each extremum found in part (a) is a **global extremum** on the interval $[0, 4]$ and identify the absolute maximum and minimum values of $f(x)$ on this interval.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute $f'(x)$:**

$$
f'(x) = 6x^2 - 18x + 12
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the first-order necessary condition $f'(x) = 0$:**

$$
6x^2 - 18x + 12 = 0
$$

Divide by 6:

$$
x^2 - 3x + 2 = 0
$$

Factor:

$$
(x - 1)(x - 2) = 0
$$

Critical points: $x_1^* = 1$ and $x_2^* = 2$.

**Step 3 — Compute $f''(x)$:**

$$
f''(x) = 12x - 18
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the second-order sufficient condition:**

At $x_1^* = 1$:

$$
f''(1) = 12(1) - 18 = -6 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1^* = 1 \text{ is a LOCAL MAXIMUM}
$$

At $x_2^* = 2$:

$$
f''(2) = 12(2) - 18 = 24 - 18 = 6 > 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2^* = 2 \text{ is a LOCAL MINIMUM}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute function values:**

$$
f(1) = 2(1) - 9(1) + 12(1) + 5 = 2 - 9 + 12 + 5 = 10
$$

$$
f(2) = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) + 5 = 16 - 36 + 24 + 5 = 9
$$

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Evaluate $f$ at the boundary points of $[0, 4]$:**

$$
f(0) = 2(0) - 9(0) + 12(0) + 5 = 5
$$

$$
f(4) = 2(64) - 9(16) + 12(4) + 5 = 128 - 144 + 48 + 5 = 37
$$

**Step 2 — Collect all candidate values:**

| **Candidate** | **Type** | **$f(x)$ Value** |
|---|---|---|
| $x = 0$ (boundary) | Endpoint | $5$ |
| $x = 1$ | Local Maximum | $10$ |
| $x = 2$ | Local Minimum | $9$ |
| $x = 4$ (boundary) | Endpoint | $37$ |

**Step 3 — Identify global extrema:**

- **Global Maximum on $[0, 4]$:** $f(4) = 37$ at $x = 4$
- **Global Minimum on $[0, 4]$:** $f(0) = 5$ at $x = 0$

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** **[7 Marks]** A closed cylindrical can is to hold $500 \, \text{cm}^3$ of liquid. Find the dimensions (radius $r$ and height $h$) that **minimize the surface area** of the can. Use single variable optimization.

**(b)** **[7 Marks]** Verify that the solution obtained in part (a) yields a **global minimum** by checking the second derivative and analyzing the behavior as $r \to 0^+$ and $r \to \infty$.

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1 — Set up the problem:**

Volume constraint (fixed): $V = \pi r^2 h = 500 \Rightarrow h = \dfrac{500}{\pi r^2}$

Surface area of closed cylinder:

$$
S(r) = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r \cdot \frac{500}{\pi r^2} = 2\pi r^2 + \frac{1000}{r}
$$

**Step 2 — First-order necessary condition:**

$$
S'(r) = 4\pi r - \frac{1000}{r^2}
$$

Set $S'(r) = 0$:

$$
4\pi r - \frac{1000}{r^2} = 0
$$

$$
4\pi r = \frac{1000}{r^2}
$$

$$
4\pi r^3 = 1000
$$

$$
r^3 = \frac{250}{\pi}
$$

$$
r^* = \left(\frac{250}{\pi}\right)^{1/3} \approx 4.301 \, \text{cm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $h$:**

$$
h^* = \frac{500}{\pi (r^*)^2} = \frac{500}{\pi \left(\frac{250}{\pi}\right)^{2/3}} = \frac{500}{\pi^{1/3} \cdot 250^{2/3}}
$$

Numerically: $h^* \approx \dfrac{500}{\pi \cdot (4.301)^2} \approx 8.603 \, \text{cm} = 2 r^*$

> [!NOTE]
> **Elegant Result:** The optimal cylinder has height equal to its diameter ($h = 2r$), which is the classical result often seen in physics and engineering design.

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1 — Second derivative test:**

$$
S''(r) = 4\pi + \frac{2000}{r^3}
$$

Since $\pi > 0$ and $r^3 > 0$ for all feasible $r$, we have:

$$
S''(r) = 4\pi + \frac{2000}{r^3} > 0 \quad \forall \, r > 0
$$

Because $S''(r^*) > 0$, $r^*$ is a **local minimum**.

**Step 2 — Global optimality check:**

- As $r \to 0^+$: $\dfrac{1000}{r} \to +\infty$, so $S(r) \to +\infty$
- As $r \to \infty$: $2\pi r^2 \to +\infty$, so $S(r) \to +\infty$

Since $S(r) \to +\infty$ at both extremes and $S$ is continuous, the unique local minimum must be the **global minimum** on $(0, \infty)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary check:** When asked for global extrema on $[a, b]$, many students only check critical points and ignore $f(a)$ and $f(b)$. This typically costs **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the justification of sufficiency:** Writing $f'(x) = 0$ is not enough. KTU examiners expect you to **explicitly state** that $f'(x^*) = 0$ is a *necessary* condition and then **invoke** the second-derivative test to *confirm* optimality. Marks are awarded separately for each step.
> 3. **Sign error in second derivative:** A frequent algebraic slip leads to misclassification (e.g., calling a maximum a minimum). Always re-verify the sign.
> 4. **Ignoring domain restrictions:** In problems like the cylindrical can, $r > 0$ must be explicitly stated; evaluating $f$ at $r = 0$ or $r < 0$ is invalid.
> 5. **Higher-order test misapplication:** Students often forget to verify that **all** lower-order derivatives are zero before identifying the first non-zero derivative.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Single variable optimization** seeks extrema of $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ for unconstrained or interval-constrained problems.
- The **first-order necessary condition** $f'(x^*) = 0$ identifies *candidates*; it is **not sufficient** on its own.
- The **second-order sufficient condition** requires $f''(x^*) > 0$ (minimum) or $f''(x^*) < 0$ (maximum).
- The **higher-order derivative test** handles the case $f'(x^*) = f''(x^*) = 0$ — the first non-zero derivative of **even order** indicates an extremum.
- On a **closed interval** $[a, b]$, the global extremum must be among $\{$critical points in $(a,b)\} \cup \{a, b\}$ (Weierstrass Theorem).
- A **convex function** ($f''(x) \ge 0$ everywhere) guarantees that any local minimum is the **global minimum**.
- The **Taylor expansion** $f(x^* + h) \approx f(x^*) + \frac{1}{2} f''(x^*) h^2$ is the mathematical foundation of all derivative-based optimality tests.
- For **engineering design problems**, always set up the constraint first, express the objective function in terms of a single variable, then optimize.
- The cylindrical can problem is a **classic KTU-favorite**: the optimal cylinder has $h = 2r$.
- Always **state conditions explicitly** in exam answers — KTU examiners allocate marks for logical structure, not just numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multivariable Optimization Without Constraints
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multivariable Optimization Without Constraints

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Multivariable Optimization Without Constraints** (also termed *unconstrained optimization in multiple dimensions*) is the mathematical discipline of finding the local minimum, maximum, or saddle point of a scalar objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$ where the decision vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is free to take any real value in an $n$-dimensional Euclidean space, subject to **no equality or inequality restrictions**.

Formally, the problem is stated as:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(\mathbf{x})$$

or equivalently $\max_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n} f(\mathbf{x})$, where $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is a sufficiently smooth (at least twice continuously differentiable, i.e., $C^2$) scalar-valued function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The keyword "Without Constraints" distinguishes this module from constrained optimization (Lagrange Multipliers, KKT conditions) covered later. Here, $\mathbf{x}$ is allowed to roam freely across the entire $n$-dimensional space $\mathbb{R}^n$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Climber

Imagine a hiker lost in a vast mountain range at night. The hiker wants to reach the **lowest valley** (minima) or the **highest peak** (maxima) of the terrain.

- The **terrain elevation** at any coordinate $(x_1, x_2)$ represents the function value $f(x_1, x_2)$.
- The **hiker can walk in any direction** — north, south, east, west — because there are no fences or walls. This represents the *unconstrained* nature.
- To descend, the hiker checks the **steepest downhill slope** at the current point. This steepest slope is precisely the **negative gradient** $-\nabla f$.
- The hiker stops at points where the terrain is locally flat in all directions. These flat spots correspond to **stationary points** where $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a mountain may have several valleys, hills, and mountain passes (saddle points), a multivariable function can have multiple critical points — each must be classified as minimum, maximum, or saddle using the **Hessian matrix**.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Engineering

| Engineering Domain | Application Example |
|---|---|
| Mechanical Design | Minimizing weight of a structural truss subject to no extra constraints beyond geometry |
| Electrical Engineering | Optimal filter coefficients in signal processing |
| Chemical Engineering | Maximizing reaction yield in parameter tuning |
| Machine Learning | Finding optimal weights in regression/neural networks (loss landscape) |
| Aerospace | Trajectory design without imposed corridor restrictions |

The gradient and Hessian — the central mathematical objects of this topic — govern nearly every modern numerical optimization algorithm, from **Newton's Method** to **Quasi-Newton (BFGS)** and **Conjugate Gradient** schemes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gradient Vector — Direction of Steepest Change

For a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ with $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the **gradient** is the column vector of all first-order partial derivatives:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} \\[4pt] \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \end{bmatrix}$$

**Key Properties:**
- **Geometric Meaning:** $\nabla f$ points in the direction of *maximum rate of increase* of $f$.
- **Perpendicularity:** At any point, $\nabla f$ is perpendicular (normal) to the level curve/surface $f(\mathbf{x}) = c$.
- **Magnitude:** $\Vert \nabla f \Vert =$ steepest slope at that point.
- **Zero Gradient:** A flat spot in the terrain — a *critical* or *stationary* point.

## 2.2 The Hessian Matrix — Curvature Information

The **Hessian matrix** $H(\mathbf{x})$ is the symmetric square matrix of all second-order partial derivatives:

$$H(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[4pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[4pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

By **Clairaut's theorem** (equality of mixed partials), the Hessian is **symmetric**: $H_{ij} = H_{ji}$ whenever $f \in C^2$.

## 2.3 The Three KTU Theorems (Critical Point Classification)

Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be a point where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.

### 2.3.1 First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If** $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local extremum** (min or max) of $f$ and $f$ is differentiable at $\mathbf{x}^*$, **then** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
>
> Conversely, $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ does **not** guarantee an extremum — the point could be a saddle.

### 2.3.2 Second-Order Necessary Condition (SONC)

> If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local minimum**, then $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and the Hessian $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must be **positive semi-definite**:
> $$\mathbf{d}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} \geq 0 \quad \forall \, \mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^n$$
> If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local maximum**, $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must be **negative semi-definite**.

### 2.3.3 Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)

> If $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ and $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite**, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum**.
> If $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **negative definite**, then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local maximum**.
> If $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **indefinite**, $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **saddle point**.

## 2.4 Sylvester's Criterion (Definiteness Test via Leading Principal Minors)

For an $n \times n$ symmetric matrix $H$, let $D_k$ denote the determinant of the $k$-th leading principal submatrix.

- $H$ is **positive definite** $\iff$ $D_k > 0$ for all $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
- $H$ is **negative definite** $\iff$ $D_k = (-1)^k (\text{positive})$ for all $k$.
- Otherwise, check **eigenvalues** or **principal minors** for indefinite/semi-definite cases.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Verdict on Critical Point** |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient (FONC) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Necessary for extremum |
| Hessian | $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$ | Symmetric, $n \times n$ |
| SONC for min | $\mathbf{d}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} \geq 0$ | $H$ positive semi-definite |
| SONC for max | $\mathbf{d}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} \leq 0$ | $H$ negative semi-definite |
| SOSC for strict min | $H$ positive definite ($D_k > 0 \, \forall k$) | Strict local minimum |
| SOSC for strict max | $H$ negative definite ($D_k$ alternates: $-, +, -, \dots$) | Strict local maximum |
| Taylor expansion (2nd order) | $f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f^T \Delta \mathbf{x} + \dfrac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^T H \Delta \mathbf{x}$ | Used for quadratic models |
| Eigenvalue test | $\lambda_i(H) > 0 \, \forall i$ | Positive definite |
| Eigenvalue test | $\lambda_i(H) < 0 \, \forall i$ | Negative definite |
| Indefinite test | $\lambda_i$ has mixed signs | Saddle point |
| Gradient as direction | $\max_{\Vert \mathbf{d} \Vert = 1} \mathbf{d}^T \nabla f = \Vert \nabla f \Vert$ | Steepest ascent direction |
| Directional derivative | $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{u}$ | Rate of change along $\mathbf{u}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Convention:** Gradients have units of [function units] / [decision variable units], while Hessian entries have units of [function units] / [decision variable units]$^2$.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

In **Newton's Method**, the iterative update rule uses both gradient and Hessian:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H^{-1}(\mathbf{x}_k) \, \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

This single equation is the backbone of every modern optimization solver (e.g., IPOPT, KNITRO, MATLAB's `fminunc`). Understanding the role of $\nabla f$ (slope) and $H$ (curvature) is therefore not merely academic — it is foundational to numerical engineering practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why $\nabla f$ Points to Steepest Ascent

**Claim:** Among all unit vectors $\mathbf{u}$ (i.e., $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$), the directional derivative $D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{u}$ is maximized when $\mathbf{u} = \dfrac{\nabla f}{\Vert \nabla f \Vert}$.

**Proof by Cauchy-Schwarz Inequality:**

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f = \nabla f^T \mathbf{u} = \Vert \nabla f \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \cdot \cos\theta$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between $\nabla f$ and $\mathbf{u}$. Since $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$ and $-1 \leq \cos\theta \leq 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\mathbf{u}} f &\leq \Vert \nabla f \Vert \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \\
&= \Vert \nabla f \Vert
\end{aligned}$$

Equality occurs when $\cos\theta = 1$, i.e., $\theta = 0$, meaning $\mathbf{u}$ is parallel to $\nabla f$. Hence, the maximum rate of increase is $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$, achieved in the direction of $\nabla f$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Taylor Series Expansion for Multivariable Functions

For a $C^2$ function $f(\mathbf{x})$ near $\mathbf{x}^*$, with $\Delta \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^*$:

$$\begin{aligned}
f(\mathbf{x}) &= f(\mathbf{x}^* + \Delta \mathbf{x}) \\
&= f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \Delta \mathbf{x} + \tfrac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \Delta \mathbf{x} + \mathcal{O}(\Vert \Delta \mathbf{x} \Vert^3)
\end{aligned}$$

This is derived by applying the single-variable Taylor series along each coordinate and collecting terms up to second order. At a stationary point $\mathbf{x}^*$ where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$, the linear term vanishes, leaving:

$$f(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \Delta \mathbf{x}$$

This quadratic form is the **theoretical heart of all second-order tests** — its sign for arbitrary $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ depends on the definiteness of $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$.

## 3.3 Detailed Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

**Problem:** Find and classify all critical points of:
$$f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^3 - 3x_1 x_2^2 + x_2^2$$

### Step 1: Compute the Gradient

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -6x_1 x_2 + 2x_2$$

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 \\ -6x_1 x_2 + 2x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 2: Apply FONC — Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$

Equation (i): $3x_1^2 - 3x_2^2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1^2 = x_2^2 \Rightarrow x_1 = \pm x_2$.

Equation (ii): $-6x_1 x_2 + 2x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow 2x_2(1 - 3x_1) = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$ or $x_1 = \tfrac{1}{3}$.

**Case A:** $x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$ (since $x_1 = \pm x_2 = 0$). Critical point: $\mathbf{x}^*_A = (0, 0)$.

**Case B:** $x_1 = \tfrac{1}{3}$ and $x_1 = \pm x_2 \Rightarrow x_2 = \pm \tfrac{1}{3}$. Critical points: $\mathbf{x}^*_B = (\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3})$ and $\mathbf{x}^*_C = (\tfrac{1}{3}, -\tfrac{1}{3})$.

### Step 3: Compute the Hessian

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} &= 6x_1 \\
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} &= -6x_1 + 2 \\
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} &= -6x_2 \\
\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} &= -6x_2
\end{aligned}$$

$$H(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} 6x_1 & -6x_2 \\ -6x_2 & -6x_1 + 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Classify Each Critical Point via SOSC

**Point A: $\mathbf{x}^*_A = (0,0)$**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

Leading principal minors: $D_1 = 0$, $D_2 = 0 \cdot 2 - 0 \cdot 0 = 0$. Sylvester's criterion fails (no strict sign). Eigenvalues: $\lambda_1 = 0, \lambda_2 = 2$. **Indefinite test inconclusive via minors** — but the function $f$ along $x_2$ axis behaves like $x_2^2$ (convex) while along $x_1$ axis it behaves like $x_1^3$ (changes sign). Hence **Saddle point**.

**Point B: $\mathbf{x}^*_B = (\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3})$**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = 2 > 0$, $D_2 = (2)(0) - (-2)(-2) = -4 < 0$. Indefinite → **Saddle point**.

**Point C: $\mathbf{x}^*_C = (\tfrac{1}{3}, -\tfrac{1}{3})$**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = 2 > 0$, $D_2 = (2)(0) - (2)(2) = -4 < 0$. Indefinite → **Saddle point**.

### Final Answer

All three critical points — $(0,0)$, $(\tfrac{1}{3}, \tfrac{1}{3})$, $(\tfrac{1}{3}, -\tfrac{1}{3})$ — are **saddle points**. The function has no local extrema.

## 3.4 Second Worked Example — Identifying a Minimum

**Problem:** Classify the critical point of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$.

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 \\ 2x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}^* = (0,0)$$

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = 2 > 0$, $D_2 = 4 > 0$. Both leading principal minors positive → **Positive definite** → $\mathbf{x}^* = (0,0)$ is a **strict local (and global) minimum**. $f(0,0) = 0$.

This is the canonical **convex quadratic** form whose graph is a paraboloid bowl — the simplest visualization of a multivariable minimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithmic Flowchart — Unconstrained Optimization Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define f of x] --> B[Compute gradient del f]
    B --> C[Compute Hessian H of x]
    C --> D[Set del f equal to zero]
    D --> E{Solutions found?}
    E -- No --> F[No critical points]
    E -- Yes --> G[List all x star candidates]
    G --> H[Evaluate H at each x star]
    H --> I{Positive Definite?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Strict Local Minimum]
    I -- No --> K{Negative Definite?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Strict Local Maximum]
    K -- No --> M{Indefinite?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Saddle Point]
    M -- No --> O[Semi definite: Inconclusive]
    O --> P[Higher order test needed]
```

## 4.2 Critical Point Classification Decision Tree (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LEVEL1 [LEVEL 1 FIRST ORDER]
        A1[Critical Point x star] --> A2[Gradient is zero vector]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL2 [LEVEL 2 SECOND ORDER]
        B1[Evaluate Hessian H at x star]
        B2[Apply Sylvester Criterion]
        B3[Determine Definiteness]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL3 [LEVEL 3 VERDICT]
        C1[Positive Definite: MIN]
        C2[Negative Definite: MAX]
        C3[Indefinite: SADDLE]
        C4[Semi Definite: INCONCLUSIVE]
    end
    LEVEL1 --> LEVEL2
    LEVEL2 --> LEVEL3
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> LEVEL3
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Gradient & Hessian Roles in Newton's Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [INPUT SPACE]
        XK[Current Iterate x sub k]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE [COMPUTE LOCAL MODEL]
        G[Gradient del f at x sub k]
        H[Hessian H at x sub k]
    end
    subgraph SOLVE [LINEAR SYSTEM]
        LS[Solve H times d equal negative del f]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT [OUTPUT]
        DK[Search Direction d sub k]
        XKP[Next Iterate x sub k plus 1]
    end
    XK --> G
    XK --> H
    G --> LS
    H --> LS
    LS --> DK
    DK --> XKP
    XKP --> COMPUTE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In Newton's Method, the Hessian $H$ acts as the **curvature-scaled metric** that warps space so that the gradient $\nabla f$ reliably points to the optimum. The Hessian's role is essential — gradient descent alone converges slowly near saddle points, whereas Newton's quadratic convergence is enabled by the Hessian.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the Hessian matrix of a multivariable function. State Sylvester's criterion for positive definiteness.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hessian matrix $H(\mathbf{x})$ of a scalar function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is the $n \times n$ symmetric matrix of all second-order partial derivatives, with $(i,j)$-entry $H_{ij} = \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_i \partial x_j}$.

**Sylvester's Criterion for Positive Definiteness:** A symmetric matrix $H$ is positive definite if and only if all its leading principal minors are strictly positive, i.e., $D_k > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$, where $D_k = \det(H_{1:k, \, 1:k})$.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC) for a local minimum.**

**Model Answer:**

Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ be a $C^2$ function and $\mathbf{x}^*$ a point such that:
1. $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ (FONC satisfied)
2. $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **positive definite** (i.e., $\mathbf{d}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} > 0$ for all nonzero $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^n$)

Then $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **strict local minimum** of $f$. The proof follows from the second-order Taylor expansion: $f(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^T H \Delta \mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\Delta \mathbf{x}$ sufficiently small, establishing $f(\mathbf{x}) > f(\mathbf{x}^*)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Solving $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ correctly but failing to classify the points → **Loss of 4-5 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *semi-definite* with *definite* — Sylvester requires *strict* positivity ($> 0$), not $\geq 0$.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to verify that the Hessian is symmetric — failing to invoke Clairaut's theorem.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using only $D_1 > 0$ for $2\times 2$ matrices — the second minor $D_2$ must also be checked.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Reporting "saddle point" without justification — always show the sign change or indefinite Hessian.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] Find the critical points of the function:
$$f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2x_1 x_2 - 6x_1 - 4x_2 + 5$$
Classify each critical point using the Hessian.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the Second-Order Necessary Condition (SONC) for a local maximum. Illustrate with a suitable example.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Compute Gradient**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 6$$

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + 2x_1 - 4$$

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 4x_1 + 2x_2 - 6 \\ 2x_1 + 2x_2 - 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$**

$$\begin{cases} 4x_1 + 2x_2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2x_1 + x_2 = 3 \\ 2x_1 + 2x_2 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 + x_2 = 2 \end{cases}$$

Subtracting: $x_1 = 1$, then $x_2 = 1$. **Unique critical point: $\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 1)$.**

**Step 3: Compute Hessian**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = 4 > 0$, $D_2 = (4)(2) - (2)(2) = 4 > 0$. **Positive definite.**

**Step 4: Classification**

By SOSC, $\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 1)$ is a **strict local minimum**.
$f(1, 1) = 2(1) + 1 + 2 - 6 - 4 + 5 = 0$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Statement of SONC for Local Maximum:**

> If $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a local maximum of a $C^2$ function $f$, then:
> 1. $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ (FONC)
> 2. $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is **negative semi-definite**: $\mathbf{d}^T H(\mathbf{x}^*) \mathbf{d} \leq 0$ for all $\mathbf{d} \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

**Illustration — Example:** $f(x_1, x_2) = -(x_1^2 + x_2^2)$ has its global maximum at $\mathbf{x}^* = (0,0)$.

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} -2x_1 \\ -2x_2 \end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}^* = (0,0)$$

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = -2 < 0$, $D_2 = 4 > 0$ → Negative definite → strict maximum. The function is a downward-opening paraboloid with peak at origin.

**Comparison with SONC:** The example uses a stronger condition (negative definite) than SONC requires (negative semi-definite). The SONC is satisfied, but it is **not sufficient** on its own — we also need SOSC (definiteness, not semi-definiteness) to *guarantee* the maximum.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the function $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^3 + x_2^3 - 3x_1 x_2$, find all critical points and classify them.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain with a proof why the gradient points in the direction of steepest ascent.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Gradient**

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 3x_1^2 - 3x_2 \\ 3x_2^2 - 3x_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Solve $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$**

From (i): $x_1^2 = x_2$. From (ii): $x_2^2 = x_1$.

Combining: $x_1 = (x_1^2)^2 = x_1^4 \Rightarrow x_1(x_1^3 - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$ or $x_1 = 1$.

- If $x_1 = 0$, then $x_2 = 0$. Critical point $\mathbf{x}^*_1 = (0, 0)$.
- If $x_1 = 1$, then $x_2 = 1$. Critical point $\mathbf{x}^*_2 = (1, 1)$.

**Step 3: Hessian**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6x_1 & -3 \\ -3 & 6x_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**At $\mathbf{x}^*_1 = (0,0)$:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -3 \\ -3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = 0$, $D_2 = 0 - 9 = -9 < 0$. Indefinite → **Saddle point**.

**At $\mathbf{x}^*_2 = (1,1)$:**

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \\ -3 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

$D_1 = 6 > 0$, $D_2 = 36 - 9 = 27 > 0$. Positive definite → **Strict local minimum**.

$f(1,1) = 1 + 1 - 3 = -1$.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Theorem:** The gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ points in the direction of maximum rate of increase of $f$.

**Proof:**

Consider an arbitrary unit direction vector $\mathbf{u}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert = 1$. The **directional derivative** along $\mathbf{u}$ is defined as:

$$D_{\mathbf{u}} f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{f(\mathbf{x}^* + h\mathbf{u}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*)}{h} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{u}$$

Using the **Cauchy-Schwarz inequality** $\vert \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b} \vert \leq \Vert \mathbf{a} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{b} \Vert$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D_{\mathbf{u}} f &= \nabla f^T \mathbf{u} \\
&\leq \Vert \nabla f \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{u} \Vert \\
&= \Vert \nabla f \Vert \cdot 1 \\
&= \Vert \nabla f \Vert
\end{aligned}$$

Equality holds if and only if $\mathbf{u}$ is **parallel to** $\nabla f$, i.e., $\mathbf{u} = \dfrac{\nabla f}{\Vert \nabla f \Vert}$.

Hence, the maximum rate of change is $\Vert \nabla f \Vert$, achieved along the gradient direction. Therefore, $\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest ascent**, and $-\nabla f$ points in the direction of **steepest descent**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Gradient** $\nabla f$: vector of first partials — points toward steepest ascent; zero at stationary points.
- ✅ **Hessian** $H$: symmetric $n \times n$ matrix of second partials — encodes local curvature.
- ✅ **FONC** (First-Order Necessary Condition): Any local extremum must satisfy $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$.
- ✅ **SONC** (Second-Order Necessary Condition): Hessian must be **semi-definite** (positive for min, negative for max).
- ✅ **SOSC** (Second-Order Sufficient Condition): Hessian must be **strictly definite** — guarantees strict extremum.
- ✅ **Sylvester's Criterion**: All leading principal minors strictly positive → positive definite; alternating signs $(-1)^k \cdot (\text{positive})$ → negative definite.
- ✅ **Indefinite Hessian** → **Saddle point** (no extremum).
- ✅ **Semi-definite case** → Inconclusive from second-order test alone — use higher-order Taylor or direct analysis.
- ✅ **Symmetry of Hessian** is guaranteed by **Clairaut's theorem** for $C^2$ functions.
- ✅ **Taylor expansion** near a critical point reduces to $f(\mathbf{x}) - f(\mathbf{x}^*) \approx \tfrac{1}{2} \Delta \mathbf{x}^T H \Delta \mathbf{x}$.
- ✅ **Newton's Method**: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - H^{-1} \nabla f$ — uses both gradient (slope) and Hessian (curvature).
- ✅ **Gradient descent**: $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \nabla f$ — first-order method, simpler but slower.
- ✅ **Eigenvalue equivalence**: Positive definite $\iff$ all $\lambda_i(H) > 0$; negative definite $\iff$ all $\lambda_i(H) < 0$; indefinite $\iff$ mixed signs.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Multivariable Optimization Without Constraints**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Minimum/Maximum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for Minimum/Maximum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **unconstrained nonlinear optimization**, when seeking the minimum or maximum of a differentiable scalar objective function $f(\mathbf{x}): \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$, a point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **candidate optimum** only if it satisfies specific mathematical conditions. These conditions are classified as:

- **Necessary Conditions (NC)** — Conditions that **must** be satisfied by any local extremum. If a point is a local min/max, it MUST satisfy these. (Failing them rules out the point.)
- **Sufficient Conditions (SC)** — Conditions that, if satisfied at a point, **guarantee** that the point is a local min/max. (Meeting them is enough to certify the optimum.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A *necessary condition* defines the class of points that CAN be optima; a *sufficient condition* identifies points that ARE definitely optima. They are not always the same — typically sufficient conditions are *stronger* than necessary conditions.

The three foundational conditions in order of strength are:

1. **First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$
2. **Second-Order Necessary Condition (SONC):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ AND $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is positive (or negative) *semi-definite*.
3. **Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC):** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ AND $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*)$ is positive (or negative) *definite*.

where $\nabla f$ is the **gradient vector** and $\mathbf{H}$ is the **Hessian matrix** (matrix of second partial derivatives).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Landscape

Imagine a hiker standing in a dense, foggy mountain range. The hiker cannot see the entire terrain, only the immediate slope under their boots. They want to find the **lowest valley** (minimum) or the **highest peak** (maximum).

- **FONC** (Flat Ground Test): "If I am exactly at the bottom of a valley or the top of a peak, the ground under my feet must be perfectly flat." A hiker standing on a slope has not yet reached an extremum. Hence, slope = 0 (gradient = 0) is **necessary**.

- **SONC** (Curvature Direction Test): "Even on flat ground, I could be on a *saddle pass* between two peaks, a flat ridge, or a true valley bottom." The ground must be *curving in the right direction* (upward for a min, downward for a max), but it can be flat in some directions. Hence, the Hessian must be *semi-definite* (curvature is non-negative in all directions).

- **SOSC** (Strict Curvature Test): "To be ABSOLUTELY CERTAIN this flat spot is a valley and not a saddle, every direction I step must curve upward." This demands the Hessian be strictly *positive definite* — no flat directions allowed. This is a **sufficient** guarantee of a local minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Saddle Point:** A stationary point that is a maximum in some directions and a minimum in others (think of a mountain pass between two peaks). The Hessian has both positive and negative eigenvalues. KTU questions frequently test students' ability to identify these.

---

### 1.3 Key Mathematical Objects (Bold Constants & Symbols)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Scalar objective function |
| $\mathbf{x}^* \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Candidate optimal point |
| $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | Gradient vector at $\mathbf{x}^*$ |
| $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}^*) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ | Symmetric Hessian matrix at $\mathbf{x}^*$ |
| $d \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | An arbitrary feasible direction vector |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Framework — The Three Conditions in Depth

**A. First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)**

If $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuously differentiable ($\mathcal{C}^1$) at a local minimum point $\mathbf{x}^*$, then:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

- **Why:** A small step $\mathbf{d}$ from $\mathbf{x}^*$ must not decrease $f$. To first order, $f(\mathbf{x}^*+\epsilon \mathbf{d}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \epsilon \nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{d}$.
