# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**State and explain the region elimination principle used in univariate optimization methods. Why is unimodality a necessary assumption?**

**Model Answer:**
The region elimination principle states that if a function $f(x)$ is **unimodal** on the closed interval $[a, b]$ (i.e., it strictly decreases to a unique minimum $x^{*}$ and then strictly increases), then any sub-interval that does not contain a function value lower than the lowest already observed can be **safely discarded** without losing the global minimum. Mathematically, if $x_1 < x_2$ and $f(x_1) \geq f(x_2)$, then $[a, x_1]$ cannot contain $x^{*}$, so we set $a = x_1$. Conversely, if $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, then $[x_2, b]$ cannot contain $x^{*}$, so we set $b = x_2$.

Unimodality is necessary because the comparison logic $f(x_1)$ vs. $f(x_2)$ only tells us the *local* direction of decrease. If the function has multiple valleys, discarding a sub-interval based on a single comparison could permanently hide the global optimum. All elimination methods — Dichotomous, Interval Halving, Fibonacci, Golden Section — assume unimodality as a foundational axiom.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List any three advantages of Fibonacci Search Method over the Equal Interval Search Method.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Optimal efficiency among direct search methods:** For a given tolerance $\varepsilon$ and interval length $L_0$, Fibonacci search achieves the smallest possible final interval $L_n$ for a fixed number of function evaluations $n$. It minimizes the maximum possible length of the final interval — hence it is provably optimal among all sequential elimination methods.
2. **Pre-planned number of evaluations:** The number of evaluations $n$ is determined *before* the search begins using $F_n \geq L_0/\varepsilon$, eliminating the need for arbitrary stopping rules.
3. **Efficient probe reuse:** At each subsequent iteration, only **one new function evaluation** is required because one of the previous two probes lies inside the new interval — saving significant computational cost for expensive objective functions.

In contrast, Equal Interval Search divides $[a, b]$ into $n$ sub-intervals uniformly and evaluates all $n+1$ boundary points at once, requiring **all** evaluations upfront with no adaptive information gain.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Minimize $f(x) = x^{3} - 6x^{2} + 11x - 6$ using the Quadratic Interpolation Method. Take initial points $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = 2$, $x_3 = 5$. Perform two iterations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Analytical check first:** $f'(x) = 3x^{2} - 12x + 11 = 0 \Rightarrow x = (12 \pm \sqrt{144 - 132})/6 = (12 \pm \sqrt{12})/6 = 2 \pm 1/\sqrt{3}$. Minimum at $x \approx 1.4226$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$f_1 = f(0) = 0 - 0 + 0 - 6 = -6$$
$$f_2 = f(2) = 8 - 24 + 22 - 6 = 0$$
$$f_3 = f(5) = 125 - 150 + 55 - 6 = 24$$

Applying the closed-form formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= (0)^2(0 - 24) + (2)^2(24 - (-6)) + (5)^2((-6) - 0) \\
&= 0 + 4(30) + 25(-6) = 120 - 150 = -30 \\
\text{Den} &= 0 \cdot (0 - 24) + 2(24 - (-6)) + 5((-6) - 0) \\
&= 0 + 60 - 30 = 30 \\
x^{*} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-30}{30} = -0.5
\end{aligned}$$

The minimum of the fitted parabola is at $x_4 = -0.5$. Update the bracket by dropping the point with the highest function value: drop $x_3 = 5$ (since $f_3 = 24$ is largest). New set: $x_1 = 0$, $x_2 = -0.5$, $x_3 = 2$.

**Iteration 2:**

$$f(-0.5) = (-0.5)^3 - 6(0.25) + 11(-0.5) - 6 = -0.125 - 1.5 - 5.5 - 6 = -13.125$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= (0)^2(0 - (-13.125)) + (-0.5)^2((-13.125) - (-6)) + (2)^2((-6) - 0) \\
&= 0 + 0.25(-7.125) + 4(-6) = -1.78125 - 24 = -25.78125 \\
\text{Den} &= 0 \cdot (13.125) + (-0.5)(-7.125) + 2(-6) \\
&= 0 + 3.5625 - 12 = -8.4375 \\
x^{*} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{-25.78125}{-8.4375} = \frac{1}{2}(3.0556) = 1.5278
\end{aligned}$$

After two iterations, $x^{*} \approx 1.5278$, which is converging toward the true minimum $\approx 1.4226$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Compare Fibonacci Search Method and Golden Section Search Method in terms of probe placement, computational cost, and reduction ratio. Why is Fibonacci called the optimal sequential search?**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Fibonacci Search | Golden Section Search |
|---|---|---|
| Probe Placement | Uses ratios of consecutive Fibonacci numbers $F_{k-1}/F_k$ | Uses fixed golden ratio $G = (\sqrt{5}-1)/2$ |
| Reduction Ratio | $L_k / L_0 = 1/F_n$ (variable, decreasing each step) | $L_k / L_0 = G^{n-1}$ (constant geometric) |
| Pre-planning | Yes — total $n$ fixed in advance | No — open-ended termination |
| Probes per Iteration | 1 new + 1 reused | 1 new + 1 reused |
| Function Evaluations | Minimum possible $n$ for given $\varepsilon$ | Approximately $n \approx \log_G(L_0/\varepsilon)$ |

**Why Fibonacci is "optimal" sequential:** Kiefer's theorem (1953) proved that among all sequential elimination methods with exactly $n$ function evaluations and a pre-specified tolerance, **Fibonacci search minimizes the largest possible final interval of uncertainty**. In other words, for a given budget $n$, no other method can guarantee a smaller worst-case $L_n$. Golden Section is the limit of Fibonacci as $n \to \infty$ and is essentially Fibonacci with a fixed reduction ratio, making it slightly suboptimal in the finite-budget case but easier to implement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Find the minimum of $f(x) = 2x^{2} - (8/3)x - 4$ in the interval $[0, 2]$ using the Dichotomous Search Method with $\delta = 0.01$. Stop when $L_k < 0.1$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Analytical check:** $f'(x) = 4x - 8/3 = 0 \Rightarrow x^{*} = 2/3 \approx 0.6667$.

**Iteration 1:** $L_0 = 2$, midpoint $= 1.0$.

$$x_1 = 1.0 - 0.01 = 0.99, \quad x_2 = 1.0 + 0.01 = 1.01$$

$f(0.99) = 2(0.9801) - (8/3)(0.99) - 4 = 1.9602 - 2.6400 - 4 = -4.6798$

$f(1.01) = 2(1.0201) - (8/3)(1.01) - 4 = 2.0402 - 2.6933 - 4 = -4.6531$

Since $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, discard right: $b_1 = x_2 = 1.01$.

$L_1 = 1.01 - 0 = 1.01$.

**Iteration 2:** midpoint $= 0.505$.

$$x_1 = 0.505 - 0.01 = 0.495, \quad x_2 = 0.505 + 0.01 = 0.515$$

$f(0.495) = 2(0.245025) - (8/3)(0.495) - 4 = 0.4901 - 1.3200 - 4 = -4.8299$

$f(0.515) = 2(0.265225) - (8/3)(0.515) - 4 = 0.5305 - 1.3733 - 4 = -4.8429$

Since $f(x_2) < f(x_1)$, discard left: $a_2 = x_1 = 0.495$.

$L_2 = 1.01 - 0.495 = 0.515$.

**Iteration 3:** midpoint $= 0.7525$.

$$x_1 = 0.7425, \quad x_2 = 0.7625$$

$f(0.7425) = 2(0.5513) - (8/3)(0.7425) - 4 = 1.1026 - 1.9800 - 4 = -4.8774$

$f(0.7625) = 2(0.5814) - (8/3)(0.7625) - 4 = 1.1628 - 2.0333 - 4 = -4.8705$

Since $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$, discard right: $b_3 = 0.7625$.

$L_3 = 0.7625 - 0.495 = 0.2675$.

**Iteration 4:** midpoint $= 0.62875$.

$$x_1 = 0.61875, \quad x_2 = 0.63875$$

$f(0.61875) = -4.8822$, $f(0.63875) = -4.8828$

Discard left: $a_4 = 0.61875$, $L_4 = 0.7625 - 0.61875 = 0.1438$.

**Iteration 5:** midpoint $= 0.6906$.

$$x_1 = 0.6806, \quad x_2 = 0.7006$$

$f(0.6806) \approx -4.8838$, $f(0.7006) \approx -4.8830$.

Discard right: $b_5 = 0.7006$, $L_5 = 0.7006 - 0.61875 = 0.0819 < 0.1$ ✓

**STOP.** Final interval $[0.61875, 0.7006]$. Best estimate $x^{*} \approx (0.61875 + 0.7006)/2 = 0.6597 \approx 2/3$ ✓

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derive the Newton–Raphson update formula for univariate minimization starting from the Taylor series expansion of $f(x)$ around $x_k$. State two limitations of the method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:** Expand $f(x)$ about $x_k$ using Taylor's theorem up to second order:

$$f(x_k + h) \approx f(x_k) + h f'(x_k) + \frac{h^{2}}{2} f''(x_k)$$

To find the stationary point of the quadratic approximation, differentiate with respect to $h$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d}{dh}\left[f(x_k) + h f'(x_k) + \frac{h^{2}}{2} f''(x_k)\right] = f'(x_k) + h f''(x_k) = 0$$

Solving for $h$:

$$h = -\frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

The new iterate is therefore:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + h = x_k - \frac{f'(x_k)}{f''(x_k)}$$

This is the **Newton–Raphson univariate minimization formula**.

**Limitations:**

1. **Requires both first and second derivatives.** Many real-world engineering problems have black-box objective functions (e.g., finite element simulations, CFD outputs) where analytical or even numerical second derivatives are unavailable or extremely expensive.

2. **Sensitive to starting point.** If $x_0$ is far from the optimum, the quadratic approximation may be poor and the iteration may diverge. Also, if $f''(x_k)$ is zero or changes sign (inflection point), the iteration fails or converges to a maximum instead of a minimum. Always verify $f''(x_k) > 0$ before each Newton step.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Skipping the unimodality assumption:** Many students apply elimination methods without stating that $f(x)$ must be unimodal on $[a, b]$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in 14-mark problems.
> 2. **Confusing reduction ratios:** Golden Section has a constant reduction $G$; Fibonacci has a *decreasing* ratio $1/F_n$. Mixing these up loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the pre-planning step:** Fibonacci Search requires $F_n \geq L_0/\varepsilon$ to be solved **before** iteration 1. Jumping straight into probes loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong probe reuse order:** When $f(x_1) < f(x_2)$ in Fibonacci, the *right* end is discarded and $x_2$ becomes the new $x_1$. Reversing this loses marks.
> 5. **Division by zero in Newton–Raphson:** Always check $f''(x_k) \neq 0$ before applying the update.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Univariate optimization** = minimize a single-variable function $f(x)$, often in a closed interval $[a, b]$.
- **Elimination methods** use only $f(x)$ values; **interpolation methods** use polynomial fits or derivatives.
- **Dichotomous Search:** two probes symmetric about midpoint; reduction ratio $\approx 1/2$.
- **Interval Halving:** three probes at quarter points; reduction ratio exactly $1/2$ per iteration.
- **Fibonacci Search:** $L_n = L_0 / F_n$; requires smallest $n$ such that $F_n \geq L_0/\varepsilon$ to be pre-computed.
- **Golden Section Search:** constant reduction $G = 0.618$; one new evaluation per iteration.
- **Quadratic Interpolation:** closed-form $x^{*}$ formula using three points and three function values; converges superlinearly.
- **Newton–Raphson univariate:** $x_{k+1} = x_k - f'(x_k)/f''(x_k)$; requires $f''(x_k) > 0$ for minimum.
- **Unimodality** is the foundational assumption for all elimination methods.
- **Fibonacci is provably optimal** among all sequential direct-search methods for a fixed evaluation budget.
- Univariate solvers are the **inner loop** of every multi-dimensional optimizer (line search role).
- Watch for **division by zero** in Newton–Raphson and **probe reuse direction** in Fibonacci.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powell's Method and Steepest Descent Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Powell's Method and Steepest Descent Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Steepest Descent Method (Cauchy’s Method)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
The Steepest Descent Method, also known as the **Gradient Descent Method** or **Cauchy’s Method**, is a first-order iterative optimization algorithm used to find the local minimum of a differentiable function $f(\mathbf{x})$. The method operates on the principle that if the real-valued function $f(\mathbf{x})$ is defined and differentiable in a neighborhood of a point $\mathbf{x}_n$, then $f(\mathbf{x})$ decreases fastest if one goes from $\mathbf{x}_n$ in the direction of the **negative gradient** $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_n)$.

Mathematically, the iterative update rule is:

$$\mathbf{x}_{n+1} = \mathbf{x}_n - \alpha_n \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_n)$$

where $\alpha_n$ is the **step size (learning rate)** chosen to minimize $f(\mathbf{x}_n + \alpha \mathbf{d}_n)$, and $\mathbf{d}_n = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_n)$ is the descent direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize This):** "Steepest Descent is an iterative line-search procedure that moves along the direction $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ (the negative gradient), which is the direction of maximum rate of decrease of the objective function at the current point."

---

### 1.2 Powell's Method (Conjugate Direction Method)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
**Powell's Method** is a numerical optimization algorithm developed by **Michael J.D. Powell** (1964) for finding the local minimum of a continuous, differentiable function without computing its gradient. It is fundamentally a **conjugate direction method** that updates its search directions during the iteration process, building up information about the curvature of the function to construct increasingly effective search directions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Powell's Method is a **direct search method** (zero-order) — it does NOT require derivative information. This makes it extremely valuable for engineering problems where derivatives are noisy, expensive, or unavailable.

The core algorithm consists of three key sub-steps:
1. **Line Search:** Perform one-dimensional minimization along each of the $n$ conjugate directions.
2. **Pattern Move:** A new search direction is constructed by extrapolating the pattern of successive minimizers.
3. **Direction Update:** One of the old directions is replaced by the new conjugate direction.

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — Hiking Down a Foggy Mountain (Steepest Descent):**
> Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain and want to reach the lowest valley. Since visibility is zero, you can only feel the slope of the ground beneath your feet. The steepest descent method is exactly this — at every step, you look at the local slope (gradient) and take a step in the steepest downhill direction. You continue until you reach a point where the slope is zero (the valley floor).
>
> **The Zig-Zag Problem:** However, on an elongated, narrow valley, this method makes you zigzag across the two sides of the valley, taking many small steps because each steepest direction points perpendicular to the valley's true length. This is a famous drawback.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — A Smart Hiker (Powell's Method):**
> Powell's method is like a hiker who, after several zig-zag descents in the fog, realizes that the overall direction of travel contains useful information. The hiker remembers the past directions and replaces the oldest direction with the new "average" direction. Over time, the hiker builds a smart set of directions that point more directly toward the valley, dramatically reducing zig-zagging — and crucially, the hiker doesn't even need to feel the slope precisely!

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

- **Convergence Rate (Steepest Descent):** Linear convergence (geometric) — relatively slow for ill-conditioned problems.
- **Convergence Rate (Powell's Method):** **Quadratic convergence** for quadratic functions — very fast.
- **Number of Function Evaluations:** Powell's method typically requires $O(n)$ line searches per iteration, each requiring multiple function evaluations.
- **Robustness:** Steepest Descent is guaranteed to converge but is slow; Powell's method is fast but can fail for non-quadratic functions due to the *direction update* step.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of Steepest Descent

The steepest descent method operates through a fixed four-step iterative loop. Let $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ be the objective function, $\mathbf{x}_k$ the current point, $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ the gradient, and $\alpha_k$ the optimal step size.

**Step 1 — Compute the Gradient:**
$$\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = \left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1}, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2}, \dots, \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n}\right]^T$$

**Step 2 — Define the Descent Direction:**
$$\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

This direction is chosen because the directional derivative $\frac{df}{d\alpha}\big|_{\alpha=0} = \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{d}_k$ is minimized (most negative) when $\mathbf{d}_k$ is anti-parallel to $\mathbf{g}_k$.

**Step 3 — Line Search for Optimal $\alpha_k$:**
The optimal step length is found by minimizing the single-variable function:
$$\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \mathbf{d}_k)$$

Setting $\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = 0$:
$$\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{d}_k = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k)^T \mathbf{d}_k = 0$$

For **quadratic functions** $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$, this yields the **closed-form Cauchy step**:
$$\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k}$$

**Step 4 — Update and Check Convergence:**
$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k + \alpha_k \mathbf{d}_k$$
Stop when $\vert\vert \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}) \vert\vert < \epsilon$ (typically $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$).

---

### 2.2 Why Steepest Descent is Slow — The Condition Number Issue

For a quadratic function with Hessian $Q$, the **convergence ratio** $\rho$ is given by:

$$\rho = \left(\frac{\lambda_{\max} - \lambda_{\min}}{\lambda_{\max} + \lambda_{\min}}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{\kappa(Q) - 1}{\kappa(Q) + 1}\right)^2$$

where $\kappa(Q) = \frac{\lambda_{\max}}{\lambda_{\min}}$ is the **condition number** of the Hessian matrix. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $\kappa(Q) \gg 1$ (ill-conditioned problems, e.g., long narrow valleys), $\rho \to 1$, and the method crawls. When $\kappa(Q) = 1$ (perfectly spherical contours), convergence happens in **exactly one iteration**.

### 2.3 The Operational Logic of Powell's Method

Powell's method is fundamentally different — it builds a set of **mutually conjugate directions** that eliminate the zig-zag behavior. Two directions $\mathbf{d}_i$ and $\mathbf{d}_j$ are **$Q$-conjugate** if:
$$\mathbf{d}_i^T Q \mathbf{d}_j = 0$$

For a quadratic function with Hessian $Q$, minimization along $n$ mutually conjugate directions is guaranteed to find the exact minimum in **at most $n$ line searches** (per full cycle).

#### 2.3.1 Powell's Original Algorithm (1964) — Step-by-Step

**Inputs:** Starting point $\mathbf{x}_0$, initial direction set $\mathbf{D} = \{\mathbf{d}_1, \mathbf{d}_2, \dots, \mathbf{d}_n\}$, tolerance $\epsilon$.

**Iteration $k$ (for $k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$):**

1. **Initialize:** Set $\mathbf{P}_0 = \mathbf{x}_k$ (current base point).

2. **Sequential Line Search:** For $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
   - Find $\alpha_i^*$ such that $f(\mathbf{P}_{i-1} + \alpha_i^* \mathbf{d}_i)$ is minimized.
   - Update: $\mathbf{P}_i = \mathbf{P}_{i-1} + \alpha_i^* \mathbf{d}_i$.

3. **Construct New Direction:** Compute the pattern vector:
   $$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_n - \mathbf{P}_0$$
   This vector points from the start to the end of the cycle.

4. **Find $\alpha^*$ for the pattern direction:** Minimize $f(\mathbf{P}_n + \alpha \mathbf{v})$ to find $\mathbf{P}_{n+1}$.

5. **Direction Replacement Rule (Powell's Heuristic):**
   - Let $f_1 = f(\mathbf{P}_0)$ (start of cycle), $f_2 = f(\mathbf{P}_n)$ (end of cycle), $f_3 = f(2\mathbf{P}_n - \mathbf{P}_0)$ (extrapolated point).
   - If $f_3 \geq f_1$ **OR** $(f_1 - 2f_2 + f_3)(f_1 - f_2 - \Delta)^2 \geq \frac{1}{2} \Delta (f_1 - f_3)^2$, **keep** the old directions and set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}_n$.
   - Otherwise, **replace** $\mathbf{d}_1$ (the oldest direction) with $\mathbf{v}$, and set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}_{n+1}$.

where $\Delta = \max \vert f(\mathbf{P}_{i-1}) - f(\mathbf{P}_i) \vert$ (largest function decrease in the cycle).

#### 2.3.2 Powell's Modified Method (Brent's Improvement)

> [!WARNING]
> Powell's original method can lose linear independence of directions for non-quadratic functions. Brent's modification only replaces a direction if the new direction provides a "significant enough" decrease, preserving stability.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Condition** | **Remarks / Use Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient | $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ | Vector of first partial derivatives |
| Steepest Descent Direction | $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$ | Negative gradient direction |
| Iterative Update | $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_n - \alpha_k \mathbf{g}_k$ | Standard recursion |
| Cauchy Step Size (Quadratic) | $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k}$ | Closed-form for quadratic $f$ |
| Convergence Ratio | $\rho = \left(\dfrac{\kappa(Q) - 1}{\kappa(Q) + 1}\right)^2$ | For quadratic; $0 \leq \rho < 1$ |
| Condition Number | $\kappa(Q) = \dfrac{\lambda_{\max}(Q)}{\lambda_{\min}(Q)}$ | Ratio of max/min eigenvalues of $Q$ |
| $Q$-Conjugacy Condition | $\mathbf{d}_i^T Q \mathbf{d}_j = 0$ | Orthogonality w.r.t. $Q$ |
| Powell New Direction | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_n - \mathbf{P}_0$ | Pattern vector / displacement |
| Powell Replacement Test | $f_3 \geq f_1$ $\text{OR}$ $(f_1-2f_2+f_3)(f_1-f_2-\Delta)^2 \geq \frac{1}{2}\Delta(f_1-f_3)^2$ | Brent's heuristic for stability |
| Stopping Criterion | $\vert\vert \mathbf{g}_k \vert\vert \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon = 10^{-6}$ typical |
| Orthogonality Property | $\mathbf{g}_{k+1} \perp \mathbf{g}_k$ | Consecutive gradients in SD are orthogonal |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Methods are Used in Industry:**
> - **Steepest Descent:** Pre-training deep neural networks, MRI image reconstruction, large-scale structural analysis (pre-conditioning step before Newton's method).
> - **Powell's Method:** Aerodynamic shape optimization (CFD where gradients are noisy), calibration of engineering models, black-box function minimization in robotics and aerospace.
> - **Powell's Hybrid (COBYLA / UOBYQA):** Embedded in MATLAB's `fminsearch` and many engineering CAD tools.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cauchy Step Size for Quadratic Functions

Let $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$, where $Q$ is symmetric positive definite ($Q = Q^T \succ 0$).

**Step 1:** Compute the gradient.
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = Q\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b} \equiv \mathbf{g}(\mathbf{x})$$

**Step 2:** Define the line-search function along $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$.
$$\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into $f$:
$$\phi(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)^T Q (\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) - \mathbf{b}^T(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + c$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate w.r.t. $\alpha$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = -\mathbf{g}_k^T Q (\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\alpha$:
$$\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T (Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b})}{\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k} = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k}$$

since $Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b} = \mathbf{g}_k$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Steepest Descent on a 2D Quadratic

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1^2 + 2x_1 x_2 + x_2^2 + 2x_1 + x_2$ starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$. Take **3 iterations**.

**Step 1 — Setup:**

$$Q = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$$

(Note: We will work with $f(\mathbf{x})$ as given and compute gradients directly.)

**Step 2 — Compute Initial Gradient at $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)$:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_1 + 2x_2 + 2 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_1 + 2x_2 + 1$$

$$\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{d}_0 = -\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Cauchy Step Size:**

$$\alpha_0 = \frac{\mathbf{g}_0^T \mathbf{g}_0}{\mathbf{g}_0^T Q \mathbf{g}_0} = \frac{(2)(2) + (1)(1)}{(2,1)\begin{bmatrix}4 & 2\\2 & 2\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\end{bmatrix}} = \frac{5}{(2,1)\begin{bmatrix}10\\6\end{bmatrix}} = \frac{5}{26}$$

**Step 4 — Update:**
$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \frac{5}{26}\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -10/26 \\ -5/26 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -5/13 \\ -5/26 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 2:**

$$\mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(-5/13) + 2(-5/26) + 2 \\ 2(-5/13) + 2(-5/26) + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -20/13 - 10/26 + 2 \\ -10/13 - 10/26 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6/13 \\ -3/13 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{d}_1 = -\mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -6/13 \\ 3/13 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{(6/13)^2 + (3/13)^2}{[6/13, -3/13] Q [6/13, -3/13]^T} = \frac{45/169}{45/169} \cdot \frac{1}{\text{denominator}}$$

Computing the denominator:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 6/13 \\ -3/13 \end{bmatrix}^T \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 6/13 \\ -3/13 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{169}\begin{bmatrix} 6 & -3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 18 \\ 6 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{90}{169}$$

$$\alpha_1 = \frac{45/169}{90/169} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Update:**
$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -5/13 \\ -5/26 \end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} -6/13 \\ 3/13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -5/13 - 3/13 \\ -5/26 + 3/26 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8/13 \\ -2/26 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8/13 \\ -1/13 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Iteration 3:**

$$\mathbf{g}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 4(-8/13) + 2(-1/13) + 2 \\ 2(-8/13) + 2(-1/13) + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -32/13 - 2/13 + 2 \\ -16/13 - 2/13 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8/13 \\ -5/13 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\alpha_2 = \frac{(-8/13)^2 + (-5/13)^2}{\text{denom}} = \frac{89/169}{\text{denom}}$$

Computing:
$$\mathbf{g}_2^T Q \mathbf{g}_2 = \frac{1}{169}\begin{bmatrix} -8 & -5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{169}\begin{bmatrix} -42 & -26 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -8 \\ -5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{466}{169}$$

$$\alpha_2 = \frac{89}{466} \approx 0.1910$$

$$\mathbf{x}_3 = \begin{bmatrix} -8/13 \\ -1/13 \end{bmatrix} - \frac{89}{466}\begin{bmatrix} -8/13 \\ -5/13 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -8/13 + 712/6058 \\ -1/13 + 445/6058 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -0.499 \\ -0.001 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** Exact optimum solves $Q\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b}$:
$$\mathbf{x}^* = Q^{-1}\mathbf{b} = \frac{1}{4}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

The iterations are clearly converging to $\mathbf{x}^* = (-0.5, 0)^T$, with the characteristic **zig-zag** pattern. $\checkmark$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Powell's Method on $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$

**Problem:** Find minimum using Powell's method starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$ with initial directions $\mathbf{d}_1 = (1, 0)^T$ and $\mathbf{d}_2 = (0, 1)^T$.

**Iteration 1, Step A — Minimize along $\mathbf{d}_1$:**

$$\phi_1(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_0 + \alpha \mathbf{d}_1) = (\alpha - 1)^2 + 1$$

Minimize: $\frac{d\phi_1}{d\alpha} = 2(\alpha - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 = 1$. 
So $\mathbf{P}_1 = (1, 0)^T$.

**Step B — Minimize along $\mathbf{d}_2$:**

$$\phi_2(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{P}_1 + \alpha \mathbf{d}_2) = 0 + (\alpha - 1)^2$$

Minimize: $\alpha_2 = 1$. So $\mathbf{P}_2 = (1, 1)^T$.

**Step C — Pattern Vector:**
$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_2 - \mathbf{P}_0 = (1, 1)^T - (0, 0)^T = (1, 1)^T$$

**Step D — Minimize along $\mathbf{v}$:**
$$\phi_3(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{P}_2 + \alpha \mathbf{v}) = (1 + \alpha - 1)^2 + (1 + \alpha - 1)^2 = 2\alpha^2$$

Minimize: $\alpha_3 = 0$. So $\mathbf{P}_3 = (1, 1)^T$.

**Step E — Replacement Test:**

$$f_1 = f(0,0) = 2, \quad f_2 = f(1,1) = 0, \quad f_3 = f(2,2) = 2$$

$$\Delta = \max(\vert 2-1 \vert, \vert 1-0 \vert) = 1$$

Test: $f_3 \geq f_1$? $2 \geq 2$ ✓. Condition triggered — **keep old directions**.

Set $\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{P}_2 = (1, 1)^T$. **Optimum reached in one cycle!** $\checkmark$

This demonstrates Powell's remarkable efficiency for quadratics — the method finds the exact 2D minimum in $n = 2$ line searches.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Steepest Descent** | **Powell's Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Order | First-order (gradient-based) | Zero-order (direct search) |
| Derivative Required? | **Yes** | **No** |
| Direction Type | $-\nabla f$ (steepest) | Conjugate (built adaptively) |
| Convergence Speed | Slow for ill-conditioned | Fast; quadratic in $n$ iters |
| Memory | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ (direction matrix) |
| Best Use Case | Smooth, well-conditioned | Noisy, black-box, smooth |
| Implementation | Very simple | Moderately complex |
| Guaranteed Convergence | Yes (with line search) | Yes (Brent's modification) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Steepest Descent — Algorithmic Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Initial Point x0]) --> initA[Set k = 0]
    initA --> gradA[Compute Gradient g_k]
    gradA --> checkA{Is norm of g_k less than epsilon?}
    checkA -- Yes --> stopA([Stop: Converged at x star])
    checkA -- No --> dirA[Set Direction d_k equals negative g_k]
    dirA --> lineA[Line Search: Minimize f of x_k plus alpha d_k over alpha]
    lineA --> alphaA[Find Optimal Step Size alpha star]
    alphaA --> updA[Update: x of k plus 1 equals x_k plus alpha star d_k]
    updA --> incA[Increment k equals k plus 1]
    incA --> gradA
```

### 4.2 Powell's Method — Algorithmic Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: x0 and initial direction set D]) --> initB[Set k = 0]
    initB --> setB[Set Base Point P0 equals x_k]
    setB --> loopB[For i from 1 to n do Line Search along d_i]
    loopB --> pnB[Get Pn after n line searches]
    pnB --> vecB[Compute Pattern Vector v equals Pn minus P0]
    vecB --> extraB[Line Search along v to find Pn plus 1]
    extraB --> testB{Apply Powell Brent Replacement Test}
    testB -- Keep Old Directions --> keepB[Set x of k plus 1 equals Pn]
    testB -- Replace Oldest Direction --> replB[Replace d1 with v and Set x of k plus 1 equals Pn plus 1]
    keepB --> convB{Converged?}
    replB --> convB
    convB -- No --> incB[Increment k and Update Direction Set]
    convB -- Yes --> stopB([Stop: Converged])
    incB --> setB
```

### 4.3 Convergence Behavior — Conceptual Comparison (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SD [Steepest Descent Path]
        s0((x0)) --> s1((x1))
        s1 --> s2((x2))
        s2 --> s3((x3))
        s3 --> s4((x4))
        s4 --> s5((x5))
    end
    subgraph PM [Powell Method Path]
        p0((x0)) --> p1((x1))
        p1 --> p2((x2))
        p2 --> pstar((x star))
    end
    s5 -.Many more zig zags.- sN((x star))
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram — Engineering Use Case

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputA [Problem Input]
        a1[Objective Function f]
        a2[Initial Guess x0]
        a3[Convergence Tolerance]
    end
    subgraph engineA [Optimization Engine]
        b1[Gradient Evaluator or Direction Updater]
        b2[Line Search Solver]
        b3[Convergence Checker]
    end
    subgraph outputA [Engineering Output]
        c1[Optimal Design Variables]
        c2[Minimum Function Value]
        c3[Iteration History Log]
    end
    a1 --> b1
    a2 --> b1
    a3 --> b3
    b1 --> b2
    b2 --> b1
    b1 --> b3
    b3 --> c1
    b3 --> c2
    b3 --> c3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
> **Define the Steepest Descent Method. Why is the negative gradient the steepest descent direction?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Steepest Descent Method is an iterative optimization algorithm that moves from the current point $\mathbf{x}_k$ along the direction of the **negative gradient** $-\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$ to find the local minimum of an unconstrained differentiable function.

The negative gradient is the steepest descent direction because, by Taylor's theorem, for any unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{d}}$:
$$f(\mathbf{x}_k + \alpha \hat{\mathbf{d}}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_k) + \alpha \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \hat{\mathbf{d}}$$

The directional derivative $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)^T \hat{\mathbf{d}}$ attains its **minimum value** when $\hat{\mathbf{d}}$ is anti-parallel to $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$. By the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, $|\nabla f^T \hat{\mathbf{d}}| \leq \vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$, with equality when $\hat{\mathbf{d}} = -\nabla f / \vert\vert \nabla f \vert\vert$. Therefore, $-\nabla f$ gives the largest rate of decrease, i.e., the steepest descent. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
> **What are conjugate directions? State the $Q$-conjugacy condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Two non-zero vectors $\mathbf{d}_i$ and $\mathbf{d}_j$ are said to be **$Q$-conjugate** (or conjugate with respect to a symmetric positive definite matrix $Q$) if they satisfy the orthogonality condition with respect to $Q$:

$$\mathbf{d}_i^T Q \mathbf{d}_j = 0$$

Conjugate directions generalize the concept of orthogonality: when $Q = I$, conjugacy reduces to standard orthogonality. For a quadratic function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$, minimization along $n$ mutually conjugate directions reaches the exact minimum in at most $n$ exact line searches — independent of the conditioning of $Q$. This property is the foundation of **conjugate gradient methods** and **Powell's method**. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **Choose ONE of the following two questions.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the optimal step size formula $\alpha_k = \dfrac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k}$ for the Steepest Descent Method applied to a quadratic function $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$, perform **two iterations** of the Steepest Descent Method on $f(x_1, x_2) = \frac{3}{2}x_1^2 + x_2^2 - x_1 x_2 - 2x_1$. Show all steps and verify convergence. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Let $f(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x} + c$, with $Q = Q^T \succ 0$.

Gradient: $\mathbf{g}_k = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k) = Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$.

Along direction $\mathbf{d}_k = -\mathbf{g}_k$, define $\phi(\alpha) = f(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)$.

$$\phi(\alpha) = \frac{1}{2}(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k)^T Q (\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) - \mathbf{b}^T(\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + c$$

$$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = -\mathbf{g}_k^T Q (\mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k) + \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{g}_k$$

Setting $\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = 0$:
$$\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha \mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$$

Since $\mathbf{g}_k = Q\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}$, we have $\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k = \mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{g}_k$.

Therefore:
$$\alpha_k = \frac{\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k}{\mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Model Solution (b):**

We have $f = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{x}^T Q \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{x}$ with $Q = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Iteration 1:** $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0,0)^T$.

$$\mathbf{g}_0 = Q\mathbf{x}_0 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$Q\mathbf{g}_0 = \begin{bmatrix} -6 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \alpha_0 = \frac{(-2)^2 + 0^2}{(-2)(-6) + (0)(2)} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$\mathbf{g}_1 = Q\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 2/3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -2/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$Q\mathbf{g}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 \\ -4/3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \alpha_1 = \frac{(2/3)^2}{(2/3)(0) + (-4/3)(-2/3)} = \frac{4/9}{8/9} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} - \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -2/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2/3 \\ 1/3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Exact optimum:** $Q\mathbf{x}^* = \mathbf{b} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}^* = Q^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

$$\det(Q) = 6 - 1 = 5, \quad Q^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{x}^* = \frac{1}{5}\begin{bmatrix} 4 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/5 \\ 2/5 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Convergence check: $\mathbf{x}_2 = (0.667, 0.333)^T$ vs $\mathbf{x}^* = (0.8, 0.4)^T$ — iterations are converging correctly. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain **Powell's Method** of optimization in detail. State clearly the step for constructing the new conjugate direction and the Powell–Brent replacement criterion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply Powell's Method to find the minimum of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 2x_1 x_2 - 4x_1$ starting from $\mathbf{x}_0 = (0, 0)^T$ with initial directions $\mathbf{d}_1 = (1, 0)^T$ and $\mathbf{d}_2 = (0, 1)^T$. Perform **one complete cycle**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Powell's Method is a **zero-order direct search technique** that builds mutually conjugate directions without computing derivatives.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Start with initial point $\mathbf{x}_0$ and initial set of linearly independent directions $\mathbf{d}_1, \dots, \mathbf{d}_n$.
2. Set base point $\mathbf{P}_0 = \mathbf{x}_k$.
3. For $i = 1, \dots, n$, perform line search along $\mathbf{d}_i$ to find $\alpha_i^*$ and update $\mathbf{P}_i = \mathbf{P}_{i-1} + \alpha_i^* \mathbf{d}_i$.
4. Construct the **pattern vector** $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_n - \mathbf{P}_0$.
5. Line search along $\mathbf{v}$ to find $\mathbf{P}_{n+1}$.
6. Apply the **Powell–Brent Replacement Test** to decide whether to replace the oldest direction $\mathbf{d}_1$ with $\mathbf{v}$.

**Powell–Brent Replacement Criterion:** Let
- $f_1 = f(\mathbf{P}_0)$, $f_2 = f(\mathbf{P}_n)$, $f_3 = f(2\mathbf{P}_n - \mathbf{P}_0)$,
- $\Delta = \max_{1 \leq i \leq n} \vert f(\mathbf{P}_{i-1}) - f(\mathbf{P}_i) \vert$.

**Replace** $\mathbf{d}_1$ with $\mathbf{v}$ if and only if:
$$f_3 < f_1 \quad \textbf{AND} \quad (f_1 - 2f_2 + f_3)(f_1 - f_2 - \Delta)^2 < \tfrac{1}{2}\Delta(f_1 - f_3)^2$$

Otherwise keep the old direction set and set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{P}_n$.

**Rationale:** The replacement test preserves **linear independence** of directions, preventing numerical breakdown for non-quadratic functions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Line search along $\mathbf{d}_1 = (1,0)^T$:**

$$\phi(\alpha) = f(\alpha, 0) = \alpha^2 - 4\alpha$$

$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = 2\alpha - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 = 2$. So $\mathbf{P}_1 = (2, 0)^T$.

**Step 2 — Line search along $\mathbf{d}_2 = (0,1)^T$:**

$$\phi(\alpha) = f(2, \alpha) = 4 + 2\alpha^2 - 4\alpha - 8 = 2\alpha^2 - 4\alpha - 4$$

$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = 4\alpha - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha_2 = 1$. So $\mathbf{P}_2 = (2, 1)^T$.

**Step 3 — Pattern Vector:**
$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_2 - \mathbf{P}_0 = (2, 1)^T$$

**Step 4 — Line search along $\mathbf{v}$:**

$$\phi(\alpha) = f(2 + 2\alpha, \alpha) = (2+2\alpha)^2 + 2\alpha^2 - 2(2+2\alpha)\alpha - 4(2+2\alpha)$$

Expanding:
$$= 4 + 8\alpha + 4\alpha^2 + 2\alpha^2 - 4\alpha - 4\alpha^2 - 8 - 8\alpha = -4\alpha^2 - 4\alpha - 4$$

$\frac{d\phi}{d\alpha} = -8\alpha - 4 = 0 \Rightarrow \alpha = -\frac{1}{2}$.

So $\mathbf{P}_3 = (2 + 2(-1/2), -1/2)^T = (1, -1/2)^T$.

**Step 5 — Brent's Replacement Test:**

$f_1 = f(0,0) = 0$, $f_2 = f(2,1) = 4 + 2 - 4 - 8 = -6$, $f_3 = f(2 \cdot 2 - 0, 2 \cdot 1 - 0) = f(4, 2) = 16 + 8 - 16 - 16 = -8$.

$\Delta = \max(\vert 0 - (-6) \vert, \vert -6 - (-8) \vert) = 6$.

Test 1: $f_3 < f_1$? $-8 < 0$ ✓ — passes.

Test 2: $(0 - 2(-6) + (-8))(0 - (-6) - 6)^2 = (4)(-12)^2 = 576$.
$\frac{1}{2}(6)(0 - (-8))^2 = 3 \cdot 64 = 192$.

$576 < 192$? ✗ — fails.

**Conclusion:** Keep old directions. Set $\mathbf{x}_1 = \mathbf{P}_2 = (2, 1)^T$.

**Exact verification:** $\nabla f = 0 \Rightarrow 2x_1 - 2x_2 - 4 = 0$ and $4x_2 - 2x_1 = 0$, giving $\mathbf{x}^* = (8/3, 4/3)^T$. The method is still some distance away, but the cycle has been completed correctly. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Direction:** Students often write $\mathbf{d}_k = +\nabla f$ instead of $-\nabla f$ — instant **-2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Hessian in Cauchy Step:** Writing $\alpha_k = 1$ instead of $\mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k / \mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k$ for quadratics.
> 3. **Skipping Convergence Check:** KTU examiners award **1 mark** for verifying $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$ (orthogonality property).
> 4. **Powell's Replacement Test:** Many students forget the **AND/OR** logical structure — replacing directions unconditionally causes failure on non-quadratics.
> 5. **Not Drawing the Zig-Zag Diagram:** For 14-mark questions on Steepest Descent, sketching the contour plot with the iteration path is worth **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY:**
>
> - **Steepest Descent** is a **first-order, gradient-based** method using direction $\mathbf{d}_k = -\nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$.
> - For **quadratics**, the closed-form step size is the **Cauchy step**: $\alpha_k = \mathbf{g}_k^T \mathbf{g}_k \, / \, \mathbf{g}_k^T Q \mathbf{g}_k$.
> - **Consecutive gradients are orthogonal:** $\mathbf{g}_{k+1}^T \mathbf{g}_k = 0$.
> - **Convergence ratio:** $\rho = (\kappa - 1)^2 / (\kappa + 1)^2$, where $\kappa$ is the Hessian condition number. Slow when $\kappa \gg 1$.
> - **Zig-zag behavior** is the chief drawback for ill-conditioned elongated valleys.
> - **Powell's Method** is a **zero-order, derivative-free** conjugate direction method.
> - It performs $n$ line searches per cycle, builds a **pattern vector** $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{P}_n - \mathbf{P}_0$.
> - **Brent's Replacement Criterion** preserves direction-set independence — critical for non-quadratics.
> - Powell's method finds the **exact minimum of a quadratic in $n$ line searches** per cycle.
> - **Steepest Descent:** guaranteed convergence, slow. **Powell's:** fast, robust for black-box problems.
> - Remember: $Q$-conjugacy is the **engine** that eliminates zig-zagging in Powell's method.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of a Constrained Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of a Constrained Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Constrained Optimization Problem** is a mathematical programming problem in which the decision variables are restricted to satisfy one or more functional relationships known as **constraints**, in addition to the objective of minimizing or maximizing the objective function.

In standard form, a constrained problem is expressed as:

$$\min \, f(\mathbf{x})$$

Subject to:

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

$$h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

$$x_i^{(L)} \leq x_i \leq x_i^{(U)}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

Where:
- $f(\mathbf{x})$ is the **objective function**.
- $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ are **inequality constraints**.
- $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ are **equality constraints**.
- The bounds define the **side constraints** (variable limits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a problem is called "constrained" if **at least one functional constraint** ($g_j$ or $h_k$) is present. A problem involving only side constraints (variable bounds) is still treated as a constrained problem under the Kuhn–Tucker framework.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are a **treasure hunter** standing in a vast open field, and your goal is to find the lowest point in the valley (minimum). Without any restrictions, you could walk anywhere on the field. However, suppose the field is now **fenced** — only certain regions are accessible to you. The **fences represent constraints**.

- **Equality constraints ($h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$)** behave like **invisible walls** you must walk *exactly along* (e.g., a tightrope you must balance on).
- **Inequality constraints ($g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$)** behave like **solid barriers** — you must remain on or inside a permitted region, but you are free to walk inside.
- The set of all points satisfying every constraint is called the **Feasible Region**.
- The optimal point lies either in the interior (unconstrained optimum) or, more typically, on the **boundary** of the feasible region where the constraint "pushes" against the objective function.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** In unconstrained problems, the optimum is found where $\nabla f = 0$. In constrained problems, the optimum is found where $\nabla f$ is **balanced** by the constraint reaction forces — captured mathematically by the **Lagrange multiplier** concept.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Meaning |
|------|--------|------------------|
| Lagrange Multiplier (equality) | $\lambda_k$ | Reaction force intensity of $k^{th}$ equality constraint |
| Lagrange Multiplier (inequality) | $\mu_j$ | Reaction force intensity of $j^{th}$ inequality constraint |
| Slack Variable | $s_j$ | Non-negative variable converting inequality to equality |
| Active Set | $\mathcal{A}$ | Set of indices $j$ for which $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ at the optimum |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Constraints

A constrained problem is characterized by the **type, number, and behavior** of its constraints. The classification is as follows:

1. **Equality Constraints** ($h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$):
   - Must be satisfied exactly.
   - Reduce the **effective dimensionality** of the design space.
   - Example: A chemical reaction balance equation: $\sum n_i \mu_i = 0$.

2. **Inequality Constraints** ($g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$):
   - Define a **boundary** of feasible space.
   - A constraint is **active** at $\mathbf{x}^*$ if $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$; otherwise it is **inactive** (strictly negative).
   - Example: Stress must not exceed yield: $\sigma \leq \sigma_y$.

3. **Side Constraints / Bounds**:
   - One-dimensional constraints on individual variables.
   - Often handled implicitly.

4. **Linear vs Nonlinear Constraints**:
   - **Linear:** $g_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x} - b_j \leq 0$ — define a convex polyhedron.
   - **Nonlinear:** $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ is a general nonlinear function — define a curved feasible region.

### 2.2 Characteristics of the Feasible Region

| Characteristic | Description | KTU Significance |
|---------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Convexity** | A region is convex if the line segment between any two feasible points is entirely feasible. | Guarantees local = global optimum |
| **Closedness** | Region includes its boundary points. | Ensures existence of optimum (Weierstrass) |
| **Boundedness** | Region is finite in extent. | Guarantees finite optimum |
| **Regularity** | Gradients of active constraints are linearly independent. | Required for KKT conditions to apply |
| **Interior Point** | Point strictly satisfying all inequality constraints ($g_j < 0$). | Behaves as unconstrained locally |

### 2.3 The Active Set Concept

At any candidate optimal point $\mathbf{x}^*$, the constraints are divided into two groups:

- **Active Set $\mathcal{A}$**: $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ — these constraints touch the boundary of feasibility.
- **Inactive Set $\mathcal{I}$**: $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ — these constraints are non-binding.

> [!TIP]
> The active set changes as the optimum moves. The **active set strategy** is fundamental in Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP) — a critical KTU Module 4 topic.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | Standard Form (min) | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_j \leq 0, \, h_k = 0$ | KTU convention |
| 2 | Lagrangian Function | $L(\mathbf{x}, \lambda, \mu) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \lambda_k h_k(\mathbf{x}) + \sum \mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Core augmented function |
| 3 | Stationarity (KKT-1) | $\nabla_{\mathbf{x}} L = \nabla f + \sum \lambda_k \nabla h_k + \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j = \mathbf{0}$ | First-order necessary condition |
| 4 | Primal Feasibility | $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0, \; h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ | Must lie in feasible region |
| 5 | Dual Feasibility | $\mu_j \geq 0, \; \forall j$ | Inequality multipliers non-negative |
| 6 | Complementary Slackness | $\mu_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \; \forall j$ | Inactive ⇒ $\mu_j = 0$; Active ⇒ $g_j = 0$ |
| 7 | Constraint Qualification (LICQ) | $\{\nabla g_j : j \in \mathcal{A}\} \cup \{\nabla h_k\}$ are linearly independent | Regularity condition |
| 8 | Number of Active Constraints | $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert \leq n$ | Cannot exceed dimension |
| 9 | Sensitivity (Shadow Price) | $\dfrac{\partial f^*}{\partial b_j} = -\mu_j^*$ | Value of relaxing constraint by 1 unit |
| 10 | Fritz John Form | If LICQ fails, $\exists (\mu_0, \mu, \lambda)$ with $\mu_0 \geq 0$, not all zero, satisfying generalized KKT | Degenerate case |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Constrained Problem |
|--------|-----------------------------------|
| **Structural Design** | Minimize weight subject to stress $\leq$ allowable, deflection $\leq$ limit |
| **Process Engineering** | Maximize yield subject to mass/energy balance (equality) and safety bounds (inequality) |
| **Mechanical (Gear Train)** | Minimize volume subject to power transmission $\geq$ requirement and bending stress constraint |
| **Electrical Networks** | Minimize power loss subject to Kirchhoff's laws (equality) and line capacity limits |
| **Manufacturing** | Minimize cost subject to quality tolerance and machine capacity |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Necessary Conditions from First Principles

We consider the constrained problem:

$$\min f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p$$

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup**

At a local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$, any small feasible displacement $\mathbf{d}\mathbf{x}$ must satisfy the **tangency condition**:

$$\nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{d}\mathbf{x} = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p$$

This is because moving *along* the constraint surface keeps $h_k$ unchanged to first order.

**Step 2 — Variation of the Objective**

For $\mathbf{x}^*$ to be a constrained minimum, the first-order change in $f$ along every feasible direction must vanish:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)^T \mathbf{d}\mathbf{x} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Combining the Two Conditions**

Both conditions mean: $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must lie in the **span** of $\{\nabla h_1, \dots, \nabla h_p\}$. Therefore, there exist scalars $\lambda_k$ such that:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Constructing the Lagrangian**

Define the **augmented function**:

$$L(\mathbf{x}, \lambda) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k h_k(\mathbf{x})$$

The necessary conditions become:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad \frac{\partial L}{\partial \lambda_k} = 0 \;\; (k = 1, \dots, p)$$

### 3.2 Extension to Inequality Constraints (KKT Derivation)

For inequality constraint $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$:

**Case A — Inactive Constraint:** $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$
A small perturbation does not violate the constraint. The optimum behaves as if unconstrained locally. Therefore $\mu_j = 0$.

**Case B — Active Constraint:** $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$
The constraint behaves locally like an equality. From the same tangency argument:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \mu_j \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

To ensure that **increasing** $g_j$ (i.e., moving into infeasibility) raises $f$, we need $\mu_j \geq 0$.

**Unified Complementary Slackness Condition:**

$$\mu_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \qquad \mu_j \geq 0$$

This single equation elegantly captures **both** cases.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Two-Variable Constrained Problem

**Problem:**
Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 2)^2 + (x_2 - 1)^2$ subject to $g(x_1, x_2) = x_1 + x_2 - 4 \leq 0$.

**Step 1 — Compute gradients**

$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_1 - 2) \\ 2(x_2 - 1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nabla g = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Test unconstrained minimum**

Setting $\nabla f = 0$ gives $(x_1, x_2) = (2, 1)$.

Check constraint: $2 + 1 - 4 = -1 \leq 0$ — feasible.

Since the unconstrained optimum lies **inside** the feasible region, **no constraint is active**. Hence $\mu = 0$ and the KKT conditions reduce to $\nabla f = 0$.

**Step 3 — Optimal solution**

$$x_1^* = 2, \quad x_2^* = 1, \quad f^* = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even though a constraint exists, it is **inactive** at the optimum. This illustrates the power of KKT — it automatically identifies when constraints are non-binding.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why KKT Works

| Condition | Mathematical Role | Intuitive Role |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| Stationarity | Balance of forces | Equilibrium |
| Primal Feasibility | Point inside feasible set | Stays inside the fence |
| Dual Feasibility ($\mu_j \geq 0$) | Correct sign of push | Push comes from outside only |
| Complementary Slackness | Links active/inactive | Either the fence pushes, or it does not |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Constrained Problems (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimization Problem] --> B{Constraints Present?}
    B -- No --> C[Unconstrained Problem]
    B -- Yes --> D[Constrained Problem]
    D --> E[By Type of Constraint]
    E --> F1[Equality Constraints hx equals 0]
    E --> F2[Inequality Constraints gx less equal 0]
    E --> F3[Side Constraints Bounds on xi]
    D --> G[By Behaviour at Optimum]
    G --> H1[Active Constraints g equals 0]
    G --> H2[Inactive Constraints g less than 0]
    D --> I[By Geometry]
    I --> J1[Convex Feasible Region]
    I --> J2[Non Convex Feasible Region]
    D --> K[Solution Method]
    K --> L1[Lagrange Multiplier Method]
    K --> L2[Kuhn Tucker KKT Conditions]
    K --> L3[Penalty Function Methods]
    K --> L4[Sequential Quadratic Programming]
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of KKT Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Problem Statement] --> B[Form Lagrangian Lx lambda mu]
    B --> C[Stationarity nabla x L equals 0]
    B --> D[Primal Feasibility gj less equal 0]
    B --> E[Dual Feasibility mu j greater equal 0]
    B --> F[Complementary Slackness mu j gj equals 0]
    C --> G{Solution Exists?}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Yes --> H[Candidate Optimum Found]
    G -- No --> I[Constraint Qualification Violated]
    I --> J[Apply Fritz John Conditions]
```

### 4.3 Geometric Picture: Feasible Region with Active/Inactive Constraints

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Decision Space Rn] --> R[Feasible Region]
    R --> Int[Interior Points: All g less than 0 Inactive]
    R --> Bnd[Boundary Points: At least one g equals 0 Active]
    Bnd --> OP[Optimal Point x star]
    OP --> AP[Active Constraint Provides Support]
    OP --> IP[Inactive Constraints Do Not Bind]
    Int --> UP[Unconstrained Optimum if inside]
    UP --> CS[Constraint is slack mu equals 0]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Takeaway:** The optimal point lies at the boundary where the **objective function's level curve** is **tangent** to the feasible region's boundary. At tangency, the gradients $\nabla f$ and $\nabla g$ are **parallel** — the basis of the Lagrange multiplier condition.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a constrained optimization problem. Distinguish between equality and inequality constraints with one engineering example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **constrained optimization problem** is one in which the decision variables must satisfy additional functional relationships (besides the bounds) while minimizing or maximizing the objective function.

**Equality constraints** ($h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$): Must be satisfied exactly. They typically arise from physical laws (e.g., Kirchhoff's current law: $\sum I_i = 0$ at a node).

**Inequality constraints** ($g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$): Define limits or bounds. Example: In a pressure vessel, hoop stress $\sigma \leq \sigma_{allowable}$.

*(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — equality with example; 1 mark — inequality with example)*

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is meant by an "active constraint"? How does it differ from an inactive constraint?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A constraint $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ is said to be **active** at a point $\mathbf{x}^*$ if $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$. It is **inactive** if $g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$.

Active constraints lie on the boundary of the feasible region and directly influence the optimum. Inactive constraints have a "buffer" and do not affect the local behavior of the optimum.

The corresponding multiplier satisfies $\mu_j \geq 0$ for active constraints and $\mu_j = 0$ for inactive constraints (complementary slackness).

*(1 mark — definition of active; 1 mark — definition of inactive; 1 mark — role via complementary slackness)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

#### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks**

**Q: (a)** State and explain the Kuhn–Tucker conditions for a constrained optimization problem with both equality and inequality constraints. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following problem using KKT conditions:
Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$
Subject to: $x_1 + x_2 \geq 2$ and $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) KKT Conditions (7 Marks):**

For the problem $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, the **Kuhn–Tucker Necessary Conditions** (assuming a regular point / LICQ holds) are:

1. **Stationarity:**
$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

2. **Primal Feasibility:**
$$g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0, \quad h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$$

3. **Dual Feasibility:**
$$\mu_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m$$

4. **Complementary Slackness:**
$$\mu_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m$$

5. **LICQ:** The gradients of active constraints and equality constraints must be linearly independent.

*(1 mark per condition, with the 5th bonus for LICQ)*

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Rewrite constraints in standard form.
- $g_1: 2 - x_1 - x_2 \leq 0$
- $g_2: -x_1 \leq 0$
- $g_3: -x_2 \leq 0$

**Step 2:** Compute gradients:
$$\nabla f = \begin{bmatrix} 2x_1 \\ 2x_2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nabla g_1 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nabla g_2 = \begin{bmatrix} -1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \nabla g_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ -1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** KKT Stationarity:
$$2x_1 - \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0$$
$$2x_2 - \mu_1 - \mu_3 = 0$$

**Step 4:** Complementary Slackness:
- If $g_1$ active ($x_1 + x_2 = 2$): expect $\mu_1 > 0$.
- If $x_1 = 0$ active: $\mu_2 > 0$ but then $x_1 = 0$, so $x_2 = 2$ gives $f = 4$.
- If $x_2 = 0$ active: $\mu_3 > 0$ but then $x_1 = 2$, $f = 4$.

The unconstrained candidate $(1,1)$ lies on $g_1$ but with $x_1, x_2 > 0$, so $\mu_2 = \mu_3 = 0$.

**Step 5:** From stationarity:
$$2(1) - \mu_1 = 0 \Rightarrow \mu_1 = 2$$

Check dual feasibility: $\mu_1 = 2 \geq 0$ ✓. All conditions satisfied.

**Step 6:** Verify by comparing with corners: $(0,2), (2,0), (0,0)$ give $f = 4, 4, 0$ respectively. Wait — $(0,0)$ violates $g_1$ since $0 + 0 < 2$. So infeasible. The closest feasible point to origin on line $x_1 + x_2 = 2$ is indeed $(1,1)$.

**Final Answer:**
$$x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 1, \quad f^* = 2, \quad \mu_1^* = 2, \quad \mu_2^* = \mu_3^* = 0$$

---

#### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — **14 Marks** *(Internal Choice)*

**Q: (a)** What are the characteristics of the feasible region of a constrained optimization problem? Explain convexity, regularity (LICQ), and active set with diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing firm produces two products $x_1$ and $x_2$. The profit function is $f = 50x_1 + 80x_2$. Due to raw material shortage, $3x_1 + 4x_2 \leq 240$, and labor constraints give $x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 100$. Both products are non-negative. Find the optimal production mix using KKT conditions. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Characteristics of Feasible Region (7 Marks):**

The feasible region $\mathcal{F} = \{\mathbf{x} : g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ has the following key characteristics:

1. **Convexity** (2 marks): $\mathcal{F}$ is convex if for any two points $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \in \mathcal{F}$ and any $\theta \in [0,1]$, the point $\theta \mathbf{x}^{(1)} + (1-\theta)\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \in \mathcal{F}$. Convex feasible regions guarantee that any local optimum is a global optimum.

2. **Regularity / LICQ** (2 marks): A point $\mathbf{x}^*$ is regular if the gradients of all active inequality constraints and all equality constraints are linearly independent. Without regularity, KKT conditions may not apply; one must then use Fritz John conditions.

3. **Active Set** (2 marks): The set $\mathcal{A}(\mathbf{x}) = \{j : g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0\}$ defines which constraints are binding. The active set strategy underlies advanced algorithms like SQP.

4. **Closed and Bounded** (1 mark): A compact feasible region guarantees the existence of an optimum by the Weierstrass theorem.

**(b) Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Standard form. Minimize $-f = -50x_1 - 80x_2$ s.t.
- $g_1: 3x_1 + 4x_2 - 240 \leq 0$
- $g_2: x_1 + 2x_2 - 100 \leq 0$
- $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 2:** Stationarity: $-\nabla f = \mu_1 \nabla g_1 + \mu_2 \nabla g_2$

$$-50 = 3\mu_1 + \mu_2$$
$$-80 = 4\mu_1 + 2\mu_2$$

**Step 3:** Solve. From first: $\mu_2 = -50 - 3\mu_1$. Substitute:

$$-80 = 4\mu_1 + 2(-50 - 3\mu_1) = 4\mu_1 - 100 - 6\mu_1 = -2\mu_1 - 100$$
$$2\mu_1 = -20 \Rightarrow \mu_1 = -10$$

Negative — **violates dual feasibility**. So this candidate (interior intersection) is invalid.

**Step 4:** Check vertices. Since negative multipliers indicate non-optimality, evaluate corners of feasible polygon (vertices):

| Vertex | $(x_1, x_2)$ | Profit $50x_1 + 80x_2$ |
|--------|--------------|------------------------|
| $(0, 0)$ | origin | 0 |
| $(80, 0)$ | $x_1$-intercept of $g_1$ | 4000 |
| $(0, 50)$ | $x_2$-intercept of $g_2$ | 4000 |
| Intersection of $g_1, g_2$ | solve system | compute |

Intersection: $3x_1 + 4x_2 = 240$ and $x_1 + 2x_2 = 100 \Rightarrow x_1 = 40, x_2 = 30$.

Profit: $50(40) + 80(30) = 2000 + 2400 = 4400$.

**Step 5:** KKT at $(40, 30)$ with both active:
$$-50 = 3\mu_1 + \mu_2$$
$$-80 = 4\mu_1 + 2\mu_2 \Rightarrow \mu_2 = -50 - 3\mu_1$$

Substituting: $-80 = 4\mu_1 + 2(-50 - 3\mu_1) \Rightarrow \mu_1 = -10$. Negative again!?

**Correction:** For a **maximization** problem, the multipliers satisfy $-\nabla f = \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j$, but the sign convention requires $\mu_j \geq 0$ for **maximization with $\leq$** form. The correct form:

For max $f$ s.t. $g_j \leq 0$: $\nabla f = \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j$ with $\mu_j \geq 0$.

$$50 = 3\mu_1 + \mu_2$$
$$80 = 4\mu_1 + 2\mu_2$$

Multiply first by 2: $100 = 6\mu_1 + 2\mu_2$. Subtract: $20 = 2\mu_1 \Rightarrow \mu_1 = 10$. Then $\mu_2 = 50 - 30 = 20$.

Both $\mu_j \geq 0$ ✓. KKT satisfied.

**Final Answer:**
$$x_1^* = 40, \quad x_2^* = 30, \quad \text{Maximum Profit} = 4400$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sign Convention Trap**
> KTU examiners consistently deduct **2 marks** if students mix up the sign convention between maximization and minimization, or fail to verify dual feasibility $\mu_j \geq 0$ after solving. **Always:**
> 1. Convert max → min by negating $f$ (or remember the dual sign rule).
> 2. After solving, **explicitly state** whether all multipliers satisfy $\mu_j \geq 0$.
> 3. Check **all** candidate vertices, not just the algebraic intersection.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Characteristics of Constrained Problem**

- **Standard Form:** Always rewrite to $\min f$ with $g_j \leq 0$, $h_k = 0$ before applying KKT.
- **Lagrangian:** $L = f + \sum \mu_j g_j + \sum \lambda_k h_k$ — central object of constrained analysis.
- **Four KKT Pillars:** Stationarity, Primal Feasibility, Dual Feasibility, Complementary Slackness.
- **Complementary Slackness** $\mu_j g_j = 0$: This is the **single most-tested KTU concept** — memorize it.
- **Active Set $\mathcal{A}$:** Carries the actual binding information; inactive constraints have $\mu_j = 0$.
- **LICQ (Regularity):** Gradients of active constraints must be linearly independent; if violated, use **Fritz John** form.
- **Shadow Price:** $\mu_j^*$ measures how much $f^*$ improves per unit relaxation of $g_j \leq 0$.
- **Convexity → Global Optimality:** If $f$ and $g_j$ are convex, any KKT point is **global**.
- **Counting Rule:** $\vert \mathcal{A} \vert \leq n$; otherwise over-determined system, no regular KKT point exists.
- **Sign Discipline:** Maximization problems need $\nabla f = \sum \mu_j \nabla g_j$ with $\mu_j \geq 0$ — different from minimization sign.
- **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting non-negativity of variables (treat them as inequality constraints $g: -x_i \leq 0$).
- **Visual Anchor:** Optimal point = tangency between objective level curve and feasible boundary.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — CLASSIFICATION OF OPTIMIZATION PROBLEMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Classification of Optimization Problems):**
> In Engineering Optimization, **classification** refers to the systematic categorization of optimization problems and methods based on well-defined mathematical properties of the objective function, constraints, decision variables, and the underlying solution environment. This taxonomy enables the engineer to select an appropriate solution algorithm and predict the qualitative nature of the optimum (local vs. global, unique vs. multiple).

Mathematically, a general constrained optimization problem is posed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Find } & \mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n]^T \\
\text{to minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p \\
& \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

Each element of the formulation — the nature of $f$, the structure of $g_j$ and $h_k$, the type of $\mathbf{x}$, and the number of objectives — provides an axis along which the problem can be classified.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Doctor's Diagnostic Chart:**
> Think of optimization problems the way a doctor triages patients in an emergency room. Before prescribing a treatment (algorithm), the doctor first classifies the ailment — is the pain **acute or chronic** (convex or non-convex), is the cause **internal or external** (unconstrained or constrained), is the patient **stationary or moving** (static or dynamic)? Without classification, the wrong medicine may be administered. Similarly, **classification is the diagnostic step that precedes algorithm selection** in engineering optimization.

### 1.3 Why Classification Matters in KTU Examinations

The KTU 2024 Scheme places classification at the very beginning of Module 3 because **every subsequent algorithm (KKT, Penalty Function, Quadratic Programming, Integer Programming, Dynamic Programming) is matched to a specific class of problems**. Examiners frequently award 3–7 marks for correctly *identifying the class* of a given problem before the student proceeds with its solution.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Reference Metric Used in Classification:**
> - **Convexity Indicator** $\Rightarrow$ Hessian matrix $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x})$
> - **Constraint Linearity Indicator** $\Rightarrow$ Degree of $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ in $\mathbf{x}$
> - **Variable Domain Indicator** $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbb{R}, \mathbb{Z}, \{0,1\}$ (continuous, integer, binary)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Optimization Problems

A problem can be classified along **eight primary axes**. Each axis is detailed below with rigorous logic and engineering context.

### Axis 1 — Presence or Absence of Constraints
- **Unconstrained Problems:** No $g_j(\mathbf{x})$ or $h_k(\mathbf{x})$; the feasible set is the entire $\mathbb{R}^n$.
  - Solution hinges entirely on $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Constrained Problems:** At least one $g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ or $h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ is active.
  - Requires **KKT conditions**, **Penalty methods**, **Lagrangian methods**, or **Barrier methods**.

### Axis 2 — Nature of the Objective Function and Constraints
- **Linear Programming (LP):** Both $f$ and all $g_j, h_k$ are linear in $\mathbf{x}$. Solved by **Simplex method / Interior Point method**.
- **Nonlinear Programming (NLP):** At least one of $f, g_j, h_k$ is nonlinear. Solved by **gradient-based** or **direct search** methods.
- **Quadratic Programming (QP):** Objective is quadratic ($\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$) and constraints are linear. Solved by **Wolfe's method, Beale's method, Hildreth–Drezner method**.

### Axis 3 — Nature of Decision Variables
- **Continuous Variable Optimization:** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$. Most classical methods apply.
- **Integer Programming (IP):** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^n$. Solved by **Branch and Bound, Cutting Plane, Gomory's method**.
- **Mixed-Integer Programming (MIP):** Some $\mathbf{x}$ continuous, some integer.
- **Binary (0–1) Programming:** $\mathbf{x}_i \in \{0,1\}$. Used in **combinatorial** and **selection** problems.
- **Discrete Variable Optimization:** $\mathbf{x}$ drawn from a finite set.

### Axis 4 — Number of Objective Functions
- **Single-Objective Optimization:** Only one $f(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Multi-Objective Optimization:** $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = [f_1, f_2, \dots, f_q]^T$ with $q \geq 2$. Solved by **weighted sum, $\epsilon$-constraint, Pareto front techniques**.

### Axis 5 — Convexity of the Feasible Region and Objective
- **Convex Optimization:** $f$ is convex and the feasible set is a convex set. **Global optimum = local optimum**.
- **Non-Convex Optimization:** Multiple local optima; global search required (genetic algorithms, simulated annealing, branch and bound).

### Axis 6 — Differentiability of Functions
- **Smooth (Differentiable) Optimization:** $f, g_j, h_k$ are $C^1$ or $C^2$; gradient/Hessian methods apply.
- **Non-Smooth Optimization:** At least one function is non-differentiable (e.g., $\lvert x \rvert$, $\max(0, x)$). Requires **sub-gradient** or **bundle methods**.

### Axis 7 — Number of Optima Expected
- **Unique Optimum Problems:** Strictly convex $f$ on a convex set.
- **Multiple Optima Problems:** Plateau or non-convexity leads to alternative optima of equal value.

### Axis 8 — Determinism of the Environment
- **Deterministic Optimization:** All parameters are known constants.
- **Stochastic Optimization:** Parameters are random variables with known distributions.
- **Dynamic Optimization:** Optimum evolves with time; governed by differential/difference equations (Pontryagin's Maximum Principle, Bellman's Dynamic Programming).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Module-3 Lens:** Module 3 primarily targets **Nonlinear Constrained Programming (NCP)** with focus on:
> 1. **Equality-constrained problems** (Lagrange multipliers)
> 2. **Inequality-constrained problems** (KKT conditions)
> 3. **Convex NLP** (Wolfe's dual / Beale's method for QP)
> 4. **Integer/Combinatorial problems** (Branch & Bound, Gomory's cuts)
> 5. **Dynamic programming** problems (Bellman's Principle of Optimality)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Sl.** | **Classification Axis** | **Sub-Class** | **Mathematical Identifier** | **Preferred Algorithm** | **Marks Weightage (Typical KTU)** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Constraints | Unconstrained | $m=0,\ p=0$ | Gradient, Newton, Quasi-Newton | 2–3 |
| 2 | Constraints | Constrained | $m \geq 1$ or $p \geq 1$ | KKT, Penalty, Barrier | 7–14 |
| 3 | Linearity | Linear | $\nabla^2 f = 0$ | Simplex, Interior Point | 3–7 |
| 4 | Linearity | Nonlinear | $\nabla^2 f \neq 0$ | KKT, SQP | 7–14 |
| 5 | Linearity | Quadratic | $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Wolfe, Beale, Hildreth–Drezner | 7–14 |
| 6 | Variable Type | Continuous | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | All classical methods | 2–3 |
| 7 | Variable Type | Integer / Discrete | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ | Branch & Bound, Gomory | 7–14 |
| 8 | Objective Count | Single | $q=1$ | All scalar methods | 2–3 |
| 9 | Objective Count | Multi-objective | $q \geq 2$ | Weighted Sum, Pareto | 3–7 |
| 10 | Convexity | Convex | $\mathbf{H} \succeq 0$ | Any local search (suffices) | 3–7 |
| 11 | Convexity | Non-Convex | $\mathbf{H} \not\succeq 0$ | Global: GA, SA, BB | 3–7 |
| 12 | Differentiability | Smooth | $f \in C^1$ or $C^2$ | Gradient-based | 2 |
| 13 | Differentiability | Non-Smooth | Has kinks/corners | Sub-gradient, Bundle | 3 |
| 14 | Determinism | Deterministic | Parameters fixed | Classical | 2 |
| 15 | Determinism | Stochastic / Dynamic | Random / time-varying | Stochastic DP, Robust Opt. | 3–7 |

### 2.3 KKT Conditions — The Master Classification Tool (Preview)

For a problem with $f, g_j, h_k \in C^1$, the first-order necessary conditions for a local minimum $\mathbf{x}^*$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j \nabla g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) &= \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(Stationarity)} \\
g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m \quad &\text{(Primal Feasibility)} \\
h_k(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p \quad &\text{(Primal Feasibility)} \\
\mu_j \, g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m \quad &\text{(Complementary Slackness)} \\
\mu_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m \quad &\text{(Dual Feasibility)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These conditions **classify** the optimum by identifying which constraints are active ($\mu_j > 0$) and which are inactive ($\mu_j = 0$).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Domain** | **Typical Optimization Class** | **Practical Example** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Structural Engineering | NLP (Convex) | Minimum-weight truss design |
| Power Systems | LP / MILP | Unit commitment, economic dispatch |
| Manufacturing | QP, NLP | Chip-cutting parameter optimization |
| Logistics & Supply Chain | IP / Binary | Vehicle routing, warehouse location |
| Control Engineering | Dynamic / Stochastic | Trajectory planning under noise |
| VLSI Design | NLP, IP | Circuit sizing with discrete transistor widths |
| Aerospace | Multi-Objective NLP | Aircraft wing shape — weight vs. drag |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Over **70%** of all real industrial optimization models fall under the **Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming (MINLP)** class. The classification step is therefore the single most important determinant of the **computational tractability** of the problem.
