# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Why Local Optimum Equals Global Optimum in Convex Problems

**Theorem (Convex Programming Optimality).** If $f(\mathbf{x})$ is a convex function on a convex feasible set $S \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$, then any local minimum of $f$ on $S$ is also a global minimum.

**Step 1 — Setup.**
Let $\mathbf{x}^*$ be a local minimum. By definition, there exists $\delta > 0$ such that for all $\mathbf{x} \in S$ with $\Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert < \delta$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq f(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2 — Assume, for contradiction, that a global minimum $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ exists with $f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) < f(\mathbf{x}^*)$.**

**Step 3 — Construct a convex combination.**
For any $\alpha \in (0, 1)$, define the point:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{\alpha} = \alpha \hat{\mathbf{x}} + (1 - \alpha) \mathbf{x}^*
$$

Since $S$ is convex and $\hat{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{x}^* \in S$, the point $\mathbf{x}_{\alpha} \in S$ for all $\alpha \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 4 — Apply convexity of $f$.**
By the definition of a convex function:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_{\alpha}) \leq \alpha f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) + (1 - \alpha) f(\mathbf{x}^*)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the assumption.**
Since $f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) < f(\mathbf{x}^*)$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_{\alpha}) \leq \alpha f(\mathbf{x}^*) + (1 - \alpha) f(\mathbf{x}^*) - \alpha [ f(\mathbf{x}^*) - f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) ]
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}_{\alpha}) \leq f(\mathbf{x}^*) - \alpha \bigl( f(\mathbf{x}^*) - f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) \bigr)
$$

**Step 6 — Choose $\alpha$ sufficiently small so that $\mathbf{x}_{\alpha}$ lies in the $\delta$-neighborhood of $\mathbf{x}^*$.**
Such an $\alpha$ exists because as $\alpha \to 0$, $\Vert \mathbf{x}_{\alpha} - \mathbf{x}^* \Vert \to 0$.

**Step 7 — Contradiction.**
The right-hand side is **strictly less than** $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$ (since $f(\mathbf{x}^*) - f(\hat{\mathbf{x}}) > 0$ and $\alpha > 0$). This contradicts the local optimality of $\mathbf{x}^*$ from Step 1.

**Conclusion.** No such $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ can exist; therefore $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a global minimum. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corollary (Classification Implication).** If during the classification stage the engineer determines that $f$ is convex and $S$ is convex, *any* descent algorithm (gradient descent, Newton, SQP) is **guaranteed** to find the global optimum. This single fact is what makes convex problems a *preferred class* in industrial practice.

## 3.2 Derivation — The Lagrangian Formulation (Foundation of Constrained Classification)

For the general equality-constrained problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Augment the objective with constraint penalties using Lagrange multipliers $\lambda_k$.**

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \, h_k(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 2 — First-order necessary condition (stationarity).**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{x}} = \nabla f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k \nabla h_k(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 3 — Constraint recovery condition.**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \boldsymbol{\lambda}} = h_k(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad k = 1, \dots, p
$$

**Step 4 — Generalize to inequality constraints by adding slack variables $s_j \geq 0$:**

$$
g_j(\mathbf{x}) + s_j^2 = 0, \quad j = 1, \dots, m
$$

Substituting $s_j$ into the Lagrangian:

$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}, \boldsymbol{\mu}, \mathbf{s}) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mu_j [ g_j(\mathbf{x}) + s_j^2 ] + \sum_{k=1}^{p} \lambda_k h_k(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 5 — Stationarity with respect to $s_j$ gives:**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial s_j} = 2 \mu_j s_j = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mu_j \geq 0
$$

**Step 6 — Combining yields the KKT classification conditions stated in Section 2.3.**

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Method-to-Problem-Class Mapping

| **Problem Class** | **Objective $f$** | **Constraints** | **Variables** | **Recommended Method** | **Why this Method?** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| LP | Linear | Linear | Continuous | Simplex | Polytope vertex visiting |
| QP | Quadratic ($\mathbf{x}^T\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T\mathbf{x}$) | Linear | Continuous | Wolfe / Beale | KKT reduces to a linear system |
| Convex NLP | Convex | Convex | Continuous | KKT + SQP | KKT sufficient for globality |
| Non-Convex NLP | Non-convex | Nonlinear | Continuous | Penalty + Multi-start | Risk of multiple local minima |
| Integer LP | Linear | Linear | Integer | Branch & Bound | LP relaxation provides bounds |
| Pure IP | Linear/Nonlinear | Linear/Nonlinear | Integer | Gomory, Cutting Plane | Gomory cuts tighten the LP hull |
| Binary | Linear | Linear | $x_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Enumeration, BB | Combinatorial explosion |
| Dynamic | Any | Any | Time-indexed | Bellman's DP, Pontryagin | State recursion principle |
| Stochastic | Any | Any | Random | SP, Robust Opt., SAA | Expected value or worst-case |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Classifying a Given Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Given:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1 - 3)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2 \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \leq 4 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Classify by Linearity of Objective.**
Expanding $f$:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 - 6x_1 + 9 + x_2^2 - 4x_2 + 4
$$

The Hessian is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Since $\mathbf{H}$ is positive definite, $f$ is **strictly convex**.

**Step 2 — Classify by Linearity of Constraints.**
The constraints are linear; therefore the feasible region is a **convex polytope**.

**Step 3 — Classify by Variable Type.**
$x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ (continuous).

**Step 4 — Classify by Constraint Count.**
Two inequality constraints (one upper bound, two non-negativity). Hence $m = 3$, $p = 0$.

**Step 5 — Overall Classification.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Class: Convex Nonlinear Programming (Convex NLP) with Inequality Constraints, Continuous Variables.**

**Step 6 — Algorithm Selection.**
Since the problem is convex NLP, **KKT conditions are both necessary and sufficient** for global optimality.

**Step 7 — Solving via KKT (Quick Check).**
Setting $\nabla f + \mu_1 \nabla g_1 = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(x_1 - 3) + \mu_1 &= 0 \\
2(x_2 - 2) + \mu_1 &= 0 \\
\mu_1 (4 - x_1 - x_2) &= 0, \quad \mu_1 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting the first two: $x_1 = x_2 + 1$. If $\mu_1 = 0$, then $x_1 = 3, x_2 = 2$ violates $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$. So $\mu_1 > 0$, forcing $x_1 + x_2 = 4$ and giving $x_2 = 1.5, x_1 = 2.5$. Optimum: $f^* = 1.25$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**Q: Classify the following optimization problem and justify your classification. State the appropriate algorithm to solve it.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & x_1 + x_2 \leq 5 \\
& x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Model Answer:**

*Step 1 — Classify by Objective Linearity:*
The objective has quadratic terms ($x_1^2, x_2^2$). Hence it is **nonlinear**. Specifically, the Hessian is:

$$
\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \quad (\text{positive definite})
$$

So $f$ is **strictly convex quadratic**.

*Step 2 — Classify by Constraint Type:*
The constraints are linear inequality, forming a convex polytope.

*Step 3 — Classify by Variable Type:*
$x_1, x_2 \in \mathbb{R}$ — **continuous**.

*Step 4 — Overall Class:*
**Convex Quadratic Programming (QP) with linear inequality constraints, continuous variables.**

*Step 5 — Recommended Algorithm:*
**Wolfe's method** or **Beale's method** of QP (since the objective is convex quadratic and constraints are linear).

*(3 marks: 1 for objective classification, 1 for constraint classification, 1 for algorithm justification.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Differentiate between a convex and a non-convex optimization problem. Why is convexity important in the classification of optimization problems?**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Convex Optimization** | **Non-Convex Optimization** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Objective $f$ | $f(\alpha \mathbf{x} + (1-\alpha)\mathbf{y}) \leq \alpha f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\alpha) f(\mathbf{y})$ | Does not satisfy the above |
| Feasible Set $S$ | For any $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in S$, the segment $[\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}] \subseteq S$ | Set is not closed under line segments |
| Number of Optima | **Unique** local minimum = global minimum | **Multiple** local minima, may not be global |
| Computational Difficulty | **Polynomial-time solvable** in most cases | **NP-hard** in general |
| Algorithms Sufficient | KKT conditions suffice (necessary = sufficient) | Heuristics: GA, SA, branch-and-bound |

**Importance:** Convexity allows the engineer to **truncate algorithm selection to KKT-based or first-order methods** with the **guarantee of global optimality**, saving enormous computational effort. In non-convex problems, the engineer must deploy **global search heuristics** and accept the risk of suboptimal solutions.

*(3 marks: 1 for the table contrast, 1 for the algorithmic implication, 1 for the optimality guarantee statement.)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> In Module-3 questions worth 14 marks, examiners explicitly test whether the student (a) **names the class** of the problem *before* solving, (b) writes the **KKT/Lagrangian conditions completely** (skipping complementary slackness = 2-mark penalty), and (c) verifies **convexity via the Hessian** wherever applicable. Students who jump straight to algebraic manipulation without classification lose 2–3 marks immediately.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the following problem. Then formulate the KKT conditions and find the optimum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimize} \quad & f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 4)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2 \\
\text{Subject to} \quad & g_1: x_1 + x_2 \leq 6 \\
& g_2: x_1, x_2 \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the classification of optimization problems based on (i) the nature of constraints and (ii) the nature of the objective function. Provide one real-world engineering example for each class.

**Model Solution to (a):**

*Step 1 — Classification.*
Expanding:

$$
f = x_1^2 - 8x_1 + 16 + x_2^2 - 8x_2 + 16
$$

The Hessian is $\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, positive definite. Hence $f$ is strictly convex; the feasible region (linear constraints) is convex. **Class: Convex NLP, inequality constrained, continuous variables.**

*Step 2 — Lagrangian with KKT multipliers $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3$ (for $g_1$, $x_1 \geq 0$, $x_2 \geq 0$):*

$$
\mathcal{L} = (x_1 - 4)^2 + (x_2 - 4)^2 + \mu_1 (x_1 + x_2 - 6) - \mu_2 x_1 - \mu_3 x_2
$$

*Step 3 — Stationarity:*

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_1} &= 2(x_1 - 4) + \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0 \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x_2} &= 2(x_2 - 4) + \mu_1 - \mu_3 = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 4 — Complementary Slackness:*

$$
\mu_1(x_1 + x_2 - 6) = 0, \quad \mu_2 x_1 = 0, \quad \mu_3 x_2 = 0
$$

*Step 5 — Dual Feasibility:* $\mu_1, \mu_2, \mu_3 \geq 0$.

*Step 6 — Case Analysis:*
- **Case A:** $g_1$ inactive → $\mu_1 = 0$, unconstrained optimum is $(4, 4)$, but $4 + 4 = 8 > 6$, so infeasible. Reject.
- **Case B:** $g_1$ active → $\mu_1 > 0$, $x_1 + x_2 = 6$. With $\mu_2 = \mu_3 = 0$ (assuming $x_1, x_2 > 0$):

$$
2(x_1 - 4) + \mu_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 4 - \mu_1/2
$$
$$
2(x_2 - 4) + \mu_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 4 - \mu_1/2
$$

Substituting into $x_1 + x_2 = 6$:

$$
8 - \mu_1 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_1 = 2
$$

Therefore $x_1 = x_2 = 3$.

*Step 7 — Verification:*
$x_1, x_2 = 3 \geq 0$ ✓, $\mu_1 = 2 \geq 0$ ✓, $\mu_2 = \mu_3 = 0$ ✓.

*Step 8 — Optimum Value:*

$$
f(3, 3) = (3-4)^2 + (3-4)^2 = 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Answer:** $x_1^* = x_2^* = 3$, $f^* = 2$.

**Model Solution to (b):**

*(i) Classification by nature of constraints:*
- **Unconstrained:** No restrictions. Example: minimizing the air resistance of a free-flying projectile in vacuum.
- **Equality Constrained:** All $g_j, h_k$ are equalities. Example: minimizing mass of a structural member subject to a fixed deflection (equality of strain energy).
- **Inequality Constrained:** All $g_j$ are inequalities. Example: minimizing production cost subject to capacity ceilings.
- **Mixed Constrained:** Some equalities, some inequalities. Example: optimization of a chemical reactor with fixed stoichiometric ratios (equality) and upper-bound on temperature (inequality).

*(ii) Classification by nature of objective function:*
- **Linear:** $f = \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$. Example: minimizing total transportation cost in a supply chain.
- **Quadratic:** $f = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{x} + \mathbf{c}^T \mathbf{x}$. Example: portfolio risk minimization in finance.
- **Nonlinear (general):** Polynomial, transcendental, etc. Example: minimizing aerodynamic drag of an airfoil (drag $\propto$ shape parameters in a nonlinear manner).
- **Geometric:** Posynomial/ynomial form. Example: optimizing amplifier gain in electronic circuit design.
- **Multi-modal:** Has multiple local optima. Example: protein folding energy minimization.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for constraint classification, 2 for objective classification, 3 for engineering examples.)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]

**(a) [7 Marks]** A manufacturing process yields profit (in thousand rupees) given by $P(x) = 5x - 0.5 x^2$, where $x$ is the production volume. Formulate this as an optimization problem, classify it, and find the optimal production volume.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a clear taxonomy, classify optimization problems based on the **number of decision variables, nature of variables, and convexity**. Discuss the algorithmic implications of each class.

**Model Solution to (a):**

*Step 1 — Formulation:*
The unconstrained problem is:

$$
\max_{x \geq 0} \quad P(x) = 5x - 0.5 x^2
$$

*Step 2 — Conversion to Standard Form:*
Maximizing $P$ is equivalent to minimizing $f(x) = -5x + 0.5 x^2$.

*Step 3 — Classification:*
- **Linearity of $f$:** Contains $x^2$ term → **nonlinear** (specifically, **univariate quadratic**).
- **Convexity:** $f''(x) = 1 > 0$, so $f$ is **strictly convex**, hence the max of $-f$ is **uniquely concave**.
- **Constraints:** $x \geq 0$ → single inequality constraint, no equality.
- **Variables:** $x \in \mathbb{R}$ → **continuous**.

**Class:** *Convex Univariate Optimization with One Inequality Constraint (Continuous Variable).*

*Step 4 — Analytical Solution (using $P'(x) = 0$):*

$$
P'(x) = 5 - x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^* = 5
$$

*Step 5 — Second-Order Check:*

$$
P''(x) = -1 < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Maximum confirmed.}
$$

*Step 6 — Optimum Profit:*

$$
P(5) = 5(5) - 0.5(25) = 25 - 12.5 = 12.5 \text{ thousand rupees}
$$

*Step 7 — KKT Verification (with $\mu$ for $-x \leq 0$):*
Stationarity: $-5 + x - \mu = 0$. With $\mu = 0$ (since $x^* = 5 > 0$): $x = 5$ ✓. Complementary slackness: $\mu \cdot x = 0$ ✓.

**Answer:** Optimal production $x^* = 5$ units, $P^* = 12.5$ thousand rupees.

**Model Solution to (b):**

| **Axis** | **Sub-Class** | **Mathematical Trait** | **Algorithmic Implication** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Number of variables | Univariate ($n=1$) | Single derivative equation | Direct root-finding / closed-form |
| Number of variables | Multivariate ($n \geq 2$) | Gradient $\nabla f = \mathbf{0}$ | Iterative descent (Newton, Quasi-Newton) |
| Nature of variables | Continuous | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ | All classical calculus-based methods |
| Nature of variables | Integer / Discrete | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{Z}^n$ | Combinatorial / Branch & Bound |
| Nature of variables | Binary | $\mathbf{x}_i \in \{0,1\}$ | Enumeration / Implicit Enumeration |
| Nature of variables | Mixed | Continuous + Integer | MINLP solvers (GAMS, BARON, Gurobi) |
| Convexity | Convex | $\mathbf{H} \succeq 0$ globally | KKT sufficient; local = global |
| Convexity | Concave | $\mathbf{H} \preceq 0$ globally | Convert to convex via $-f$ |
| Convexity | Non-convex | $\mathbf{H}$ indefinite | Multi-start, GA, SA, Tunneling |
| Convexity | Quasi-convex | Sub-level sets convex | Sub-gradient methods |

**Algorithmic Implications Summary:**
- *Univariate convex problems* can be solved by direct differentiation.
- *Multivariate convex* problems → KKT conditions are necessary and sufficient; gradient methods converge to global optimum.
- *Integer* problems are inherently combinatorial; LP relaxation with Branch & Bound is the workhorse.
- *Non-convex* problems require **global optimization** methods since local optima are not unique.

*(Marks distribution: 2 for taxonomy table, 2 for variable-class discussion, 2 for convexity discussion, 1 for engineering implication.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Classification (Module 3)**

- [x] **Classification is a prerequisite to algorithm selection** — always classify *before* solving.
- [x] The **eight classification axes** are: constraints, function nature, variable type, objective count, convexity, differentiability, determinism, and solution environment.
- [x] **Hessian matrix test** $\mathbf{H} = \nabla^2 f$ is the *primary* tool for convexity classification.
- [x] A strictly positive-definite Hessian $\Rightarrow$ strict convexity $\Rightarrow$ **unique** global minimum.
- [x] **KKT conditions** unify Lagrange multipliers (equality) and complementary slackness (inequality) — write all 5 components in exam.
- [x] **LP** $\rightarrow$ Simplex; **QP** $\rightarrow$ Wolfe/Beale; **Convex NLP** $\rightarrow$ KKT/SQP; **IP** $\rightarrow$ Branch & Bound; **Dynamic** $\rightarrow$ Bellman DP.
- [x] **Convex programming theorem:** *local optimum = global optimum* (proof by contradiction via $\alpha$-convex combination).
- [x] The Lagrangian is the **master unifying object**: $\mathcal{L} = f + \sum \mu_j g_j + \sum \lambda_k h_k$.
- [x] **Complementary slackness:** $\mu_j g_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ — if $\mu_j > 0$ then $g_j$ is **active**; if $g_j < 0$ (strict) then $\mu_j = 0$.
- [x] **Dual feasibility:** $\mu_j \geq 0$ for *all* inequality constraints (this is a frequent marking trap).
- [x] In KTU valuation, an unmentioned **classification statement** costs **2–3 marks** even if the rest of the solution is correct.
- [x] **MINLP** = Mixed-Integer Nonlinear Programming — the most general class; covers ~70% of industrial problems.
- [x] **Geometric Programming** is a class where the objective and constraints are *posynomials*; transformed to convex form via log-change of variables.
- [x] **Stochastic programming** classification requires distinguishing between *expected value*, *chance-constrained*, and *robust* formulations.
- [x] **Dynamic programming** classification requires identifying a *sequential decision structure* and verifying **Bellman's Principle of Optimality** holds.
- [x] For 14-mark KTU Module-3 questions, allocate **3 marks to classification** + **3 marks to KKT setup** + **8 marks to solution & verification**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mnemonic — "C-F-V-O-C-D-D-S":**
> **C**onstraints → **F**unction nature → **V**ariables → **O**bjective count → **C**onvexity → **D**ifferentiability → **D**eterminism → **S**olution environment. Use this 8-letter code in your exam introduction to instantly score the classification marks!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Approach of Penalty Function Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Approach of Penalty Function Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Penalty Function Method (PFM)** is a classical technique used in constrained non-linear programming to transform a constrained optimization problem into a sequence of unconstrained optimization problems. This is achieved by augmenting the original objective function with a **penalty term** that assigns a high cost (penalty) to any solution that violates the constraints. As the penalty parameter is progressively increased (in the exterior method) or decreased (in the interior method), the sequence of unconstrained minima converges to the constrained optimum of the original problem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement:** The Penalty Function Method is classified under *Transformation Methods of Constrained Optimization*. It is the foundation for advanced techniques such as the *Augmented Lagrangian Method* and *Sequential Quadratic Programming (SQP)*.

The general mathematical statement of the method is:

**Given a constrained non-linear program (NLP):**

$$
\min \; f(\mathbf{x})
$$

$$
\text{subject to} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m
$$

$$
h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p
$$

The penalty function method constructs an augmented objective function $\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$ where $r_k$ is the penalty parameter, such that the unconstrained minimizer of $\phi$ converges to the constrained minimizer of $f$ as $r_k \to \infty$ (exterior) or $r_k \to 0$ (interior).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Fenced Garden" Problem:**
> Imagine you are trying to find the lowest point in a hilly landscape (the objective function $f(\mathbf{x})$), but you are restricted to stay **inside a fenced boundary** (the feasible region defined by constraints). Two strategies exist:
>
> 1. **Exterior (Fence-Pushing) Strategy:** Walk freely anywhere on the terrain. Every time you step *outside* the fence, the ground becomes agonizingly hot — the *hotter* it gets the further you stray. You naturally settle near the boundary because that is where the "heat relief" and the "gravity" of the valley best balance out. As the heat becomes more intense, you press right against the fence.
>
> 2. **Interior (Wall-Leaning) Strategy:** You are *blindfolded* and pushed *into* the fenced region. As you approach the fence, the wall itself repels you back with increasing force (like a soft cushion getting stiffer). You settle at a point well inside the fence where the "valley's pull" balances the "wall's repulsion." As the cushion gets softer (penalty decreases), you can lean closer and closer to the actual constrained minimum.

The **fence** is the constraint. The **heat / repulsion** is the penalty function. The **penalty parameter $r_k$** controls the intensity.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Box

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know Terms for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Penalty Parameter ($r_k$ or $\mu_k$):** A scalar multiplier that governs the severity of the penalty.
> - **Penalty Function ($\phi$ or $P$):** The augmented objective $f(\mathbf{x}) + \text{penalty term}$.
> - **Feasible Region ($\mathcal{F}$):** The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying all constraints.
> - **Infeasible Region ($\mathcal{R}^n \setminus \mathcal{F}$):** The complement of the feasible region.
> - **Sequential Unconstrained Minimization Technique (SUMT):** The classical umbrella term for penalty and barrier methods.
> - **Ill-Conditioning:** Numerical difficulty that arises as $r_k \to \infty$ in exterior methods.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Principal Variants

The penalty function method broadly splits into two categories, and the **KTU 2024 syllabus** explicitly demands familiarity with both:

#### A. Exterior Penalty Function Method (Infeasible Approach)
- Starts from an **infeasible** point (may violate constraints).
- Penalty is applied **only when constraints are violated**.
- Solution trajectory lies **outside the feasible region** until convergence.
- Uses a **quadratic penalty** for inequality constraints.
- $r_k \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.

#### B. Interior Penalty Function Method (Barrier Method / Feasible Approach)
- Must start from a **strictly feasible interior point**.
- Penalty is applied **only when approaching the constraint boundary from inside**.
- Solution trajectory **stays strictly inside** the feasible region.
- Uses a **logarithmic** or **inverse (reciprocal) barrier** function.
- $r_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**For the Exterior Penalty Method:**

1. **Formulation Step:** Define the penalty function $\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$.
2. **Initialization Step:** Choose an initial point $\mathbf{x}_0$ (can be infeasible), initial penalty parameter $r_1 > 0$ (typically $r_1 = 1$), and a scaling factor $c > 1$ (typically $c = 5$ or $c = 10$).
3. **Unconstrained Minimization Step:** Solve $\min_{\mathbf{x}} \phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$ using any unconstrained optimizer (e.g., Newton's method, steepest descent, conjugate gradient).
4. **Convergence Check:** If $\|\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{k-1}\| < \varepsilon_1$ AND $|r_k \cdot \max(0, g_i(\mathbf{x}_k))| < \varepsilon_2$, stop.
5. **Update Step:** $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k$ and set $\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k^*$ (current minimizer).
6. **Loop:** Return to Step 3.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential** for KTU PERUT867.

| **Component** | **Exterior Method** | **Interior (Barrier) Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Inequality Penalty $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$ | $\frac{1}{2} r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\max(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x}))\right]^2$ | $-r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln\!\left(-g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)$ |
| Equality Penalty $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ | $\frac{1}{2} r_k \sum_{j=1}^{p} \left[h_j(\mathbf{x})\right]^2$ | Not applicable (only inequality) |
| Augmented Objective $\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$ | $f(\mathbf{x}) + \text{quadratic penalty}$ | $f(\mathbf{x}) - r_k \sum \ln(-g_i)$ |
| Penalty Parameter Update | $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k, \; c > 1$ | $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k, \; 0 < c < 1$ |
| Limit Behavior | $r_k \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$ | $r_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ |
| Starting Point | Any $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \mathbb{R}^n$ (may be infeasible) | Strictly feasible interior $\mathbf{x}_0 \in \text{int}(\mathcal{F})$ |
| Convergence Target | $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min\{f(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}\}$ | $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\min\{f(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{F}\}$ |
| Failure Mode | Ill-conditioning near optimum | Cannot handle equality constraints directly |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Structural Optimization:** Optimizing truss weight subject to stress and displacement constraints.
- **Process Control in Chemical Engineering:** Reactor design with safety and yield constraints.
- **Aerospace Trajectory Planning:** Minimum-fuel paths subject to obstacle-avoidance constraints.
- **Machine Learning (Support Vector Machines):** Hinge-loss penalty is a direct descendant.
- **Robust Design:** Handling probabilistic constraints in mechanical system synthesis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Exterior Quadratic Penalty Function

We begin with the constrained problem:

$$
\min \; f(\mathbf{x}) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; i = 1, \dots, m
$$

**Step 1:** Define an indicator-like penalty that activates only for constraint violation.

For a single inequality $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0$, define the violation function:

$$
v_i(\mathbf{x}) = \max\!\left(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Construct a non-negative, monotonically increasing penalty for any positive violation.

We use the **squared** form so the function is continuously differentiable (this is critical for gradient-based solvers):

$$
P_i(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = \frac{1}{2}\, r_k \left[v_i(\mathbf{x})\right]^2 = \frac{1}{2}\, r_k \left[\max\!\left(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)\right]^2
$$

**Step 3:** Sum penalties over all $m$ constraints and add to the objective.

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{2}\, r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\max\!\left(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)\right]^2
$$

**Step 4 (Equality constraints):** For $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$, the violation is $|h_j(\mathbf{x})|$. The squared form gives:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{2}\, r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\max\!\left(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)\right]^2 + \frac{1}{2}\, r_k \sum_{j=1}^{p} \left[h_j(\mathbf{x})\right]^2
$$

**Step 5:** Show convergence. Let $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k)$ denote the unconstrained minimizer. Then we claim:

$$
\lim_{r_k \to \infty} \mathbf{x}^*(r_k) = \mathbf{x}^{*}
$$

*Justification sketch:* Suppose $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k)$ is feasible. Then the penalty term is zero, and $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k)$ is an unconstrained local minimum of $f$. If it were *not* the constrained optimum, the penalty term would increase (or $f$ would be higher) at the constrained optimum, contradiction. As $r_k \to \infty$, any non-zero violation makes $\phi$ infinite, forcing feasibility.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Interior Logarithmic Barrier Function

For inequality constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}) < 0$ (strict interior), define:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) - r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln\!\left(-g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)
$$

**Step 1:** Inside the feasible region, $-g_i(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, so the logarithm is well-defined.

**Step 2:** As $\mathbf{x}$ approaches the boundary where $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \to 0^-$, the term $-g_i \to 0^+$, so $\ln(-g_i) \to -\infty$, and $-r_k \ln(-g_i) \to +\infty$. This creates a repulsive "barrier" that prevents escape from the feasible interior.

**Step 3:** As $r_k \to 0^+$, the barrier's strength vanishes, and the minimizer of $\phi$ approaches the true constrained optimum from the interior.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x - 2)^2$ subject to $g(x) = x - 4 \le 0$.

**Without constraints:** The unconstrained minimum is $x = 2$ (already feasible since $2 \le 4$).

**With exterior penalty function:**

$$
\phi(x, r_k) = (x - 2)^2 + \frac{1}{2} r_k \left[\max(0, x - 4)\right]^2
$$

**Case 1: Infeasible region ($x > 4$):** $\phi = (x-2)^2 + \frac{1}{2} r_k (x-4)^2$.

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dx} = 2(x-2) + r_k (x-4) = 0
$$

$$
2x - 4 + r_k x - 4 r_k = 0
$$

$$
x (2 + r_k) = 4 + 4 r_k
$$

$$
x^*(r_k) = \frac{4(1 + r_k)}{2 + r_k} = 4 - \frac{4}{2 + r_k}
$$

**Limit as $r_k \to \infty$:**

$$
\lim_{r_k \to \infty} x^*(r_k) = \lim_{r_k \to \infty} \left( 4 - \frac{4}{2 + r_k} \right) = 4 - 0 = 4
$$

This is precisely the constrained boundary point — confirming convergence.

**Case 2: Feasible region ($x \le 4$):** Penalty is zero, $\phi = (x-2)^2$, minimizer at $x = 2$. For $r_k \ge 0$, the unconstrained optimum of the case-1 expression satisfies $x^*(r_k) = 4 - \frac{4}{2+r_k} < 4$ for all finite $r_k > 0$, confirming the trajectory stays just outside the boundary.

### 3.4 Convergence and Ill-Conditioning Analysis

As $r_k \to \infty$ in the exterior method, the **Hessian** of $\phi$ grows unbounded:

$$
\nabla^2 \phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{2} r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla^2 \left[\max(0, g_i)\right]^2 + \frac{1}{2} r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \nabla g_i \nabla g_i^T
$$

The condition number of $\nabla^2 \phi$ grows linearly with $r_k$, causing severe numerical ill-conditioning in Newton's method. This is the **fundamental weakness** of the exterior method and the motivation for the **Augmented Lagrangian Method**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Penalty Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Define Constrained NLP Problem"] --> B["Choose Method Type Exterior or Interior"]
    B --> C["Initialize Penalty Parameter r1 and Scaling Factor c"]
    C --> D["Select Initial Point x0 Feasible for Interior, Any for Exterior"]
    D --> E["Formulate Augmented Objective phi(x, rk)"]
    E --> F["Solve Unconstrained Minimization min phi(x, rk)"]
    F --> G["Obtain Candidate Solution x star k"]
    G --> H{"Check Convergence: Norm Change and Constraint Violation"}
    H -- "Not Converged" --> I["Update Penalty Parameter rk plus 1 = c times rk"]
    I --> F
    H -- "Converged" --> J["Report Solution x star as Constrained Optimum"]
    J --> K["End: Stop Algorithm"]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: Exterior vs. Interior Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Constrained Optimization Problem"] --> B{"Initial Point Feasible?"}
    B -- "No, Infeasible OK" --> C["Exterior Penalty Path"]
    B -- "Yes, Must be Strict Interior" --> D["Interior Barrier Path"]
    C --> E["Quadratic Penalty on Violations"]
    D --> F["Logarithmic Barrier on Boundary"]
    E --> G["Increase rk: rk+1 = c rk, c greater than 1"]
    F --> H["Decrease rk: rk+1 = c rk, 0 less than c less than 1"]
    G --> I["Converges to Boundary from Outside"]
    H --> J["Converges to Optimum from Inside"]
    I --> K["Constrained Optimum x star"]
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Penalty Function Geometry Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Original Objective f(x)"] --> B["Add Penalty Term P(x, rk)"]
    B --> C["Construct Augmented phi(x, rk)"]
    C --> D{"Solution Status?"}
    D -- "Feasible: gx less than or equal to 0" --> E["Penalty = 0, phi equals f"]
    D -- "Infeasible: gx greater than 0" --> F["Penalty greater than 0, phi greater than f"]
    E --> G["Unconstrained Minimizer lies in Feasible Region"]
    F --> H["Large rk forces minimizer toward Feasible Boundary"]
    G --> I["Final Converged Solution x star"]
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Algorithmic State Machine (Iteration Cycle)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialization
    Initialization --> Minimization: Set rk, x0
    Minimization --> ConvergenceCheck: Find x star k
    ConvergenceCheck --> Update: Not Converged
    ConvergenceCheck --> [*]: Converged
    Update --> Minimization: rk plus 1 equals c rk
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
*State the basic concept of the penalty function method in constrained optimization. Differentiate between the exterior penalty function method and the interior barrier function method.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The penalty function method transforms a constrained optimization problem into a sequence of unconstrained problems by adding a penalty term to the objective function. The penalty is non-zero only when constraints are violated, thereby discouraging infeasible solutions.

**Differences (2 of the following 4 needed for full marks):**

- **Starting point:** Exterior allows any point (even infeasible); Interior requires a strictly feasible interior point.
- **Trajectory:** Exterior solutions approach the optimum from outside the feasible region; Interior solutions approach from inside.
- **Penalty parameter update:** Exterior increases $r_k$ ($c > 1$); Interior decreases $r_k$ ($0 < c < 1$).
- **Constraint handling:** Exterior handles both equality and inequality constraints naturally; Interior handles only inequality constraints.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3]**
*Write the mathematical form of the exterior quadratic penalty function for a problem with both inequality and equality constraints.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For the problem $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ subject to $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \; i=1,\dots,m$ and $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \; j=1,\dots,p$, the exterior quadratic penalty function is:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + \frac{1}{2} r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[\max\!\left(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x})\right)\right]^2 + \frac{1}{2} r_k \sum_{j=1}^{p} \left[h_j(\mathbf{x})\right]^2
$$

where $r_k > 0$ is the penalty parameter that is progressively increased ($r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k$ with $c > 1$) to force the unconstrained minimizer toward the feasible region.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — Full Solution

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified Model]**

> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> Solve the following constrained optimization problem using the **exterior penalty function method** with two iterations. Use $r_1 = 1$ and $c = 10$.
>
> $$
> \min \; f(x) = x^2 + 1
> $$
>
> $$
> \text{subject to} \quad g(x) = x - 3 \le 0
> $$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Formulate and Solve the First Iteration

**Formulation:** The exterior quadratic penalty function is:

$$
\phi(x, r_k) = x^2 + 1 + \frac{1}{2} r_k \left[\max(0, x - 3)\right]^2
$$

**Iteration 1 ($r_1 = 1$):**

**Case 1 (Feasible, $x \le 3$):** $\phi = x^2 + 1$. Minimum at $x = 0$. This is feasible. So $x_1^* = 0$.

**Case 2 (Infeasible, $x > 3$):** $\phi = x^2 + 1 + \frac{1}{2}(x-3)^2$. Taking derivative:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dx} = 2x + (x - 3) = 3x - 3 = 0
$$

$$
x = 1
$$

But $x = 1$ violates the assumption $x > 3$. So no stationary point in the infeasible region. The minimum of Case 2 in the region $x > 3$ is at the boundary $x = 3$, giving $\phi = 10 + 0 = 10$.

**Comparison:** $\phi(0, 1) = 1$ vs. $\phi(3, 1) = 10$. Minimum is at $x_1^* = 0$ with $\phi = 1$.

> **Answer (a):** $x_1^* = 0, \phi(x_1^*, 1) = 1$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Perform Second Iteration and Comment on Convergence

**Iteration 2 ($r_2 = c \cdot r_1 = 10$):**

**Case 1 (Feasible, $x \le 3$):** $\phi = x^2 + 1$. Minimum still at $x = 0$, feasible. $\phi(0, 10) = 1$.

**Case 2 (Infeasible, $x > 3$):** $\phi = x^2 + 1 + 5(x-3)^2$. Derivative:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{dx} = 2x + 10(x-3) = 12x - 30 = 0
$$

$$
x_2^* = \frac{30}{12} = 2.5
$$

But $2.5 < 3$ contradicts $x > 3$. So again no infeasible stationary point; the boundary point $x = 3$ gives $\phi(3, 10) = 10 + 0 = 10$.

**Comparison:** $\phi(0, 10) = 1$ vs. $\phi(3, 10) = 10$. Minimum at $x_2^* = 0$, $\phi = 1$.

> **Answer (b):** $x_2^* = 0$ for both iterations; the algorithm converges immediately because the unconstrained minimum $x = 0$ is already feasible, so the penalty is never activated. The constrained optimum is $\mathbf{x^* = 0}$ with $f(x^*) = 1$.

**Comment on Convergence:** In this problem, the unconstrained minimum happens to lie inside the feasible region. Hence, the penalty function method identifies the optimum in a single iteration regardless of $r_k$. This illustrates that the penalty method is most useful when the unconstrained and constrained optima differ.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified Model]**

> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = (x_1 - 1)^2 + (x_2 - 2)^2$ subject to $x_1 + x_2 \le 4$, using the **exterior penalty function method** with $r_1 = 1, c = 5$. Perform **two iterations**.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: First Iteration

**Formulation:** $\phi = (x_1-1)^2 + (x_2-2)^2 + \frac{1}{2} r_k \left[\max(0, x_1+x_2-4)\right]^2$.

**Iteration 1 ($r_1 = 1$):**

**Feasible region ($x_1 + x_2 \le 4$):** $\phi = (x_1-1)^2 + (x_2-2)^2$. Setting partial derivatives to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_1} = 2(x_1 - 1) = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x_2} = 2(x_2 - 2) = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 2
$$

Check feasibility: $1 + 2 = 3 \le 4$. ✓ Feasible.

**Answer (a):** $x_1^{(1)} = 1, \; x_2^{(1)} = 2, \; \phi = 0$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Second Iteration and Convergence Comment

**Iteration 2 ($r_2 = 5$):** Same feasible region gives same optimum $(1, 2)$ with $\phi = 0$.

**Convergence comment:** The unconstrained minimizer $(1, 2)$ lies strictly inside the feasible region ($1 + 2 = 3 < 4$). Thus, the penalty term is never activated, and both iterations yield the same result. The constrained optimum is $\mathbf{x^* = (1, 2)^T}$ with $f(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\max(0, g_i)$ term in the exterior penalty:** Many students write $r_k \sum g_i^2$ directly, which incorrectly penalizes feasible points where $g_i < 0$. Always wrap with $\max(0, g_i)$ or $[g_i^+]^2$.
> 2. **Wrong penalty direction:** Exterior methods **increase** $r_k$ ($c > 1$); Interior methods **decrease** $r_k$ ($0 < c < 1$). Mixing these up is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Ignoring the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor:** Some textbooks omit the $\frac{1}{2}$, leading to different numerical answers. KTU follows the convention **WITH** the $\frac{1}{2}$.
> 4. **Confusing barrier with penalty terminology:** "Barrier" applies to interior methods only; "Penalty" typically refers to exterior methods. Examiners expect precise terminology.
> 5. **Not checking feasibility of the unconstrained minimum:** When the unconstrained minimum is feasible, the algorithm converges in one shot — students often incorrectly iterate further.
> 6. **Ignoring equality constraints in the interior method:** The interior barrier method **cannot handle equality constraints directly**; they must be eliminated or handled separately.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Penalty Function Method (Module 3):**
> - PFM converts **constrained → unconstrained** by adding a penalty term.
> - **Exterior Method:** Quadratic penalty, $r_k \to \infty$, $r_{k+1} = c r_k, c > 1$, infeasible start allowed.
> - **Interior Method (Barrier):** Logarithmic barrier $\phi = f - r_k \sum \ln(-g_i)$, $r_k \to 0$, $r_{k+1} = c r_k, 0 < c < 1$, strictly feasible start required.
> - **Equality constraints** are handled in the exterior method as $\frac{1}{2} r_k \sum h_j^2$.
> - The penalty parameter is updated **geometrically** with scaling factor $c$ (typical: $c = 5$ or $c = 10$).
> - **Convergence criterion:** $\|\mathbf{x}_k - \mathbf{x}_{k-1}\| < \varepsilon_1$ AND $r_k \cdot \text{constraint violation} < \varepsilon_2$.
> - **Main weakness of exterior method:** Ill-conditioning of the Hessian as $r_k \to \infty$.
> - **Main weakness of interior method:** Cannot handle equality constraints; requires strictly feasible starting point.
> - **Augmented Lagrangian Method** combines the best of both: penalty + explicit Lagrange multipliers, avoiding ill-conditioning.
> - The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes PFM under "**Transformation Methods**" alongside the **Method of Multipliers** and **Sequential Unconstrained Minimization Technique (SUMT)**.
> - **Board-exam tip:** Always write the **general form** of the penalty function first, then substitute specific problem data — examiners award method marks generously.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Approaches of Interior and Exterior Penalty Function Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Basic Approaches of Interior and Exterior Penalty Function Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Constrained Optimization — The Core Problem

In real engineering design problems, almost every decision variable is bounded by physical, economic, or safety limits. The general **Non-Linear Programming (NLP)** constrained problem is formally stated as:

$$\min \; f(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

$$h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

$$x_l \leq x_k \leq x_u$$

Where:
- **$f(\mathbf{x})$** is the **objective function** (e.g., cost, weight, energy).
- **$g_i(\mathbf{x})$** are the **inequality constraints**.
- **$h_j(\mathbf{x})$** are the **equality constraints**.
- **$\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)^T$** is the vector of design variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Penalty function methods belong to the family of **"Sequential Unconstrained Minimization Techniques (SUMT)"**. They convert a *constrained* problem into a series of *unconstrained* problems — a brilliant workaround because unconstrained algorithms (like steepest descent, Newton, conjugate gradient) are mathematically far simpler to implement.

---

## 1.2 What is a Penalty Function Method?

A **Penalty Function Method** is an algorithm that solves a constrained optimization problem by:
1. Embedding the constraints directly into the objective function.
2. Adding a **penalty term** that becomes large when the design point violates a constraint.
3. Solving a sequence of *unconstrained* sub-problems whose solutions converge to the original constrained optimum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Wording):**
> *"A Penalty Function Method is a class of SUMT in which a constrained optimization problem is reformulated as an unconstrained problem by augmenting the objective function with a penalty term that assigns a high cost to constraint violation, thereby forcing the minimizer to remain within (or approach) the feasible region."*

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🏛️

Imagine you are **parking a car in a narrow garage** (the *feasible region*):

- The **objective** is to park the car as close to the wall as possible (minimize distance to a target).
- The **constraints** are the side walls, front wall, and rear wall.
- A **mathematical unconstrained algorithm** (like a freely roaming robot) would happily drive *through* the wall — it has no concept of "wall."

**Penalty Function Methods act like an invisible bumper system:**
- The car is free to move *anywhere*.
- Every time the bumper touches a wall, a strong repulsive **force** pushes the car back.
- As the bumper gets stronger with each iteration, the car settles into the tightest possible spot — the **constrained optimum**.

| Method | "Bumper" Behaviour | Starting Position | Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interior** (Barrier) | Soft repulsive wall (gentle) | Strictly **inside** the garage | Touches wall at the limit |
| **Exterior** | Hard spring wall (stiff) | May start **outside** the garage | Forced to slide inside |

---

## 1.4 Two Fundamental Families

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two Basic Approaches (Module 3 — KTU 2024 Scheme):**

### A. Interior Penalty Function Method (Barrier Method)
- The penalty term is constructed so that the search point **must start strictly inside** the feasible region.
- The penalty **diverges to infinity** as the constraint boundary is approached from inside.
- The penalty parameter **$r_k \to 0$** (penalty weakens) as the iteration number $k \to \infty$.
- It **cannot handle equality constraints** in its pure form.
- Final solution always **lies on the boundary** of the feasible region.

### B. Exterior Penalty Function Method
- The search point may start **inside OR outside** the feasible region.
- The penalty term is **zero inside** the feasible region and grows quadratically (or otherwise) as the constraint is violated.
- The penalty parameter **$r_k \to \infty$** (penalty strengthens) as $k \to \infty$.
- It **can handle both equality and inequality constraints**.
- Final solution **approaches the boundary from outside**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Conceptual Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> - *Interior* = penalty **weakens** over iterations ($r_k \downarrow 0$).
> - *Exterior* = penalty **strengthens** over iterations ($r_k \uparrow \infty$).
> - *Interior* = search stays **inside** feasible region.
> - *Exterior* = search converges **towards** feasible region from outside.

---

## 1.5 Role in Engineering Design

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| **Structural Optimization** | Minimize weight subject to stress/deflection limits |
| **Mechanical Design** | Optimize gear sizes under geometric constraints |
| **Process Engineering** | Maximize yield subject to purity, temperature bounds |
| **Electrical/Electronics** | Filter design with frequency-domain constraints |
| **Aerospace** | Trajectory optimization under path/control bounds |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Interior Penalty Function Method — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Concept

For inequality constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$, the feasible region is $\{\mathbf{x} \mid g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0\}$. As we approach the **boundary** $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = 0$ from inside (i.e., as $g_i \to 0^-$), the penalty function must shoot up to $+\infty$, creating an impassable **barrier**.

### 2.1.2 Construction of the Augmented Function

The general interior penalty (barrier) function is:

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \cdot B(\mathbf{x})$$

Where:
- $B(\mathbf{x})$ = the **barrier function** (diverges as we approach constraint boundary).
- $r_k > 0$ = the **barrier (penalty) parameter**, a monotonically decreasing sequence.
- As $k \to \infty$: $r_k \to 0$, and $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k) \to \mathbf{x}^*$ (the true constrained optimum).

### 2.1.3 Two Common Barrier Functions

#### (a) Inverse Barrier Function (Classical — Courant / Fiacco)

$$B(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{-g_i(\mathbf{x})}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{1}{-g_i(\mathbf{x})}\;}$$

#### (b) Logarithmic Barrier Function (Modern — Fiacco-McCormick)

$$B(\mathbf{x}) = - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln\bigl(-g_i(\mathbf{x})\bigr)$$

$$\boxed{\;\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) - r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln\bigl(-g_i(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logarithmic barrier is preferred in modern KTU numerical problems** because it has a **smoother gradient** and faster convergence near the optimum. The inverse barrier is the classical form.

### 2.1.4 Algorithm Logic — Interior Method

1. Choose a strictly feasible starting point $\mathbf{x}_0$ (must satisfy $g_i < 0$).
2. Set initial barrier parameter $r_1 > 0$ (e.g., $r_1 = 1$ or $r_1 = 10$).
3. Set reduction factor $c \in (0, 1)$ (typically $c = 0.1$ or $c = 0.5$).
4. **Repeat** for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ until convergence:
    - Solve $\min \Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$ using any unconstrained algorithm → solution $\mathbf{x}_k^*$.
    - Update $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k$.
    - Use $\mathbf{x}_k^*$ as the warm-start for the next iteration.
5. **Stopping Criteria**:
    - $r_{k+1} \leq \epsilon_1$ (penalty is small enough)
    - OR $\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1}^* - \mathbf{x}_k^*\| \leq \epsilon_2$ (successive iterates are close)
    - OR $|\Phi(\mathbf{x}_{k+1}, r_{k+1}) - \Phi(\mathbf{x}_k, r_k)| \leq \epsilon_3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the reduction factor?**
> A small $r_1$ keeps the contour "smooth" but converges slowly. A large $r_1$ makes the contours very steep near the boundary, making unconstrained minimization hard. Starting large and geometrically reducing $r_k$ balances both.

---

## 2.2 Exterior Penalty Function Method — Theoretical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Concept

Here, the penalty is **zero** in the feasible region and **grows** outside it. The starting point may be infeasible, and the penalty gradually forces it into the feasible set.

### 2.2.2 Construction of the Augmented Function

The general exterior penalty function:

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \cdot P(\mathbf{x})$$

Where:
- $P(\mathbf{x})$ is the **exterior penalty term**.
- $r_k \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$.
- Penalty is **active** only when constraints are violated.

### 2.2.3 Common Exterior Penalty Functions

#### (a) Quadratic Penalty (Most Common)

For **inequality** constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \bigl[\max(0,\; g_i(\mathbf{x}))\bigr]^2\;}$$

For **equality** constraints $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$:

$$\boxed{\;\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \sum_{j=1}^{p} \bigl[h_j(\mathbf{x})\bigr]^2\;}$$

The $\max(0, g_i)$ operator is called a **"hinge"** or **"clamp"** function — it only penalizes positive (violated) values.

#### (b) Linear / Absolute Penalty

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k \sum_{j=1}^{p} \vert h_j(\mathbf{x}) \vert$$

Less smooth; used rarely in textbook problems.

### 2.2.4 Algorithm Logic — Exterior Method

1. Choose any starting point $\mathbf{x}_0$ (feasibility NOT required).
2. Set initial penalty $r_1 > 0$ (e.g., $r_1 = 1$).
3. Set amplification factor $c > 1$ (typically $c = 5$ or $c = 10$).
4. **Repeat** for $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ until convergence:
    - Solve $\min \Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k)$ → solution $\mathbf{x}_k^*$.
    - Update $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k$.
    - Use $\mathbf{x}_k^*$ as warm-start.
5. **Stopping**: same criteria as interior, OR penalty $r_k \geq$ large threshold with no significant change.

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Pitfall (KTU Examiner's trap):**
> In the exterior method, $r_k \to \infty$. For very large $r_k$, the Hessian matrix of $\Phi$ becomes **ill-conditioned** (very large condition number), causing numerical instability. This is a classic source of marks lost in KTU board exams.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Method |
|---|---|---|
| $\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) + r_k B(\mathbf{x})$ | General penalty form | Both |
| $B(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \dfrac{1}{-g_i(\mathbf{x})}$ | Inverse barrier | Interior |
| $B(\mathbf{x}) = -\sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln\bigl(-g_i(\mathbf{x})\bigr)$ | Log barrier | Interior |
| $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k, \; c \in (0,1)$ | Penalty reduction | Interior |
| $P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \bigl[\max(0, g_i)\bigr]^2$ | Quadratic (inequality) | Exterior |
| $P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \bigl[h_j(\mathbf{x})\bigr]^2$ | Quadratic (equality) | Exterior |
| $r_{k+1} = c \cdot r_k, \; c > 1$ | Penalty amplification | Exterior |
| $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k) \to \mathbf{x}^*$ as $r_k \to 0$ | Convergence (interior) | Interior |
| $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k) \to \mathbf{x}^*$ as $r_k \to \infty$ | Convergence (exterior) | Exterior |
| $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$ | Feasible region definition | Both |
| $\mathbf{x}_0$ strictly feasible | Starting requirement | Interior only |
| $r_k$ monotonic scalar sequence | Penalty parameter | Both |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Design**: Penalty function converts stress constraints ($σ \leq σ_{allow}$) into an equivalent cost in weight minimization.
- **Process Optimization**: Pollution, temperature, pressure bounds handled as penalty walls.
- **Machine Learning**: The **log-barrier** is the foundation of *interior-point methods* for Linear/Quadratic Programming (e.g., SVM, LASSO regression).
- **Trajectory Planning**: Path constraints in aerospace (altitude, fuel) are elegantly enforced by barrier terms.
- **Robotics**: Joint limits and collision-avoidance are barrier-penalized in real-time motion planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Interior Penalty (Log Barrier)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-Type):**
> Minimize $f(x) = (x - 2)^2$ subject to $g(x) = -x \leq 0$ (i.e., $x \geq 0$).
> Use the **logarithmic barrier** method with two iterations, $r_1 = 1$, reduction factor $c = 0.1$.

### Step 1 — Formulate the Barrier Function

The constraint $-x \leq 0 \Rightarrow -g(x) = x$. The log-barrier function:

$$\Phi(x, r_k) = (x - 2)^2 - r_k \ln(x)$$

### Step 2 — Stationary Condition (Unconstrained Minimization)

Take derivative and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} = 2(x - 2) - \frac{r_k}{x} = 0$$

Multiplying by $x$:

$$2x(x - 2) - r_k = 0$$

$$2x^2 - 4x - r_k = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 + 8r_k}}{4} = 1 \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4 + 2r_k}$$

### Step 3 — Select the Smaller Root (Closer to Feasible Region)

For minimization, the smaller root is correct (verified by second derivative $> 0$):

$$x_k^* = 1 - \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4 + 2r_k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** As $r_k \to 0$, $x_k^* \to 1 - 1 = 0$. Wait — that would mean the unconstrained optimum of $f$ is at $x = 2$, but the feasible region forces $x \geq 0$. So as the barrier weakens, the barrier-minimum should approach the *constrained* optimum.

**Re-evaluation:** The constraint is $g(x) = -x \leq 0 \Rightarrow x \geq 0$. The unconstrained minimum of $f$ is $x = 2$, which IS feasible ($x \geq 0$ satisfied). Hence the constrained optimum is $x^* = 2$, and $f(x^*) = 0$.

Let us check: as $r_k \to 0$ in $x_k^* = 1 + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4 + 2r_k}$ (taking the **+** sign because $x \geq 0$):

$$x_k^* \to 1 + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4} = 1 + 1 = 2 \quad \checkmark$$

We must select the **larger** root (the $+$ sign) for this problem.

### Step 4 — Iteration 1 ($r_1 = 1$)

$$x_1^* = 1 + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4 + 2(1)} = 1 + \frac{\sqrt{6}}{2} \approx 1 + 1.2247 = 2.2247$$

$$\Phi(x_1^*, 1) = (2.2247 - 2)^2 - 1 \cdot \ln(2.2247) = 0.0504 - 0.8005 = -0.7501$$

### Step 5 — Iteration 2 ($r_2 = c \cdot r_1 = 0.1 \times 1 = 0.1$)

$$x_2^* = 1 + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4 + 2(0.1)} = 1 + \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{4.2} = 1 + 1.0247 = 2.0247$$

$$\Phi(x_2^*, 0.1) = (2.0247 - 2)^2 - 0.1 \cdot \ln(2.0247) = 0.00061 - 0.0705 = -0.0699$$

### Step 6 — Verify Convergence to True Optimum $x^* = 2$

| Iteration $k$ | $r_k$ | $x_k^*$ | $f(x_k^*)$ | Error $\|x_k^* - 2\|$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.0 | 2.2247 | 0.0504 | 0.2247 |
| 2 | 0.1 | 2.0247 | 0.00061 | 0.0247 |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $\infty$ | 0 | 2.0000 | 0.00000 | 0.0000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** As $r_k$ decreases geometrically, the iterates approach the true optimum $x^* = 2$. This confirms the **convergence theorem** of the interior penalty method.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Exterior Penalty (Quadratic)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-Type):**
> Minimize $f(x) = x^2$ subject to $h(x) = x - 1 = 0$.
> Use the **quadratic exterior penalty** with two iterations, $r_1 = 1$, amplification factor $c = 10$.

### Step 1 — Formulate the Penalty Function

$$\Phi(x, r_k) = x^2 + r_k (x - 1)^2$$

### Step 2 — Stationary Condition

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x} = 2x + 2r_k(x - 1) = 0$$

$$2x + 2r_k x - 2r_k = 0$$

$$2x(1 + r_k) = 2r_k$$

$$\boxed{\;x_k^* = \frac{r_k}{1 + r_k}\;}$$

### Step 3 — Iteration 1 ($r_1 = 1$)

$$x_1^* = \frac{1}{1 + 1} = 0.5$$

$$\Phi(0.5, 1) = 0.25 + 1 \cdot (0.5 - 1)^2 = 0.25 + 0.25 = 0.5$$

### Step 4 — Iteration 2 ($r_2 = 10 \times 1 = 10$)

$$x_2^* = \frac{10}{1 + 10} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.9091$$

$$\Phi(0.9091, 10) = (0.9091)^2 + 10 \cdot (0.9091 - 1)^2 = 0.8264 + 0.8264 = 1.6529$$

### Step 5 — Convergence to True Optimum $x^* = 1$

| Iteration $k$ | $r_k$ | $x_k^*$ | $f(x_k^*)$ | Error $\|x_k^* - 1\|$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0.5000 | 0.2500 | 0.5000 |
| 2 | 10 | 0.9091 | 0.8264 | 0.0909 |
| 3 | 100 | 0.9901 | 0.9803 | 0.0099 |
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** As $r_k \to \infty$, $x_k^* \to 1$, the true optimum. The objective $f(x_k^*) \to 1$ as well (the true optimum of the constrained problem). This is a classic exterior method demonstration.

---

## 3.3 KKT Conditions — Linkage to Penalty Methods

The true optimum of a constrained problem satisfies the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions**:

$$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_i \nabla g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \mu_j \nabla h_j(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$$

With:
- $\lambda_i \geq 0$ (inequality multipliers)
- $\lambda_i \cdot g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ (complementary slackness)
- $\mu_j$ unrestricted (equality multipliers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to Penalty Methods:**
> In both interior and exterior methods, the penalty parameter $r_k$ is **analogous to** (and asymptotically equivalent to) the KKT multipliers $\lambda_i$. As $r_k \to 0$ (interior) or $r_k \to \infty$ (exterior), the KKT conditions are recovered in the limit. This is a **2-mark KTU question** that appears frequently.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Interior vs. Exterior

| Feature | Interior (Barrier) | Exterior (Penalty) |
|---|---|---|
| Starting point | Must be **strictly feasible** | Can be **infeasible** |
| Penalty parameter trend | $r_k \downarrow 0$ | $r_k \uparrow \infty$ |
| Handles equality $h_j = 0$ | **No** (in classical form) | **Yes** |
| Handles inequality $g_i \leq 0$ | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Final solution | On the **boundary** | Approaches **boundary from outside** |
| Constraint activity | Always active at limit | May be active in limit |
| Numerical stability | Smooth contours | Ill-conditioning at large $r_k$ |
| Common barrier/penalty | $1/(-g)$ or $-\ln(-g)$ | $[\max(0, g)]^2$ or $h^2$ |
| Multistart needed? | Yes (different $\mathbf{x}_0$) | No |
| Convergence guarantee | Yes, under convexity | Yes, under convexity |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture — Penalty Function Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Define Constrained NLP Problem"] --> B["Identify objective f of x"]
    B --> C["Identify inequality g of x and equality h of x"]
    C --> D{"Choose Method"}
    D -- Interior Barrier --> E["Select strictly feasible x0 with g less than 0"]
    D -- Exterior Penalty --> F["Select any starting point x0"]
    E --> G["Set initial r1 greater than 0 and reduction c in 0 to 1"]
    F --> H["Set initial r1 greater than 0 and amplification c greater than 1"]
    G --> I["Iteration k: Minimize Phi of x rk unconstrained"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Obtain xk star via gradient = 0 or Newton"]
    J --> K{"Convergence check epsilon"}
    K -- No --> L["Update r k plus 1 = c times rk"]
    L --> I
    K -- Yes --> M["OUTPUT: x star as constrained optimum"]
    M --> N["END"]
```

## 4.2 Interior vs Exterior — Block-Level Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Interior["INTERIOR PENALTY METHOD BLOCK"]
        I1["Input: Strictly Feasible x0"] --> I2["Build Phi = f + rk times B of x"]
        I2 --> I3["Solve unconstrained minimization"]
        I3 --> I4["Update rk plus 1 = c times rk with c in 0 1"]
        I4 --> I5{"r k less than epsilon"}
        I5 -- No --> I2
        I5 -- Yes --> I6["Output: Boundary Optimum"]
    end

    subgraph Exterior["EXTERIOR PENALTY METHOD BLOCK"]
        E1["Input: Any x0 feasible or not"] --> E2["Build Phi = f + rk times P of x"]
        E2 --> E3["Solve unconstrained minimization"]
        E3 --> E4["Update rk plus 1 = c times rk with c greater than 1"]
        E4 --> E5{"r k greater than M or change less than epsilon"}
        E5 -- No --> E2
        E5 -- Yes --> E6["Output: Approached Optimum from Outside"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Penalty Term Behaviour — Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Constraint Boundary g of x equals 0"] --> B["Interior: 1 over minus g or minus ln minus g"]
    A --> C["Exterior: max 0 g squared or h squared"]
    B --> D["Penalty goes to plus infinity as x approaches boundary from inside"]
    C --> E["Penalty is zero inside feasible region"]
    C --> F["Penalty grows as x moves outside feasible region"]
    D --> G["Search NEVER crosses boundary"]
    E --> H["Search can be anywhere in space"]
    F --> I["Search is gradually pushed towards boundary"]
```

## 4.4 Augmented Objective Contour — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["Original Objective f of x"] --> A["Add Penalty rk times B of x"]
    A --> P["Augmented Function Phi x rk"]
    P --> Q["Solve unconstrained problem"]
    Q --> R["Sequence of optima xk star rk"]
    R --> S["Limit as rk goes to 0 or infinity"]
    S --> T["True Constrained Optimum x star"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between Interior and Exterior Penalty Function Methods.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Interior (Barrier) | Exterior (Penalty) |
|---|---|---|
| Starting point | Must be strictly feasible | Any point (feasible or not) |
| Penalty parameter | $r_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$ | $r_k \to \infty$ as $k \to \infty$ |
| Equality constraints | Not handled | Handled directly |
| Limit position | On the boundary | Approaches from outside |

The interior method keeps the iterates inside the feasible region by using barrier terms that diverge to infinity at the boundary, while the exterior method allows infeasible iterates and uses growing penalty terms to drive them back into the feasible set. **(3 Marks)**

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **State the logarithmic barrier function for a problem with inequality constraints $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq 0$. Mention its advantage over the inverse barrier.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The logarithmic barrier function is:

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = f(\mathbf{x}) - r_k \sum_{i=1}^{m} \ln\bigl(-g_i(\mathbf{x})\bigr)$$

with $r_k > 0$ and $r_k \to 0$ as $k \to \infty$. **(2 Marks)**

**Advantage over inverse barrier** $(\Phi = f + r_k \sum 1/(-g_i))$: The log-barrier is **smoother near the boundary** (its derivative grows only as $1/(-g_i)$ rather than $1/(-g_i)^2$), which makes unconstrained minimization algorithms like Newton's method behave better numerically and converge faster. **(1 Mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Solve the following NLP using the Quadratic Exterior Penalty Method for two iterations:**

$$\min \; f(x) = (x - 3)^2$$

subject to

$$h(x) = x - 2 = 0$$

Use $r_1 = 0.5$ and amplification factor $c = 4$.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Formulate the penalty function and derive the optimality equation.**

**Solution:**

The penalty function is:

$$\Phi(x, r_k) = (x - 3)^2 + r_k (x - 2)^2$$

Setting the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d\Phi}{dx} = 2(x - 3) + 2r_k (x - 2) = 0$$

$$x(1 + r_k) = 3 + 2r_k$$

$$x_k^* = \frac{3 + 2r_k}{1 + r_k} \quad \text{(7 marks — penalty formulation + differentiation + closed-form solution)}$$

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Compute the two iterations and verify convergence.**

**Solution:**

**Iteration 1: $r_1 = 0.5$**

$$x_1^* = \frac{3 + 2(0.5)}{1 + 0.5} = \frac{4}{1.5} = 2.6667$$

$$\Phi(2.6667, 0.5) = (2.6667 - 3)^2 + 0.5(2.6667 - 2)^2 = 0.1111 + 0.2222 = 0.3333$$

**Update: $r_2 = 4 \times 0.5 = 2$**

**Iteration 2: $r_2 = 2$**

$$x_2^* = \frac{3 + 2(2)}{1 + 2} = \frac{7}{3} = 2.3333$$

$$\Phi(2.3333, 2) = (2.3333 - 3)^2 + 2(2.3333 - 2)^2 = 0.4444 + 0.2222 = 0.6667$$

**Convergence check:**

| $k$ | $r_k$ | $x_k^*$ | Error $\|x_k^* - 2\|$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 2.6667 | 0.6667 |
| 2 | 2.0 | 2.3333 | 0.3333 |
| $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 2.0000 | 0.0000 |

The iterates approach the true optimum $x^* = 2$ (where $h(x) = 0$) as $r_k \to \infty$. **(7 marks — iteration calculations + convergence table)**

---

> ### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Solve the following NLP using the Logarithmic Interior Penalty (Barrier) Method for two iterations:**

$$\min \; f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2$$

subject to

$$g(x_1, x_2) = 1 - x_1 - x_2 \leq 0$$

Use $r_1 = 2$, $c = 0.25$, and a feasible starting point.

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]: Construct the barrier function and derive the necessary conditions.**

**Solution:**

The constraint requires $1 - x_1 - x_2 \leq 0 \Rightarrow x_1 + x_2 \geq 1$. The barrier function is:

$$\Phi(\mathbf{x}, r_k) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - r_k \ln(1 - x_1 - x_2)$$

Partial derivatives set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 + \frac{r_k}{1 - x_1 - x_2} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 + \frac{r_k}{1 - x_1 - x_2} = 0$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$2x_1 - 2x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = x_2 \quad \text{(by symmetry)}$$

Substituting back:

$$2x_1 + \frac{r_k}{1 - 2x_1} = 0 \Rightarrow 2x_1(1 - 2x_1) + r_k = 0$$

$$4x_1^2 - 2x_1 - r_k = 0$$

$$x_1 = \frac{2 \pm \sqrt{4 + 16r_k}}{8} = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 + 4r_k}}{4}$$

Selecting the **smaller** root (to stay inside the feasible region):

$$x_1^* = x_2^* = \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 + 4r_k}}{4} \quad \text{(7 marks)}$$

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]: Compute the iterates and discuss the limit.**

**Solution:**

**Iteration 1: $r_1 = 2$**

$$x_1^* = \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 + 8}}{4} = \frac{1 - 3}{4} = -0.5$$

This violates feasibility! Hence we need a **strictly feasible** starting point and the smaller root gives a boundary-adjacent point. The correct branch is the **larger** root:

$$x_1^* = x_2^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 8}}{4} = \frac{1 + 3}{4} = 1.0$$

Check: $1 - x_1 - x_2 = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 < 0$ ✓ (strictly feasible).

**Update: $r_2 = 0.25 \times 2 = 0.5$**

**Iteration 2: $r_2 = 0.5$**

$$x_1^* = x_2^* = \frac{1 + \sqrt{1 + 2}}{4} = \frac{1 + \sqrt{3}}{4} = \frac{1 + 1.7321}{4} = 0.6830$$

Check feasibility: $1 - 0.683 - 0.683 = -0.366 < 0$ ✓.

**Convergence:**

| $k$ | $r_k$ | $x_1^* = x_2^*$ | $f(\mathbf{x}_k^*)$ | $1 - x_1 - x_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.0 | 1.0000 | 2.0000 | $-1.000$ |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0.6830 | 0.9329 | $-0.366$ |
| 3 | 0.125 | 0.5915 | 0.6997 | $-0.183$ |
| $\infty$ | 0 | 0.5000 | 0.5000 | $0$ (boundary) |

**Limit as $r_k \to 0$:** $x_1^* = x_2^* = 1/2$, with $f = 0.5$ and the constraint active on the boundary. The interior method correctly converges to the true constrained optimum. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong root selection** (≈2 marks lost): In the quadratic solution of penalty problems, the wrong sign of $\pm$ gives an infeasible point. Always verify $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) \leq 0$ before finalizing.
>
> 2. **Confusing $c$ values**: Interior uses $c \in (0,1)$ (reduction); Exterior uses $c > 1$ (amplification). Reversing this is a **2-mark direct deduction**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $\max(0, g_i)$ hinge**: For inequality exterior penalty, students often write $r_k \sum g_i^2$ which penalizes feasible points too. Always include the **clamp** $\max(0, g_i)$.
>
> 4. **Ignoring starting-point feasibility for interior method**: If the question says "interior method" but you start outside, the logarithm will be **undefined** ($-\ln(0) = \infty$). Always check $\mathbf{x}_0$ is strictly feasible.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to verify convergence to the true optimum**: A full 14-mark answer must include the limit analysis showing $\mathbf{x}^*(r_k) \to \mathbf{x}^*$ as $r_k \to 0$ (or $\infty$).
>
> 6. **Not mentioning KKT connection**: Even a one-line statement linking $r_k$ to the KKT multipliers $\lambda_i$ often fetches a **bonus 1 mark** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — KTU 2024 Scheme):**

- ✅ **Penalty Function Methods** transform a *constrained* NLP into a *sequence* of unconstrained problems.
- ✅ **Two basic approaches**:
    - **Interior (Barrier)**: $r_k \to 0$, starting point strictly feasible, final answer on boundary.
    - **Exterior (Penalty)**: $r_k \to \infty$, any starting point, can handle equalities, approaches boundary from outside.
- ✅ **Inverse Barrier**: $B = \sum 1/(-g_i)$. Diverges as $1/(-g_i)$ at boundary.
- ✅ **Log Barrier**: $B = -\sum \ln(-g_i)$. Smoother; preferred in modern interior-point solvers.
- ✅ **Quadratic Exterior Penalty** (inequality): $P = \sum [\max(0, g_i)]^2$.
- ✅ **Quadratic Exterior Penalty** (equality): $P = \sum [h_j]^2$.
- ✅ **Interior algorithm**: pick strictly feasible $\mathbf{x}_0$, reduce $r_k$ by factor $c \in (0,1)$ each iteration.
- ✅ **Exterior algorithm**: any $\mathbf{x}_0$, amplify $r_k$ by factor $c > 1$ each iteration.
- ✅ **Convergence** requires the unconstrained problem to be solved accurately for each $r_k$.
- ✅ **Stopping criteria**: small $r_k$ OR small $\|\mathbf{x}_{k+1}^* - \mathbf{x}_k^*\|$ OR negligible objective change.
- ✅ **KKT linkage**: $r_k$ asymptotically corresponds to KKT multipliers $\lambda_i$.
- ✅ **Numerical caveat**: Exterior methods suffer from ill-conditioning at large $r_k$; interior methods need careful selection of strictly feasible $\mathbf{x}_0$.
- ✅ **Engineering applications**: structural optimization, process design, ML (interior-point SVM/LASSO), robotics, aerospace trajectory planning.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Convex Programming Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Convex Programming Problem # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Convex Programming Problem (CPP) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Convex Programming Problem (CPP)** is a non-linear programming problem in which the **objective function is a convex function** to be minimized, and the **feasible region defined by the constraints is a convex set** (or equivalently, the inequality constraint functions are convex and the equality constraint functions are affine). **Standard Mathematical Form of a CPP:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Minimize} \quad & f(\mathbf{x}) \\ \text{Subject to} \quad & g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\ & h_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, p \\ & \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \end{aligned} $$ Where: - **$f(\mathbf{x})$** is a **convex** objective function. - **$g_i(\mathbf{x})$** are **convex** inequality constraint functions. - **$h_j(\mathbf{x})$** are **affine (linear)** equality constraint functions of the form $h_j(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}_j^T \mathbf{x} - b_j = 0$. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** In a convex program, we typically minimize a convex function over a convex set. If we were to *maximize* a convex function, the problem becomes non-convex and loses its elegant theoretical properties. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bowl-Shaped Valley" Imagine you are **blindfolded and dropped into a hilly terrain**, and your goal is to find the *lowest point possible*. - **Non-Convex Problem (General NLP):** The terrain is full of many valleys and peaks. A valley you walk into might not be the deepest one — you could get *trapped* in a small valley that is not the true lowest point. These are **local minima**. - **Convex Programming Problem (CPP):** The terrain is shaped like a **single, smooth bowl** (like the inside of a sphere). No matter where you stand on the rim and start walking downhill, you will *always* slide into the **same unique lowest point** at the center. This single lowest point is the **global minimum**. > [!TIP] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The "bowl-shape" guarantee means engineers and data scientists can trust that any optimization algorithm converging to a stationary point has actually found the *best possible* solution in the entire search space — a luxury unavailable in general non-linear programming. ## 1.3 Why Convex Programming Matters in Engineering Convex programming is one of the most important classes of optimization problems in engineering because: | Real-World Application | Engineering Discipline | | :--- | :--- | | **Portfolio Optimization** (minimize risk) | Financial Engineering | | **Optimal Power Flow** in electrical grids | Electrical Engineering | | **Structural Design** (minimize weight under stress) | Mechanical / Civil Engineering | | **Support Vector Machines (SVMs)** | Machine Learning / AI | | **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** | Control Systems Engineering | | **Network Flow & Resource Allocation** | Operations Research | | **Beamforming & Signal Processing** | Telecommunications | --- **Special Benchmark Constants / Metrics** (used throughout Module 3): - **Feasible Region ($\mathcal{S}$)** — The set of all $\mathbf{x}$ satisfying every constraint. - **Optimal Value ($p^*$)** — The minimum value of $f(\mathbf{x})$ achieved at the optimum. - **Optimal Set ($\mathcal{X}_{opt}$)** — The set of all $\mathbf{x}^*$ where $p^* = f(\mathbf{x}^*)$. - **Active Set ($\mathcal{A}$)** — The set of indices $i$ where $g_i(\mathbf{x}^*) = 0$ at the optimum. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks of CPP A convex programming problem rests on **two pillars**: convex sets and convex functions. If either pillar collapses, the problem is no longer convex. ### 2.1.1 Convex Sets — The "Feasible Region" A set $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is **convex** if the line segment connecting any two points in the set lies entirely within the set. $$ \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{C} \implies \lambda \mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{C} \quad \forall \lambda \in [0, 1] $$ **Geometric Interpretation:** Take *any* two points inside the set. The straight line joining them must never exit the set. A circle is convex; a star shape is not. **Common Convex Sets (KTU High-Yield Examples):** 1. **Hyperplane:** $\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{x} = b\}$ 2. **Halfspace:** $\{\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n : \mathbf{a}^T\mathbf{x} \le b\}$ 3. **Polyhedron:** Intersection of finitely many halfspaces and hyperplanes. 4. **Euclidean Ball:** $\{\mathbf{x} : \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_c \Vert_2 \le r\}$ 5. **Ellipsoid:** $\{\mathbf{x} : (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_c)^T \mathbf{P}^{-1} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_c) \le 1\}$ for symmetric positive definite $\mathbf{P}$. 6. **Norm Ball:** $\{\mathbf{x} : \Vert \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_c \Vert \le r\}$ for *any* norm. ### 2.1.2 Convex Functions — The "Objective & Constraints" A function $f: \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ is **convex** if its epigraph (the region above its graph) is a convex set. Equivalently: $$ f(\lambda \mathbf{x} + (1-\lambda)\mathbf{y}) \le \lambda f(\mathbf{x}) + (1-\lambda) f(\mathbf{y}) \quad \forall \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \text{dom}(f), \, \lambda \in [0,1] $$ **Geometric Interpretation:** The chord (straight line) connecting any two points on the graph of $f$ must lie *above* the graph itself. The function "curves upward" — never downward. ### 2.1.3 The Two Conditions for Convexity (First-Order Test) If $f$ is differentiable, then $f$ is convex if and only if: $$ f(\mathbf{y}) \ge f(\mathbf{x}) + \nabla f(\mathbf{x})^T (\mathbf{y} - \mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \text{dom}(f) $$ This is the **first-order Taylor approximation** acting as a *global under-estimator* of $f$. ### 2.1.4 The Hessian Test (Second-Order Condition) If $f$ is twice differentiable, then $f$ is convex if and only if its **Hessian matrix is positive semidefinite (PSD)** everywhere in the domain: $$ \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) \succeq 0 \quad \forall \mathbf{x} \in \text{dom}(f) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Memory Aid:** Think of the Hessian as a "curvature matrix."
