The past is irrelevant; only the present state and the remaining stages matter.* These two concepts are the philosophical and mathematical cornerstones of **Dynamic Programming (DP)** — without sub-optimization, DP cannot be applied, and without the Principle of Optimality, the recursive decomposition breaks down. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Road-Trip Analogy (Route from Kochi → Bengaluru via Palakkad & Salem)** Imagine you are planning the cheapest route from **Kochi** to **Bengaluru**, and you know the optimal route must pass through **Palakkad** and **Salem** as intermediate stops. - You choose Kochi → Palakkad (Stage 1 decision). - Once you have arrived at **Palakkad**, your problem *reduces* to: *"What is the cheapest path from Palakkad to Bengaluru?"* - The Principle of Optimality guarantees that **the remainder of your journey (Palakkad → Salem → Bengaluru) must itself be the cheapest possible route starting from Palakkad**. Suppose, by bad luck, your Stage-1 choice was not the globally cheapest first leg. Even so, from Palakkad onward, you must still take the optimal sub-route. The optimality of the *remaining* path is independent of the *past* path. This is sub-optimization at work. **Another Everyday Analogy — Climbing Down a Staircase in the Dark:** If you slip on the 3rd step but a friend tells you the **optimal** way to descend the remaining steps from your current footing, that advice is valid *even though* you got to step 3 by a non-optimal path. The optimal sub-strategy from any reachable state is the same — past mistakes do not poison future optimality. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Terminology The following terminology is standardized in the KTU 2024 scheme and must be used verbatim in examinations: | Term | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Stage** | $n$ | A single point where a decision is made | | **State** | $s$ | The condition/position of the system at a stage | | **Decision Variable** | $x_k$ | The choice made at stage $k$ | | **Return / Stage Cost** | $d_k(s, x_k)$ | Immediate cost incurred by choosing $x_k$ at state $s$ | | **Optimal Value Function** | $f_n(s)$ | Minimum (or maximum) total cost from state $s$ with $n$ stages to go | | **Policy** | $\pi$ | Complete sequence of decisions $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ | | **Optimal Policy** | $\pi^*$ | A policy achieving $f_n(s)$ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why Sub-optimization Holds The Principle of Optimality can be unpacked into four logical steps that examiners love to test: 1. **Decomposition**: The original $n$-stage problem is decomposed into $n$ single-stage sub-problems, each linked by the *state variable* $s$. 2. **Recursive Structure**: The cost-to-go from state $s$ with $k$ stages remaining depends only on (a) the immediate decision $x_k$ and (b) the optimal cost from the *next* state with $k-1$ stages remaining. 3. **Independence of Past**: Whatever sequence of decisions brought the system to state $s$ is irrelevant — only $s$ and the number of remaining stages affect the future optimal cost. 4. **Optimal Sub-paths**: If a global optimal path $\pi^*$ passes through state $s$ at stage $k$, the tail of $\pi^*$ from $s$ onward is itself the optimal path from $s$ to the end. > [!TIP] > **Why is this useful?** It converts a problem with exponentially many policies (each policy is a sequence of $n$ decisions) into $n$ small problems, each solvable independently — reducing complexity from $O(m^n)$ to $O(n \cdot m \cdot s)$ where $m$ = decisions per stage, $s$ = states per stage. ### 2.2 Conditions Necessary for the Principle to Apply A problem must satisfy **all three** conditions below; otherwise DP is not applicable: - (i) The problem can be partitioned into stages with a single decision per stage. - (ii) Each stage has a finite number of states (the state must fully describe the system's future). - (iii) The return function $d_k(s, x_k)$ is well-defined for every state-decision pair. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the boxed recurrence — this is the most-tested equation in Module 4. $$ f_n(s) = \min_{x_n \in D_n(s)} \Big[\, d_n(s, x_n) \;+\; f_{n-1}(x_n) \,\Big] $$ For a **maximization** problem (e.g., longest path, maximum profit): $$ f_n(s) = \max_{x_n \in D_n(s)} \Big[\, d_n(s, x_n) \;+\; f_{n-1}(x_n) \,\Big] $$ Boundary condition (base case): $$ f_1(s) = d_1(s, x_1) $$ | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning / When to Use | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $f_n(s) = \min/\max \{ d_n(s,x_n) + f_{n-1}(x_n) \}$ | **Bellman's Recurrence** — core of every DP problem | Cost units (\$ / km / hr) | | 2 | $f_1(s) = d_1(s,x_1)$ | Boundary condition for the last stage | Same as $d_n$ | | 3 | $x_n^*(s) = \arg\min/\max \{ d_n(s,x_n) + f_{n-1}(x_n) \}$ | Optimal decision rule at state $s$, stage $n$ | Decision index | | 4 | $f_n(s) = \min_x \{c(s,x) + f_{n-1}(T(s,x))\}$ | Generalized form with state-transition $T(s,x)$ | Cost units | | 5 | $\pi^* = \{x_1^*, x_2^*, \ldots, x_n^*\}$ | Optimal policy reconstructed backward | — | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications The Principle of Optimality governs a vast range of engineering problems: - **Shortest-Path Algorithms** (Dijkstra's algorithm on a DAG is a DP application). - **Resource Allocation in Manufacturing** — distributing $W$ units of capital across $n$ projects. - **Inventory Control & Replenishment Planning** in supply-chain engineering. - **Structural Engineering** — optimal design of trusses with multi-stage loading. - **Control Systems & Robotics** — optimal trajectory planning under fuel/energy constraints. - **VLSI Chip Routing** — wire-length minimization in integrated-circuit design. - **Network Reliability** — minimum-cost flow and reliability maximization in communication networks. - **Knapsack-type Combinatorial Problems** — cargo loading in aircraft/ships. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Bellman's Recurrence from the Principle of Optimality We rigorously derive the functional equation that defines $f_n(s)$ — the optimal value function. **Setup.** Let the system be in state $s$ at stage $n$ with $n$ stages to go before termination. Let $D_n(s)$ denote the admissible set of decisions at this stage. Choosing a decision $x_n \in D_n(s)$ produces two consequences: - **Immediate cost:** $d_n(s, x_n)$ — incurred at this stage. - **New state:** $s' = T_n(s, x_n)$ — the state at the start of stage $n-1$ (one less stage to go). Let $f_{n-1}(s')$ denote the optimal total cost from the new state $s'$ with $n-1$ stages remaining. By the **Principle of Optimality**, regardless of how $s'$ was reached, $f_{n-1}(s')$ is the minimum achievable cost of the tail. Therefore, the total cost when decision $x_n$ is chosen is: $$ \text{Total cost with decision } x_n = d_n(s, x_n) + f_{n-1}\big(T_n(s, x_n)\big) $$ To obtain the **optimal** value $f_n(s)$, we minimize (or maximize) over all admissible decisions: $$ f_n(s) = \min_{x_n \in D_n(s)} \Big[\, d_n(s, x_n) + f_{n-1}\big(T_n(s, x_n)\big) \,\Big] $$ For maximization problems (e.g., longest path, maximum reliability), replace $\min$ with $\max$: $$ f_n(s) = \max_{x_n \in D_n(s)} \Big[\, d_n(s, x_n) + f_{n-1}\big(T_n(s, x_n)\big) \,\Big] $$ The decision that achieves this extremum is the optimal decision: $$ x_n^*(s) = \arg\min_{x_n \in D_n(s)} \Big[\, d_n(s, x_n) + f_{n-1}\big(T_n(s, x_n)\big) \,\Big] $$ **Boundary Condition.** When only one stage remains ($n=1$), the cost is just the immediate return: $$ f_1(s) = \min_{x_1 \in D_1(s)} d_1(s, x_1) $$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** The recurrence is solved **backward** (from $n=1$ up to $n=N$) because $f_{n-1}(\cdot)$ must be known before $f_n(\cdot)$ can be evaluated. ### 3.2 Worked Example — Shortest Path on a 3-Stage Network **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A courier must deliver a parcel from **node A** (Stage 3) to **node G** (Stage 0) via the network shown below. The numbers on each arc represent distances in **kilometers**. Find the shortest route using the Principle of Optimality. **Network (stages and arcs):** - Stage 3: node A → {B, C} - Stage 2: B → {D, E}; C → {E, F} - Stage 1: D → G; E → G; F → G - Arc costs: A→B=4, A→C=3, B→D=2, B→E=5, C→E=1, C→F=6, D→G=7, E→G=4, F→G=3 **Step 1 — Stage 1 (last stage, $n=1$):** The optimal cost from each node at stage 1 is simply the cost to reach G: $$ f_1(D) = 7, \quad f_1(E) = 4, \quad f_1(F) = 3 $$ **Step 2 — Stage 2 ($n=2$):** Compute $f_2(B)$ and $f_2(C)$ by minimizing immediate cost + $f_1(\cdot)$: $$ f_2(B) = \min\big[\,d_2(B,D) + f_1(D),\; d_2(B,E) + f_1(E)\,\big] $$ $$ f_2(B) = \min[\,2 + 7,\; 5 + 4\,] = \min[\,9,\; 9\,] = 9 $$ Both decisions (D or E) are tied. Optimal decision set: $x_2^*(B) \in \{D, E\}$. $$ f_2(C) = \min\big[\,d_2(C,E) + f_1(E),\; d_2(C,F) + f_1(F)\,\big] $$ $$ f_2(C) = \min[\,1 + 4,\; 6 + 3\,] = \min[\,5,\; 9\,] = 5 $$ Optimal decision: $x_2^*(C) = E$. **Step 3 — Stage 3 ($n=3$, initial node A):** $$ f_3(A) = \min\big[\,d_3(A,B) + f_2(B),\; d_3(A,C) + f_2(C)\,\big] $$ $$ f_3(A) = \min[\,4 + 9,\; 3 + 5\,] = \min[\,13,\; 8\,] = 8 \;\text{km} $$ Optimal decision: $x_3^*(A) = C$. **Step 4 — Reconstruct the Optimal Path (forward pass):** $$ A \xrightarrow{3 \text{ km}} C \xrightarrow{1 \text{ km}} E \xrightarrow{4 \text{ km}} G $$ Total shortest distance = $\mathbf{8\;\text{km}}$. ### 3.3 Verification of the Principle of Optimality To verify, examine all $2 \times 2 \times 3 = 12$ possible paths exhaustively: | Path | Total Cost (km) | |---|---| | A→B→D→G | $4 + 2 + 7 = 13$ | | A→B→E→G | $4 + 5 + 4 = 13$ | | A→C→E→G | $3 + 1 + 4 = \mathbf{8}$ ← optimal | | A→C→F→G | $3 + 6 + 3 = 12$ | The DP solution matches the brute-force enumeration, confirming the Principle of Optimality is valid for this network. ### 3.4 Counter-Example — When the Principle Fails > [!WARNING] > The Principle of Optimality is **not** universal. It fails when decisions in earlier stages affect the *feasibility* (not just the cost) of later stages. **Example:** A salesman must visit 3 cities. The cost matrix is non-additive — a "discount coupon" applies only if the tour visits city-1 *before* city-2. Here, swapping the order of cities-1 and 2 changes not only cost but eligibility for the coupon. The optimal suffix from any state is **not** independent of how you got there. → DP does **not** apply. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the DP Solver The following flowchart illustrates how the Principle of Optimality is operationalized in a generic DP solver: ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: Cost matrix d_n(s,x),
State set S, Decision set X] --> B[Initialize f_1 s
Boundary condition] B --> C[Set stage counter n = 2] C --> D{All stages processed?} D -- No --> E[Iterate over all states s in S_n] E --> F[Iterate over all decisions x in D_n s] F --> G[Compute candidate cost
cand = d_n s,x + f_{n-1} x] G --> H{Minimize or
Maximize?} H -- Minimize --> I[Update f_n s = min f_n s, cand
Store argmin] H -- Maximize --> J[Update f_n s = max f_n s, cand
Store argmax] I --> K{More decisions?} J --> K K -- Yes --> F K -- No --> L[Record f_n s and x_n star s] L --> M[Increment n = n + 1] M --> D D -- Yes --> N[Backward pass complete
Optimal value function f_N s_0 known] N --> O[Forward reconstruction
Trace x_n star to recover path pi star] O --> P[Output: Optimal policy pi star
and minimum cost f_N s_0] ``` ### 4.2 Stage-Wise Decision Tree (3-Stage Worked Example) The diagram below maps the recursive evaluation of $f_n(s)$ for the courier example: ```mermaid flowchart LR sA[Stage 3
State A
f3 A = 8] --> sB[Stage 2
State B
f2 B = 9] sA --> sC[Stage 2
State C
f2 C = 5] sB --> sD[Stage 1
State D
f1 D = 7] sB --> sE[Stage 1
State E
f1 E = 4] sC --> sE sC --> sF[Stage 1
State F
f1 F = 3] sD --> sG[Terminal
State G
f0 G = 0] sE --> sG sF --> sG sE -. OPTIMAL PATH .-> sG ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Each **node** = a state $s$ at stage $n$ with computed value $f_n(s)$. - Each **edge** = a decision $x_n$ leading to the next stage. - The dashed edge traces the optimal policy: $A \to C \to E \to G$ with cost **8 km**. ### 4.3 Conceptual Map of Sub-Optimization ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Original Problem P
n stages, state s_0] --> S1[Sub problem P_1
Stage 1 decision] P --> S2[Sub problem P_2
Stage 2 decision] P --> Sn[Sub problem P_n
Stage n decision] S1 --> O1[Optimal sub solution S_1 star] S2 --> O2[Optimal sub solution S_2 star] Sn --> On[Optimal sub solution S_n star] O1 --> GlobOpt[Global Optimal Solution S star] O2 --> GlobOpt On --> GlobOpt ``` **Interpretation:** If $S^*$ is the global optimum, then each constituent $S_i^*$ is provably optimal for its own sub-problem — this is the essence of sub-optimization, and it is precisely what permits the recursive decomposition. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *State the Principle of Optimality as formulated by Richard Bellman. Mention any two conditions under which dynamic programming can be applied to a problem.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Principle of Optimality (Bellman, 1957):** *"An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."* > > **Conditions for applicability of DP:** > 1. The problem can be broken into stages with a single decision per stage. > 2. Each stage has a finite (and small) number of states. > 3. The return function $d_n(s, x_n)$ is well-defined and the state transition is deterministic or has known probabilities. *(3 marks: 1 mark for exact statement, 2 marks for conditions)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Explain the concept of sub-optimization with a suitable engineering example.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Sub-optimization** is the property that an optimal solution to a multi-stage problem is composed of optimal solutions to each of its sub-problems. In other words, if a global optimal path passes through state $s$ at stage $k$, the *tail* of that path from $s$ to the terminal state is itself the optimal solution of the reduced sub-problem starting at $s$. > > **Example:** In the design of a multi-stage truss bridge with minimum total weight subject to load constraints, the optimal design of each intermediate span must itself be optimal for the loads and reactions transmitted to it — even though the loads it receives depend on how the previous spans were designed. The optimality of each span's design (sub-solution) holds because the Principle of Optimality applies. *(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks example with engineering context)* --- ### Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]** **(a)** *Derive the functional recurrence equation of dynamic programming from the Principle of Optimality. Clearly state all symbols used.* **[7 Marks]** **(b)** *A manufacturing firm must allocate ₹ 6 lakhs among 3 projects. The returns (in lakhs) for different investment levels are given below. Use Dynamic Programming to find the optimal investment allocation that maximizes total return.* **[7 Marks]** | Investment (₹ lakhs) | Project 1 | Project 2 | Project 3 | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 1 | 5 | 4 | 3 | | 2 | 9 | 7 | 6 | | 3 | 12 | 10 | 11 | | 4 | 14 | 12 | 13 | **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of the DP Recurrence:** Let the system be in state $s$ at stage $n$ with $n$ stages remaining. A decision $x_n$ taken from admissible set $D_n(s)$ yields: - Immediate return $d_n(s, x_n)$ - New state $x_n$ (since the state variable equals the resource remaining). By the Principle of Optimality, the optimal value from $s$ satisfies: $$ f_n(s) = \max_{x_n \in D_n(s)}\Big[\,d_n(s, x_n) + f_{n-1}(x_n)\,\Big] $$ with boundary condition $f_1(s) = \max_{x_1 \in D_1(s)} d_1(s, x_1)$. *(7 marks breakdown: 2 marks setup, 3 marks derivation, 2 marks boundary condition)* **(b) DP Solution — Resource Allocation:** **Stage 3 → Stage 1 backward recursion. State $s$ = capital available; Decision $x_n$ = capital allocated to project $n$.** **Stage 1 (Project 3):** $f_1(s) = g_3(s)$ where $g_3$ is the return from project 3 with investment $s$: | $s$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $f_1(s)$ | 0 | 3 | 6 | 11 | 13 | 13 | 13 | **Stage 2 (Project 2):** Allocate $x_2$ to Project 2, leaving $s - x_2$ for Project 3: $$ f_2(s) = \max_{0 \le x_2 \le s}\big[\,g_2(x_2) + f_1(s - x_2)\,\big] $$ For $s = 6$: try $x_2 = 0,1,2,3,4$: - $x_2=0$: $0 + f_1(6) = 0 + 13 = 13$ - $x_2=1$: $4 + f_1(5) = 4 + 13 = 17$ - $x_2=2$: $7 + f_1(4) = 7 + 13 = \mathbf{20}$ ← max - $x_2=3$: $10 + f_1(3) = 10 + 11 = 21$ ← max - $x_2=4$: $12 + f_1(2) = 12 + 6 = 18$ So $f_2(6) = 21$, $x_2^* = 3$. **Stage 3 (Project 1):** Allocate $x_3$ to Project 1, $6 - x_3$ to remaining: $$ f_3(6) = \max_{0 \le x_3 \le 6}\big[\,g_1(x_3) + f_2(6 - x_3)\,\big] $$ - $x_3=0$: $0 + f_2(6) = 0 + 21 = 21$ - $x_3=1$: $5 + f_2(5)$. Compute $f_2(5)$ similarly: $x_2=3 \Rightarrow 10 + f_1(2) = 10+6 = 16$. So $5+16 = 21$. - $x_3=2$: $9 + f_2(4) = 9 + 13 = 22$ - $x_3=3$: $12 + f_2(3)$. $f_2(3) = \max\{0+f_1(3), 4+f_1(2), 7+f_1(1), 10+f_1(0)\} = \max\{11, 10, 10, 10\} = 11$. So $12+11 = 23$ ← max - $x_3=4$: $14 + f_2(2) = 14 + 6 = 20$ - $x_3=5$: $14 + f_2(1) = 14 + 3 = 17$ - $x_3=6$: $14 + f_2(0) = 14 + 0 = 14$ **Optimal allocation:** $x_3^* = 3$ (Project 1), $x_2^* = 3$ (Project 2), $x_1^* = 0$ (Project 3). **Maximum total return = ₹ 23 lakhs.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** *Explain in detail the difference between problems that satisfy the Principle of Optimality and those that do not. Give one engineering example of each category.* **[7 Marks]** **(b)** *Solve the following shortest-route problem using dynamic programming. The network has 4 stages and the numbers on arcs represent distances in km. Find the shortest route from S to T.* **[7 Marks]** - Stage 4: S → {A, B} - Stage 3: A → {C, D}; B → {D, E} - Stage 2: C → {F, G}; D → {G, H}; E → {H, I} - Stage 1: F → T; G → T; H → T; I → T - Distances: S→A=2, S→B=5, A→C=4, A→D=3, B→D=2, B→E=6, C→F=4, C→G=5, D→G=1, D→H=7, E→H=3, E→I=4, F→T=6, G→T=3, H→T=5, I→T=2 **Model Solution:** **(a)** *(See Section 3.4 above. Compare additive cost networks where DP applies vs. feasibility-dependent problems like non-additive travelling salesman tours where it fails.)* **(b) DP Solution:** **Stage 1:** $f_1(F) = 6$, $f_1(G) = 3$, $f_1(H) = 5$, $f_1(I) = 2$. **Stage 2:** - $f_2(C) = \min[4+6,\, 5+3] = \min[10, 8] = 8$ via G. - $f_2(D) = \min[1+3,\, 7+5] = \min[4, 12] = 4$ via G. - $f_2(E) = \min[3+5,\, 4+2] = \min[8, 6] = 6$ via I. **Stage 3:** - $f_3(A) = \min[4+f_2(C),\, 3+f_2(D)] = \min[4+8,\, 3+4] = \min[12, 7] = 7$ via D. - $f_3(B) = \min[2+f_2(D),\, 6+f_2(E)] = \min[2+4,\, 6+6] = \min[6, 12] = 6$ via D. **Stage 4:** $f_4(S) = \min[2+f_3(A),\, 5+f_3(B)] = \min[2+7,\, 5+6] = \min[9, 11] = 9$ via A. **Reconstructing the path:** $S \to A \to D \to G \to T$ with cost $2 + 3 + 1 + 3 = \mathbf{9\;\text{km}}$. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic — KTU 2024 evaluation key insights:** > > 1. **Quoting the Principle incorrectly** — students paraphrase it. Use Bellman's verbatim phrasing or the explicit form *"the remaining decisions must be optimal w.r.t. the state resulting from the first decision."* > 2. **Missing the boundary condition** — every recurrence solution *must* explicitly state $f_1(s) = d_1(s, x_1)$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if omitted. > 3. **Forward vs. backward confusion** — the recurrence is solved **backward** ($n=1 \to N$), but the optimal *path* is reconstructed **forward** from $s_0$. > 4. **Forgetting sub-optimization verification** — when applying DP, you must justify *why* the Principle of Optimality holds for *your* specific problem. Otherwise the solution earns partial credit only. > 5. **Unit errors** — always carry the unit (km, ₹, hours) until the final answer. Marks lost in answer-only numerical questions. > 6. **For maximization problems** — flipping the sign and treating them as minimization is a common error; check the objective function first. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4 / Concept of Sub-optimization & Principle of Optimality** > > - **Sub-optimization** = optimal global solution is composed of optimal sub-solutions; essential prerequisite for DP. > - **Principle of Optimality** = Bellman's theorem: future decisions must be optimal w.r.t. current state, independent of past. > - **Bellman's Recurrence (canonical form):** > > $$ > f_n(s) = \min_{x_n \in D_n(s)}\big[\,d_n(s, x_n) + f_{n-1}(x_n)\,\big] > $$ > > - **Boundary condition:** $f_1(s) = \min d_1(s, x_1)$. > - **Computation order:** recurrence evaluated **backward** ($n=1$ to $N$); policy recovered **forward**. > - **DP applicability conditions:** (i) stage-wise decomposition, (ii) finite state space, (iii) well-defined return function. > - **DP inapplicable when:** decisions in earlier stages change the *feasibility set* of later stages (non-additive objectives, state-dependent constraints). > - **Complexity reduction:** from $O(m^n)$ policies to $O(n \cdot m \cdot s)$ sub-problems. > - **Standard symbols to memorize:** $f_n(s)$ (optimal value), $d_n(s, x_n)$ (stage return), $x_n^*$ (optimal decision), $D_n(s)$ (admissible decision set). > - **Engineering applications:** shortest path, knapsack, resource allocation, inventory, structural design, VLSI routing, optimal control. > - **Common pitfalls:** wrong direction of recursion, missing boundary condition, paraphrasing the Principle incorrectly, forgetting units.

topics.title: Computational Procedure in Dynamic Programming
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computational Procedure in Dynamic Programming

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Programming (DP)** is a mathematical optimization methodology that solves a complex, multi-stage decision problem by breaking it down recursively into a sequence of smaller, simpler sub-problems, where the **Principle of Optimality** (Bellman, 1957) guarantees that an optimal policy from any state depends only upon the current state and not upon the prior history of decisions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Dynamic Programming is treated as a **deterministic sequential decision-making algorithm** that systematically evaluates every feasible state to determine the optimal sequence of decisions. The **Computational Procedure** refers to the explicit, ordered sequence of mathematical operations used to translate a real-world multi-stage decision problem into a solvable **recursive functional equation**.

### 1.2 Core Nomenclature (KTU Mandatory Terminology)

| KTU Term | Mathematical Symbol | Plain English Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage** | $n$ | The discrete point in time / step where a decision is taken. |
| **State** | $s_n$ | A complete description of the system at the start of stage $n$. |
| **Decision / Control** | $d_n$ (or $x_n$) | The action chosen at stage $n$. |
| **Return Function** | $r_n(s_n, d_n)$ | Immediate cost or profit of choosing $d_n$ at $s_n$. |
| **Optimal Value Function** | $f_n(s_n)$ | Best possible return from state $s_n$ to terminal stage. |
| **Transformation Function** | $s_{n+1} = T(s_n, d_n)$ | How the state evolves after a decision. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Hiking Map Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are hiking down a mountain to a village.** You stand at a junction with multiple paths. You cannot see the entire mountain, but you can see the *next* junction. 
> 
> **Naive Approach**: Try every possible path until the bottom, then remember the cheapest. This is **enumeration** — it works for tiny mountains, but is computationally disastrous for large ones.
> 
> **Dynamic Programming Approach**: At every junction (state), you ask: *"Of all the paths leading from THIS junction onward, which is the cheapest?"* You write this minimum cost on a signboard at the junction. The next hiker arriving at this junction simply reads the signboard and proceeds optimally. The **signboard is the optimal value function $f_n(s_n)$**.

### 1.4 The Principle of Optimality (Bellman's Axiom)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bellman's Principle of Optimality:** *An optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision.*

In other words, **if the optimal route from City A to City Z passes through City M, then the sub-route from City M to City Z must itself be the optimal route from M to Z.** A student who forgets to check this principle before applying DP is guaranteed to lose marks in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Recursive Computational Engine

The computational procedure in Dynamic Programming is governed by a **functional recurrence equation** (often called the *Bellman Equation*). The general form is:

$$
f_n(s_n) = \mathop{\text{opt}}_{d_n \in D_n(s_n)} \left\{ r_n(s_n, d_n) \oplus f_{n+1}(s_{n+1}) \right\}
$$

where $\oplus$ is a generic operator that becomes **addition (for cost minimization)** or **addition (for profit maximization)** in the KTU syllabus. The symbol $\text{opt}$ is replaced by $\min$ or $\max$ depending on the problem objective.

### 2.2 The Two Recursion Directions

| Direction | Equation Form | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Backward Recursion** | $f_n(s_n) = \min_{d_n}\{r_n + f_{n+1}(s_{n+1})\}$ | Problems with a known terminal condition; stagecoach problem, shortest path. |
| **Forward Recursion** | $f_n(s_n) = \min_{d_n}\{r_n + f_{n-1}(s_{n-1})\}$ | Problems with a known initial state; some knapsack variants, allocation. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the 2024 Scheme, both directions are credited with equal marks. However, **backward recursion** is the *default* expected approach in ESE questions unless the question explicitly mentions forward recursion.

### 2.3 The 7-Step KTU Computational Procedure (Mandatory Framework)

Every DP problem solved for KTU 2024 examination **must** explicitly demonstrate the following steps. The model answer key allots a fixed number of marks for each:

1. **Step 1 — Stage Definition:** Partition the problem into $N$ discrete decision points. Identify the stage variable $n$.
2. **Step 2 — State Variable Definition:** Define $s_n$ as the minimal set of information required to make the next decision. *A state that retains unnecessary information is a state-violation in KTU valuation.*
3. **Step 3 — Decision Variable Definition:** Identify $d_n$ and the feasible set $D_n(s_n)$.
4. **Step 4 — Return Function:** Write $r_n(s_n, d_n)$ as the immediate stage contribution.
5. **Step 5 — State Transformation:** Express $s_{n+1}$ as a function of $s_n$ and $d_n$.
6. **Step 6 — Boundary Condition:** Specify $f_{N+1}(s_{N+1})$ for backward recursion (typically $0$).
7. **Step 7 — Recursive Equation & Solution:** Solve stage-by-stage and trace back the optimal policy.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **formula cheat sheet** for the Computational Procedure in DP. It is the most heavily tested area in Module 4.

| KTU Problem Type | Recursive Equation | Boundary Condition | Opt Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shortest Path (Stagecoach)** | $f_n(s_n) = \min_{d_n}\{c_{n,n+1} + f_{n+1}(s_{n+1})\}$ | $f_{N+1}(s_{N+1}) = 0$ | $\min$ |
| **Knapsack (0/1)** | $f_n(y) = \max_{x_n}\{w_n x_n + f_{n-1}(y - w_n x_n)\}$ | $f_0(y) = 0$ for $y \geq 0$ | $\max$ |
| **Resource Allocation** | $f_n(s_n) = \max_{d_n}\{g_n(s_n,d_n) + f_{n-1}(s_n - d_n)\}$ | $f_0(0) = 0$ | $\max$ |
| **Production Lot-Sizing** | $f_n(i) = \min_{d_n}\{ \text{cost}_n + h(i) + f_{n-1}(s) \}$ | $f_0(s) = 0$ | $\min$ |
| **Reliability Allocation** | $f_n(R) = \max\{ \text{reliability} \cdot f_{n-1}(R/r) \}$ | $f_0(R) = R$ | $\max$ |
| **Inventory (EOQ Type)** | $f_n(s) = \min_{d_n}\{ c\cdot d_n + h\cdot s + f_{n-1}(s + d_n - D) \}$ | $f_0(s) = 0$ | $\min$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** DP is used in **flight control trajectory optimization (NASA)**, **chemical process plant scheduling**, **VLSI circuit partitioning**, **dynamic economic dispatch in power systems**, and **vehicle routing in logistics**. Every KTU Module 4 question carries an implicit industrial context.

### 2.5 Worked Example 1 — The Classic Stagecoach Problem (Backward Recursion)

**Problem Statement:** A salesman must travel from Stage 1 (Origin O) to Stage 5 (Destination T). The state graph and inter-stage travel costs (in ₹) are given below. Find the cheapest route.

**Network Diagram (costs in ₹):**

| From \\ To | Stage 2 States | | Stage 3 States | | | Stage 4 States | | | Stage 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| | $A$ | $B$ | $C$ | $D$ | $E$ | $F$ | $G$ | $T$ |
| $O$ (Stage 1) | **4** | 6 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| $A$ (Stage 2) | — | — | **3** | 5 | 4 | — | — | — |
| $B$ (Stage 2) | — | — | 5 | **2** | 3 | — | — | — |
| $C$ (Stage 3) | — | — | — | — | — | 6 | 8 | — |
| $D$ (Stage 3) | — | — | — | — | — | 4 | **5** | — |
| $E$ (Stage 3) | — | — | — | — | — | 7 | 5 | — |
| $F$ (Stage 4) | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | 4 |
| $G$ (Stage 4) | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | 3 |

The bold values represent the optimal choices that the DP algorithm will discover.

**Step 1: Stage Definition** — $n \in \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, with 4 decision points (O, Stage 2, Stage 3, Stage 4) and the terminal arrival at $T$.

**Step 2: State Variable** — $s_n$ = city at which the salesman arrives at stage $n$.

**Step 3: Decision Variable** — $d_n$ = next city to travel to from $s_n$.

**Step 4: Return Function** — $r_n(s_n, d_n) = c_{s_n, d_n}$ (direct travel cost).

**Step 5: State Transformation** — $s_{n+1} = d_n$.

**Step 6: Boundary Condition** — $f_5(T) = 0$.

**Step 7: Recursive Equation & Solution** — see Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Stage-by-Stage Solution of Worked Example 1 (Stagecoach Problem)

**Stage 4 (Last decision stage):** The salesman is at either $F$ or $G$. From $F$ to $T$, cost is ₹4. From $G$ to $T$, cost is ₹3.

$$
f_4(F) = c_{F,T} + f_5(T) = 4 + 0 = 4
$$

$$
f_4(G) = c_{G,T} + f_5(T) = 3 + 0 = 3
$$

Optimal policy: $d_4^*(F) = T$, $d_4^*(G) = T$. The **4** in the network table next to $F \to T$ and **3** next to $G \to T$ is now justified.

**Stage 3:** The salesman can be at $C$, $D$, or $E$.

$$
f_3(C) = \min\{c_{C,F} + f_4(F),\ c_{C,G} + f_4(G)\}
$$

$$
f_3(C) = \min\{6 + 4,\ 8 + 3\} = \min\{10,\ 11\} = 10
$$

$$
f_3(D) = \min\{c_{D,F} + f_4(F),\ c_{D,G} + f_4(G)\}
$$

$$
f_3(D) = \min\{4 + 4,\ 5 + 3\} = \min\{8,\ 8\} = 8
$$

$$
f_3(E) = \min\{c_{E,F} + f_4(F),\ c_{E,G} + f_4(G)\}
$$

$$
f_3(E) = \min\{7 + 4,\ 5 + 3\} = \min\{11,\ 8\} = 8
$$

Optimal policies: $d_3^*(C) = F$, $d_3^*(D) = F$ or $G$ (both optimal), $d_3^*(E) = G$. Justifies the **6** next to $C \to F$, **4** next to $D \to F$, **5** next to $D \to G$, and **5** next to $E \to G$.

**Stage 2:** The salesman is at $A$ or $B$.

$$
f_2(A) = \min\{c_{A,C} + f_3(C),\ c_{A,D} + f_3(D),\ c_{A,E} + f_3(E)\}
$$

$$
f_2(A) = \min\{3 + 10,\ 5 + 8,\ 4 + 8\} = \min\{13,\ 13,\ 12\} = 12
$$

$$
f_2(B) = \min\{c_{B,C} + f_3(C),\ c_{B,D} + f_3(D),\ c_{B,E} + f_3(E)\}
$$

$$
f_2(B) = \min\{5 + 10,\ 2 + 8,\ 3 + 8\} = \min\{15,\ 10,\ 11\} = 10
$$

Optimal policies: $d_2^*(A) = E$, $d_2^*(B) = D$. Justifies the **3** next to $A \to C$, **5** next to $A \to D$, **4** next to $A \to E$, **5** next to $B \to C$, **2** next to $B \to D$, and **3** next to $B \to E$.

**Stage 1 (Origin):** The salesman is at $O$.

$$
f_1(O) = \min\{c_{O,A} + f_2(A),\ c_{O,B} + f_2(B)\}
$$

$$
f_1(O) = \min\{4 + 12,\ 6 + 10\} = \min\{16,\ 16\} = 16
$$

Optimal policies: $d_1^*(O) = A$ or $B$ (both give minimum cost ₹16). Justifies the **4** next to $O \to A$ and **6** next to $O \to B$.

**Optimal Path Tracing:** Choose $O \to A \to E \to G \to T$ → Total cost = $4 + 4 + 5 + 3 = \mathbf{16}$ ₹. Alternative optimal path: $O \to A \to E \to F \to T$ → $4 + 4 + 7 + 4 = 19$? No, that's not optimal. Re-check: $O \to A \to E \to G \to T$ gives $4+4+5+3=16$, confirmed. Another: $O \to B \to D \to F \to T$ gives $6+2+4+4=16$, also optimal. A third: $O \to B \to D \to G \to T$ gives $6+2+5+3=16$, also optimal.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — 0/1 Knapsack Problem (Forward Recursion)

**Problem Statement:** A hiker must select items from a list to maximize value subject to a knapsack weight capacity of $W = 10$ kg. The items are:

| Item | Weight $w_i$ (kg) | Value $v_i$ (₹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3 | 7 |
| 2 | 4 | 9 |
| 3 | 5 | 11 |
| 4 | 2 | 5 |

**Step 1:** Stage $i$ = item being considered, $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 2:** State $y$ = remaining knapsack capacity.

**Step 3:** Decision $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ — take the item or not.

**Step 4:** Return = $v_i \cdot x_i$ (value if taken).

**Step 5:** Transformation — $y_{i-1} = y - w_i \cdot x_i$.

**Step 6:** Boundary — $f_0(y) = 0$ for all $y \geq 0$.

**Step 7:** Recursive equation:

$$
f_i(y) = \max\{f_{i-1}(y),\ v_i + f_{i-1}(y - w_i)\}
$$

**Stage 1 ($i=1$, $w_1=3, v_1=7$):**

$$
f_1(y) = \max\{f_0(y),\ 7 + f_0(y-3)\}
$$

| $y$ | $f_0(y)$ | $7 + f_0(y-3)$ | $f_1(y)$ | $x_1^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | — | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | — | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | — | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | $7+0=7$ | 7 | 1 |
| 4 | 0 | $7+0=7$ | 7 | 1 |
| $\vdots$ | 0 | 7 | 7 | 1 |
| 10 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 1 |

**Stage 2 ($i=2$, $w_2=4, v_2=9$):**

$$
f_2(y) = \max\{f_1(y),\ 9 + f_1(y-4)\}
$$

| $y$ | $f_1(y)$ | $9 + f_1(y-4)$ | $f_2(y)$ | $x_2^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | — | 0 | 0 |
| 4 | 7 | $9+0=9$ | 9 | 1 |
| 7 | 7 | $9+7=16$ | 16 | 1 |
| 10 | 7 | $9+7=16$ | 16 | 1 |

**Stage 3 ($i=3$, $w_3=5, v_3=11$):**

$$
f_3(y) = \max\{f_2(y),\ 11 + f_2(y-5)\}
$$

| $y$ | $f_2(y)$ | $11 + f_2(y-5)$ | $f_3(y)$ | $x_3^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | — | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 9 | $11+0=11$ | 11 | 1 |
| 7 | 16 | $11+0=11$ | 16 | 0 |
| 10 | 16 | $11+9=20$ | 20 | 1 |

**Stage 4 ($i=4$, $w_4=2, v_4=5$):**

$$
f_4(y) = \max\{f_3(y),\ 5 + f_3(y-2)\}
$$

| $y$ | $f_3(y)$ | $5 + f_3(y-2)$ | $f_4(y)$ | $x_4^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | — | 0 | 0 |
| 5 | 11 | $5+0=5$ | 11 | 0 |
| 9 | 20 | $5+11=16$ | 20 | 0 |
| 10 | 20 | $5+16=21$ | 21 | 1 |

**Optimal Solution:** $f_4(10) = \mathbf{21}$ ₹. Traceback: $x_4^* = 1$ (take item 4), remaining capacity $= 10 - 2 = 8$. At $y=8$ in Stage 3, $f_3(8) = ?$ (between values; conservatively, take $x_3 = 0$ as in $y=7$ case). At $y=8$ in Stage 2, $f_2(8) = 16$, with $x_2^* = 1$ (take item 2), remaining $= 8-4=4$. At $y=4$ in Stage 1, $f_1(4) = 7$, with $x_1^* = 1$ (take item 1).

**Optimal selection: Items 1, 2, and 4 → Total weight = $3+4+2 = 9$ kg, Total value = $7+9+5 = 21$ ₹.** ✓

### 3.3 Numerical Trade-off Table — DP vs. Exhaustive Enumeration

| Items ($N$) | Enumeration ($2^N$) | DP States | Speed-up Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 32 | 15 | 2.1× |
| 10 | 1024 | 60 | 17× |
| 20 | 1048576 | 220 | 4767× |
| 50 | $1.12 \times 10^{15}$ | 1275 | $8.83 \times 10^{11}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> This table demonstrates why DP is the industrial standard: for $N=50$, full enumeration would take longer than the age of the universe, while DP solves it in milliseconds.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the DP Computational Procedure

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **execution flow** of the 7-step computational procedure that the KTU examiner expects to see in your answer script.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start KTU DP Problem]) --> s1[Step 1 Define Stage Variable n]
    s1 --> s2[Step 2 Define State Variable s_n]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3 Define Decision Variable d_n]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4 Write Return Function r_n]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5 Write State Transformation s_n+1]
    s5 --> s6[Step 6 Specify Boundary Condition f_N+1 or f_0]
    s6 --> s7[Step 7 Formulate Bellman Equation]
    s7 --> chkPrinciple{Principle of Optimality Satisfied?}
    chkPrinciple -- No --> failA[STOP DP Not Applicable]
    chkPrinciple -- Yes --> direction{Choose Direction}
    direction -- Backward --> bwd[Initialize f_N+1 equal 0]
    direction -- Forward --> fwd[Initialize f_0 equal 0]
    bwd --> solveA[Iterate n equal N down to 1]
    fwd --> solveB[Iterate n equal 1 up to N]
    solveA --> updateA[Compute f_n s_n and d_n star]
    solveB --> updateB[Compute f_n s_n and d_n star]
    updateA --> more1{All n Done?}
    updateB --> more2{All n Done?}
    more1 -- No --> solveA
    more2 -- No --> solveB
    more1 -- Yes --> traceA[Traceback Optimal Policy]
    more2 -- Yes --> traceB[Traceback Optimal Policy]
    traceA --> outputA([Output Optimal Cost and Path])
    traceB --> outputB([Output Optimal Cost and Path])
```

### 4.2 State Transition Network for the Stagecoach Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Stage 1 O Origin] -- cost 4 --> node2[Stage 2 A]
    node1 -- cost 6 --> node3[Stage 2 B]
    node2 -- cost 3 --> node4[Stage 3 C]
    node2 -- cost 5 --> node5[Stage 3 D]
    node2 -- cost 4 --> node6[Stage 3 E]
    node3 -- cost 5 --> node4
    node3 -- cost 2 --> node5
    node3 -- cost 3 --> node6
    node4 -- cost 6 --> node7[Stage 4 F]
    node4 -- cost 8 --> node8[Stage 4 G]
    node5 -- cost 4 --> node7
    node5 -- cost 5 --> node8
    node6 -- cost 7 --> node7
    node6 -- cost 5 --> node8
    node7 -- cost 4 --> node9[Stage 5 T Destination]
    node8 -- cost 3 --> node9
    node9 -- end --> endX([Optimal Path Found])
```

### 4.3 Recursive Equation Flow Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input s_n and n] --> loopA{For each d_n in D_n}
    loopA -- Pick d_n --> cand[Compute candidate value r_n + f_n+1]
    cand --> comp[Compare with current best]
    comp --> loopA
    loopA -- Done --> outputA[Store f_n s_n and d_n star]
    outputA --> decN[Decrement n]
    decN --> chkN{n equal 1?}
    chkN -- No --> inputA
    chkN -- Yes --> retr[Retrieve f_1 s_1 optimal value]
    retr --> doneA([DP Solve Complete])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. State and explain Bellman's Principle of Optimality.**
>
> **Model Answer:** *Bellman's Principle of Optimality states that "an optimal policy has the property that whatever the initial state and initial decision are, the remaining decisions must constitute an optimal policy with regard to the state resulting from the first decision."* **(1 mark)** *In other words, an optimal sequence of decisions has the property that regardless of the initial state and decision, the subsequent decisions must form an optimal policy with respect to the resulting state.* **(1 mark)** *This principle allows the decomposition of a complex multi-stage problem into simpler sub-problems, enabling recursive solution.* **(1 mark)**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between forward and backward recursion in Dynamic Programming.**
>
> **Model Answer:** *Backward recursion solves the problem starting from the terminal stage and works backwards, expressed as $f_n(s_n) = \min_{d_n}\{r_n(s_n,d_n) + f_{n+1}(s_{n+1})\}$ with boundary condition $f_{N+1}(s_{N+1}) = 0$.* **(1.5 marks)** *Forward recursion solves starting from the initial stage moving forward, expressed as $f_n(s_n) = \min_{d_n}\{r_n(s_n,d_n) + f_{n-1}(s_{n-1})\}$ with boundary condition $f_0(s_0)$ given.* **(1.5 marks)** *Backward recursion is preferred when terminal conditions are known; forward when initial conditions dominate.*

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**
> **Q3. (a) Solve the following shortest-route (stagecoach) problem using Dynamic Programming. The salesman must travel from node 1 to node 10. Stage-wise network costs are given below. Find the minimum-cost route.**
> 
> | From \ To | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | From \ To | 5 | 6 | 7 | From \ To | 8 | 9 | 10 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | **1** | 9 | 7 | 3 | 2 | **4** | 4 | 6 | 2 | **6** | 1 | 4 | 6 |
> | **2** | — | — | 4 | 1 | **5** | — | 3 | 1 | **7** | 6 | 3 | 8 |
> | **3** | — | — | 1 | 5 | | | | | | | | |
>
> **(7 marks)**

### 5.3 Part B Question A — Complete Model Solution

**Step 1: Stage Definition** — $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$ (4 decision stages: 1, Stage 2, Stage 3, Stage 4, then arrival at 10). Last stage is $n=4$ for transitions to nodes 8, 9, 10.

**Step 2: State Variable** — $s_n$ = current node at the start of stage $n$.

**Step 3: Decision** — $d_n$ = next node.

**Step 4: Return Function** — $r_n = c_{s_n, d_n}$ (table entry).

**Step 5: Transformation** — $s_{n+1} = d_n$.

**Step 6: Boundary** — $f_5(s_5) = 0$ where $s_5 \in \{8, 9, 10\}$.

**Step 7: Backward Recursion — Stage 4 Computation:**

For nodes 6 and 7, the destinations are 8, 9, 10.

$$
f_4(6) = \min\{1+0,\ 4+0,\ 6+0\} = 1
$$
Optimal: $6 \to 8$.

$$
f_4(7) = \min\{6+0,\ 3+0,\ 8+0\} = 3
$$
Optimal: $7 \to 9$.

**Stage 3 Computation (nodes 4 and 5):**

$$
f_3(4) = \min\{4+f_4(6),\ 6+f_4(7),\ 2+0\} = \min\{4+1,\ 6+3,\ 2+0\} = 2
$$
Optimal: $4 \to 10$.

$$
f_3(5) = \min\{3+f_4(6),\ 1+f_4(7)\} = \min\{3+1,\ 1+3\} = \min\{4,\ 4\} = 4
$$
Optimal: $5 \to 6$ or $5 \to 7$.

**Stage 2 Computation (nodes 2 and 3):**

$$
f_2(2) = \min\{4+f_3(4),\ 1+f_3(5)\} = \min\{4+2,\ 1+4\} = \min\{6,\ 5\} = 5
$$
Optimal: $2 \to 5$.

$$
f_2(3) = \min\{1+f_3(4),\ 5+f_3(5)\} = \min\{1+2,\ 5+4\} = \min\{3,\ 9\} = 3
$$
Optimal: $3 \to 4$.

**Stage 1 Computation (node 1):**

$$
f_1(1) = \min\{9+f_2(2),\ 7+f_2(3),\ 3+f_3(4),\ 2+f_3(5)\}
$$

$$
f_1(1) = \min\{9+5,\ 7+3,\ 3+2,\ 2+4\} = \min\{14,\ 10,\ 5,\ 6\} = 5
$$
Optimal: $1 \to 4$.

**Optimal Route Traceback:** $1 \to 4 \to 10$ with total cost $= 3 + 2 = \mathbf{5}$.

> **(b) Explain the seven-step computational procedure of Dynamic Programming. What is the role of the state variable in DP? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The seven steps of the DP computational procedure are: **(1 mark each)**
1. **Stage Definition:** Partition the problem into $N$ stages indexed by $n$, each representing a point where a decision is taken.
2. **State Variable:** Define $s_n$ — the link between past decisions and future optimization.
3. **Decision Variable:** Define $d_n$ from the feasible set $D_n(s_n)$.
4. **Return Function:** $r_n(s_n, d_n)$ measures the immediate stage contribution.
5. **State Transformation:** $s_{n+1} = T(s_n, d_n)$ governs state evolution.
6. **Boundary Condition:** Specifies $f_{N+1}(s_{N+1})$ or $f_0(s_0)$.
7. **Recursive Equation:** $f_n(s_n) = \text{opt}_{d_n}\{r_n + f_{n+1}(s_{n+1})\}$.

**Role of State Variable (2 marks):** The state variable $s_n$ is the *connective tissue* of the DP algorithm. It contains *all* the information necessary to make the optimal decision at stage $n$ — nothing more, nothing less. If the state is defined too broadly, the DP table becomes large and computation is wasted; if too narrow, the Principle of Optimality is violated and the result is incorrect. The state variable ensures that the future of the process is determined only by its present, not its past.

### 5.4 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Q4. (a) Solve the following 0/1 knapsack problem using Dynamic Programming. Capacity $W = 8$ kg. Items: A (weight 3, value 7), B (weight 4, value 10), C (weight 5, value 12), D (weight 2, value 4). Find the optimal subset. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A company has 4 units of capital to allocate among 3 projects. The returns (in ₹ lakhs) for each project based on the units allocated are given below. Use DP to find the optimal allocation. (7 marks)**
> 
> | Capital \ Project | Project 1 | Project 2 | Project 3 |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
> | 1 | 5 | 4 | 3 |
> | 2 | 9 | 8 | 7 |
> | 3 | 12 | 11 | 10 |
> | 4 | 14 | 13 | 12 |

### 5.5 Part B Question B — Complete Model Solution

#### 5.5.1 Solution to Q4(a) — Knapsack Problem

**Formulation:** Maximize $Z = 7x_1 + 10x_2 + 12x_3 + 4x_4$ subject to $3x_1 + 4x_2 + 5x_3 + 2x_4 \leq 8$ and $x_i \in \{0,1\}$.

**Recursion:** $f_i(y) = \max\{f_{i-1}(y),\ v_i + f_{i-1}(y - w_i)\}$ with $f_0(y) = 0$.

**Stage 1 (Item A: $w=3, v=7$):**

| $y$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 7 |

**Stage 2 (Item B: $w=4, v=10$):**

| $y$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 7 | 7 |
| $10+f_1(y-4)$ | — | — | — | — | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 17 |
| $f_2(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 17 |

**Stage 3 (Item C: $w=5, v=12$):**

| $y$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_2(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 17 |
| $12+f_2(y-5)$ | — | — | — | — | — | 12 | 12 | 12 | 22 |
| $f_3(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7 | 10 | 12 | 12 | 12 | 22 |

**Stage 4 (Item D: $w=2, v=4$):**

| $y$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_3(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 7 | 10 | 12 | 12 | 12 | 22 |
| $4+f_3(y-2)$ | — | — | 4 | 4 | 14 | 16 | 16 | 16 | 26 |
| $f_4(y)$ | 0 | 0 | 4 | 7 | 14 | 16 | 16 | 16 | 26 |

**Optimal Solution:** $f_4(8) = 26$. Traceback: $x_4 = 1$ (take D), remaining $= 8-2=6$. $f_3(6)=12$, with $x_3=0$ (skip C). $f_2(6)=10$, with $x_2=1$ (take B), remaining $= 6-4=2$. $f_1(2)=0$, with $x_1=0$ (skip A).

**Optimal subset: B + D**, weight = $4+2 = 6 \leq 8$, value = $10+4 = \mathbf{14}$? Wait — let me recheck $f_4(8)=26$ trace.

Correction: $f_4(8)=26$ means take D ($x_4=1$), check $f_3(6)$. Looking at Stage 3 row: $f_3(6)=12$. At $y=6$, $12+f_2(1)=12+0=12 < f_2(6)=10$? No, $12 > 10$, so $x_3=1$ would give 12, not 10. The maximum is 12 with $x_3=1$ taking C, but $f_2(1) = 0$ so $f_3(6) = \max\{10, 12+0\}=12$. This means we take C.

Re-trace: $f_4(8)=26$ → take D, $f_3(6)=12$ → take C, $f_2(1)=0$ → skip B, $f_1(1)=0$ → skip A. Total: C+D, weight $= 5+2=7$, value $= 12+4=16$? But $f_3(6) = 12$ should equal C alone... Yes $f_3(6) = 12$ with C only.

Re-trace correction: $f_4(8) = 26$, $x_4 = 1$ (D taken), $y_{\text{remaining}}=6$. $f_3(6)=12$, $x_3 = 1$ (C taken), $y_{\text{remaining}}=1$. $f_2(1)=0$, $x_2 = 0$. $f_1(1)=0$, $x_1=0$. Items: **C and D**, weight $7 \leq 8$, value $= 12+4 = 16$? But the table says $f_4(8)=26$. There's a mistake — let me recompute the Stage 4 row.

Recomputing Stage 4: $f_4(y) = \max\{f_3(y), 4 + f_3(y-2)\}$.
- $y=4$: $\max\{10, 4+f_3(2)\} = \max\{10, 4+0\} = 10$. (Not 14 — I had error).
- $y=8$: $\max\{22, 4+f_3(6)\} = \max\{22, 4+12\} = \max\{22, 16\} = 22$.

So $f_4(8) = 22$, $x_4 = 0$ (skip D). Items: C alone, value $12$? No — let me recheck Stage 3.

Stage 3: $f_3(y) = \max\{f_2(y), 12 + f_2(y-5)\}$.
- $y=8$: $\max\{17, 12+f_2(3)\} = \max\{17, 12+7\} = \max\{17, 19\} = 19$. (Not 22 — I had error earlier).

Recompute Stage 4 at $y=8$: $f_4(8) = \max\{19, 4+f_3(6)\} = \max\{19, 4+12\} = 19$.

Re-trace: $f_4(8) = 19$, $x_4 = 0$. $f_3(8) = 19$, $x_3 = 1$ (take C). $f_2(3) = 7$, $x_2 = 0$. $f_1(3) = 7$, $x_1 = 1$ (take A).

**Optimal subset: A + C**, weight $= 3+5=8$, value $= 7+12 = \mathbf{19}$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Stage Re-computation:** The first attempt shows the **most common valuation error in DP**: an arithmetic mistake in the cumulative stage tables propagates to give a wrong final answer. In the KTU answer script, you are required to **show every stage table in full**. A single omitted row costs 1 mark; an error in $f_2(y)$ at a single $y$ cascades to invalidate the final answer, costing 3–4 marks. **Always re-verify your DP table column-by-column before writing the final traceback.**

#### 5.5.2 Solution to Q4(b) — Resource Allocation Problem

**Formulation:** Maximize $Z = g_1(d_1) + g_2(d_2) + g_3(d_3)$ subject to $d_1 + d_2 + d_3 \leq 4$, $d_i \geq 0$ integer, where $g_i$ is read from the table.

**Recursion:** $f_n(s_n) = \max_{d_n}\{g_n(d_n) + f_{n-1}(s_n - d_n)\}$ with $f_0(0) = 0$.

**Stage 1 (Project 1):**

| $s_1$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $f_1(s_1)$ | 0 | 5 | 9 | 12 | 14 |
| $d_1^*$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |

**Stage 2 (Project 2):** $f_2(s_2) = \max_{d_2 \in [0,s_2]}\{g_2(d_2) + f_1(s_2 - d_2)\}$.

| $s_2$ | Computation | $f_2(s_2)$ | $d_2^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0+f_1(0)=0$ | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | $\max\{0+f_1(1)=5,\ 4+f_1(0)=4\}=5$ | 5 | 0 |
| 2 | $\max\{0+9=9,\ 4+5=9,\ 8+0=8\}=9$ | 9 | 0 or 1 |
| 3 | $\max\{0+12,\ 4+9,\ 8+5,\ 11+0\}=\max\{12,13,13,11\}=13$ | 13 | 1 or 2 |
| 4 | $\max\{0+14,\ 4+12,\ 8+9,\ 11+5,\ 13+0\}=\max\{14,16,17,16,13\}=17$ | 17 | 2 |

**Stage 3 (Project 3):** $f_3(s_3) = \max_{d_3 \in [0,s_3]}\{g_3(d_3) + f_2(s_3 - d_3)\}$.

| $s_3$ | Computation | $f_3(s_3)$ | $d_3^*$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0+f_2(0)=0$ | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | $\max\{0+5,\ 3+0\}=5$ | 5 | 0 |
| 2 | $\max\{0+9,\ 3+5,\ 7+0\}=9$ | 9 | 0 |
| 3 | $\max\{0+13,\ 3+9,\ 7+5,\ 10+0\}=13$ | 13 | 0 |
| 4 | $\max\{0+17,\ 3+13,\ 7+9,\ 10+5,\ 12+0\}=\max\{17,16,16,15,12\}=17$ | 17 | 0 |

**Optimal Allocation:** $d_3^* = 0$ (Project 3 gets nothing), remaining $s_2 = 4$, $d_2^* = 2$ (Project 2 gets 2 units), remaining $s_1 = 2$, $d_1^* = 2$ (Project 1 gets 2 units).

**Total Return:** $f_1(2) + g_2(2) + g_3(0) = 9 + 8 + 0 = 17$ ₹ lakhs. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Traceback Discipline:** A frequent error in KTU valuation: students write only the **final value** but forget to **traceback the optimal policy** $d_n^*$. The final numerical value fetches ~3 marks; the traceback fetches ~4 marks. **Always show the traceback explicitly** with each $d_n^*$ and the remaining state.

### 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Computational Procedure in Dynamic Programming (Module 4):**

- **Axiom:** *Bellman's Principle of Optimality* — an optimal sub-policy is itself optimal; this is the *precondition* for DP applicability.
- **Algorithm Skeleton:** Always write the 7 steps explicitly: stage, state, decision, return, transformation, boundary, recursion.
- **Boundary Condition:** $f_{N+1}(s_{N+1}) = 0$ (backward) or $f_0(s_0)$ given (forward).
- **Backward vs Forward:** Choose backward recursion when terminal state is known; forward when initial state is known. KTU defaults to backward.
- **State Definition Discipline:** The state $s_n$ must capture **all** information needed for future decisions — neither more nor less. A state with redundant dimensions is a state violation.
- **Traceback is Mandatory:** Solving $f_n(s_n)$ is half the work. KTU 2024 valuation explicitly marks the optimal policy traceback at 3–4 marks.
- **Optimal Value Function $f_n(s_n)$:** Represents the *best achievable* return from stage $n$ to the terminal stage, given the state $s_n$.
- **Computational Cost:** DP is *polynomial* ($O(N \cdot \text{states})$) versus enumeration's *exponential* ($2^N$) — this is its industrial appeal.
- **Common Mistakes:** Skipping the principle-of-optimality check, errors in stage tables, omitting traceback, and wrong direction of recursion.
- **Engineering Domains:** DP is used in *trajectory optimization, dynamic economic dispatch, inventory management, resource allocation, shortest path networks, and structural reliability design* — expect a 2-mark question linking DP to a real engineering system.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples Illustrating the Calculus Method of Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples Illustrating the Calculus Method of Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
The **Calculus Method of Solution** in Engineering Optimization refers to a classical, analytical approach used to determine the stationary (optimum) points of a continuous and differentiable objective function by equating its first derivatives to zero (necessary conditions) and then verifying the nature of these stationary points using second-order derivative tests (sufficient conditions). For a function $f(\mathbf{x})$ where $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)^T$, the method relies on:

- **Gradient Condition:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$
- **Hessian Condition:** Definiteness of the Hessian matrix $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ decides whether the stationary point is a **local minimum**, **local maximum**, or **saddle point**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Hiking to the Mountain Peak"**
> Imagine you are blindfolded on a hilly terrain and asked to find the lowest valley. You can only feel the slope beneath your feet. The **Calculus Method** says:
> - **First, find where the slope is flat (zero gradient).** That is the bottom of the hill or top of the hill (stationary point).
> - **Then, check the curvature of the ground.** If the ground curves *upward* like a bowl ($\cup$), you are at a minimum. If it curves *downward* like a dome ($\cap$), you are at a maximum. If it curves one way in one direction and another way in another direction, it is a saddle (a mountain pass).
> - In 2D or 3D, you check the curvature in *every* direction using the **Hessian matrix**.

### 1.3 Syllabus Highlights
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlights for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - The Calculus Method applies only to **unconstrained** continuous differentiable functions.
> - Necessary condition $\nabla f = 0$ is **first-order**.
> - Sufficient conditions use the **second-order Hessian** test.
> - Boundary points are NOT considered in pure calculus method (these fall under constrained optimization).
> - The method is **analytical** and yields **exact** solutions, unlike numerical search methods.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Order of differentiability required:** $f(\mathbf{x})$ must be at least **twice continuously differentiable** ($C^2$).
- **Stationary point**: a point $\mathbf{x}^*$ where $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Hessian matrix**: square symmetric matrix of second partial derivatives of size $n \times n$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step
The Calculus Method follows a rigid, sequential procedure:

- **Step 1: Verify Differentiability**
  The function $f(\mathbf{x})$ must be continuous and at least twice differentiable in the region of interest. If not, the calculus method is inapplicable.

- **Step 2: Compute the Gradient Vector**
  The gradient $\nabla f(\mathbf{x})$ is the column vector of first-order partial derivatives:
  $$\nabla f(\mathbf{x}) = \left[ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1},\ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2},\ \dots,\ \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_n} \right]^T$$
  *Why?* — The gradient points in the direction of steepest ascent. At an optimum, there is no preferred direction, so the gradient must vanish.

- **Step 3: Solve the Stationarity Equations**
  Set each component of the gradient to zero:
  $$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$
  Solve the resulting system of $n$ equations in $n$ unknowns to obtain candidate points $\mathbf{x}^*$.

- **Step 4: Construct the Hessian Matrix**
  The Hessian is the symmetric matrix of second partial derivatives:
  $$H(\mathbf{x}) = \nabla^2 f(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_n} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2 \partial x_n} \\[6pt] \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_1} & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n \partial x_2} & \cdots & \dfrac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_n^2} \end{bmatrix}$$

- **Step 5: Apply the Second-Order Sufficient Conditions**
  Evaluate $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ at each candidate point and check definiteness:
  - If $H$ is **positive definite** $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local minimum**.
  - If $H$ is **negative definite** $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local maximum**.
  - If $H$ is **indefinite** $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **saddle point**.

- **Step 6: Definiteness Testing (Leading Principal Minors)**
  For a symmetric matrix $H$, examine the signs of leading principal minors $\Delta_1, \Delta_2, \dots, \Delta_n$:
  - $H$ is **positive definite** if all $\Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$.
  - $H$ is **negative definite** if $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$ (i.e., signs alternate starting with $\Delta_1 < 0$).
  - If some are zero and rest same sign $\Rightarrow$ **positive/negative semi-definite** (test inconclusive).
  - Mixed signs $\Rightarrow$ **indefinite**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Interpretation |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Necessary condition (unconstrained) | $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ | Stationary point |
| 2 | Sufficient condition for local min | $H(\mathbf{x}^*) > 0$ (positive definite) | All eigenvalues $> 0$ |
| 3 | Sufficient condition for local max | $H(\mathbf{x}^*) < 0$ (negative definite) | All eigenvalues $< 0$ |
| 4 | Saddle point condition | $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ indefinite | Mixed eigenvalue signs |
| 5 | Leading principal minor test (PD) | $\Delta_k > 0\ \forall k$ | Positive definite |
| 6 | Leading principal minor test (ND) | $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0\ \forall k$ | Negative definite |
| 7 | Single variable min/max | $f'(x^*) = 0,\ f''(x^*) > 0\ /\ < 0$ | One-dimensional case |
| 8 | Gradient (column vector) | $\nabla f = \left[\dfrac{\partial f}{\partial x_i}\right]^T$ | Direction of steepest ascent |
| 9 | Hessian symmetry | $H_{ij} = H_{ji}$ (Clairaut) | $f \in C^2$ |
| 10 | Eigenvalue equivalence | PD $\iff$ all $\lambda_i > 0$ | Alternative test |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications:**
> - **Structural Design:** Minimizing weight of a truss subject to stress constraints (with penalty method).
> - **Process Optimization:** Maximizing yield in chemical reactors, minimizing energy in distillation columns.
> - **Machine Learning:** Loss function minimization in linear regression, logistic regression (closed-form solution using normal equations).
> - **Control Systems:** Minimizing performance index (e.g., LQR — Linear Quadratic Regulator).
> - **Economics:** Profit maximization, cost minimization, utility optimization.
> - **Mechanical Design:** Optimal gear ratios, optimal cross-section, optimal spring design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 EXAMPLE 1 — Single Variable Unconstrained Minimization

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2$.

**Step 1: Compute the first derivative.**
$$f'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 2\right)$$
$$f'(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 9$$

**Step 2: Set the first derivative to zero (stationarity condition).**
$$3x^2 - 12x + 9 = 0$$
$$3(x^2 - 4x + 3) = 0$$
$$3(x - 1)(x - 3) = 0$$
$$x_1^* = 1, \quad x_2^* = 3$$

**Step 3: Compute the second derivative.**
$$f''(x) = \frac{d}{dx}(3x^2 - 12x + 9) = 6x - 12$$

**Step 4: Apply the second-order test at each candidate point.**

At $x_1^* = 1$:
$$f''(1) = 6(1) - 12 = -6 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{local maximum}$$

At $x_2^* = 3$:
$$f''(3) = 6(3) - 12 = +6 > 0 \Rightarrow \text{local minimum}$$

**Step 5: Compute the optimal function values.**

At $x = 1$ (local maximum):
$$f(1) = 1^3 - 6(1)^2 + 9(1) + 2 = 1 - 6 + 9 + 2 = 6$$

At $x = 3$ (local minimum):
$$f(3) = 3^3 - 6(3)^2 + 9(3) + 2 = 27 - 54 + 27 + 2 = 2$$

**Conclusion:** The function has a **local maximum** of $6$ at $x = 1$ and a **local minimum** of $2$ at $x = 3$.

---

### 3.2 EXAMPLE 2 — Two-Variable Unconstrained Minimization (Full Hessian Analysis)

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 - 4x_1 - 6x_2 + 13$.

**Step 1: Compute the first partial derivatives.**
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 - 6$$

**Step 2: Set the gradient to zero (necessary condition).**
$$2x_1 - 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 2$$
$$2x_2 - 6 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 3$$

So $\mathbf{x}^* = (2,\ 3)^T$.

**Step 3: Compute the second partial derivatives (Hessian entries).**
$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = 0$$

**Step 4: Construct the Hessian matrix.**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Apply the leading principal minor test.**
$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$$
$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (2)(2) - (0)(0) = 4 > 0$$

Since both $\Delta_1 > 0$ and $\Delta_2 > 0$, the Hessian is **positive definite**.

**Step 6: Compute the minimum value.**
$$f(2, 3) = (2)^2 + (3)^2 - 4(2) - 6(3) + 13 = 4 + 9 - 8 - 18 + 13 = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The function has a **strict local (and global) minimum** of $f^* = 0$ at $\mathbf{x}^* = (2, 3)^T$.

---

### 3.3 EXAMPLE 3 — Two-Variable Function with a Saddle Point

**Problem:** Find and classify the stationary points of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 - x_2^2$.

**Step 1: Compute the gradient.**
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = -2x_2$$

**Step 2: Set the gradient to zero.**
$$2x_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0$$
$$-2x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 0$$

So $\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 0)^T$ is the only stationary point.

**Step 3: Compute the Hessian matrix.**
$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = -2, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = 0$$

$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Apply the leading principal minor test.**
$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$$
$$\Delta_2 = \det(H) = (2)(-2) - (0)(0) = -4 < 0$$

Signs are **mixed** ($+$, $-$) $\Rightarrow$ $H$ is **indefinite**.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**
Since the Hessian is indefinite, $\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 0)$ is a **saddle point** (neither a max nor a min). The function value is $f(0,0) = 0$, but in any neighborhood there are points where $f < 0$ and points where $f > 0$.

---

### 3.4 EXAMPLE 4 — Three-Variable Function (Engineering Application)

**Problem:** A company produces three products with profits given by:
$$f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = 5x_1 + 6x_2 + 4x_3 - x_1^2 - x_2^2 - x_3^2$$
Find the production levels that maximize profit.

**Step 1: Compute the gradient (first partial derivatives).**
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 5 - 2x_1$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 6 - 2x_2$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_3} = 4 - 2x_3$$

**Step 2: Set the gradient to zero (stationarity).**
$$5 - 2x_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 2.5$$
$$6 - 2x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 3.0$$
$$4 - 2x_3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_3 = 2.0$$

So $\mathbf{x}^* = (2.5,\ 3.0,\ 2.0)^T$.

**Step 3: Construct the Hessian matrix.**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Apply the principal minor test.**
$$\Delta_1 = -2 < 0$$
$$\Delta_2 = (-2)(-2) - 0 = 4 > 0$$
$$\Delta_3 = \det(H) = (-2)^3 = -8 < 0$$

Signs alternate: $(-)^1,\ (+),\ (-)^3$ — i.e., $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0$ holds for all $k$.
Therefore, $H$ is **negative definite**.

**Step 5: Conclusion.**
Since $H < 0$, $\mathbf{x}^*$ is a **local maximum** (profit maximum).

**Step 6: Maximum profit value.**
$$f^* = 5(2.5) + 6(3.0) + 4(2.0) - (2.5)^2 - (3.0)^2 - (2.0)^2$$
$$= 12.5 + 18.0 + 8.0 - 6.25 - 9.0 - 4.0$$
$$= 38.5 - 19.25 = 19.25$$

---

### 3.5 EXAMPLE 5 — Three-Variable Problem (Mechanical Design Context)

**Problem:** Minimize the function $f(x_1, x_2, x_3) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 + 3x_3^2 - 4x_1 - 8x_2 - 12x_3 + 50$.

**Step 1: First partial derivatives.**
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 4$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 - 8$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_3} = 6x_3 - 12$$

**Step 2: Solve the system of equations.**
$$2x_1 - 4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 2$$
$$4x_2 - 8 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 2$$
$$6x_3 - 12 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_3 = 2$$

**Step 3: Construct the Hessian matrix.**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{bmatrix}$$

This is already a diagonal matrix.

**Step 4: Principal minor test.**
$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$$
$$\Delta_2 = (2)(4) = 8 > 0$$
$$\Delta_3 = (2)(4)(6) = 48 > 0$$

All leading principal minors are positive $\Rightarrow$ $H$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 5: Compute the minimum value.**
$$f^* = (2)^2 + 2(2)^2 + 3(2)^2 - 4(2) - 8(2) - 12(2) + 50$$
$$= 4 + 8 + 12 - 8 - 16 - 24 + 50$$
$$= 24 - 48 + 50 = 26$$

**Conclusion:** The minimum value is $f^* = 26$ at $\mathbf{x}^* = (2, 2, 2)^T$.

---

### 3.6 EXAMPLE 6 — Case with No Real Stationary Point

**Problem:** Find the stationary points of $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2$.

**Step 1: Compute the gradient.**
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 = 0 \Rightarrow x_1 = 0$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 2x_2 = 0 \Rightarrow x_2 = 0$$

**Step 2: Hessian evaluation.**
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$
Both $\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$ and $\Delta_2 = 4 > 0$, so $H$ is positive definite.

**Step 3: Conclusion.**
$\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 0)^T$ is a **strict local (and global) minimum** with $f^* = 2$.

> [!NOTE]
> This example illustrates that a simple positive-definite quadratic always has a unique stationary point, which is the global minimum.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Calculus Method of Solution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Objective Function f of x"] --> B["Verify: f is C2 Continuous and Differentiable"]
    B --> C["STEP 1: Compute Gradient Vector grad f of x"]
    C --> D["STEP 2: Set grad f of x EQUALS Zero Vector"]
    D --> E{"STEP 3: Solve the System of n Equations"}
    E -- "No Real Solution" --> F["EXIT: No Unconstrained Optimum Exists"]
    E -- "Solution Found" --> G["STEP 4: Construct Hessian Matrix H of x"]
    G --> H["STEP 5: Evaluate H at each x star"]
    H --> I["STEP 6: Apply Leading Principal Minor Test"]
    I --> J{"Check Sign Pattern of Deltas"}
    J -- "All Delta k GREATER 0" --> K["RESULT: Local MINIMUM at x star"]
    J -- "Alternating Signs" --> L["RESULT: Local MAXIMUM at x star"]
    J -- "Mixed Signs" --> M["RESULT: SADDLE POINT at x star"]
    J -- "Inconclusive" --> N["RESULT: Higher Order Test Required"]
    K --> O["END: Report Optimal Value f x star"]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hessian Definiteness Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Hessian Matrix H of x star"] --> B["Compute Leading Principal Minors D1 D2 ... Dn"]
    B --> C{"Sign Pattern Analysis"}
    C -- "All Delta k POSITIVE" --> D["H is Positive Definite: LOCAL MIN"]
    C -- "Alternating NEG POS NEG ..." --> E["H is Negative Definite: LOCAL MAX"]
    C -- "Mixed Signs" --> F["H is Indefinite: SADDLE POINT"]
    C -- "Some Delta k ZERO Rest Same Sign" --> G["H is Semi Definite: INCONCLUSIVE"]
    C -- "Eigenvalue Test All lambda POSITIVE" --> H["Alternative: PD via Eigenvalues"]
    C -- "Eigenvalue Test All lambda NEGATIVE" --> I["Alternative: ND via Eigenvalues"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Engineering Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real World Engineering Problem"] --> B["Formulate Objective Function f of x"]
    B --> C{"Unconstrained Problem?"}
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Apply Calculus Method"]
    C -- "No" --> E["Use Lagrangian or KKT Conditions"]
    D --> F["Stationary Point x star"]
    F --> G["Hessian Definiteness Test"]
    G --> H["Classify: Min Max or Saddle"]
    H --> I["Optimal Design Parameters"]
    I --> J["Implement in Real System"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a point $\mathbf{x}^*$ to be a local minimum of an unconstrained differentiable function $f(\mathbf{x})$.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Necessary Condition (First-Order):** The gradient at $\mathbf{x}^*$ must vanish, i.e., $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$.
- **Sufficient Condition (Second-Order):** The Hessian matrix $H(\mathbf{x}^*)$ must be **positive definite**, i.e., all its leading principal minors $\Delta_k > 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$, or equivalently all its eigenvalues are positive.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q2. What is a saddle point? How is it identified using the Hessian matrix?**

**Model Answer:**
A **saddle point** is a stationary point that is neither a local maximum nor a local minimum — the function increases in some directions and decreases in others. It is identified by the **Hessian matrix** being **indefinite** (mixed signs in the leading principal minors, or eigenvalues of opposite signs). Example: $f(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 - x_2^2$ has a saddle at the origin.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style]**

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Find the stationary points of $f(x_1, x_2) = 3x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 4x_1 x_2 - 8x_1 + 12$ and classify them using the Hessian test. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** First partial derivatives.
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 6x_1 - 4x_2 - 8$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 - 4x_1$$

**Step 2:** Set gradient to zero.
$$6x_1 - 4x_2 - 8 = 0 \quad \cdots (1)$$
$$-4x_1 + 4x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = x_1 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute (2) into (1).
$$6x_1 - 4x_1 - 8 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2x_1 = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 4$$
$$x_2 = 4$$

So $\mathbf{x}^* = (4, 4)^T$.

**Step 4:** Compute second partial derivatives.
$$\frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1^2} = 6, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_2^2} = 4, \quad \frac{\partial^2 f}{\partial x_1 \partial x_2} = -4$$

**Step 5:** Construct the Hessian.
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -4 \\ -4 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6:** Principal minor test.
$$\Delta_1 = 6 > 0$$
$$\Delta_2 = (6)(4) - (-4)(-4) = 24 - 16 = 8 > 0$$

Both $\Delta_1, \Delta_2 > 0 \Rightarrow$ Hessian is **positive definite**.

**Step 7:** Conclusion.
$\mathbf{x}^* = (4, 4)^T$ is a **local minimum**.
$$f(4,4) = 3(16) + 2(16) - 4(16) - 8(4) + 12 = 48 + 32 - 64 - 32 + 12 = -4$$

**(b)** A firm manufactures two products with cost function $C(x_1, x_2) = x_1^2 + 2x_2^2 - 5x_1 - 6x_2 + 30$. Find the production levels that minimize cost and determine the minimum cost. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** First partial derivatives.
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x_1} = 2x_1 - 5$$
$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial x_2} = 4x_2 - 6$$

**Step 2:** Set to zero.
$$2x_1 - 5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 2.5$$
$$4x_2 - 6 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 1.5$$

**Step 3:** Hessian.
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Principal minors.
$$\Delta_1 = 2 > 0, \quad \Delta_2 = 8 > 0$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Positive definite** $\Rightarrow$ **local minimum**.

**Step 5:** Minimum cost.
$$C(2.5, 1.5) = (2.5)^2 + 2(1.5)^2 - 5(2.5) - 6(1.5) + 30$$
$$= 6.25 + 4.5 - 12.5 - 9 + 30 = 19.25$$

**Result:** Produce $x_1 = 2.5$ and $x_2 = 1.5$ units; **minimum cost = 19.25**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Determine the maximum value of $f(x_1, x_2) = 2x_1 + 3x_2 - x_1^2 - x_2^2$. Show all necessary and sufficient conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Gradient.
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 2 - 2x_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 1$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 3 - 2x_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = 1.5$$

**Step 2:** Hessian.
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Principal minors.
$$\Delta_1 = -2 < 0, \quad \Delta_2 = 4 > 0$$
Alternating signs with $\Delta_1 < 0 \Rightarrow$ **Negative definite**.

**Step 4:** Conclusion.
$\mathbf{x}^* = (1, 1.5)^T$ is a **local maximum**.
$$f_{\max} = 2(1) + 3(1.5) - 1 - 2.25 = 2 + 4.5 - 3.25 = 3.25$$

**(b)** Find the stationary point of $f(x_1, x_2) = 4x_1 x_2 - x_1^2 - x_2^2$ and classify it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Gradient.
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_1} = 4x_2 - 2x_1 = 0 \quad \cdots (1)$$
$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x_2} = 4x_1 - 2x_2 = 0 \quad \cdots (2)$$

**Step 2:** From (1): $x_2 = x_1/2$. Substitute into (2):
$$4x_1 - 2\left(\frac{x_1}{2}\right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4x_1 - x_1 = 3x_1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 = 0$$
$$x_2 = 0$$

**Step 3:** Hessian.
$$H = \begin{bmatrix} -2 & 4 \\ 4 & -2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Principal minors.
$$\Delta_1 = -2 < 0$$
$$\Delta_2 = (-2)(-2) - (4)(4) = 4 - 16 = -12 < 0$$

**Step 5:** Classification.
Since both $\Delta_1 < 0$ and $\Delta_2 < 0$, the condition for **negative definite** requires $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0$:
- $k=1$: $-\Delta_1 = 2 > 0$ ✓
- $k=2$: $\Delta_2 = -12 < 0$ ✗

So $H$ is **not negative definite**. With both leading principal minors negative but second test failing, this is actually **indefinite** by checking eigenvalues: $\lambda = 2$ and $\lambda = -6$. Mixed signs $\Rightarrow$ **saddle point**.

**Step 6:** Conclusion.
$\mathbf{x}^* = (0, 0)^T$ is a **saddle point** with $f(0,0) = 0$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Second-Derivative Test:** Many students only solve $\nabla f = 0$ and stop. You MUST classify the point using the Hessian — otherwise you lose 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Wrong Sign in the Hessian:** Mix-up between $\partial^2 f / \partial x_1^2$ and $\partial^2 f / \partial x_2^2$ is common. Always re-differentiate from the original function.
> 3. **Misapplying the Principal Minor Test:** For **negative definite**, the condition is $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0$ for ALL $k$ — students often just check $\Delta_1 < 0$ and assume the rest. You must verify all.
> 4. **Not Verifying Differentiability:** Calculus method is invalid for non-smooth functions (e.g., $|x|$, $\max(\cdot)$, $\min(\cdot)$).
> 5. **Confusing Saddle Points with Extrema:** If eigenvalues (or principal minors) give mixed signs, NEVER call it a maximum or minimum — call it a saddle.
> 6. **Arithmetic Errors in Solving $\nabla f = 0$:** Always re-substitute your answer into the gradient to verify it equals zero.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] The **Calculus Method** is for **unconstrained** problems with **$C^2$ continuous** functions.
- [ ] **Necessary condition:** $\nabla f(\mathbf{x}^*) = \mathbf{0}$ — gives candidate points only.
- [ ] **Sufficient condition:** Hessian positive/negative definite — confirms the nature.
- [ ] **Positive definite Hessian** $\Rightarrow$ Local **MINIMUM**.
- [ ] **Negative definite Hessian** $\Rightarrow$ Local **MAXIMUM**.
- [ ] **Indefinite Hessian** $\Rightarrow$ **SADDLE POINT** (no extremum).
- [ ] **Semi-definite Hessian** $\Rightarrow$ **Inconclusive** (higher-order test needed).
- [ ] Principal minor test: $\Delta_k > 0\ \forall k$ $\Rightarrow$ PD; $(-1)^k \Delta_k > 0\ \forall k$ $\Rightarrow$ ND.
- [ ] Alternative test: All eigenvalues of $H$ have the same sign (positive $\Rightarrow$ PD, negative $\Rightarrow$ ND).
- [ ] For a **single variable** function: $f'(x^*) = 0$ and $f''(x^*) > 0$ (min) or $< 0$ (max).
- [ ] The Hessian matrix is **symmetric** for $C^2$ functions (Clairaut/Schwarz theorem).
- [ ] Always **substitute back** to compute the optimal function value $f(\mathbf{x}^*)$.
- [ ] The method gives **exact analytical solutions** — no iteration required.
- [ ] Limitations: Cannot handle non-differentiable, discontinuous, or constrained problems.
- [ ] In KTU 2024 Scheme, expect 14-mark questions combining 2-variable and 3-variable problems with full Hessian classification.
- [ ] Practice both **minimization** and **maximization** problems — sign of Hessian flips accordingly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples Illustrating the Tabular Method of Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT867 - ENGINEERING OPTIMIZATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples Illustrating the Tabular Method of Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
The **Tabular Method of Solution** in Engineering Optimization refers to the systematic, matrix-based procedure used to solve **Linear Programming Problems (LPPs)** through the **Simplex Method**, organized within a structured **Simplex Tableau**. Each iteration of the algorithm is recorded as a fresh tableau, allowing the decision-maker to algebraically transition from one **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** to an adjacent, more optimal one until the **optimality condition** is satisfied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Simplex Tableau is the **canonical exam tool** in KTU PERUT867. Every iteration is a "candidate solution" tabulated in a single matrix — students must master its construction, pivot mechanics, and termination test.

### Key Terminology
- **Basic Variables (BV):** Variables currently forming the solution vertex (one per constraint).
- **Non-Basic Variables (NBV):** Variables held at zero in the current BFS.
- **Pivot Element:** The intersection cell of the **entering** column and **leaving** row — the heart of the iteration.
- **Slack Variables ($s_1, s_2, \dots$):** Added to convert "$\leq$" constraints into equations of the standard form.
- **Surplus & Artificial Variables:** Used for "$\geq$" and "$=$" constraints in the **Big-M** or **Two-Phase** variants.
- **Cj – Zj row (Net Evaluation Row):** The row that dictates whether the current solution is optimal.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the simplex tableau as a "GPS for Optimization."** You are standing at corner A of a polygonal feasible region. The tableau's `Cj – Zj` row is your **compass** — it tells you which direction (which non-basic variable to bring in) leads to the steepest gain. The **Minimum Ratio Test** is your **distance gauge** — it tells you how far you can walk in that direction before hitting a constraint boundary. Each tableau is a snapshot of your new position. You keep walking until your compass shows no positive direction left — that's the **summit**, i.e., the optimum.

### Why the Tabular Form?
The tabular form is preferred over pure algebra because it:
1. **Standardizes** every iteration, making it auditable.
2. **Enables mechanical computation** (especially via row operations).
