3. **Self-documents** the BFS, objective value, and feasibility simultaneously.
4. **Aligns with KTU valuation keys**, where partial credit is given for **tabular correctness** even if a sign error occurs in arithmetic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units/Constants:** No physical constants appear here — the method is **unit-agnostic**, but conventionally objective values are reported in monetary units (₹, $) and resource RHS values in available units (hours, kg, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of the Tabular Method
The complete procedure is broken into **6 mechanical steps** for every iteration:

1. **Formulate the Standard Form:**
   - Maximize $Z = \sum C_j x_j$ subject to equality constraints with $x_j \geq 0$.
   - Add slack variables to "$\leq$" constraints so that an **initial BFS** is trivially $(0,0,\dots)$.

2. **Build the Initial Simplex Tableau** with the following anatomy:
   - **Cj row** (top): objective coefficients of all variables.
   - **Basis / Cb columns**: the variable names currently in the basis and their objective coefficients.
   - **Body**: coefficients of constraints in terms of decision + slack variables.
   - **RHS column** ($X_B$): the current solution's resource consumption.
   - **Ratio column** (computed per iteration): $\theta_i = \dfrac{X_{B_i}}{a_{ip}}$ for the entering column.
   - **Zj row**: $Z_j = \sum C_{B_i} \cdot a_{ij}$.
   - **Cj – Zj row** (Net Evaluation): the **decision row**.

3. **Optimality Test:**
   - For **maximization**: optimal iff all $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$.
   - For **minimization**: optimal iff all $C_j - Z_j \geq 0$.

4. **Select Entering Variable (Pivot Column):**
   - For max: the column with the **largest positive** $C_j - Z_j$.
   - For min: the column with the **most negative** $C_j - Z_j$.

5. **Select Leaving Variable (Pivot Row) via Minimum Ratio Rule:**
   - Compute $\theta_i = \dfrac{X_{B_i}}{a_{ip}}$ for all $a_{ip} > 0$.
   - Pick the row with the **smallest non-negative** $\theta_i$.

6. **Perform Gauss-Jordan Row Operations:**
   - **New Pivot Row** = (Old Pivot Row) ÷ (Pivot Element).
   - **All Other Rows**: $R_{\text{new}} = R_{\text{old}} - (\text{pivot column coefficient}) \times (\text{New Pivot Row})$.
   - Return to Step 3.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Operation | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Form Conversion ($\leq$) | $a_{i1}x_1 + a_{i2}x_2 + s_i = b_i$ | $s_i \geq 0$ is the slack |
| Objective Function (Z-row) | $Z_j = \displaystyle\sum_{i \in \text{Basis}} C_{B_i} \cdot a_{ij}$ | Computed for **every** $j$ |
| Net Evaluation Row | $C_j - Z_j$ | Test of optimality |
| Optimality (Max) | $C_j - Z_j \leq 0 \;\;\forall j$ | Stop & read off basis |
| Optimality (Min) | $C_j - Z_j \geq 0 \;\;\forall j$ | Stop & read off basis |
| Minimum Ratio Test | $\theta_{\min} = \displaystyle\min_{i: a_{ip}>0} \left\lvert \dfrac{X_{B_i}}{a_{ip}} \right\rvert$ | Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation; $\theta \geq 0$ only |
| Pivot Element | $a_{p,q}$ where col $q$ = entering, row $p$ = leaving | Strictly **positive** |
| New Pivot Row | $R_p^{\text{new}} = \dfrac{R_p^{\text{old}}}{a_{p,q}}$ | Normalize to 1 |
| New Non-Pivot Row | $R_i^{\text{new}} = R_i^{\text{old}} - a_{i,q} \cdot R_p^{\text{new}}$ | Zero out pivot column |
| Optimal $Z$ Value | $Z^* = \displaystyle\sum_{i \in \text{Basis}} C_{B_i} \cdot X_{B_i}$ | Read from RHS $\times$ Cb |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
The tabular simplex is the workhorse behind:
- **Production planning** in manufacturing industries.
- **Diet & blending problems** in food engineering.
- **Transportation & assignment** in logistics.
- **Refinery yield optimization** in chemical engineering.
- **Power dispatch** in electrical grid optimization.
- **Mix design** in civil engineering (concrete, asphalt).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters to KTU examiners:** The tableau is the **only artifact** against which they value. A perfectly framed problem solved with a one-number error in the tableau still fetches 80% marks. A perfectly correct algebraic answer without tableau loses 40% marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Example 1: A Two-Variable Maximization Problem
**Problem Statement:**
A small engineering workshop produces two products, P1 and P2. Each unit of P1 yields a profit of **₹3**, and each unit of P2 yields **₹2**. Production is constrained by:
- Machine-A capacity: $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$ (hours).
- Machine-B capacity: $x_1 - x_2 \leq 2$ (hours).
- Non-negativity: $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$.

**Formulate and solve using the Simplex Tabular Method.**

---

#### Step 1 — Convert to Standard Form
Add slack variables $s_1, s_2$ to convert inequalities to equalities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } Z &= 3x_1 + 2x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2 \\
\text{s.t. }\quad x_1 + x_2 + s_1 &= 4 \\
x_1 - x_2 + s_2 &= 2 \\
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The initial BFS is $s_1 = 4$, $s_2 = 2$, with $x_1 = x_2 = 0$.

---

#### Step 2 — Initial Simplex Tableau

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|c|}
\hline
C_j \rightarrow & & 3 & 2 & 0 & 0 & & \\
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & X_B & \theta \\
\hline
0 & s_1 & 1 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 4 & 4/1 = 4 \\
0 & s_2 & 1 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 & 2/1 = \mathbf{2} \leftarrow \\
\hline
& Z_j & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \\
& C_j - Z_j & \mathbf{3} & 2 & 0 & 0 & & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- The $Z_j$ row is computed as $Z_j = \sum C_{B_i} \cdot a_{ij}$. Since $C_B = (0, 0)$, every $Z_j = 0$.
- $C_j - Z_j = (3, 2, 0, 0)$ — all non-negative, so **not optimal**.
- **Entering column:** $x_1$ (largest positive $C_j - Z_j = 3$).
- **Leaving row:** $s_2$ (smallest $\theta = 2$).
- **Pivot element:** $a_{21} = 1$.

---

#### Step 3 — Iteration 1 (Row Operations)
The pivot element is already **1**, so the new pivot row is unchanged.

**Update R1 (old $s_1$ row):** Subtract the new $x_1$ row multiplied by the pivot-column entry 1.
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1^{\text{new}} &= R_1 - 1 \cdot R_2 \\
&= (1, 1, 1, 0 \mid 4) - (1, -1, 0, 1 \mid 2) \\
&= (0, 2, 1, -1 \mid 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**New Tableau after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
C_j \rightarrow & & 3 & 2 & 0 & 0 & \\
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & X_B \\
\hline
3 & x_1 & 1 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
0 & s_1 & 0 & 2 & 1 & -1 & 2 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 3 & -3 & 0 & 3 & 6 \\
& C_j - Z_j & 0 & \mathbf{5} & 0 & 3 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- $Z_j$ row: $Z_j = 3 \cdot a_{1j} + 0 \cdot a_{2j} = 3a_{1j}$.
- $C_j - Z_j = (0, 5, 0, 3)$ — still not optimal ($x_2$ enters).

---

#### Step 4 — Iteration 2
- **Entering column:** $x_2$ ($C_j - Z_j = 5$).
- **Ratio test:** $\theta_{x_1} = 2/(-1)$ (negative, ignore); $\theta_{s_1} = 2/2 = 1$ ✓.
- **Pivot element:** $a_{22} = 2$ (the 2 in $s_1$ row, $x_2$ column).

**Normalize new pivot row ($R_2$):**
$$
R_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{R_2}{2} = (0, 1, 0.5, -0.5 \mid 1)
$$

**Update old $x_1$ row ($R_1$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1^{\text{new}} &= R_1 - (-1) \cdot R_2^{\text{new}} \\
&= (1, -1, 0, 1 \mid 2) + (0, 1, 0.5, -0.5 \mid 1) \\
&= (1, 0, 0.5, 0.5 \mid 3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
C_j \rightarrow & & 3 & 2 & 0 & 0 & \\
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & X_B \\
\hline
3 & x_1 & 1 & 0 & 0.5 & 0.5 & 3 \\
2 & x_2 & 0 & 1 & 0.5 & -0.5 & 1 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 3 & 2 & 2.5 & 0.5 & \mathbf{11} \\
& C_j - Z_j & 0 & 0 & -2.5 & -0.5 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Optimality Check:** All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ ✓ ⇒ **OPTIMAL**.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 3$, $x_2 = 1$, $s_1 = s_2 = 0$, $Z_{\max} = \mathbf{11}$.

**Verification:**
- $3(3) + 2(1) = 11$ ✓
- $3 + 1 = 4 \leq 4$ ✓
- $3 - 1 = 2 \leq 2$ ✓

---

### Example 2: A Four-Constraint Maximization Problem
**Problem Statement:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } Z &= 5x_1 + 4x_2 \\
\text{s.t. }\quad 6x_1 + 4x_2 &\leq 24 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 &\leq 6 \\
-x_1 + x_2 &\leq 1 \\
x_2 &\leq 2 \\
x_1, x_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Standard Form** (add $s_1, s_2, s_3, s_4$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
6x_1 + 4x_2 + s_1 &= 24 \\
x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 &= 6 \\
-x_1 + x_2 + s_3 &= 1 \\
x_2 + s_4 &= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccccc|c|c|}
\hline
C_j & & 5 & 4 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & & \\
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & s_3 & s_4 & X_B & \theta \\
\hline
0 & s_1 & 6 & 4 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 24 & 4 \\
0 & s_2 & 1 & 2 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
0 & s_3 & -1 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 1 & - \\
0 & s_4 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 & - \\
\hline
& Z_j & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \\
& C_j - Z_j & \mathbf{5} & 4 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- **Entering:** $x_1$ ($C_j-Z_j = 5$). **Leaving:** $s_1$ ($\theta = 24/6 = 4$).
- **Pivot element:** $a_{11} = 6$.

**Step 3 — Iteration 1:**
- $R_1^{\text{new}} = R_1 / 6 = (1, 2/3, 1/6, 0, 0, 0 \mid 4)$.
- $R_2^{\text{new}} = R_2 - 1 \cdot R_1^{\text{new}} = (0, 4/3, -1/6, 1, 0, 0 \mid 2)$.
- $R_3^{\text{new}} = R_3 - (-1) \cdot R_1^{\text{new}} = (0, 5/3, 1/6, 0, 1, 0 \mid 5)$.
- $R_4^{\text{new}} = R_4 = (0, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1 \mid 2)$.

**Tableau after Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccccc|c|}
\hline
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & s_3 & s_4 & X_B \\
\hline
5 & x_1 & 1 & 2/3 & 1/6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 4 \\
0 & s_2 & 0 & 4/3 & -1/6 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
0 & s_3 & 0 & 5/3 & 1/6 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 5 \\
0 & s_4 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 5 & 10/3 & 5/6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 20 \\
& C_j - Z_j & 0 & \mathbf{2/3} & -5/6 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

- **Entering:** $x_2$ ($C_j-Z_j = 2/3$).
- **Ratios:** $4/(2/3) = 6$, $2/(4/3) = 1.5$ ✓, $5/(5/3) = 3$, $2/1 = 2$.
- **Leaving:** $s_2$ (min $\theta = 1.5$). **Pivot element:** $4/3$.

**Step 4 — Iteration 2:**
- $R_2^{\text{new}} = R_2 \times 3/4 = (0, 1, -1/8, 3/4, 0, 0 \mid 1.5)$.
- $R_1^{\text{new}} = R_1 - (2/3)R_2^{\text{new}} = (1, 0, 1/4, -1/2, 0, 0 \mid 3)$.
- $R_3^{\text{new}} = R_3 - (5/3)R_2^{\text{new}} = (0, 0, 3/8, -5/4, 1, 0 \mid 2.5)$.
- $R_4^{\text{new}} = R_4 - 1 \cdot R_2^{\text{new}} = (0, 0, 1/8, -3/4, 0, 1 \mid 0.5)$.

**Final Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccccc|c|}
\hline
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & s_3 & s_4 & X_B \\
\hline
5 & x_1 & 1 & 0 & 1/4 & -1/2 & 0 & 0 & 3 \\
4 & x_2 & 0 & 1 & -1/8 & 3/4 & 0 & 0 & 1.5 \\
0 & s_3 & 0 & 0 & 3/8 & -5/4 & 1 & 0 & 2.5 \\
0 & s_4 & 0 & 0 & 1/8 & -3/4 & 0 & 1 & 0.5 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 5 & 4 & 3/4 & 1/2 & 0 & 0 & \mathbf{21} \\
& C_j - Z_j & 0 & 0 & -3/4 & -1/2 & 0 & 0 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Optimality Check:** All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ ✓ ⇒ **OPTIMAL**.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 3$, $x_2 = 1.5$, $Z_{\max} = \mathbf{21}$.

**Verification:**
- $5(3) + 4(1.5) = 21$ ✓
- $6(3) + 4(1.5) = 24 \leq 24$ ✓
- $3 + 2(1.5) = 6 \leq 6$ ✓
- $-3 + 1.5 = -1.5 \leq 1$ ✓
- $1.5 \leq 2$ ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Simplex Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Standard Form LPP"] --> B["Construct Initial Simplex Tableau"]
    B --> C["Compute Zj row and Cj - Zj row"]
    C --> D{"Is Cj - Zj le 0 for all j?"}
    D -- "Yes, STOP" --> E["Report Optimal Solution and Z*"]
    D -- "No, continue" --> F["Identify Entering Variable: max Cj - Zj column"]
    F --> G["Apply Minimum Ratio Rule: pick smallest non-negative XBi divided by aip"]
    G --> H{"Is there a valid leaving row?"}
    H -- "No" --> I["Unbounded LPP - Exit"]
    H -- "Yes" --> J["Identify Pivot Element apq"]
    J --> K["Normalize new pivot row: Rp equals Rp divided by apq"]
    K --> L["Update all other rows: Ri equals Ri minus aiq times Rp new"]
    L --> M["Update Basis column and Cb values"]
    M --> C
```

### 4.2 Anatomy of a Simplex Tableau (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TBL["Simplex Tableau Anatomy"]
        H["Cj Row: Objective Coefficients"]
        CB1["C_B Column: Cost of Basic Variables"]
        BA1["Basis Column: Names of Basic Variables"]
        B1["Body Matrix: Constraint Coefficients a_ij"]
        XB1["X_B Column: Current RHS Solution Values"]
        RA1["Theta Column: Ratio X_B divided by a_ip"]
        ZJ["Z_j Row: Sum of C_B times a_ij"]
        CJZJ["C_j minus Z_j Row: Net Evaluation"]
    end
    H --> ZJ
    CB1 --> ZJ
    ZJ --> CJZJ
    H --> CJZJ
    B1 --> ZJ
    XB1 --> RA1
    B1 --> RA1
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of an Iteration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INP["Input Layer: Current Tableau T_k"]
        I1["Basis Vector B_k"]
        I2["Body Matrix A_k"]
        I3["RHS Vector b_k"]
    end
    subgraph DEC["Decision Layer"]
        D1["Compute Cj - Zj"]
        D2["Optimality Test"]
        D3["Entering Column Selection"]
        D4["Leaving Row via Min Ratio"]
    end
    subgraph TRX["Transformation Layer: Gauss-Jordan"]
        R1["Normalize Pivot Row"]
        R2["Eliminate Pivot Column Entries"]
    end
    subgraph OUT["Output Layer: Next Tableau T_k plus 1"]
        O1["New Basis B_k plus 1"]
        O2["New RHS b_k plus 1"]
        O3["New C_B values"]
    end
    INP --> DEC --> TRX --> OUT
    OUT --> INP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** The inner loop `OUT → INP` represents the iterative nature of the simplex method. KTU examiners often award 2 bonus marks for clearly annotating the **entering/leaving variable** and **pivot element** in the final answer script.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*In the Simplex Tabular Method, explain the role of the Cj – Zj row. What does it mean when all entries in this row are non-positive?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Cj – Zj row** (also called the **net evaluation row**) is computed as $C_j - Z_j = C_j - \sum C_{B_i} a_{ij}$. It serves **two purposes** in the simplex tableau:
1. **Optimality Indicator:** When solving a **maximization** problem, all entries $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ implies the current BFS is **optimal** — no non-basic variable can improve the objective by entering the basis.
2. **Entering Variable Selector:** The most positive $C_j - Z_j$ identifies the **steepest improving direction**; its column becomes the next pivot column.

Hence, all $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ signals that the algorithm has reached the **global optimum** for a maximization LPP. **(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the "Minimum Ratio Rule" in the Simplex Tabular Method? Why must negative ratios be ignored?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **Minimum Ratio Rule** is used to select the **leaving variable** from the current basis when a new variable (identified by the entering column) is to be introduced.

The ratio for each row is computed as:
$$
\theta_i = \frac{X_{B_i}}{a_{ip}}
$$
where $a_{ip}$ is the entering column's coefficient in row $i$. The row with the **smallest non-negative $\theta$** is chosen as the pivot row.

**Negative ratios must be ignored** because a negative $a_{ip}$ would cause the corresponding basic variable to **increase** (rather than decrease to zero) as the entering variable grows, violating the feasibility requirement that basic variables remain non-negative. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PERUT867]**
A manufacturing firm produces two products, A and B. Each unit of A requires **2 hours** of machining and **1 hour** of finishing, yielding a profit of **₹4**. Each unit of B requires **1 hour** of machining and **3 hours** of finishing, yielding **₹5**. Available capacities: machining = **40 hours**, finishing = **45 hours**.

**(a)** Formulate the LPP and convert to standard form. Add all necessary slack variables. **(3 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve the problem completely using the **Simplex Tabular Method**, showing **all** tableaus and row operations. Identify the optimal production plan and maximum profit. **(11 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A:**

**(a) Formulation (3 Marks):**

Let $x_1$ = units of A, $x_2$ = units of B.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } Z &= 4x_1 + 5x_2 \\
\text{s.t. }\quad 2x_1 + x_2 &\leq 40 \quad \text{(machining)} \\
x_1 + 3x_2 &\leq 45 \quad \text{(finishing)} \\
x_1, x_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard Form** (slacks $s_1, s_2$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 &= 40 \\
x_1 + 3x_2 + s_2 &= 45 \\
\text{Max } Z &= 4x_1 + 5x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Tabular Solution (11 Marks):**

**Initial Tableau:**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|c|}
\hline
C_j & & 4 & 5 & 0 & 0 & & \\
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & X_B & \theta \\
\hline
0 & s_1 & 2 & 1 & 1 & 0 & 40 & 40 \\
0 & s_2 & 1 & 3 & 0 & 1 & 45 & \mathbf{15} \leftarrow \\
\hline
& Z_j & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \\
& C_j - Z_j & 4 & \mathbf{5} & 0 & 0 & & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Entering:** $x_2$ (Cj - Zj = 5). **Leaving:** $s_2$ (min $\theta = 15$). **Pivot:** $a_{22} = 3$.

**Iteration 1:**
- $R_2^{\text{new}} = R_2/3 = (1/3, 1, 0, 1/3 \mid 15)$.
- $R_1^{\text{new}} = R_1 - 1 \cdot R_2^{\text{new}} = (5/3, 0, 1, -1/3 \mid 25)$.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & X_B \\
\hline
0 & s_1 & 5/3 & 0 & 1 & -1/3 & 25 \\
5 & x_2 & 1/3 & 1 & 0 & 1/3 & 15 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 5/3 & 5 & 0 & 5/3 & 75 \\
& C_j - Z_j & \mathbf{7/3} & 0 & 0 & -5/3 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Entering:** $x_1$ ($C_j-Z_j = 7/3$). **Ratios:** $25/(5/3) = \mathbf{15}$ ✓, $15/(1/3) = 45$. **Leaving:** $s_1$. **Pivot:** $5/3$.

**Iteration 2:**
- $R_1^{\text{new}} = R_1 \cdot 3/5 = (1, 0, 3/5, -1/5 \mid 15)$.
- $R_2^{\text{new}} = R_2 - (1/3)R_1^{\text{new}} = (0, 1, -1/5, 2/5 \mid 10)$.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccc|c|}
\hline
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & s_1 & s_2 & X_B \\
\hline
4 & x_1 & 1 & 0 & 3/5 & -1/5 & 15 \\
5 & x_2 & 0 & 1 & -1/5 & 2/5 & 10 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 4 & 5 & 7/5 & 6/5 & \mathbf{110} \\
& C_j - Z_j & 0 & 0 & -7/5 & -6/5 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Optimality:** All $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$ ✓ ⇒ **OPTIMAL**.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 15$, $x_2 = 10$, $Z_{\max} = \mathbf{₹110}$. **(11 Marks)**

**Verification:** $4(15) + 5(10) = 60 + 50 = 110$ ✓; $2(15)+10 = 40 \leq 40$ ✓; $15+3(10) = 45 \leq 45$ ✓.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
A diet problem requires two nutrients, N1 and N2. Food F1 provides 2 units of N1 and 3 units of N2 per kg, costing ₹5. Food F2 provides 4 units of N1 and 2 units of N2 per kg, costing ₹4. Minimum daily requirements: N1 ≥ 8, N2 ≥ 6.

**(a)** Formulate the minimization LPP and convert to standard form using the **Big-M method** (introduce surplus & artificial variables). **(5 Marks)**

**(b)** Solve using the Simplex Tabular Method and report the minimum cost diet. **(9 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B:**

**(a) Formulation (5 Marks):**
Let $x_1, x_2$ = kg of F1, F2.
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min } Z &= 5x_1 + 4x_2 \\
\text{s.t. }\quad 2x_1 + 4x_2 &\geq 8 \\
3x_1 + 2x_2 &\geq 6 \\
x_1, x_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Big-M Standard Form** (surplus $e_i$, artificial $A_i$, $M \gg 0$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + 4x_2 - e_1 + A_1 &= 8 \\
3x_1 + 2x_2 - e_2 + A_2 &= 6 \\
\text{Min } Z &= 5x_1 + 4x_2 + 0e_1 + 0e_2 + M A_1 + M A_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Tabular Solution (9 Marks):**

**Initial Tableau (Min → multiply Cj - Zj logic):**

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccccc|c|c|}
\hline
C_j & & 5 & 4 & 0 & 0 & M & M & & \\
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & e_1 & e_2 & A_1 & A_2 & X_B & \theta \\
\hline
M & A_1 & 2 & 4 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 8 & 2 \\
M & A_2 & 3 & 2 & 0 & -1 & 0 & 1 & 6 & 3 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 5M & 6M & -M & -M & M & M & 14M & \\
& C_j - Z_j & 5-5M & 4-6M & M & M & 0 & 0 & & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Note:** For minimization, the most negative $C_j - Z_j$ enters. Here, $4-6M$ is the most negative (assuming $M$ is large).

**Entering:** $x_2$. **Ratios:** $8/4 = \mathbf{2}$ ✓, $6/2 = 3$. **Leaving:** $A_1$. **Pivot:** 4.

**Iteration 1:**
- $R_1^{\text{new}} = R_1/4 = (1/2, 1, -1/4, 0, 1/4, 0 \mid 2)$.
- $R_2^{\text{new}} = R_2 - 2R_1^{\text{new}} = (2, 0, 1/2, -1, -1/2, 1 \mid 2)$.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccccc|c|}
\hline
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & e_1 & e_2 & A_1 & A_2 & X_B \\
\hline
4 & x_2 & 1/2 & 1 & -1/4 & 0 & 1/4 & 0 & 2 \\
M & A_2 & 2 & 0 & 1/2 & -1 & -1/2 & 1 & 2 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 2+2M & 4 & -1-M/2 & -M & 1-M/2 & M & 8+2M \\
& C_j - Z_j & 3-2M & 0 & 1+M/2 & 0 & M/2-1 & 0 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Entering:** $x_1$ ($C_j-Z_j = 3-2M$, most negative). **Ratios:** $2/(1/2) = 4$, $2/2 = \mathbf{1}$ ✓. **Leaving:** $A_2$. **Pivot:** 2.

**Iteration 2:**
- $R_2^{\text{new}} = R_2/2 = (1, 0, 1/4, -1/2, -1/4, 1/2 \mid 1)$.
- $R_1^{\text{new}} = R_1 - (1/2)R_2^{\text{new}} = (0, 1, -3/8, 1/4, 3/8, -1/4 \mid 1.5)$.

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|cccccc|c|}
\hline
C_B & \text{Basis} & x_1 & x_2 & e_1 & e_2 & A_1 & A_2 & X_B \\
\hline
4 & x_2 & 0 & 1 & -3/8 & 1/4 & 3/8 & -1/4 & 1.5 \\
5 & x_1 & 1 & 0 & 1/4 & -1/2 & -1/4 & 1/2 & 1 \\
\hline
& Z_j & 5 & 4 & 1/2 & -3/2 & 1/2 & 3/2 & 11 \\
& C_j - Z_j & 0 & 0 & -1/2 & 3/2 & -1/2 & M-3/2 & \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Optimality (Min):** All $C_j - Z_j \geq 0$ ✓ ⇒ **OPTIMAL**.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 1$ kg, $x_2 = 1.5$ kg, $Z_{\min} = \mathbf{₹11}$. **(9 Marks)**

**Verification:** $5(1) + 4(1.5) = 11$ ✓; $2(1)+4(1.5) = 8 \geq 8$ ✓; $3(1)+2(1.5) = 6 \geq 6$ ✓.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in the Tabular Method:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Ratio Column header** (KTU deducts 1 mark): The $\theta$ column must be explicitly computed in **every** iteration where an entering column exists.
> 2. **Sign confusion in $C_j - Z_j$**: For max, **stop when all are $\leq 0$**; for min, **stop when all are $\geq 0$**. Mixing these up forfeits 3–5 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the negative ratio rule**: If you accidentally pick a row with negative $\theta$, your next tableau will have **negative RHS** ⇒ infeasible BFS ⇒ 4-mark penalty.
> 4. **Big-M initialization error**: The initial basis for Big-M problems **must** be the artificial variables — never the slacks/surplus alone.
> 5. **Skipping row operation steps**: KTU values **row-operation clarity**. Showing $R_i^{\text{new}} = R_i - a_{iq} \cdot R_p^{\text{new}}$ explicitly earns full marks; jumping directly to the next tableau without arithmetic trail loses 2 marks per iteration.
> 6. **Wrong optimality check** for degenerate BFS: Even if $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$, re-check that the **basis is feasible** (RHS $\geq 0$). Degeneracy cycling is rare in KTU problems but penalizes 2 marks if missed.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Tabular Method of Solution**

- [ ] **Standard form is mandatory**: Slack for "$\leq$", Surplus + Artificial for "$\geq$" or "$=$".
- [ ] **Cj row** sits on top; **Cj – Zj row** sits at the bottom — together they form the **decision bookends** of the tableau.
- [ ] **Zj** is always a **row vector** computed column-wise: $Z_j = \sum C_{B_i} a_{ij}$.
- [ ] **Optimality for Max:** all $C_j - Z_j \leq 0$. **For Min:** all $C_j - Z_j \geq 0$.
- [ ] **Entering column** = max $C_j - Z_j$ (max) or min $C_j - Z_j$ (min).
- [ ] **Leaving row** = minimum **non-negative** ratio $\theta = X_B / a_{ip}$.
- [ ] **Pivot element** is at the intersection; **always strictly positive**.
- [ ] **Gauss-Jordan steps**: (i) normalize pivot row, (ii) zero out the rest of the pivot column.
- [ ] **Final answer is read off** the Basis column and the RHS ($X_B$) column.
- [ ] **Z\*** equals the bottom-right value of the Zj row.
- [ ] **Always verify** by plugging the solution back into the original constraints.
- [ ] **Big-M method** uses penalty $M$ for artificial variables; optimal solution **must have all artificial variables = 0**.
- [ ] **Two-phase method** is an alternative to Big-M — Phase I minimizes artificials; Phase II solves the real objective.
- [ ] **Unbounded LPP**: no valid leaving row (all ratios $\leq 0$ or negative).
- [ ] **Infeasible LPP (Big-M)**: optimal solution has non-zero artificial variable.
- [ ] **Degenerate BFS**: some $X_{B_i} = 0$ in the optimal tableau — possible cycling (rare in KTU).

> **Final Tip:** In the KTU answer script, **draw the tableau with ruled boxes** — examiners literally scan for boxed tableaus. A clean, well-aligned tableau is worth **at least 2 visual-impression marks** even before content evaluation.
